topics.title: Functions and Characteristics - Types of Pavement: Flexible Pavement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET745 - PAVEMENT CONSTRUCTION AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functions and Characteristics - Types of Pavement: Flexible Pavement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Pavement?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (IRC:37-2018 / Khanna & Justo)**
> A **pavement** is a durable, engineered structural surface constructed over a prepared subgrade (natural soil) whose primary functions are to transmit the wheel load of vehicles to the subgrade safely, provide a smooth riding surface, adequate skid resistance, and resist the deteriorating effects of traffic, climate, and environment throughout its design life.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the term *pavement* is treated as the **complete load-bearing structure** — not merely the visible top layer. It is an integrated multi-layer system whose design life for major highways in India is typically **15 years** (as per IRC standards), with the design traffic measured in **million standard axles (msa)**.

## 1.2 Functions of a Pavement

A pavement must perform the following simultaneous engineering functions:

| Sl. No. | Function | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Load Distribution** | Spread the concentrated wheel load over a larger area of subgrade so the stress on soil remains within its bearing capacity. |
| 2 | **Riding Quality / Surface Smoothness** | Provide a uniformly even surface to ensure vehicle comfort at design speed. Measured via **International Roughness Index (IRI, m/km)**. |
| 3 | **Skid Resistance** | Offer sufficient surface friction (coefficient of friction ≥ 0.35–0.40 wet) for safe braking. |
| 4 | **Drainage** | Quickly remove surface and infiltrated water to prevent structural weakening. |
| 5 | **Structural Protection** | Protect the subgrade from frost, erosion, and excessive deformation. |
| 6 | **Durability / Service Life** | Resist weathering, abrasion, and fatigue cracking for the design period. |
| 7 | **Light Reflection & Visibility** | Provide adequate contrast for night driving safety. |

## 1.3 Types of Pavement — KTU Classification

> [!NOTE]
> As per the KTU 2024 syllabus, pavements are broadly classified into three families:

1. **Flexible Pavement** — Bituminous / Asphalt layers over granular base & sub-base.
2. **Rigid Pavement** — Cement concrete slab (PQC) over a prepared base.
3. **Semi-Rigid / Composite Pavement** — Bituminous layer over a cementitious (or pozzolanic) base, or white-topping.

*This module focuses exclusively on **Flexible Pavement**.*

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Onion Layer" Concept

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine stacking a sequence of foam mattresses on soft mud. The top mattress (the asphalt) feels the heel of your foot directly. By the time the pressure reaches the mud (subgrade), it has been spread over a wide area, so the mud does not yield. A **flexible pavement behaves exactly like this** — strong, expensive material at the top (where loads are intense but localized) and progressively weaker, cheaper material as you go deeper (where stresses are spread but smaller).

This explains why flexible pavements are designed as a **multi-layer elastic system** with quality decreasing with depth — a direct contrast to rigid pavements, which act like a stiff beam.

## 1.5 Formal Definition of Flexible Pavement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Definition:**
> A **flexible pavement** is a pavement structure composed of one or more courses of bituminous-treated or untreated (granular) materials placed over a prepared subgrade, in which the structural capacity is derived from the **load-distributing property of the layered system**. The pavement deflects (flexes) under traffic loading, and each layer redistributes the wheel load over a wider area until the stress intensity at the subgrade level is reduced to a tolerable limit.

**Key Identifier Word:** *Flexes* (elastically deforms) under load.

---

## 1.6 Distinguishing Flexible vs. Rigid Pavement at a Glance

| Parameter | Flexible Pavement | Rigid Pavement |
|---|---|---|
| Flexural rigidity | Negligible — bends under load | High — acts as a beam/slab |
| Load transfer | Through layer-to-layer grain interlock & stiffness | Through slab action / bending |
| Stiffness Modulus | Decreases with depth | Constant (concrete) or higher at top |
| Design philosophy | Layered elastic theory (IRC:37) | Plate theory / Westergaard's solution (IRC:58) |
| Typical life | **15 years** | **30–40 years** |
| Initial cost | Lower | Higher (1.5–2×) |
| Maintenance | Frequent (overlay) | Low |
| Surface | Black (bitumen) | Grey (concrete) |
| Load-duration behaviour | Visco-elastic | Elastic |

---

## 1.7 Bituminous Surfacing — The Visible Top

The topmost layer of a flexible pavement (the *bituminous surfacing*) may itself be of three sub-types used widely in India:

- **Bituminous Surface Dressing (BSD)** — single/double coat, for low-volume roads.
- **Premix Carpet (PMC)** — 20 mm thick, with Seal Coat on top.
- **Bituminous Concrete (BC)** / **Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM)** / **Asphalt Concrete (AC)** — modern preferred specifications under **MoRTH (Ministry of Road Transport and Highways)** for NH/SH.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test the *distinction between "Bituminous Macadam", "Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM)", and "Bituminous Concrete (BC)"* — memorise their nominal size and grading as per **MoRTH Table 500-14, 500-17 & 500-19**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Composition of a Flexible Pavement

A typical flexible pavement for an Indian National Highway (as per **MoRTH Specifications, 5th Revision**) consists of the following courses from top to bottom:

| Course (Top → Bottom) | Typical Material | Approx. Thickness | Primary Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Bituminous Wearing Course (BC / SDBC)** | Bituminous Concrete | 25–40 mm | Skid resistance, smooth riding, waterproofing |
| **2. Bituminous Binder Course (DBM / BM)** | Dense Bituminous Macadam | 50–100 mm | Main load-spreading bituminous layer |
| **3. Granular Base Course (WBM / WMM / CTSB)** | Crushed stone aggregate | 150–300 mm | Structural load distribution |
| **4. Granular Sub-base Course** | Granular material (GSB) | 150–300 mm | Drainage + further load spread |
| **5. Subgrade (Prepared Soil)** | Compacted natural soil / improved soil | Semi-infinite | Foundation; receives reduced stress |

> [!NOTE]
> **Total flexible pavement thickness** for an NH with 30 msa traffic may range from **600 mm to 1000 mm** depending on subgrade CBR.

## 2.2 Functions of Each Layer — Engineering Logic

### 2.2.1 Bituminous Surfacing / Wearing Course
- **Why?** The contact stress under a truck tyre is **~0.56 MPa (≈5.6 kg/cm²)**; this layer must withstand:
  - **Vertical compressive stress**
  - **Tensile (bending) strain at the bottom** causing fatigue cracking
  - **Shear stress** causing rutting
- **How?** Achieved through dense-graded bituminous concrete with **3–5% bitumen** (VG-30 grade for hot climates in Kerala as per IS:73).

### 2.2.2 Bituminous Binder Course
- **Why?** Reduces the vertical stress arriving at the granular base from ~1.5 MPa to ~0.3 MPa.
- **How?** Stiffer layer (DBM with 4–5% bitumen) provides structural strength.

### 2.2.3 Granular Base Course
- **Why?** Further reduces stress on subgrade to **<0.07 MPa (subgrade bearing capacity)**.
- **How?** Through aggregate interlock and high stiffness. Two common types:
  - **Wet Mix Macadam (WMM)** — modern, more uniform.
  - **Water Bound Macadam (WBM)** — older, partially phased out.

### 2.2.4 Granular Sub-base Course
- **Why?** Acts as a **drainage layer** (low-permeability fines <5%) and protection against frost heave & capillary moisture.
- **How?** Uses locally available, lower-quality material.

### 2.2.5 Subgrade
- **Why?** The foundation — if subgrade fails, the entire pavement fails (pumping, rutting, undulations).
- **How?** Compacted to **≥97% of MDD (Maximum Dry Density)** as per IS:2720; CBR tested per IS:2720 (Part 16).

## 2.3 Stress-Strain Behaviour in Flexible Pavement

When a wheel load $P$ is applied at the surface, the load is transmitted through layers of decreasing stiffness. The principal stresses & strains to be evaluated for design (per IRC:37-2018) are:

1. **Vertical compressive stress ($\sigma_v$) on subgrade** — controls *rutting*.
2. **Horizontal tensile strain ($\epsilon_t$) at the bottom of bituminous layer** — controls *fatigue cracking*.
3. **Vertical compressive strain ($\epsilon_v$) on subgrade** — controls *rutting* (alternative criterion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Repeated Question Theme:** *"How is the wheel load distributed in a flexible pavement?"* — Answer must use the **pyramidal/cone-of-pressure** concept with layered elastic theory.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Boussinesq's vertical stress** under a point load: $\sigma_z = \dfrac{3P}{2\pi z^2}\cos^5\theta$ | Stress at depth $z$ below a point load $P$ (single wheel) | kPa, m, kN |
| 2 | **Equivalent Single Axle Load (ESAL) / Standard Axle** | $1 \text{ ESAL} = 1$ passage of a **single axle with dual wheels, each wheel load = 20 kN** (= 80 kN axle) | kN |
| 3 | **Fourth-power law (Load Equivalence Factor):** $LEF = \left(\dfrac{L}{L_s}\right)^4$ | Ratio of damaging effect of any axle load $L$ to standard axle $L_s = 80$ kN | dimensionless |
| 4 | **Contact pressure:** $p = \dfrac{P}{A_c}$ where $A_c = \pi a^2$ (circular) or $\pi a b$ (elliptical) | Tyre contact pressure, $a$ = half contact length, $b$ = half contact width | kPa |
| 5 | **Subgrade strength:** $CBR = \dfrac{\text{Load at 2.5 mm penetration of test sample}}{\text{Load at 2.5 mm of standard crushed stone}} \times 100\%$ | California Bearing Ratio per IS:2720 Part-16 | % |
| 6 | **Total design traffic (msa):** $N = \dfrac{365 \times [(1+r)^n - 1]}{r} \times A \times D \times F$ | $A$ = commercial vehicles/day, $r$ = growth rate, $n$ = design life, $D$ = lane distribution factor, $F$ = VDF | msa |
| 7 | **IRC:37 chart-based design:** pavement thickness as a function of CBR (%) and design traffic (msa) | Catalogues published by IRC (Plate 1–9) | mm |
| 8 | **Stiffness Modulus of subgrade (Heukelom & Klomp):** $E_{sg} = 10 \times CBR$ (MPa) | Empirical relation for untreated subgrade | MPa |
| 9 | **Stiffness Modulus of granular layer:** $E_{gr} = 0.2 \times h^{0.45} \times E_{sg}$ | $h$ = thickness of granular layer (mm) | MPa |
| 10 | **Effective modulus of bituminous layer:** $E_{bm} = 1000 \times T^{0.15} \times VMA$ | $T$ = temperature, VMA = voids in mineral aggregate | MPa |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not write "$|x|$" inside any markdown table — use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to prevent the table parser from breaking the cell boundary.

## 2.5 Engineering Significance

- The **pyramidal stress distribution** under each wheel explains why pavement design relies on *layered system analysis* rather than a single strength check.
- A pavement with **CBR = 5%** requires ~**3× the thickness** of a pavement with **CBR = 15%** for the same design traffic — subgrade improvement is the most cost-effective design lever.
- In **Kerala's lateritic & alluvial soils** (often CBR 4–8%), subgrade stabilisation with **c/lime/CBR-plus** is common before pavement construction.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of Flexible Pavement Design |
|---|---|
| National Highways (NH-66, NH-544) | Sustains 30–150 msa over 15 years |
| State Highways & Rural Roads (PMGSY) | Cost-effective bituminous alternative to rigid |
| Airport Runways (non-jet aircraft) | Adapted flexible design, but with higher-grade bitumen (VG-40 / Modified bitumen) |
| Urban Arterial Roads | Used where speed is moderate and noise is acceptable |
| Bridge Approaches | Flexible pavement used as transition to avoid slab-end jump |

> [!TIP]
> **Why not always use rigid pavement?** Because **flexible pavements**:
> - Cost less initially
> - Are constructible in stages (stage construction)
> - Can be opened to traffic within 24 hours
> - Are easier to repair and overlay
> - Provide quieter, smoother ride at low–moderate speeds
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vertical Stress at Subgrade Level (Boussinesq's Equation for Single Wheel)

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions
Consider a single wheel load $P$ applied at the surface of a **homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic, semi-infinite mass** (the subgrade).

**Boussinesq's assumptions:**
1. The medium is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic.
2. The material extends to infinity in all horizontal and downward directions.
3. The load is a point load acting on the surface.
4. Self-weight of the medium is neglected.

**Objective:** Determine the vertical normal stress $\sigma_z$ at any point located at depth $z$ and radial distance $r$ from the line of action of the load.

### 3.1.2 Geometric Relations

From the geometry of the right triangle formed by the point load $P$, the point of interest $(r, z)$, and the vertical projection:

$$
R = \sqrt{r^2 + z^2}
$$

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{z}{R} = \frac{z}{\sqrt{r^2 + z^2}}
$$

where:
- $R$ = radial distance from load to point of interest
- $\theta$ = angle between the vertical axis and the line $R$
- $z$ = depth below surface

### 3.1.3 Boussinesq's Expression

The **Boussinesq equation** for vertical stress due to a point load on a semi-infinite elastic medium is:

$$
\sigma_z = \frac{3P}{2\pi z^2} \cos^5\theta
$$

### 3.1.4 Substituting the Geometric Relation

Substitute $\cos\theta = \dfrac{z}{\sqrt{r^2 + z^2}}$ into the above:

$$
\sigma_z = \frac{3P}{2\pi z^2} \left(\frac{z}{\sqrt{r^2 + z^2}}\right)^{5}
$$

$$
\sigma_z = \frac{3P}{2\pi z^2} \cdot \frac{z^5}{(r^2 + z^2)^{5/2}}
$$

$$
\sigma_z = \frac{3P z^3}{2\pi (r^2 + z^2)^{5/2}}
$$

### 3.1.5 Special Case — Stress Directly Below the Load

When the point of interest lies **directly below the load** ($r = 0$):

$$
\sigma_z \big|_{r=0} = \frac{3P z^3}{2\pi (z^2)^{5/2}} = \frac{3P z^3}{2\pi \, z^5} = \frac{3P}{2\pi z^2}
$$

This is the **maximum vertical stress** at depth $z$ on the axis of loading.

### 3.1.6 Influence Factor Notation

The vertical stress is conventionally written using an **influence coefficient** $K$ (Boussinesq's influence factor):

$$
\sigma_z = K \cdot \frac{P}{z^2}
$$

where:

$$
K = \frac{3}{2\pi} \left(\frac{1}{1 + (r/z)^2}\right)^{5/2}
$$

| $r/z$ | 0.0 | 0.2 | 0.5 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $K$ | 0.4775 | 0.4329 | 0.2733 | 0.0844 | 0.0251 | 0.0085 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** As $z$ increases, $\sigma_z$ decreases **inversely with the square of depth**. This mathematically proves why pavement design *adds more layers on top* — each layer "intercepts" and spreads the load before it reaches the weak subgrade.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equivalent Single Axle Load (ESAL) and Fourth-Power Law

### 3.2.1 Concept

Different vehicles with different axle loads cause **different damage** to a flexible pavement. To design uniformly, all axles are converted to a **standard axle load** of **80 kN (single axle with dual tyres, each tyre = 20 kN)**. This conversion uses the **Fourth-Power Law** (AASHTO).

### 3.2.2 The Fourth-Power Law Statement

The damage caused by an axle of magnitude $L$ is proportional to the **fourth power** of the ratio of its load to the standard axle load $L_s$:

$$
LEF = \left(\frac{L}{L_s}\right)^{4}
$$

where:
- $LEF$ = Load Equivalence Factor (also called *Vehicle Damage Factor, VDF*)
- $L$ = actual axle load (kN)
- $L_s$ = standard axle load = **80 kN**

### 3.2.3 Derivation Intuition (AASHTO Road Test)

The AASHTO Road Test (1958) empirically established that pavement serviceability loss is proportional to the **4th power of axle load**. Hence the "fourth-power law" — this is **empirical**, not theoretical, but is universally accepted in pavement engineering.

### 3.2.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 5-axle truck has axle loads of **60 kN, 100 kN, 100 kN, 120 kN, 120 kN**. Compute the total ESAL per passage. Take $L_s = 80$ kN.

**Step 1:** Compute LEF for each axle.

$$
LEF_1 = \left(\frac{60}{80}\right)^4 = (0.75)^4 = 0.3164
$$

$$
LEF_2 = \left(\frac{100}{80}\right)^4 = (1.25)^4 = 2.4414
$$

$$
LEF_3 = \left(\frac{100}{80}\right)^4 = 2.4414
$$

$$
LEF_4 = \left(\frac{120}{80}\right)^4 = (1.5)^4 = 5.0625
$$

$$
LEF_5 = \left(\frac{120}{80}\right)^4 = 5.0625
$$

**Step 2:** Sum the LEFs.

$$
ESAL_{\text{total}} = 0.3164 + 2.4414 + 2.4414 + 5.0625 + 5.0625
$$

$$
ESAL_{\text{total}} = 15.3242 \text{ standard axles per truck passage}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiners Love This Question:** *"Compute the ESAL for a multi-axle vehicle using the fourth-power law."* — Always show all five working steps for full 7 marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Vertical Stress at Subgrade Through a Two-Layer System (Burmister's Concept)

### 3.3.1 Burmister's Assumption

A flexible pavement is treated as a **two-layer elastic system**:
- Upper layer: pavement (modulus $E_1$, thickness $h$)
- Lower layer (subgrade): modulus $E_2$, infinite depth

The **modular ratio** $E_1/E_2$ controls the spreading.

### 3.3.2 Equivalent Radius Concept

For design charts (IRC:37 uses Burmister charts, Plate 1), the contact area of a dual-wheel assembly is converted to a **single equivalent circular radius** $a_e$:

$$
a_e = \sqrt{\frac{A_c}{\pi}}
$$

where $A_c$ = total contact area of all tyres in the assembly.

For a **single tyre**: $A_c = \pi a^2$ and $a_e = a$.

### 3.3.3 Vertical Stress at Subgrade Top

The vertical stress at the top of subgrade is read from Burmister's chart as:

$$
\sigma_z = \frac{p \cdot Z}{1000} \text{ (in kPa)}
$$

where $p$ = contact pressure (kPa), and $Z$ is a chart-lookup factor (function of $h/a_e$ and $E_1/E_2$).

### 3.3.4 Relation Between $E$ and $CBR$

Empirically (Heukelom & Klomp):

$$
E_{\text{subgrade}} \, (\text{MPa}) = 10 \cdot CBR
$$

$$
E_{\text{granular}} \, (\text{MPa}) = 0.2 \cdot h^{0.45} \cdot E_{\text{subgrade}}
$$

This relation is critical for entering IRC design charts.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes of Flexible Pavement

| Failure Type | Location | Cause | Indicator | Remediation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rutting** | Wheel path, all layers | Permanent deformation due to subgrade/shear failure | Surface depression > 25 mm | Overlay, full-depth reclamation |
| **Fatigue Cracking (Alligator)** | Bottom of bituminous layer | Repeated tensile strain $\epsilon_t > 70 \times 10^{-6}$ | Interconnected cracks ("crocodile") | Overlay, milling + AC |
| **Thermal Cracking** | Top of bituminous layer | Low temperature, aged bitumen | Transverse cracks | Crack sealing, overlay |
| **Ravelling** | Surface course | Loss of aggregate-bitumen bond | Pitted surface | Fog seal, slurry seal |
| **Bleeding** | Surface course | Excess bitumen | Sticky shiny surface | Sand blot, milling |
| **Potholes** | Surface course | Local disintegration | Bowl-shaped cavities | Patch repair |
| **Edge Cracking** | Pavement edges | Lack of lateral support | Crescent-shaped cracks | Shoulder strengthening |
| **Reflection Cracking** | Bituminous layer | Cracks propagating from underlying layer | Vertical crack pattern | SAMI (Stress Absorbing Membrane Interlayer) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2-Mark Quick Question:** *"Name any four types of flexible pavement failures."* — Memorise at least these eight.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — IRC:37 Pavement Thickness Design

**Given:**
- Design traffic = **30 msa** (million standard axles)
- Subgrade CBR = **8%**
- Use IRC:37-2018 catalogue charts

**Step 1:** Identify the design traffic class. For 30 msa → use Plate corresponding to **20–30 msa** (or closest available plate).

**Step 2:** Identify the subgrade strength class. CBR 8% falls in the **CBR 8–10%** range.

**Step 3:** From IRC:37 Plate 1 (Flexible Pavement Design Catalogue) → read cumulative thickness above subgrade required:

$$
h_{\text{total}} \approx 750 \text{ mm} \text{ (approximate catalogue value)}
$$

**Step 4:** Distribute thickness across layers (typical MoRTH design for 30 msa):
- BC (Bituminous Concrete) = 40 mm
- DBM (Dense Bituminous Macadam) = 90 mm
- WMM (Wet Mix Macadam) base = 250 mm
- GSB (Granular Sub-Base) = 200 mm
- Subgrade preparation = 200 mm improvement if needed
- **Total = ~780 mm**

**Step 5:** Verify using Burmister's chart. Compute modular ratio:

$$
E_2 = 10 \times 8 = 80 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
E_1 \approx 1500 \text{ MPa} \text{ (bituminous mix)}
$$

$$
\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{1500}{80} = 18.75
$$

With $h/a_e \approx 4$ and $E_1/E_2 = 18.75$, the catalogue lookup confirms $\sigma_z$ at subgrade is within the allowable limit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always end your design with a *validation statement* that the calculated stress is below subgrade's bearing capacity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Layered Architecture of a Flexible Pavement (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Traffic Load Applied at Surface] --> A2[Bituminous Wearing Course BC]
    A2 --> A3[Bituminous Binder Course DBM]
    A3 --> A4[Granular Base Course WMM or WBM]
    A4 --> A5[Granular Sub-Base Course GSB]
    A5 --> A6[Prepared Subgrade Natural Soil]
    A6 --> A7[Safe Stress Dissipated to Earth]

    A2 -.- F1[Function: Smooth ride, skid resistance, waterproofing]
    A3 -.- F2[Function: Primary load spreading, fatigue resistance]
    A4 -.- F3[Function: Interlock load distribution, stiffness]
    A5 -.- F4[Function: Drainage, capillary cut-off, frost protection]
    A6 -.- F5[Function: Foundation; receives reduced stress below allowable]
```

> **Caption:** The five-tier load path of a flexible pavement showing both the layer sequence and the engineering function of each course. The diagram demonstrates the **pyramidal stress distribution** principle — load intensity drops with depth.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Stress Distribution Under a Wheel Load (Influence Bubble)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Wheel Load P at Surface] --> P1[Contact Pressure ~0.56 MPa]
    P1 --> S1[Stress at Top of Bituminous Layer]
    S1 --> S2[Reduced Stress at Top of Base Course]
    S2 --> S3[Further Reduced Stress at Subgrade]
    S3 --> S4[Safe Bearing Capacity Achieved]

    P1 -.- N1[Area small, stress HIGH]
    S1 -.- N2[Area spreads, stress MEDIUM]
    S2 -.- N3[Area larger, stress LOW]
    S3 -.- N4[Area largest, stress SAFE]
```

> **Caption:** Visual representation of how stress intensity reduces as the load spreads over progressively larger areas through the pavement layers. Inverse-square law of Boussinesq governs the rate of decrease.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Decision Flow: Selection of Pavement Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Project Requirements: Traffic, Soil, Budget, Life] --> D1{Traffic > 100 msa?}
    D1 -- YES --> D2[Consider Rigid Pavement or Composite]
    D1 -- NO --> D3{Subgrade CBR less than 5 percent?}
    D3 -- YES --> D4[Subgrade Stabilisation Required]
    D3 -- NO --> D5{Initial budget constrained?}
    D4 --> D5
    D5 -- YES --> D6[Flexible Pavement with Stabilised Subgrade]
    D5 -- NO --> D7{Design life > 30 years?}
    D6 --> D7
    D7 -- YES --> D8[Consider Rigid Alternative]
    D7 -- NO --> D9[Adopt Flexible Pavement with Bituminous Concrete Surface]
```

> **Caption:** Decision flow chart used in preliminary project screening. KTU students should understand *when* a flexible pavement is the appropriate choice versus when a rigid or composite design is preferred.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Typical Cross-Section (Schematic) of a Flexible Pavement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T1[Surface Course]
        X1[Bituminous Concrete BC 30 to 40 mm]
    end
    subgraph T2[Binder Course]
        X2[Dense Bituminous Macadam DBM 50 to 100 mm]
    end
    subgraph T3[Base Course]
        X3[Wet Mix Macadam WMM 150 to 300 mm]
    end
    subgraph T4[Sub-Base Course]
        X4[Granular Sub-Base GSB 150 to 300 mm]
    end
    subgraph T5[Subgrade]
        X5[Compacted Natural Soil]
    end
    T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 --> T5
```

> **Caption:** Layered cross-section as per MoRTH specifications, fifth revision. Note the decreasing material quality with depth — strongest, most expensive material at the top.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Failure Mechanism Tree for Flexible Pavements

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FM1[Flexible Pavement Failure] --> FM2[Structural Failure]
    FM1 --> FM3[Functional Failure]

    FM2 --> FM21[Rutting]
    FM2 --> FM22[Fatigue Cracking]
    FM2 --> FM23[Shoving]

    FM3 --> FM31[Roughness]
    FM3 --> FM32[Skid Resistance Loss]
    FM3 --> FM33[Ravelling]
    FM3 --> FM34[Bleeding]
```

> **Caption:** Two broad families of flexible pavement failure — **structural** (load-bearing capacity exceeded) and **functional** (serviceability lost even though structure is intact). KTU exams test the *cause-effect* mapping.

---

## 4.6 ASCII Schematic — Boussinesq Stress Bulb

```
        |        P (wheel load)
        v
   _____________
  |   CONTACT   |   <-- Surface
  |_____________|
        |
        |  \  /  \  /
        |   \/    \/  <-- Stress Influence Zone (Boussinesq bulb)
        |  /\    /\
        | /  \  /  \
        |/    \/    \
        |     |     |  <-- Stress is highest at axis, decreases radially
        |     |     |
        |     |     |
        v     v     v
    Z = depth below surface
    σ_z = 3P/(2πz²) along axis (max)
```

> **Caption:** Conceptual stress bulb (pressure bulb) under a concentrated wheel load. The isobar of equal vertical stress forms a "bulb-shaped" zone. The pavement thickness must exceed the depth where stress falls to the safe subgrade bearing capacity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024] *(3 Marks)*

**Define a flexible pavement. List any four characteristics of a flexible pavement.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A flexible pavement is a multi-layered pavement structure composed of bituminous surfacing over granular base and sub-base courses, in which the structural capacity is derived from the load-distributing action of the layered system. The pavement **flexes (elastically deflects)** under traffic loading, transferring stress progressively to deeper layers.

**Four Characteristics:**

1. **Layered elastic behaviour:** The pavement deforms elastically under load, with vertical stress and strain decreasing with depth.
2. **Load transfer by grain interlock:** Load is transmitted through aggregate-to-aggregate contact in the granular layers.
3. **Stiffness reduces with depth:** The upper layers (bituminous) are stiffer, and lower layers (granular, subgrade) are progressively less stiff.
4. **High initial cost but lower construction time:** Bituminous courses cost more than WBM/granular layers, but construction is rapid.
5. *Additional:* Requires periodic maintenance (overlay) every 5–7 years; design life is **15 years** per IRC:37.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question Paper] *(3 Marks)*

**Differentiate between flexible and rigid pavements in any six aspects.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Flexible Pavement | Rigid Pavement |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Material | Bituminous surfacing + granular base | Cement concrete (PQC) slab |
| 2. Flexural rigidity | Negligible — flexes under load | High — acts as beam |
| 3. Load transfer | Through layer-to-layer grain interlock | Through slab action over full area |
| 4. Design theory | Layered elastic theory (IRC:37) | Plate/Westergaard theory (IRC:58) |
| 5. Stiffness distribution | Decreases with depth | Constant throughout slab |
| 6. Initial cost | Lower | Higher (1.5–2×) |
| 7. Maintenance | Frequent overlays required | Low maintenance |
| 8. Life | 15 years | 30–40 years |

*(Any six of the above)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Style Aligned]* *(14 Marks)*

**Explain the functions of each component layer of a flexible pavement. With a neat sketch, describe the typical cross-section of a flexible pavement for a National Highway carrying 30 msa traffic.**

#### (a) Functions of Each Component Layer *(7 Marks)*

The flexible pavement consists of **five major courses** (top to bottom), each with a specific function:

1. **Bituminous Wearing Course (Surface Course)**
   - The topmost layer (25–40 mm of Bituminous Concrete).
   - **Functions:** (i) Provides a smooth, skid-resistant riding surface; (ii) Resists abrasion and weathering; (iii) Waterproofs the underlying layers; (iv) Withstands high contact stress (~0.56 MPa) from tyres.
   - Made with **VG-30 bitumen** and dense-graded aggregate.

2. **Bituminous Binder Course (DBM)**
   - Lies below the wearing course (50–100 mm).
   - **Functions:** (i) Main load-spreading bituminous layer; (ii) Provides structural strength; (iii) Reduces vertical stress reaching the granular base.

3. **Granular Base Course (WMM / WBM)**
   - High-quality crushed stone (150–300 mm).
   - **Functions:** (i) Major load distribution; (ii) Provides stiffness and rigidity through aggregate interlock; (iii) Drains infiltrated water.

4. **Granular Sub-Base Course (GSB)**
   - Local granular material (150–300 mm).
   - **Functions:** (i) Secondary load spread; (ii) Prevents capillary rise from subgrade; (iii) Serves as a drainage and filter layer; (iv) Protects against frost heave.

5. **Subgrade (Prepared Soil Foundation)**
   - Compacted natural soil (CBR tested).
   - **Functions:** (i) Foundation of the entire pavement; (ii) Must support the reduced stress arriving at its top; (iii) Compacted to ≥97% MDD.

#### (b) Typical Cross-Section for 30 msa NH Traffic *(7 Marks)*

For a National Highway with design traffic of **30 msa** and assumed subgrade CBR of **8%**, the IRC:37-2018 catalogue (Plate 1) recommends the following layered cross-section:

| Layer | Material | Thickness |
|---|---|---|
| Bituminous Concrete (BC) | Wearing course | 40 mm |
| Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM) | Binder course | 90 mm |
| Wet Mix Macadam (WMM) | Base course | 250 mm |
| Granular Sub-Base (GSB) | Sub-base course | 200 mm |
| **Total Pavement Thickness** | — | **~780 mm** |

**Sketch Description (text form):**

```
   ==========================================  <-- Surface (BC 40mm)
   .........................................
   ==========================================  <-- DBM 90mm
   .........................................
   [[[[[[[[ WMM Base 250mm ]]]]]]]]]
   .........................................
   [[[[ GSB Sub-Base 200mm ]]]]]
   .........................................
   vvvvvvvvv Compacted Subgrade vvvvvvvvv
```

**Validation:** Using Burmister's chart with $h/a_e \approx 4$ and $E_1/E_2 \approx 18.75$, the vertical stress at the subgrade top is well within the allowable subgrade bearing capacity (~0.07 MPa for 8% CBR).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Style Aligned]* *(14 Marks)*

**Discuss the stress distribution concept in a flexible pavement. Derive Boussinesq's equation for vertical stress at a depth z below a point load P acting on the surface of a homogeneous elastic half-space.**

#### (a) Stress Distribution Concept in Flexible Pavement *(7 Marks)*

When a wheel load $P$ acts on the surface of a flexible pavement, the load spreads through successive layers over a progressively larger area. This phenomenon is called **stress distribution** or **load spreading**.

**Key Concepts:**

1. **Concentrated Load at Top → Distributed Stress at Subgrade:** At the surface, the wheel load is applied over a small contact area (high contact pressure, ~0.56 MPa). As it passes through the pavement layers, the same total force is spread over a larger area, reducing the stress intensity at each lower level.

2. **Pressure Bulb (Isobar Concept):** The locus of points having equal vertical stress forms a "bulb" beneath the load. The maximum stress lies directly below the load and decreases radially and with depth.

3. **Multi-layer Action:** Each bituminous/granular layer has a stiffness higher than the subgrade. The stiffer layer "intercepts" the load and spreads it. Hence, the modular ratio $E_1/E_2$ controls the spreading efficiency.

4. **Engineering Use:** The design of flexible pavement thickness aims to ensure that the stress at the subgrade top is **less than the safe bearing capacity** of the soil. This is the basis of **Boussinesq's theory** (for homogeneous subgrade) and **Burmister's two-layer theory** (for layered systems).

5. **Mathematical Expression of Spreading:** The vertical stress at depth $z$ on the axis of loading is:

$$
\sigma_z = \frac{3P}{2\pi z^2}
$$

This shows that stress decreases with the **inverse square of depth**.

#### (b) Derivation of Boussinesq's Equation for Vertical Stress *(7 Marks)*

**Boussinesq's Assumptions** (recap):
1. Soil is homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic.
2. Soil is a semi-infinite half-space.
3. Load $P$ is a point load.
4. Self-weight is neglected.

**Geometry:** Let the load act at origin O. The point of interest is at depth $z$ and radial distance $r$. Form the right triangle.

$$
R = \sqrt{r^2 + z^2}
$$

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{z}{R}
$$

**Standard form of Boussinesq's equation:**

$$
\sigma_z = \frac{3P}{2\pi z^2} \cos^{5}\theta
$$

**Substituting $\cos\theta = \dfrac{z}{\sqrt{r^2 + z^2}}$:**

$$
\sigma_z = \frac{3P}{2\pi z^2} \left(\frac{z}{\sqrt{r^2 + z^2}}\right)^{5}
$$

**Simplifying:**

$$
\sigma_z = \frac{3P}{2\pi z^2} \cdot \frac{z^{5}}{(r^2 + z^2)^{5/2}}
$$

$$
\sigma_z = \frac{3P \, z^{3}}{2\pi (r^2 + z^2)^{5/2}}
$$

**Special Case — On the axis of loading (r = 0):**

$$
\sigma_z \big|_{r=0} = \frac{3P \, z^{3}}{2\pi \, z^{5}} = \frac{3P}{2\pi z^2}
$$

This is the **maximum vertical stress** at depth $z$ directly below the point load.

**Influence Coefficient Form:**

$$
\sigma_z = K \cdot \frac{P}{z^2}, \quad \text{where} \quad K = \frac{3}{2\pi} \left[\frac{1}{1 + (r/z)^2}\right]^{5/2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Direct Q&A):**
> - **Don't** skip the geometric derivation of $\cos\theta$ — most students lose **2 marks** here.
> - **Don't** write the final equation without showing the substitution step.
> - **Don't** forget the boundary conditions ($r = 0$ and the limits of $K$).
> - **Always** state the assumptions of Boussinesq's theory before derivation.
> - In numerical problems, units must be consistent (kN, m → kPa).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ Flexible pavement is a **layered elastic system** with stiffness decreasing with depth.
- ✅ Major courses: **BC → DBM → WMM/WBM → GSB → Subgrade** (top to bottom).
- ✅ Functions: **load distribution, smooth riding surface, skid resistance, drainage, durability, frost protection**.
- ✅ **Pyramidal/pressure-bulb concept** governs stress distribution.
- ✅ **Boussinesq's equation:** $\sigma_z = \dfrac{3P}{2\pi z^2} \cos^{5}\theta$.
- ✅ On the loading axis ($r = 0$): $\sigma_z = \dfrac{3P}{2\pi z^2}$.
- ✅ **Standard axle load** = **80 kN** (single axle, dual wheels, 20 kN each).
- ✅ **Fourth-power law:** $LEF = (L/L_s)^4$.
- ✅ **Heukelom & Klomp:** $E_{sg} = 10 \times CBR$ (MPa).
- ✅ **Design life** = **15 years** (IRC:37).
- ✅ Failure modes: **Rutting, Fatigue (alligator) cracking, Thermal cracking, Ravelling, Bleeding, Potholes, Edge cracking, Reflection cracking.**
- ✅ Material specifications: **MoRTH 5th Revision**, IS:73 (bitumen), IS:2720 (soil).
- ✅ Kerala specifics: **VG-30 bitumen**, **lateritic subgrade**, **CBR 4–8% typical**, monsoon drainage critical.
- ✅ KTU frequently tests: **layer functions, Boussinesq derivation, fourth-power law, IRC:37 plate reading, failure modes**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Layer Order (Top→Bottom):**
> **"Big Dogs Win Grand Slams"** = **B**C → **D**BM → **W**MM → **G**SB → **S**ubgrade.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rigid Pavement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET745 - PAVEMENT CONSTRUCTION AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Rigid Pavement # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rigid Pavement (Portland Cement Concrete – PCC Pavement):** > A pavement structure whose surface course is constructed with **Portland Cement Concrete (PCC)**, possessing a **flexural rigidity (flexural strength) of 40–50 kg/cm² (≈ 3.9–4.9 MPa)** and a high **modulus of elasticity (≈ 3 × 10⁵ kg/cm² ≈ 30 GPa)**. It distributes wheel loads primarily through **beam (slab) action** over a relatively wider area of the subgrade, in contrast to flexible pavements which rely on layer action. The characteristic feature of a rigid pavement is that the **concrete slab itself is the principal load-carrying element**. Because of its high stiffness, the slab bridges over minor irregularities in the subgrade and distributes the wheel load over a large contact area, resulting in very low subgrade stress. ## 1.2 Types of Rigid Pavement (KTU Classification) | Type | Full Form | Reinforcement | Joint Spacing | Key Use | |------|-----------|---------------|---------------|---------| | **JPCP** | Jointed Plain Concrete Pavement | None (only dowel/tie bars) | 4.5 – 6.0 m | Most common in India (NH/SH) | | **JRCP** | Jointed Reinforced Concrete Pavement | Mesh reinforcement | 7.5 – 30 m | Old IRC design | | **CRCP** | Continuously Reinforced Concrete Pavement | Continuous longitudinal steel (0.6–0.7%) | No transverse joints | High-traffic expressways | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (The "Stone Slab on Mud" Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine a **thick marble floor tile** placed on soft, damp soil (the subgrade). When you place a heavy dining table leg on the tile, the tile does not bend visibly — it acts as a **rigid beam** and spreads the concentrated point load across a large patch of the soft soil beneath. The soil pressure underneath is therefore very small. The marble tile in this analogy is the **concrete slab**, and the soft soil is the **subgrade**. This is exactly how a rigid pavement behaves — *slab action dominates over layer action*. ## 1.4 Components of a Rigid Pavement (Layered System) A rigid pavement typically consists of the following layers from top to bottom: 1. **PCC Slab (Surface Course)** — thickness **200–300 mm** (for highways), M40/M45 grade concrete. 2. **Base Course (Dry Lean Concrete / DLC / Granular)** — **100–150 mm** thick, provides uniform support and prevents pumping. 3. **Subbase (Granular / Stabilized)** — **100–200 mm** thick, prevents mud-pumping and frost damage. 4. **Subgrade** — prepared natural soil with **CBR ≥ 8%** (as per IRC:15) and compacted to ≥ 97% MDD. ## 1.5 Salient Features of Rigid Pavement (KTU Highlights) > [!IMPORTANT] > - Load is carried primarily by **slab action** (bending). > - **Modulus of elasticity of concrete, E_c ≈ 3 × 10⁵ kg/cm² (30 GPa)**. > - **Modulus of subgrade reaction, k (≈ 6.5–15 kg/cm³ for typical Indian subgrades)** is the key subgrade property. > - **No rutting** occurs — only structural cracking and faulting at joints. > - Design life: **30 years** (vs. 15–20 years for flexible). > - Initial cost is **2–3 times** that of flexible pavement, but life-cycle cost is lower. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Load Carrying Mechanism — The Slab Action Theory When a wheel load **P** is applied on the slab: - The slab deflects into a shallow basin shape. - The deflection depends on the **relative stiffness** of the slab versus the subgrade. - Subgrade is modeled as a **dense liquid (Winkler foundation)** — pressure **p = k·δ**, where **k** is the modulus of subgrade reaction (kg/cm³) and **δ** is the deflection (cm). ## 2.2 Westergaard's Theory of Stresses (1925–1933) > [!IMPORTANT] > **H.C. Westergaard (1925)** analyzed the stresses in an infinite concrete slab resting on a Winkler foundation, considering three critical loading positions. This forms the **foundation of rigid pavement design** worldwide. ### 2.2.1 The Three Critical Loading Positions 1. **Interior Loading** — load applied far from any free edge. 2. **Edge Loading** — load applied at a free longitudinal edge. 3. **Corner Loading** — load applied at a corner formed by two intersecting free edges. ### 2.2.2 Radius of Relative Stiffness (ℓ) The single most important parameter in rigid pavement analysis: $$ \ell \;=\; \left[\dfrac{E \cdot h^{3}}{12\,k\,(1-\mu^{2})}\right]^{1/4} $$ Where: - $E$ = modulus of elasticity of concrete (kg/cm²) - $h$ = slab thickness (cm) - $k$ = modulus of subgrade reaction (kg/cm³) - $\mu$ = Poisson's ratio of concrete (≈ 0.15) **Physical meaning:** $\ell$ (typically 50–100 cm) is the **radius of the equivalent foundation zone** over which a concentrated load is effectively distributed. ### 2.2.3 The Three Westergaard Stress Formulas | Loading Position | Maximum Stress Formula (kg/cm²) | Applies When | |------------------|---------------------------------|--------------| | **Interior** | $\sigma_i = \dfrac{0.316\,P}{h^{2}} \left[4\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\ell}{b}\right) + 1.069\right]$ | $b \le 1.724\,h$ | | **Interior (modern)** | $\sigma_i = \dfrac{3P}{h^{2}}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{\ell}\right)^{0.6}\right]$ | General use | | **Edge** | $\sigma_e = \dfrac{0.803\,P}{h^{2}} \left[4\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\ell}{b}\right) + 0.666\right]$ | $b \le 1.724\,h$ | | **Edge (modern)** | $\sigma_e = \dfrac{3P}{h^{2}}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{a}{\ell}\right)^{0.6}\right]$ | General use | | **Corner** | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{3P}{h^{2}}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{\ell}\right)^{1.2}\right]$ | Corner break | Where: - $P$ = wheel load (kg) - $b$ = radius of equivalent circular loaded area (cm); $b = a$ when $a \ge 1.724\,h$, else $b = \sqrt{1.6\,a^{2} + h^{2}} - 0.675\,h$ - $a$ = radius of circular contact area (cm) > [!NOTE] > **For an interior-loaded slab, the maximum tensile stress occurs at the bottom of the slab directly under the load.** For an **edge-loaded slab**, the maximum tensile stress occurs at the **bottom of the slab along the edge**. For a **corner-loaded slab**, the maximum tensile stress occurs at the **top of the slab along the bisector of the corner** — this causes the classic diagonal "corner break." ## 2.3 Temperature Stresses (Warping and Frictional) > [!IMPORTANT] > Rigid pavements are subject to **two types of temperature-induced stresses** in addition to traffic loads. These must be added algebraically to load stresses during design (Daytime: warping stress top-fiber; Nighttime: warping stress bottom-fiber). ### 2.3.1 Warping Stress (Bradbury's Coefficients) During the day, the top of the slab is hotter than the bottom → slab **curls downward** at edges → bottom fibres stretch → tensile stress at bottom. At night, the reverse occurs. $$ \sigma_{tw} \;=\; \dfrac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta t}{2} \cdot \dfrac{C_{x} + \mu\, C_{y}}{1 - \mu^{2}} $$ Where: - $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion of concrete (≈ $10 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C) - $\Delta t$ = temperature differential between top and bottom of slab (°C) - $C_x, C_y$ = **Bradbury's warping stress coefficients** for slab length Lx and Ly respectively - $\mu$ = Poisson's ratio > [!NOTE] > Bradbury's coefficients $C_x$ and $C_y$ are functions of $L/\ell$ (ratio of slab length to radius of relative stiffness), and are read from the standard **Bradbury chart** provided in IRC:58. ### 2.3.2 Frictional Stress (Subgrade Restraint) When the slab expands due to uniform temperature rise, friction at the slab-subbase interface resists the movement. $$ \sigma_{tf} \;=\; \dfrac{W \cdot L \cdot f}{2 \times 10^{4}} $$ Where: - $W$ = unit weight of concrete (≈ 2400 kg/m³) - $L$ = slab length (m) - $f$ = coefficient of friction between slab and subbase (≈ 1.5) - $10^{4}$ is the conversion from m² to cm² to keep units consistent with kg/cm² ## 2.4 Critical Stress Combination (KTU Design Check) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Equation (IRC:58 / 2011):** > > $$ > \sigma_{\text{critical}} \;=\; \sigma_{\text{edge,load}} \;+\; \sigma_{\text{warping}} \;\;\le\;\; \text{Flexural strength of concrete} / \text{FOS} > $$ The **edge load + nighttime warping** combination is generally the most critical because both stresses produce **tensile stress at the bottom of the slab** simultaneously. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Use | Units | |---|---------|-----|-------| | 1 | $\ell = \left[\dfrac{Eh^{3}}{12k(1-\mu^{2})}\right]^{1/4}$ | Radius of relative stiffness | cm | | 2 | $\sigma_i = \dfrac{0.316P}{h^{2}}\left[4\log_{10}(\ell/b) + 1.069\right]$ | Interior stress (Westergaard) | kg/cm² | | 3 | $\sigma_e = \dfrac{0.803P}{h^{2}}\left[4\log_{10}(\ell/b) + 0.666\right]$ | Edge stress (Westergaard) | kg/cm² | | 4 | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{3P}{h^{2}}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{\ell}\right)^{1.2}\right]$ | Corner stress (Westergaard) | kg/cm² | | 5 | $\sigma_{tw} = \dfrac{E \alpha \Delta t}{2} \cdot \dfrac{C_x + \mu C_y}{1-\mu^{2}}$ | Warping stress (Bradbury) | kg/cm² | | 6 | $\sigma_{tf} = \dfrac{W L f}{2 \times 10^{4}}$ | Frictional stress | kg/cm² | | 7 | $b = \sqrt{1.6 a^{2} + h^{2}} - 0.675 h$ | Equivalent radius (when $a < 1.724h$) | cm | | 8 | $\text{CBR}_{\text{subgrade}} \ge 8\%$ | IRC:15 minimum | % | | 9 | $E_c \approx 5700 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (MPa) | Elastic modulus of concrete | MPa | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility These formulas are used in: - **IRC:58 (2011)** — Guidelines for the Design of Plain Jointed Rigid Pavements for Highways (India). - **AASHTO 1993** — Guide for Design of Pavement Structures (USA, uses different SN-based approach). - **PCA (Portland Cement Association)** method — Spreadsheet-based thickness design. - **IRC:15** — Standard specifications for the subgrade of rigid pavements. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation Setup — The Westergaard Plate Equation Consider an infinite thin elastic plate of thickness $h$, modulus of elasticity $E$, and Poisson's ratio $\mu$, resting on a Winkler foundation (modulus $k$). A vertical load $P$ acts on the surface. **Step 1 — Governing Differential Equation (Plate Bending)** Classical plate theory (Kirchhoff–Love) gives: $$ D \nabla^{4} w(x,y) \;+\; k \cdot w(x,y) \;=\; q(x,y) $$ Where: - $D = \dfrac{E h^{3}}{12(1-\mu^{2})}$ is the **flexural rigidity of the slab** (kg·cm) - $w(x,y)$ is the **vertical deflection** (cm) - $q(x,y)$ is the applied distributed load (kg/cm²) - $k \cdot w$ is the **Winkler subgrade reaction** **Step 2 — Bending Moment and Stress Relationship** The bending moment per unit length along the x-axis is: $$ M_x \;=\; -D\!\left(\dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}} + \mu \dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}}\right) $$ The maximum bending stress at the bottom fibre is: $$ \sigma_x \;=\; \dfrac{6 M_x}{h^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{E \cdot h}{2(1-\mu^{2})}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}} + \mu \dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}}\right) $$ **Step 3 — For a Concentrated Load P, the Solution is in Closed Form (Westergaard 1925)** For a point load $P$ at the origin, on an infinite slab, the deflection field involves **Bessel functions** of zeroth order. The result, expressed in terms of the radius of relative stiffness $\ell$, gives the maximum bending moment under the load. After simplification: $$ M_{\text{interior}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{P}{8}\,(1+\mu)\,\ell\,\left[0.4\,+\,2\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\ell}{a}\right)\right] $$ The $\log_{10}$ form arises directly from the asymptotic expansion of the modified Bessel function $K_0$ (used in the original Westergaard analysis). **Step 4 — Conversion to Stress** Using $\sigma = \dfrac{6M}{h^{2}}$ and substituting the constants $(0.316)$, and noting that $\ell$ already contains $D$ and $k$: $$ \sigma_i \;=\; \dfrac{0.316\,P}{h^{2}}\!\left[4\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\ell}{a}\right) + 1.069\right] $$ This is the classical **interior stress** formula. ## 3.2 Worked Example — Interior Stress Calculation (Typical KTU Problem) > [!NOTE] > **Given:** Wheel load $P = 5100$ kg, slab thickness $h = 20$ cm, $E = 3 \times 10^{5}$ kg/cm², $\mu = 0.15$, $k = 8$ kg/cm³, contact radius $a = 15$ cm. **Step 1: Compute $\ell$** $$ \ell \;=\; \left[\dfrac{(3 \times 10^{5}) \times (20)^{3}}{12 \times 8 \times (1 - 0.15^{2})}\right]^{1/4} $$ $$ \ell \;=\; \left[\dfrac{3 \times 10^{5} \times 8000}{96 \times 0.9775}\right]^{1/4} \;=\; \left[\dfrac{2.4 \times 10^{9}}{93.84}\right]^{1/4} \;=\; \left[2.557 \times 10^{7}\right]^{1/4} $$ $$ \ell \;\approx\; 71.1 \text{ cm} $$ **Step 2: Check $a$ vs $1.724 h$** $$ 1.724 \times 20 \;=\; 34.48 \text{ cm} \;>\; a = 15 \text{ cm} $$ Hence use the **modified equivalent radius** $b$: $$ b \;=\; \sqrt{1.6 a^{2} + h^{2}} - 0.675 h \;=\; \sqrt{1.6(225) + 400} - 13.5 $$ $$ b \;=\; \sqrt{760} - 13.5 \;=\; 27.57 - 13.5 \;=\; 14.07 \text{ cm} $$ **Step 3: Compute $\sigma_i$** $$ \sigma_i \;=\; \dfrac{0.316 \times 5100}{20^{2}}\left[4\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{71.1}{14.07}\right) + 1.069\right] $$ $$ \sigma_i \;=\; \dfrac{1611.6}{400}\left[4\log_{10}(5.053) + 1.069\right] $$ $$ \log_{10}(5.053) \;\approx\; 0.7035 $$ $$ \sigma_i \;=\; 4.029 \times \left[4(0.7035) + 1.069\right] \;=\; 4.029 \times \left[2.814 + 1.069\right] $$ $$ \sigma_i \;=\; 4.029 \times 3.883 \;\approx\; \mathbf{15.64 \text{ kg/cm}^{2}} $$ **Step 4: Compare with allowable flexural strength** For M40 concrete, modulus of rupture $f_r \approx 45$ kg/cm². With FOS = 1.5: $$ \sigma_{\text{allowable}} = 45 / 1.5 = 30 \text{ kg/cm}^{2} $$ **Result:** $\sigma_i = 15.64$ kg/cm² $<$ 30 kg/cm² ⇒ **Slab is safe under interior loading**. ✔ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Stress Positions | Loading Position | Magnitude Order | Critical Location | Failure Pattern | |------------------|-----------------|-------------------|-----------------| | **Interior** | Lowest | Bottom of slab under load | Mid-slab transverse crack | | **Edge** | ~ 2× interior | Bottom of slab at edge | Edge crack (longitudinal) | | **Corner** | Highest (if no load transfer) | Top of slab on corner bisector | Diagonal corner break | > [!NOTE] > This is why **edge loading is the governing design case** in IRC:58 — it gives the largest tensile stress in the slab for typical wheel loads and slab thicknesses. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Layered Cross-Section of a Rigid Pavement (Block Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["WHEEL LOAD P
Dual Axle / Single Wheel"]:::load B["PCC SLAB h = 200 to 300 mm
M40 M45 Concrete"]:::slab C["BASE COURSE DLC or CTB
100 to 150 mm"]:::base D["SUBBASE Granular or Stabilized
100 to 200 mm"]:::subbase E["SUBGRADE Prepared Soil
CBR Greater than 8 percent"]:::subgrade F["FOUNDATION Winkler Spring Bed
k = Modulus of Subgrade Reaction"]:::foundation A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F G["WESTERGAARD STRESS
Sigma_i Sigma_e Sigma_c"]:::stress B --> G H["TEMPERATURE STRESS
Warping Sigma_tw plus Friction Sigma_tf"]:::temp B --> H classDef load fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef slab fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef base fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef subbase fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef subgrade fill:#f0d6b3,stroke:#8b4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef foundation fill:#e2d5e7,stroke:#4a235a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef stress fill:#ffe6f0,stroke:#cc0066,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef temp fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 The Three Westergaard Loading Positions (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["INTERIOR LOADING
Load far from edges
Max stress at bottom under load
Sigma_i formula"]:::i B["EDGE LOADING
Load at free edge
Max stress at bottom of edge
Sigma_e formula"]:::e C["CORNER LOADING
Load at slab corner
Max stress at top along bisector
Sigma_c formula"]:::c A --> D["SLAB PLAN VIEW"]:::view B --> D C --> D D --> E["Governing Design Case
Sigma_edge plus Sigma_warping"]:::gov classDef i fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef e fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef c fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef view fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gov fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Joints in Rigid Pavement (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["JOINTS IN RIGID PAVEMENT"]:::title A --> B["EXPANSION JOINTS
Full depth gap 20 to 25 mm
Filler + dowel bar
Allow expansion"]:::ex A --> C["CONTRACTION JOINTS
Dummy groove 30 to 40 percent depth
No gap saw cut
Control shrinkage cracking"]:::ct A --> D["CONSTRUCTION JOINTS
Transverse or Longitudinal
Formed at end of day pour
Tie bars for longitudinal"]:::cj A --> E["LONGITUDINAL JOINTS
Between two lanes
Tie bars at 600 mm c c
Depth 30 to 40 percent"]:::lj classDef title fill:#003366,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef ex fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ct fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cj fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef lj fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Load Transfer Mechanism at a Joint (Dowel Bar Function) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["APPROACH SLAB
Deflects under load"]:::as B["DOWEL BAR
Smooth steel bar 25 to 32 mm dia
450 mm long at 300 mm c c
Embedded 225 mm each side"]:::db C["LEAVE SLAB
Deflects equally if dowel is good"]:::ls A --> B B --> C D["Load Transfer Efficiency LTE percent"]:::lte B --> D D --> E["Target LTE Greater than 70 percent
Failure LTE less than 50 percent
Causes faulting and roughness"]:::tar classDef as fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef db fill:#ffe6f0,stroke:#cc0066,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef ls fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef lte fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef tar fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Differentiate between rigid and flexible pavements. (3 marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Rigid Pavement | Flexible Pavement | |-----------|---------------|-------------------| | Surface course | PCC (concrete) | Bituminous (asphalt) | | Load transfer | Slab (beam) action | Layer (multi-layer) action | | Modulus | E_c ≈ 3 × 10⁵ kg/cm² | E_bitumen ≈ 2000–3000 kg/cm² | | Design governed by | Flexural strength | Subgrade CBR | | Failure mode | Cracking | Rutting, fatigue cracking | | Design life | 30 years | 15–20 years | *(Any 3 valid points – 3 marks)* ### Q2. What is the modulus of subgrade reaction (k) and state its typical range? (3 marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** The modulus of subgrade reaction, **k**, is defined as the **pressure per unit deflection** of the subgrade, expressed in **kg/cm³ (or kPa/mm)**. It is obtained experimentally by the **Plate Bearing Test (PBT)** using a 75 cm diameter plate. $$ k = \dfrac{p}{\delta} \quad \text{(kg/cm³)} $$ **Typical range for Indian subgrades: 6.5 to 15 kg/cm³.** For weak subgrade, a DLC base or treated subbase is provided to increase the effective k-value. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **Q. A rigid pavement slab of thickness 22 cm is constructed with M40 concrete. The modulus of subgrade reaction is 7 kg/cm³. The wheel load is 5000 kg with a contact radius of 15 cm. Compute the radius of relative stiffness, the equivalent radius of the loaded area, and the maximum interior stress using Westergaard's theory. Take E = 3 × 10⁵ kg/cm² and μ = 0.15.** (14 marks) **Model Solution:** **Given:** h = 22 cm, k = 7 kg/cm³, P = 5000 kg, a = 15 cm, E = 3 × 10⁵ kg/cm², μ = 0.15 #### Part (a) — Radius of Relative Stiffness ℓ (7 marks) $$ \ell \;=\; \left[\dfrac{E \cdot h^{3}}{12\,k\,(1-\mu^{2})}\right]^{1/4} \;=\; \left[\dfrac{(3 \times 10^{5})(22)^{3}}{12 \times 7 \times (1 - 0.15^{2})}\right]^{1/4} $$ Step-by-step: - $h^{3} = 22^{3} = 10648$ cm³ - $1 - \mu^{2} = 1 - 0.0225 = 0.9775$ - Numerator = $3 \times 10^{5} \times 10648 = 3.194 \times 10^{9}$ - Denominator = $12 \times 7 \times 0.9775 = 82.11$ $$ \ell \;=\; \left[\dfrac{3.194 \times 10^{9}}{82.11}\right]^{1/4} \;=\; (3.89 \times 10^{7})^{1/4} $$ $$ \boxed{\ell \;\approx\; 79.06 \text{ cm}} $$ #### Part (b) — Interior Stress σ_i (7 marks) Check: $1.724 h = 1.724 \times 22 = 37.93$ cm $>$ $a = 15$ cm, so use modified radius $b$: $$ b \;=\; \sqrt{1.6 a^{2} + h^{2}} - 0.675 h \;=\; \sqrt{1.6 \times 225 + 484} - 14.85 $$ $$ b \;=\; \sqrt{844} - 14.85 \;=\; 29.05 - 14.85 \;=\; \mathbf{14.20 \text{ cm}} $$ Now compute stress: $$ \sigma_i \;=\; \dfrac{0.316 \times 5000}{(22)^{2}} \left[4\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{79.06}{14.20}\right) + 1.069\right] $$ $$ \sigma_i \;=\; \dfrac{1580}{484}\left[4\log_{10}(5.568) + 1.069\right] $$ $$ \log_{10}(5.568) \approx 0.7458 $$ $$ \sigma_i \;=\; 3.264 \times \left[4(0.7458) + 1.069\right] \;=\; 3.264 \times [2.983 + 1.069] \;=\; 3.264 \times 4.052 $$ $$ \boxed{\sigma_i \;\approx\; 13.23 \text{ kg/cm}^{2}} $$ This is well below the allowable flexural stress of M40 concrete (≈ 30 kg/cm² after FOS), so the slab is **safe** under interior loading. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q. (a) Explain Westergaard's theory of stresses in rigid pavements. What are the assumptions made in the analysis? (7 marks)** **(b) A concrete slab of thickness 20 cm rests on a subgrade with k = 6 kg/cm³. If the maximum temperature differential Δt = 20°C between the top and bottom of the slab, and the slab length is 5 m, determine the maximum warping stress and the frictional stress. Take E = 3 × 10⁵ kg/cm², α = 10 × 10⁻⁶ /°C, μ = 0.15, and C_x = 0.95 (assume C_y ≈ C_x for square slab). Unit weight of concrete = 2400 kg/m³, f = 1.5. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Westergaard's Theory and Assumptions (7 marks) **Theory:** Westergaard analyzed an **infinite, thin, elastic concrete slab** resting on a **Winkler foundation** (dense liquid subgrade) subjected to concentrated wheel loads. He solved the plate-bending differential equation $$ D \nabla^{4} w + k w = q $$ and derived the maximum tensile stress for three critical load positions: **interior, edge, and corner** (formulas as in the cheat sheet above). The analysis uses the **radius of relative stiffness ℓ** to characterize the slab-foundation interaction. **Key Assumptions:** 1. The slab is **homogeneous, isotropic, and elastic**. 2. The subgrade behaves as a **dense liquid (Winkler model)**: pressure is proportional to deflection, $p = k \delta$. 3. The slab is **infinite in plan** (free edge effects handled separately). 4. **Plane sections remain plane** (Kirchhoff plate theory). 5. **Poisson's ratio of concrete** is taken as **0.15** (some codes use 0.20). 6. Wheel load is **circular in contact area**, with radius $a$. *(Any 5 assumptions + brief theory – 7 marks)* #### Part (b) — Warping and Frictional Stress (7 marks) **Step 1: Warping stress** $$ \sigma_{tw} \;=\; \dfrac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta t}{2} \cdot \dfrac{C_x + \mu C_y}{1-\mu^{2}} $$ For square slab, $C_y = C_x = 0.95$: $$ C_x + \mu C_y \;=\; 0.95 + 0.15(0.95) \;=\; 0.95 \times 1.15 \;=\; 1.0925 $$ $$ \sigma_{tw} \;=\; \dfrac{(3 \times 10^{5})(10 \times 10^{-6})(20)}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1.0925}{0.9775} $$ $$ \sigma_{tw} \;=\; \dfrac{60}{2} \times 1.118 \;=\; 30 \times 1.118 \;=\; \mathbf{33.54 \text{ kg/cm}^{2}} $$ **Step 2: Frictional stress** $$ \sigma_{tf} \;=\; \dfrac{W \cdot L \cdot f}{2 \times 10^{4}} \;=\; \dfrac{2400 \times 5 \times 1.5}{2 \times 10^{4}} $$ $$ \sigma_{tf} \;=\; \dfrac{18000}{20000} \;=\; \mathbf{0.90 \text{ kg/cm}^{2}} $$ **Observation:** Warping stress (33.54 kg/cm²) is **dominant** compared to frictional stress (0.90 kg/cm²) — by a factor of ~37×. This is why warping stress is the **governing temperature stress** in rigid pavement design. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes (Real KTU Board Patterns):** > > 1. **Forgetting to check** whether $a < 1.724 h$ — and using raw contact radius $a$ directly inside the log term. Always check and compute $b$ when $a < 1.724h$. (Loss: 2 marks) > > 2. **Mixing units**: $E$ in kg/cm², $k$ in kg/cm³, $h$ in cm — keep them consistent. Do **not** use MPa unless you also convert $k$ to kPa/mm. > > 3. **Using $C_x = 1$ for all slabs** — Bradbury's coefficient is a function of $L/\ell$, not constant. Many students lose marks by skipping this. > > 4. **In edge loading**, students often use the interior formula. **Edge stress is roughly 2× the interior stress** — using the wrong formula yields a non-conservative design. > > 5. **Forgetting the denominator $1-\mu^{2}$** in the warping stress formula — a frequent, costly omission. > > 6. **Failing to compare** $\sigma_{\text{critical}}$ with **flexural strength / FOS** — the design check is incomplete without this comparison. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Minute Review):** > > - **Rigid pavement** = PCC slab; load carried by **slab action**, not layer action. > - **E_c ≈ 30 GPa (3 × 10⁵ kg/cm²)**, **μ ≈ 0.15**, **α ≈ 10 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**. > - **Radius of relative stiffness** $\ell = \left[\dfrac{Eh^{3}}{12k(1-\mu^{2})}\right]^{1/4}$ — **must be the first quantity computed**. > - **Three Westergaard cases:** Interior, Edge, Corner — **Edge is the most critical** in design. > - **Modified equivalent radius** $b = \sqrt{1.6a^{2}+h^{2}} - 0.675h$ is used when $a < 1.724h$. > - **Warping stress** (Bradbury) uses $C_x, C_y$ coefficients read from chart; function of $L/\ell$. > - **Frictional stress** is small compared to warping stress — usually **secondary** in design. > - **Critical combination:** Edge load stress + Nighttime warping stress (both tensile at bottom). > - **Joints are essential:** Expansion, Contraction, Construction, Longitudinal. > - **Dowel bars** transfer load across transverse joints; **tie bars** tie longitudinal joints. > - **Design codes:** IRC:58-2011 (India), AASHTO 1993 (USA), PCA (US, spreadsheet). > - **Design life:** **30 years** for rigid pavements. > - **Subgrade requirement:** **CBR ≥ 8%** (IRC:15) and **k ≥ 6.5 kg/cm³**.

topics.title: Comparison - Different Layers of Flexible and Rigid Pavement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET745 - PAVEMENT CONSTRUCTION AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Comparison - Different Layers of Flexible and Rigid Pavement # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **pavement** is a durable surfacing of a road, airstrip, or similar area, built to withstand vehicular load and environmental stresses. The structural configuration is broadly classified into two families based on the load-distribution mechanism: - **Flexible Pavement**: A layered system composed of bituminous (asphaltic) surfacing over granular bases, in which vertical compressive stresses are distributed through **grain-to-grain load transfer** in the aggregate structure. The pavement "flexes" or deflects under traffic loading. (Typical design theory: Burmister's Layered System Theory.) - **Rigid Pavement**: A pavement structure where the surfacing is a **Portland Cement Concrete (PCC) slab** possessing high **flexural rigidity** (modulus of rupture, typically **3.7–4.5 MPa** at 28 days for M40 grade). Loads are transferred over a wide area through **slab action** governed by **Westergaard's Theory of Plate Loading**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor (CO1, K2 – Understand):** > "Distinguish flexible and rigid pavements by their constituent layers, load transfer mechanism, and function of each layer in distributing wheel loads to the subgrade." --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English) Imagine two ways to spread the weight of a heavy elephant standing on soft mud: - **Flexible Pavement** = A stack of soft mattresses laid one on top of the other. The top mattress (asphalt) bends downward, the next mattress (granular base) takes some load, and so on. **Each layer spreads the load a bit more** through friction between the mattress fibers (grain-to-grain contact). - **Rigid Pavement** = A single thick, stiff wooden plank placed on the mud. The plank does not bend easily — instead, it bridges across the soft spot and distributes the elephant's weight over a much larger area because of its stiffness. **The slab itself does the load spreading**, not the layers below. > [!NOTE] > The "elephant" = a standard **80 kN (8170 kg)** axle load (IRC:3 / IRC:37 standard). > The "soft mud" = the natural **subgrade soil** with California Bearing Ratio (CBR) usually 5–10%. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Flexible Pavement | Rigid Pavement | |---|---|---| | Surface Modulus (E) | **500 – 3500 MPa** | **30,000 – 40,000 MPa** | | Poisson's Ratio (μ) | **0.35 – 0.50** | **0.15** | | Flexural Strength (MR) | Negligible | **3.7 – 4.5 MPa** | | Design Life | **15 years** (with periodic overlay) | **30 – 40 years** | | Design Theory | Burmister's Layered Elastic Theory | Westergaard's Plate Theory | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Layer-by-Layer Functional Analysis ### FLEXIBLE PAVEMENT — 4 Functional Layers (Top to Bottom) **Layer 1: Surface Course (Bituminous Layer)** - **Sub-divided into**: - *Wearing Course* (top 20–40 mm): Dense-graded asphalt concrete, provides skid resistance, smoothness, water resistance. - *Binder Course* (40–100 mm): Provides main structural strength of the bituminous layer, larger nominal aggregate size. - **Function**: Impermeable, smooth riding surface; resists rutting; resists shear stresses from tires. - **Materials**: Bituminous Concrete (BC), Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM), Stone Matrix Asphalt (SMA). **Layer 2: Base Course** - **Function**: Main load-distributing layer in the granular structure; carries the brunt of compressive stress from traffic. - **Materials**: Wet Mix Macadam (WMM), Water Bound Macadam (WBM), Crusher Run Macadam, or Bituminous Macadam (for heavy traffic). - **Thickness**: Typically **100 – 300 mm**. - **Strength**: CBR ≥ **80%** (IRC standards). **Layer 3: Subbase Course** - **Function**: Provides additional load spread; prevents intrusion of subgrade fines into the base (separation); improves drainage; acts as a working platform during construction. - **Materials**: Lower quality granular material (gravel, moorum, stabilized soil), often locally available. - **Thickness**: Typically **100 – 300 mm**. - **Strength**: CBR ≥ **20–30%**. **Layer 4: Subgrade (Prepared Natural Soil)** - **Function**: The foundation; receives the distributed load and ultimately carries it. Must be compacted to specified density. - **Strength**: CBR ≥ **5–10%** (minimum for pavement design). --- ### RIGID PAVEMENT — 5 Functional Layers (Top to Bottom) **Layer 1: Pavement Quality Concrete (PQC) Slab** - **Function**: Primary structural element; resists flexure; provides riding surface. - **Material**: M40–M50 grade PCC, often with dowel bars (load transfer across transverse joints) and tie bars (longitudinal joints). - **Thickness**: Typically **200 – 350 mm** for highways. - **Key Property**: Modulus of Elasticity, E ≈ **30,000 MPa**; Flexural Strength MR ≈ **4.5 MPa**. **Layer 2: Dry Lean Concrete (DLC) / Base Course** - **Function**: Provides uniform, stable support to the PQC slab; prevents pumping of subgrade fines; serves as construction platform. - **Material**: M10 grade plain cement concrete (no reinforcement). - **Thickness**: Typically **100 – 150 mm**. **Layer 3: Subbase Course** - **Function**: Drainage layer; frost protection; load distribution; prevents mud-pumping. - **Material**: Granular subbase (GSB), or cement/lime-stabilized soil. - **Thickness**: Typically **150 – 200 mm**. - **Strength**: CBR ≥ **15–20%**. **Layer 4: Subgrade (Prepared & Often Stabilized)** - **Function**: Ultimate load-bearing layer. - **Strength**: CBR ≥ **8–10%** (higher requirement due to rigidity and brittleness of the slab). **Optional Layer 5: Separation / Drainage Layer** - Geotextile or filter layer between subgrade and subbase to prevent fines migration. --- ## 2.2 Why & How — Load Transfer Mechanism > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Core Distinction (Most Asked KTU Question):** > - **Flexible**: Load distribution occurs **vertically through the layers** by inter-particle friction. Each layer is **stronger than the one below it**. Stress decreases with depth. > - **Rigid**: Load distribution occurs **horizontally through the slab** by flexural rigidity. The concrete slab acts like a plate, with the **subbase/subgrade being weaker than the slab above it**. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Formula | Application | |---|---|---| | Equivalent Single Axle Load (ESAL) | $\text{ESAL} = \sum_{i} \left(\dfrac{L_i}{L_{\text{std}}}\right)^4 \times \text{MSA}_i$ | Traffic loading for design | | Vertical Stress (Flexible – Boussinesq) | $\sigma_z = \dfrac{3P}{2\pi z^2} \cos^5 \theta$ | Stress at depth z in flexible system | | Critical Stress Location (Rigid – Westergaard) | Corner: $P_c = \dfrac{4P}{h^2}\left[1 - \dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{l}\right]$ | Edge loading corner stress | | Radius of Relative Stiffness (Rigid) | $l = \left[\dfrac{E h^3}{12 k (1-\mu^2)}\right]^{1/4}$ | Defines slab-subgrade interaction | | Modulus of Subgrade Reaction (k) | $k = \dfrac{P}{\Delta}$ (MPa/m) | Foundation stiffness for rigid pavement | | Effective CBR (Flexible) | $\text{CBR}_{\text{eff}} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{Total thickness}}$ | Subgrade strength assessment | | Burmister Deflection Factor | $F_2 = f\!\left(\dfrac{E_1}{E_2}, \dfrac{h}{a}, \dfrac{z}{a}\right)$ | Layered system deflection | > [!NOTE] > Standard design wheel load $P = 40 \text{ kN}$ (single wheel) or $80 \text{ kN}$ (single axle, dual wheel). --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Highways & Expressways** (NH, SH): Use **flexible pavements** (bituminous) because they are cheaper, can be constructed in stages (stage construction), and are easily repaired with overlays. - **Urban Arterials, Airport Runways, Container Yards, Toll Plazas**: Use **rigid pavements** because of superior durability, fuel-resistance, ability to bear standing loads, and longer design life. - **Bridge Approaches**: Often rigid-to-flexible transition must be carefully designed to avoid differential settlement / "bump at the end of the bridge." # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Radius of Relative Stiffness (Rigid Pavement — Westergaard) This is the single most important KTU derivation for rigid pavements and is asked repeatedly. **Statement (Westergaard's Hypothesis):** A concrete pavement slab of finite thickness, resting on a dense liquid subgrade (Winkler foundation), behaves as an isotropic, elastic, homogeneous plate. The subgrade reaction per unit area at any point is directly proportional to the deflection at that point. ### Step 1: Define the Governing Equation of Plate Theory For a thin elastic plate resting on an elastic foundation, the classical plate equation (from Timoshenko's plate theory) modified for foundation support is: $$D \nabla^4 w + k w = q(x,y)$$ where: - $D = \dfrac{E h^3}{12 (1-\mu^2)}$ is the **flexural rigidity of the slab** (N·m). - $w$ is the vertical deflection. - $q(x,y)$ is the applied load distribution. - $k$ is the **modulus of subgrade reaction** (MPa/m or N/m³). - $E$ is Young's modulus of concrete. - $h$ is the slab thickness. - $\mu$ is Poisson's ratio of concrete. ### Step 2: Non-Dimensionalize Using Characteristic Length Introduce the dimensionless length scale $r/l$ where $l$ is to be determined such that the equation reduces to: $$\nabla^4 w + w = 0 \quad \text{(for unit loading)}$$ Substituting $w = f(r/l)$ and applying the chain rule four times for the Laplacian in polar coordinates, the natural length scale that emerges from dimensional analysis is: $$l = \sqrt[4]{\dfrac{E h^3}{12 k (1-\mu^2)}}$$ ### Step 3: Verify Dimensional Consistency $$[l] = \left[\dfrac{\text{N/m}^2 \cdot \text{m}^3}{\text{N/m}^3 \cdot \text{dimensionless}}\right]^{1/4} = \left[\dfrac{\text{N} \cdot \text{m}}{\text{N/m}}\right]^{1/4} = \left[\text{m}^2\right]^{1/4} = \text{m}^{1/2} \cdot \text{m}^{1/4} = \text{m}$$ > [!NOTE] > Confirms $l$ has units of length — physically representing the **"stiffness radius"** of the slab. ### Step 4: Physical Interpretation - If $l$ is **small** → slab is **soft**, subgrade is **stiff** → slab behaves locally (load spread is small). - If $l$ is **large** → slab is **stiff**, subgrade is **soft** → slab bridges over weak spots (efficient load spread over wide area). ### Step 5: Numerical Verification (KTU Typical Problem) **Given**: $E = 30 \times 10^3 \text{ MPa} = 30 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$; $h = 0.25 \text{ m}$; $\mu = 0.15$; $k = 30 \text{ MPa/m} = 30 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^3$. $$l = \left[\dfrac{30 \times 10^9 \times (0.25)^3}{12 \times 30 \times 10^6 \times (1 - 0.15^2)}\right]^{1/4}$$ Calculate numerator: $$30 \times 10^9 \times 0.015625 = 4.6875 \times 10^{8} \text{ N·m}$$ Calculate denominator: $$12 \times 30 \times 10^6 \times (1 - 0.0225) = 360 \times 10^6 \times 0.9775 = 3.519 \times 10^8 \text{ N/m}$$ So: $$l = \left[\dfrac{4.6875 \times 10^{8}}{3.519 \times 10^{8}}\right]^{1/4} = [1.332]^{1/4}$$ $$l = (1.332)^{0.25} \approx 1.075 \text{ m}$$ > This is the **typical range (0.7 – 1.2 m)** expected for highway rigid pavements — confirms the formula is realistic. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (High-Yield) | Comparison Parameter | Flexible Pavement | Rigid Pavement | |---|---|---| | Surface material | Bituminous (asphalt) concrete | Portland Cement Concrete (PCC) | | Design theory | Burmister's layered elastic theory | Westergaard's plate theory | | Load transfer mechanism | Grain-to-grain contact (vertical) | Slab action / flexural rigidity (horizontal + vertical) | | Flexural strength contribution | Negligible (relies on layer thicknesses) | High (MR 4.5 MPa is primary) | | Stress distribution with depth | Decreases gradually over total thickness | Concentrated at slab-subbase interface, then drops sharply | | Layer strength hierarchy | Top layer STRONGEST, decreases downward | Slab STRONGEST, subbase weaker, subgrade weakest | | Temperature sensitivity | Susceptible to high-temp rutting | Susceptible to temperature-induced curling | | Moisture sensitivity | High (stripping, potholes) | Low | | Construction time | Faster, can be opened to traffic sooner | Slower, requires curing (14–28 days) | | Initial cost | Lower | Higher (1.5 to 2.5 times flexible) | | Life-cycle cost | Lower initial, higher maintenance | Higher initial, lower maintenance | | Joints | Not required (except in cement-treated bases) | Required (expansion, contraction, longitudinal) | | Repair | Easy (overlay, patch) | Difficult, costly (slab replacement) | | Typical thickness | 600 – 1000 mm (total) | 200 – 350 mm (PQC only) | | Suitable for | Highways, rural roads, stage construction | Airports, urban arterials, toll plazas, container terminals | --- ## 3.3 Stress Distribution Comparison — Quantitative For a wheel load $P = 40 \text{ kN}$ applied at surface (contact pressure $p = 0.56 \text{ MPa}$): **Flexible Pavement (Boussinesq)** — at depth $z$: $$\sigma_z = \dfrac{3P}{2\pi z^2} \cos^5 \theta$$ At $z = 0.5 \text{ m}$ directly below load ($\theta = 0$, $\cos\theta = 1$): $$\sigma_z = \dfrac{3 \times 40{,}000}{2\pi \times 0.25} \times 1 \approx 76{,}394 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.076 \text{ MPa}$$ **Rigid Pavement (Westergaard, Interior Loading)** — subgrade stress: $$\sigma_0 = \dfrac{P}{2\pi l^2}\left[1 + \dfrac{a^2}{l^2}\right]^{-3/2} \approx \dfrac{P}{2\pi l^2}$$ Using $l = 1.075 \text{ m}$ from §3.1: $$\sigma_0 \approx \dfrac{40{,}000}{2\pi \times 1.156} \approx 5{,}508 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.0055 \text{ MPa}$$ > The **rigid pavement distributes the stress approximately 14 times more effectively** than a flexible pavement at the subgrade level — this is the **engineering reason** for its design efficiency. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparative Layer Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A["FLEXIBLE PAVEMENT SYSTEM"]:::flex B["RIGID PAVEMENT SYSTEM"]:::rigid A1["Layer 1: Surface Course
Wearing Course 20-40 mm
Binder Course 40-100 mm
Material: Bituminous Concrete
E = 500 to 3500 MPa"]:::flexL A2["Layer 2: Base Course
Thickness 100 to 300 mm
WMM or WBM Granular
CBR greater than 80 percent"]:::flexL A3["Layer 3: Subbase Course
Thickness 100 to 300 mm
Granular or Stabilized
CBR 20 to 30 percent"]:::flexL A4["Layer 4: Subgrade
Prepared Natural Soil
CBR 5 to 10 percent
Load Carrier"]:::flexL B1["Layer 1: PQC Slab
Thickness 200 to 350 mm
M40 to M50 PCC
MR = 4.5 MPa, E = 30000 MPa"]:::rigidL B2["Layer 2: DLC Base
Thickness 100 to 150 mm
M10 Lean Concrete
Uniform Support"]:::rigidL B3["Layer 3: Subbase
Thickness 150 to 200 mm
Granular GSB
CBR 15 to 20 percent"]:::rigidL B4["Layer 4: Subgrade
Stabilized or Natural
CBR 8 to 10 percent
Foundation"]:::rigidL A --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 B --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 LT["LOAD TRANSFER"]:::note A -.->|Grain to Grain
Vertical Distribution| LT B -.->|Slab Action
Horizontal then Vertical| LT classDef flex fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef rigid fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef flexL fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef rigidL fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef note fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph WL["WHEEL LOAD 80 kN"] WL1["Truck Axle Loading"] end subgraph FP["FLEXIBLE PATH"] F1["Bituminous Layer
