(3 marks: ½ mark for each layer + ½ mark each for function ⇒ 3 × 1 = 3 marks) --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Differentiate between flexible and rigid pavements based on their load transfer mechanism.** **Model Answer:** - **Flexible Pavement**: Loads are transferred through **grain-to-grain contact** in the aggregate structure. Vertical compressive stresses decrease progressively with depth as each layer spreads the load over a wider area. The pavement deflects under load (multi-layer elastic theory / Burmister). - **Rigid Pavement**: Loads are transferred primarily through the **flexural rigidity of the concrete slab**. The slab acts like a plate, distributing the load over a large horizontal area before transferring it to the foundation (Westergaard's plate theory). Deflections are small and localized. (1½ marks each ⇒ 3 marks total) --- ## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **QUESTION A** (14 Marks) — Flexible Pavement Focus **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *(a) Describe the different layers of a flexible pavement with a neat sketch. State the function of each layer.* **(7 marks)** *(b) Explain the design philosophy of flexible pavements. How does Burmister's layered theory govern the design?* **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] A flexible pavement is a multi-layered system constructed over the prepared subgrade. The four principal layers are: **Layer 1 — Surface Course (1 mark)** This is the topmost layer, consisting of a wearing course (20–40 mm of dense-graded bituminous concrete) and a binder course (40–100 mm of DBM/SMA). **Function**: Provides a smooth, skid-resistant, impermeable riding surface; resists shear stresses and surface abrasion; protects lower layers from water ingress. **Layer 2 — Base Course (1 mark)** The principal structural layer. Made of WMM, WBM, or crusher-run macadam (100–300 mm). **Function**: Carries the bulk of vertical compressive stress transmitted from the surface course and distributes it to the subbase; must have CBR ≥ 80%. **Layer 3 — Subbase Course (1 mark)** Lower-quality granular layer (100–300 mm) made of locally available gravel/morrum or stabilized soil. **Function**: Acts as a working platform during construction; improves drainage; prevents pumping of subgrade fines into the base; provides additional load distribution. **Layer 4 — Subgrade (1 mark)** The top 300 mm of the prepared natural soil, compacted to ≥ 95% Modified Proctor density. **Function**: Ultimate load-bearing layer; receives the widely distributed load. **Neat sketch (3 marks)** — A labelled cross-section showing the four layers in correct order with approximate thicknesses and material names. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] The design philosophy of flexible pavements rests on **Burmister's Layered System Theory (1943)**, which idealizes the pavement as a semi-infinite, isotropic, elastic, homogeneous layered medium. **Key Concepts (3 marks)**: 1. The pavement is modelled as a **multi-layered half-space** with each layer characterized by its **modulus of elasticity (E)** and **Poisson's ratio (μ)**. 2. Each successive layer below is **less stiff** than the one above (E₁ > E₂ > E₃ > E_subgrade). 3. A wheel load applied at the surface produces **vertical stress (σ_z)**, **horizontal stress (σ_x, σ_y)**, and **shear stress (τ)** that decrease with depth. 4. The **vertical compressive strain** at the top of the subgrade governs pavement design (limiting subgrade strain criterion, IRC:37). **Design Equation — Vertical Stress (2 marks)**: $$\sigma_z = \dfrac{q \cdot a^2 \cos^2 \theta + \sigma_r \cdot z^2}{(a^2 + z^2 - 2 a z \cos\theta)^{3/2}} \quad \text{(integrated form)}$$ The simpler form for load on the surface is: $$\sigma_z = \dfrac{3 P}{2 \pi z^2} \cos^5 \theta$$ **Governing Design Criterion (2 marks)**: The pavement thickness is designed so that the **vertical compressive strain at the subgrade surface** does not exceed the allowable value: $$\epsilon_z^{\text{subgrade}} \leq \epsilon_{\text{allowable}}$$ This is implemented in **IRC:37 (2018)** through empirical charts relating cumulative ESALs, subgrade CBR, and total pavement thickness. --- ### **QUESTION B** (14 Marks) — Rigid Pavement Focus **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *(a) Describe the different layers of a rigid pavement with a neat sketch. Compare each layer with its counterpart in a flexible pavement.* **(7 marks)** *(b) Derive the expression for the radius of relative stiffness as per Westergaard's theory. State its significance.* **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Layer Description with Flexible Comparison (1 mark each layer × 3 layers + 1 mark for sketch + 3 marks for comparison details)**: **Layer 1 — PQC Slab (Rigid) vs. Surface Course (Flexible)**: - *Rigid*: 200–350 mm of M40–M50 Portland Cement Concrete; reinforced or unreinforced, with dowel & tie bars. MR ≈ 4.5 MPa, E = 30,000 MPa. - *Flexible*: 60–140 mm of bituminous concrete; E = 500–3500 MPa; no flexural strength. **Layer 2 — DLC Base (Rigid) vs. Base Course (Flexible)**: - *Rigid*: 100–150 mm of M10 dry lean concrete; provides uniform support, prevents pumping. - *Flexible*: 100–300 mm of granular WMM/WBM; distributes load via inter-particle friction. **Layer 3 — Subbase (Both)**: - *Rigid*: 150–200 mm granular GSB; CBR ≥ 15–20%; primary purpose is drainage and prevention of pumping. - *Flexible*: 100–300 mm granular subbase; CBR ≥ 20–30%; purpose is drainage, separation, and additional load distribution. **Layer 4 — Subgrade (Both)**: - *Rigid*: CBR ≥ 8–10% (higher requirement because rigid slab is brittle and sensitive to differential settlement). - *Flexible*: CBR ≥ 5–10%. **Key Distinctions (3 marks total)**: - Rigid has an **intermediate DLC layer** that flexible does not have. - Flexible's strength is **layered**; rigid's strength is **concentrated in the slab**. - Rigid subgrade requirement is **stricter** due to slab brittleness. **Sketch (1 mark)** — Labelled cross-section showing PQC slab with DLC, subbase, subgrade, with dowel/tie bar positions. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] The derivation is shown in detail in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The student should reproduce: 1. **Plate Equation on Winkler Foundation** (2 marks): $$D \nabla^4 w + k w = q(x,y)$$ $$D = \dfrac{E h^3}{12(1-\mu^2)}$$ 2. **Non-dimensionalization and Characteristic Length** (3 marks): The natural length scale that arises from dimensional analysis of the plate equation is the radius of relative stiffness: $$l = \sqrt[4]{\dfrac{E h^3}{12 k (1-\mu^2)}}$$ 3. **Dimensional check** (1 mark): $$[l] = \left[\dfrac{\text{N·m}^2 \cdot \text{m}^3}{\text{N/m}^3}\right]^{1/4} = \text{m}$$ 4. **Numerical verification with typical values** (1 mark): $E = 30 \times 10^9$ Pa, $h = 0.25$ m, $\mu = 0.15$, $k = 30 \times 10^6$ N/m³ → $l \approx 1.075$ m. **Significance** (≥ 3 sentences for full marks): The radius of relative stiffness $l$ is a **measure of the structural stiffness of the slab relative to the subgrade**. It defines the effective radius of the structural cone of influence beneath a wheel load. A **larger $l$** indicates that the slab is effective in spreading the load over a wider area, which reduces subgrade stress and improves pavement performance. It is fundamental in computing critical stresses at **interior, edge, and corner loading** positions per Westergaard's equations. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (verified from KTU valuation patterns):** > > 1. **Confusing layer names**: Students often write "subgrade" as the top layer or interchange "base" and "subbase." Always memorize top-to-bottom order: **Surface → Base → Subbase → Subgrade**. > > 2. **Forgetting the DLC layer** in rigid pavement diagrams — Examiners specifically look for it; missing it costs at least 1–2 marks. > > 3. **Misstating load transfer mechanism**: A frequent error is writing that rigid pavements transfer load through "layers" — this is wrong. The correct phrase is **"through slab action due to flexural rigidity."** > > 4. **In Westergaard derivation**: Skipping the **plate equation step** ($D \nabla^4 w + k w = q$) and directly jumping to the final formula. Examiners allocate **2 marks specifically for this governing equation**. > > 5. **Not labelling thickness / material in sketch**: A "neat sketch" without thickness values (e.g., 250 mm WMM, 280 mm PQC) is considered incomplete and may lose **1–2 marks**. > > 6. **Using `|` inside markdown tables or expressions** — Avoid confusion with the modulus sign; write it as `\vert x \vert` or `abs(x)` in the answer sheet if needed. > > 7. **Mixing up CBR values**: Flexible pavement subgrade CBR minimum is **5–10%**; Rigid pavement subgrade CBR minimum is **8–10%** (and usually higher). Mixing them is a guaranteed loss of ½ mark per instance. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ Flexible pavement has **4 layers**: Surface Course, Base Course, Subbase Course, Subgrade. - ✅ Rigid pavement has **4–5 layers**: PQC Slab, DLC Base, Subbase, Subgrade, and optional separation/drainage layer. - ✅ **Flexible load transfer**: Grain-to-grain contact (vertical, layer-by-layer); design by **Burmister's theory**. - ✅ **Rigid load transfer**: Slab action (horizontal + vertical); design by **Westergaard's theory**. - ✅ **Strength hierarchy**: Flexible = top strongest, bottom weakest; Rigid = slab strongest, subbase/subgrade weaker. - ✅ Standard axle load: **80 kN** (dual wheel); wheel load = **40 kN**; contact pressure = **0.56 MPa**. - ✅ Concrete: $E = 30{,}000 \text{ MPa}$, $\mu = 0.15$, MR $= 4.5 \text{ MPa}$. - ✅ **Radius of Relative Stiffness**: $l = \sqrt[4]{\dfrac{E h^3}{12 k (1-\mu^2)}}$ — has units of length, typically 0.7–1.2 m. - ✅ **Modulus of Subgrade Reaction** $k$ (MPa/m) is the rigid-pavement equivalent of CBR for flexible pavements. - ✅ **CBR requirements**: Flexible subgrade ≥ 5–10%; Rigid subgrade ≥ 8–10%; Base courses ≥ 80%. - ✅ **Boussinesq equation** gives vertical stress at depth in flexible systems; **Westergaard** gives critical stresses at corner/edge/interior for rigid slabs. - ✅ **Initial cost**: Flexible cheaper; **life-cycle**: Rigid cheaper (less maintenance, longer life 30–40 yrs). - ✅ **Joints**: Required in rigid pavement (expansion, contraction, longitudinal); not required in flexible. - ✅ **Failure mode**: Flexible = rutting, fatigue cracking, potholes; Rigid = cracking (corner, edge, pumping, faulting). - ✅ **ESAL** = Equivalent Single Axle Load — used for flexible pavement traffic design per **IRC:37**. - ✅ Always include a **neat, labelled sketch with thicknesses and materials** in any 7-mark layer question. - ✅ Remember the **plate equation** ($D \nabla^4 w + k w = q$) — it is the heart of every rigid pavement derivation question.

topics.title: Functions and Characteristics of Layers; Pavement Materials: Properties of Aggregates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET745 - PAVEMENT CONSTRUCTION AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functions and Characteristics of Layers; Pavement Materials: Properties of Aggregates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pavement: The Engineering Definition

A **pavement** is a durable, engineered surface structure constructed over a natural soil subgrade to support vehicular traffic loads and distribute them safely to the subgrade without causing excessive deformation or shear failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A pavement is a structure consisting of superimposed layers of selected and processed materials placed on a prepared subgrade, whose primary function is to transmit traffic loads to the subgrade within permissible stress limits and provide a safe, comfortable, and economical riding surface throughout its design life (typically **15–40 years** for KTU/MoRTH standards).

### 1.1.1 The Real-World Analogy — "The Layered Cake of Highways"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (The Layered Cake Concept):** Imagine a highway is just like a multi-tiered **Black Forest Cake**. The bottom layer (the plate) is the **natural soil** — strong enough to hold everything, but easily damaged. The next thick layer is the **sponge base** (subbase/base course) — distributes weight over a wide area. The thin top frosting is the **wearing course** — what touches the spoon (tyre). If the cake is too thin at the top, the cherry on top (your car) sinks in. If the bottom sponge is weak, the entire cake collapses. Each layer has a distinct job, and they work **together** to keep the cherry from sinking.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Pavements

KTU 2024 categorises pavements into two primary families based on **structural behaviour under load**:

### A. Flexible Pavement
A pavement structure that maintains intimate contact with the underlying layer and **bends (deflects)** under traffic loads. Load is transferred via **grain-to-grain contact** of aggregates.

### B. Rigid Pavement
A pavement structure with a **Portland Cement Concrete (PCC) slab** that possesses high flexural rigidity and distributes loads over a wide area through **slab action**.

### C. Composite / Semi-Rigid Pavement
A combination where a flexible layer (bituminous) is laid over a rigid (cement-treated) base.

---

## 1.3 The Pavement Layer System — Functions and Characteristics

A typical pavement cross-section (top to bottom) consists of the following engineered layers. Each layer has a **specific function, material, and characteristic behaviour**.

### 1.3.1 Surface Course (Wearing Course + Binder Course)
- **Function:** The topmost layer that directly bears the traffic, provides skid resistance, smoothness, and resistance to rutting, polishing, and weather.
- **Characteristics:** Must be durable, impermeable (to prevent water ingress), skid-resistant, and resistant to deformation. Typically composed of **Bituminous Concrete (BC)** or **Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM)** in flexible pavements, or **PQC** in rigid pavements.
- **Thickness:** Generally **50–100 mm** for BC and **50–100 mm** for DBM in flexible pavements.

### 1.3.2 Base Course
- **Function:** The principal load-distributing layer. It receives loads from the surface course and spreads them over a larger area, reducing stress on the subbase. Provides the bulk of the **structural capacity** of the pavement.
- **Characteristics:** Constructed from high-strength, angular aggregates (crushed stone or WBM). Should possess high stiffness, resistance to deformation, and adequate drainage.
- **Materials:** Wet Mix Macadam (WMM), Water Bound Macadam (WBM), Crusher Run Macadam, Cement Treated Base (CTB).
- **Thickness:** Usually **100–300 mm**.

### 1.3.3 Subbase Course
- **Function:** Acts as a **secondary load distributor** and serves as a **separation layer** preventing fines from the subgrade migrating into the base course. Provides additional structural capacity and **drainage** functions.
- **Characteristics:** Uses lower-quality aggregates than the base course but must still meet gradation and strength criteria. Often acts as a **frost protection layer** in cold regions.
- **Materials:** Granular subbase (GSB), natural sand, gravel, moorum, or stabilized soil.
- **Thickness:** Usually **100–200 mm**.

### 1.3.4 Subgrade
- **Function:** The **foundation** of the entire pavement system. It is the prepared and compacted natural soil that ultimately receives the distributed load.
- **Characteristics:** Must be evaluated for its **CBR (California Bearing Ratio)** value. Adequately compacted to ≥ **97% of Modified Proctor Maximum Dry Density (MPMDD)**. Insufficient CBR requires **subgrade improvement** through stabilization or replacement.
- **Required CBR:** Typically **≥ 8%** for rural roads and **≥ 10%** for expressways (MoRTH specifications).

### 1.3.5 Shoulders and Side Ditches (Auxiliary Components)
- **Function:** Lateral support to the pavement, emergency parking, drainage.
- **Characteristics:** Typically built with the same structural layers as the main carriageway, but may use slightly inferior materials for cost economy.

---

## 1.4 Pavement Materials: Aggregates — The Skeletal Backbone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Aggregates are inert, granular materials such as sand, gravel, crushed stone, slag, or recycled concrete that are bound together by a binding medium (bitumen or cement) to form a pavement layer. Aggregates constitute **90–95% of the total weight** and **75–85% of the total volume** of a bituminous mix, making their quality the **single most critical factor** governing pavement performance.

### 1.4.1 The Real-World Analogy — "Aggregates are the Bones of the Body"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (The Skeleton Analogy):** If bitumen/cement is the **flesh and skin** of the pavement, then aggregates are its **bones** — they give the structure its shape, its strength to stand upright, and its resistance to crushing under load. A pavement made of weak, rounded, dusty aggregates (like a body with osteoporotic bones) will fail quickly under traffic. Hence, aggregate selection is not just a step — it is **the** foundation of pavement design.

---

## 1.5 Primary Properties of Aggregates Required for KTU Examinations

| Property | Brief Description |
|---|---|
| **Shape** | Rounded, Angular, Flaky, Elongated, or Cubical |
| **Surface Texture** | Smooth, Rough, Glassy, Crystalline, Porous |
| **Size & Gradation** | Coarse aggregate (> 4.75 mm), Fine aggregate (75 µm – 4.75 mm) |
| **Strength** | Aggregate Crushing Value, Aggregate Impact Value |
| **Toughness** | Los Angeles Abrasion Value |
| **Durability** | Soundness (resistance to weathering) |
| **Specific Gravity** | Ratio of weight to weight of equal volume of water |
| **Water Absorption** | Porosity indicator |
| **Adhesion with Bitumen** | Affinity between aggregate and binder |
| **Cleanliness** | Free from deleterious materials (clay, silt, organic matter) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functions of Each Layer — The "Why" Behind the Design

> [!NOTE]
> Every layer of the pavement is engineered to perform a **specific mechanical, hydrological, or functional role**. Removal of any layer compromises the entire structural integrity, much like removing a single ingredient from a concrete mix (cement, sand, aggregate, water) breaks the binding.

### 2.1.1 Why Do We Need Multiple Layers?

A uniform, single-layer road built directly on weak soil would either:
- **Rut excessively** (if the layer is soft), OR
- **Shatter under load** (if the layer is stiff but thin).

The **Layered System Theory** (Boussinesq / Burmister / Odemark) tells us that stress from a wheel load **diminishes with depth** following a roughly conical distribution. Therefore, we can use **higher-quality, stiffer, more expensive materials at the top** (where stress is highest) and **progressively lower-quality materials at the bottom** (where stress is dispersed). This is the **principle of economy in material selection** — a concept heavily tested in KTU exams.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Functional Analysis of Each Layer

### 2.2.1 Surface Course — The "First Line of Defence"
- **Mechanical Role:** Resists **tangential (shear) stresses** from accelerating, braking, and turning vehicles. Resists **vertical compression** and **abrasion** from tyre-pavement interaction.
- **Hydrological Role:** Acts as an **impermeable umbrella**, preventing rainwater from infiltrating into the lower layers (which would weaken them).
- **Functional Role:** Provides **skid resistance** (safe stopping), **smoothness** (riding comfort), and **low noise** (environmental).
- **Key Tests:** Polishing Stone Value, Aggregate Abrasion Value, Marshall Stability.

### 2.2.2 Base Course — The "Spine of the Pavement"
- **Mechanical Role:** Distributes wheel load from a small contact area (say, 200 cm² per tyre) to a much larger area on the subgrade. The **load distribution angle** for a granular base is typically **~45°** (1V:1.5H to 1V:2H in stabilized bases).
- **Hydrological Role:** Must be **free-draining** to remove infiltrated water.
- **Structural Role:** Carries the **highest compressive stress** and **resists shear deformation (rutting)**.
- **Key Tests:** Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV ≤ 30% for WBM, ≤ 28% for WMM as per MoRTH), Aggregate Impact Value (AIV ≤ 30%).

### 2.2.3 Subbase Course — The "Insurance Layer"
- **Mechanical Role:** Additional load spreading, prevents stress concentration on subgrade.
- **Hydrological Role:** **Capillary cut-off** — prevents capillary rise of water from the subgrade into the base course.
- **Functional Role:** Construction platform during paving operations; prevents fines migration from subgrade to base (acts as a **filter layer**).
- **Key Tests:** CBR ≥ 20–30% for GSB, gradation as per MoRTH Table 400-1.

### 2.2.4 Subgrade — The "Foundation"
- **Mechanical Role:** The ultimate load receiver. Insufficient bearing capacity → **rutting, heaving, fatigue cracking**.
- **Hydrological Role:** Controls **moisture equilibrium** in the pavement system.
- **Key Parameter:** **Effective CBR** (soaked CBR at **4 days soaking**, per IS 2720 Part 16).
- **Stabilisation:** If CBR < required value, use lime/cement/fly-ash stabilization.

---

## 2.3 Aggregates: The In-Depth Property Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 exams, the following aggregate properties are **high-priority**. Memorize the **formulas, test codes, and permissible limits** in the tables below.

### 2.3.1 Physical Properties of Aggregates

#### A. Shape of Aggregates
- **Rounded:** Smooth all sides, river-run gravel, low interlocking, **not preferred** for high-strength pavement.
- **Angular:** Sharp edges, freshly crushed stone, **best interlocking**, **preferred for bituminous mixes**.
- **Flaky:** Thickness < 0.6 × Mean Sieve Size. Allowed limit: **≤ 15–20%** (IS 2386 Part I).
- **Elongated:** Length > 1.8 × Mean Sieve Size. Allowed limit: **≤ 15%**.
- **Cubical:** Best shape — equal dimensions, high internal friction, **most desired**.

> **Why Shape Matters:** Angular particles interlock like puzzle pieces, creating a high **angle of internal friction (φ)**, which directly translates to higher **shear strength** of the mix. Rounded particles roll over each other → lower φ → more rutting.

#### B. Surface Texture
- **Glassy/Smooth:** Polishing tendency high → **low skid resistance**.
- **Granular/Rough:** Best for skid resistance and bitumen adhesion.
- **Porous/Honeycombed:** High absorption → uses more bitumen, may degrade under traffic.

#### C. Specific Gravity
- **Most common range:** **2.6 – 2.8** for natural aggregates.
- **Heavy-weight aggregates:** Specific gravity > 4.0 (barytes, magnetite) — used in special applications.

#### D. Water Absorption
- **Permissible limit:** **≤ 2%** for most pavement applications (IS 2386 Part III).
- **Why it matters:** High absorption → aggregate pores filled with water → weak bitumen adhesion → **stripping failure**.

### 2.3.2 Mechanical Properties of Aggregates

#### A. Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV)
- **Test Reference:** IS 2386 Part IV.
- **What it measures:** Resistance of aggregate to **gradual compressive load**.
- **Permissible limits (MoRTH):**
  - WBM/WMM base course: **ACV ≤ 30%**
  - Bituminous Macadam: **ACV ≤ 30%**
  - High-quality bituminous layers: **ACV ≤ 26%**

#### B. Aggregate Impact Value (AIV)
- **Test Reference:** IS 2386 Part IV.
- **What it measures:** Resistance to **sudden impact / shock load** (15 blows of 14 kg hammer from 380 mm).
- **Permissible limits:**
  - Wearing course: **AIV ≤ 24%** (some specs ≤ 18% for high-traffic)
  - Bituminous Macadam: **AIV ≤ 27%**

#### C. Los Angeles Abrasion Value (LAAV)
- **Test Reference:** IS 2386 Part IV.
- **What it measures:** Combined resistance to **abrasion, impact, and grinding** in a rotating steel drum.
- **Permissible limits:**
  - Surface course: **LAAV ≤ 30%**
  - Base course: **LAAV ≤ 35%**
  - Subbase: **LAAV ≤ 40%**

#### D. Ten Percent Fines Value (TFV)
- **What it measures:** Load required to produce **10% fines** during crushing. Higher TFV → tougher aggregate.
- **Typical Required:** **≥ 50 kN** (for moderate traffic) and **≥ 100 kN** (for heavy traffic).

### 2.3.3 Durability Properties

#### A. Soundness Test
- **Test Reference:** IS 2386 Part V.
- **What it measures:** Resistance to **weathering** by alternate cycles of soaking in **sodium sulphate (Na₂SO₄) or magnesium sulphate (MgSO₄)**.
- **Permissible limits:** **Weighted loss ≤ 12%** (5 cycles) for most pavement applications; stricter for high-altitude or coastal areas.

#### B. Alkali-Aggregate Reaction (AAR)
- A chemical reaction between reactive silica in aggregate and alkalis in cement → **gel formation → expansion → cracking**.
- Avoid reactive aggregates; use low-alkali cement if unavoidable.

### 2.3.4 Surface & Adhesion Properties
- **Coating Ability:** Aggregate should be **hydrophobic (water-repelling)** with bitumen.
- **Hydrophilic Aggregates:** Quartzite, sandstone, feldspar — **poor bitumen adhesion**, prone to **stripping** in presence of water.
- **Hydrophobic Aggregates:** Limestone, basalt, granite — **good adhesion** with bitumen.
- **Anti-stripping agents** (e.g., hydrated lime) are used to improve adhesion of hydrophilic aggregates.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Aggregate Tests)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **repeatedly asked** in KTU exams. Memorize these along with the **test codes and permissible values**.

| Test | Formula | Permissible Limit | IS Code |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV)** | $ACV = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | ≤ 30% | IS 2386 Part IV |
| **Aggregate Impact Value (AIV)** | $AIV = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | ≤ 24% | IS 2386 Part IV |
| **Los Angeles Abrasion Value (LAAV)** | $LAAV = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | ≤ 30% | IS 2386 Part IV |
| **Flakiness Index (FI)** | $FI = \dfrac{W_p}{W_t} \times 100$ | ≤ 15% | IS 2386 Part I |
| **Elongation Index (EI)** | $EI = \dfrac{W_e}{W_t} \times 100$ | ≤ 15% | IS 2386 Part I |
| **Specific Gravity (Bulk)** | $G_{bulk} = \dfrac{W_a}{W_a - W_w}$ | 2.6 – 2.8 | IS 2386 Part III |
| **Apparent Specific Gravity** | $G_{app} = \dfrac{W_a}{W_a - W_s}$ | — | IS 2386 Part III |
| **Water Absorption** | $WA = \dfrac{W_{ssd} - W_a}{W_a} \times 100$ | ≤ 2% | IS 2386 Part III |
| **Angularity Number** | $AN = 67 - \dfrac{100 \times V_s}{W_c/\rho_d}$ | — | IS 2386 Part I |
| **Soundness (5 cycles)** | $S = \dfrac{W_{loss}}{W_{initial}} \times 100$ | ≤ 12% (Na₂SO₄); ≤ 18% (MgSO₄) | IS 2386 Part V |

**Where:**
- $W_1$ = Weight of aggregate before test (oven-dry)
- $W_2$ = Weight of fines passing **2.36 mm** sieve after test
- $W_p$ = Weight of flaky particles
- $W_t$ = Total weight of sample
- $W_a$ = Weight of oven-dry aggregate in air
- $W_w$ = Weight of aggregate in water (saturated)
- $W_s$ = Weight of saturated-surface-dry aggregate in water
- $W_{ssd}$ = Weight of saturated surface-dry aggregate in air

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Concepts

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Connection:** These tests are performed daily in MoRTH-approved laboratories across India. A highway built with aggregates failing ACV/AIV limits will show **rutting within 1–2 monsoon seasons**, costing crores in rehabilitation. For example, the **Eastern Peripheral Expressway** and **Mumbai-Vadodara Expressway** specify LAAV ≤ 24% for surface courses — a level achievable only with high-quality trap rock (basalt) or granite.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV) Test

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Background

The ACV test is a **measure of compressive strength** under a slowly applied load. The conceptual basis is that under the action of a wheel load, aggregate particles in the lower layers experience slow compressive stress. The test replicates this with a **gradual loading rate of 4 minutes** to reach **400 kN**.

> **The test setup consists of:**
> - A cylindrical steel mould of **15.0 cm internal diameter** and **13.5 cm height**.
> - A plunger that fits the mould.
> - A 400-tonne (or kN) compression testing machine.
> - Aggregate sample of **12.5 mm to 10 mm** size (passing 12.5 mm, retained on 10 mm), weighing approximately **3,200 g**.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Procedure (Logical Flow)

1. **Sample Preparation:** Oven-dry the aggregate at 100–110°C to constant weight. Sieve and collect material passing **12.5 mm** and retained on **10 mm** sieves.
2. **Filling the Mould:** Fill the mould in **3 equal layers**, tamping each layer with **25 strokes** of a tamping rod (16 mm diameter, 600 mm height, 1.4 kg mass).
3. **Apply Plunger & Load:** Place the plunger on the top surface and apply load in the compression testing machine at a **uniform rate** such that the full **400 kN** is reached in **10 minutes** (some specs: total time of 10 minutes, including the first 5 minutes for 150 kN and then increasing to 400 kN).
4. **Discharge & Sieve:** Release the load, remove the crushed aggregate, and sieve through a **2.36 mm IS sieve**.
5. **Weigh the Fines:** The material passing 2.36 mm is the "crushed fines" ($W_2$).

### 3.1.3 Mathematical Formulation

The Aggregate Crushing Value is defined as the **ratio of the weight of fines generated to the total weight of the sample, expressed as a percentage.**

$$
ACV = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

Where:
- $W_1$ = total weight of dry aggregate sample before the test (g)
- $W_2$ = weight of fines passing 2.36 mm sieve after test (g)

**Step-by-step logic of the formula:**
- The denominator $W_1$ represents the **total load-bearing mass** available in the test specimen.
- The numerator $W_2$ represents the **fraction that failed** under the applied compressive stress.
- The ratio $W_2 / W_1$ is the **fractional mass loss**, which is multiplied by 100 to express it as a **percentage**.

**Physical Interpretation:** A **lower ACV** means the aggregate **resisted crushing better**. A higher ACV means the aggregate is **weak** and unsuitable for high-stress pavement layers.

### 3.1.4 Worked Example (Numerical)

**Problem:** In an ACV test, the oven-dry weight of the aggregate sample is **3,200 g**. After applying the 400 kN load, the weight of fines passing the 2.36 mm sieve is **768 g**. Calculate the ACV and comment on the suitability of this aggregate for WMM base course.

**Solution:**

$$
W_1 = 3{,}200 \text{ g}
$$

$$
W_2 = 768 \text{ g}
$$

$$
ACV = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

$$
ACV = \frac{768}{3{,}200} \times 100
$$

$$
ACV = 0.24 \times 100
$$

$$
ACV = 24\%
$$

**Step-by-step reasoning of arithmetic:**

- Divide 768 by 3,200: $768 \div 3{,}200 = 0.24$
- Multiply by 100: $0.24 \times 100 = 24$
- Units: The unit of weight (g) cancels, leaving a dimensionless ratio expressed as a percentage.

**Comment:** Since the calculated ACV is **24%**, which is **less than the MoRTH permissible limit of 30%** for WMM base course, the aggregate is **suitable for use** in WMM base course. However, for a wearing course (which requires ACV ≤ 26%), this aggregate is **just within limit** and acceptable for moderate-traffic conditions.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Los Angeles Abrasion Value (LAAV)

### 3.2.1 Theoretical Basis

The LAAV test simulates the **combined effects of abrasion, impact, and grinding** that aggregates experience in a pavement under traffic loading. The test uses a **drum rotating at 30–33 rpm** for **500 revolutions** with a specified number of **steel spheres** (charge) depending on the grading.

### 3.2.2 Mathematical Formulation

$$
LAAV = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

Where:
- $W_1$ = Original weight of oven-dry aggregate sample (g)
- $W_2$ = Weight retained on **1.70 mm IS sieve** after the test (g)

**Step-by-step logic:**
- $W_1 - W_2$ = weight of material abraded (passed through 1.70 mm sieve)
- Dividing by $W_1$ gives the **fraction abraded**.
- Multiplying by 100 gives a **percentage**.

### 3.2.3 Worked Example (Numerical)

**Problem:** In an LAAV test on a sample grading B, 5,000 g of oven-dry aggregate is placed in the drum with **11 steel balls of 46.8 mm diameter** (total charge 4584 ± 25 g). After 500 revolutions, the retained weight on 1.70 mm sieve is **3,650 g**. Calculate LAAV and comment on its suitability for a surface course.

**Solution:**

$$
W_1 = 5{,}000 \text{ g}, \quad W_2 = 3{,}650 \text{ g}
$$

$$
LAAV = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

$$
LAAV = \frac{5{,}000 - 3{,}650}{5{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
LAAV = \frac{1{,}350}{5{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
LAAV = 0.27 \times 100
$$

$$
LAAV = 27\%
$$

**Step-by-step arithmetic:**
- $5{,}000 - 3{,}650 = 1{,}350$
- $1{,}350 \div 5{,}000 = 0.27$
- $0.27 \times 100 = 27$
- Units cancel, result in **percentage**.

**Comment:** LAAV = 27% is **less than the 30% limit** for surface course (per MoRTH). Hence, this aggregate is **suitable for surface course in moderate-traffic roads**, but for expressways where ≤ 24% is preferred, the aggregate may be **borderline acceptable**.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Specific Gravity and Water Absorption

### 3.3.1 Conceptual Basis

**Specific Gravity** is a measure of density relative to water. For aggregates, it determines the **density of the aggregate skeleton** and is essential for **bituminous mix design** (to compute VMA, VFB, and unit weight).

Three definitions of specific gravity are used:
- **Bulk Specific Gravity ($G_{bulk}$):** Includes the volume of impermeable + permeable voids.
- **Saturated Surface-Dry Specific Gravity ($G_{ssd}$):** Includes the volume of impermeable + permeable voids, with permeable voids filled with water.
- **Apparent Specific Gravity ($G_{app}$):** Includes only the volume of impermeable voids.

### 3.3.2 Bulk Specific Gravity

$$
G_{bulk} = \frac{W_a}{W_a - W_w}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
- $W_a$ = weight of oven-dry aggregate in air.
- $W_a - W_w$ = weight of the same aggregate **submerged in water** (Archimedes' principle — the difference equals the weight of water displaced by the **bulk volume** of the aggregate, including all voids).
- The ratio gives the **bulk specific gravity**.

### 3.3.3 Apparent Specific Gravity

$$
G_{app} = \frac{W_a}{W_a - W_s}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
- $W_s$ = weight of saturated-surface-dry aggregate in water.
- $W_a - W_s$ = weight of water displaced by the **impermeable volume only** (water has already filled the permeable voids in the SSD state).
- This gives the **apparent specific gravity** (always ≥ $G_{bulk}$).

### 3.3.4 Water Absorption

$$
WA = \frac{W_{ssd} - W_a}{W_a} \times 100
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
- $W_{ssd}$ = weight of the saturated surface-dry aggregate in air.
- $W_{ssd} - W_a$ = weight of water absorbed in the permeable pores.
- Dividing by $W_a$ gives fractional water absorbed.
- Multiplying by 100 gives **percentage water absorption**.

### 3.3.5 Worked Example (Numerical)

**Problem:** In a specific gravity test, an oven-dry aggregate sample weighs **480 g** in air. The SSD weight in air is **488 g**. The weight of the SSD sample in water is **312 g**. Calculate: (a) Bulk Specific Gravity, (b) Apparent Specific Gravity, and (c) Water Absorption. Comment on the suitability of the aggregate.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- $W_a = 480 \text{ g}$ (oven-dry weight in air)
- $W_{ssd} = 488 \text{ g}$ (SSD weight in air)
- $W_s = 312 \text{ g}$ (SSD weight in water)

**Part (a) — Bulk Specific Gravity:**

$$
G_{bulk} = \frac{W_a}{W_a - W_w}
$$

We need $W_w$ (weight of oven-dry aggregate in water). To get this, we use the relation:
$W_w \approx W_s - (W_{ssd} - W_a)$ [Accounting for water absorbed in pores]

For simplicity, in many textbooks, the dry weight in water is calculated as:

$$
W_w = W_{ssd} - \rho_w \cdot (V_{total})
$$

But practically, $W_w$ is measured directly. Assume here that $W_w = 305 \text{ g}$ (a typical value consistent with the data).

$$
G_{bulk} = \frac{480}{480 - 305} = \frac{480}{175} = 2.743
$$

**Part (b) — Apparent Specific Gravity:**

$$
G_{app} = \frac{W_a}{W_a - W_s} = \frac{480}{480 - 312} = \frac{480}{168} = 2.857
$$

**Part (c) — Water Absorption:**

$$
WA = \frac{W_{ssd} - W_a}{W_a} \times 100 = \frac{488 - 480}{480} \times 100 = \frac{8}{480} \times 100 = 1.667\%
$$

**Step-by-step arithmetic:**

- For $G_{bulk}$: $480 \div 175 = 2.7428 \approx 2.74$
- For $G_{app}$: $480 \div 168 = 2.8571 \approx 2.86$
- For WA: $8 \div 480 = 0.01667$; $\times 100 = 1.67\%$

**Comment:**
- Specific gravity ≈ 2.74 — within normal range of 2.6–2.8. ✓
- Water absorption = 1.67% — **less than 2%** permissible limit. ✓
- Therefore, the aggregate is **suitable for bituminous mix design** with normal binder content.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation: Flakiness and Elongation Indices

### 3.4.1 Flakiness Index (FI)

A flaky particle is one whose **thickness is less than 0.6 times its mean sieve size** (the average of the sieve sizes it is retained between).

$$
FI = \frac{W_p}{W_t} \times 100
$$

Where:
- $W_p$ = total weight of flaky particles (passing the corresponding thickness gauge)
- $W_t$ = total weight of the sample tested

### 3.4.2 Elongation Index (EI)

An elongated particle is one whose **length is greater than 1.8 times its mean sieve size**.

$$
EI = \frac{W_e}{W_t} \times 100
$$

Where $W_e$ = total weight of elongated particles.

### 3.4.3 Worked Example (Numerical)

**Problem:** A sample of coarse aggregate weighing **2,000 g** is separated into flaky and non-flaky fractions. The flaky fraction weighs **240 g**. Another sample weighing **3,000 g** gives an elongated fraction of **360 g**. Calculate (a) Flakiness Index, (b) Elongation Index, and comment on suitability.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Flakiness Index:**

$$
FI = \frac{W_p}{W_t} \times 100 = \frac{240}{2{,}000} \times 100 = 12\%
$$

**Part (b) — Elongation Index:**

$$
EI = \frac{W_e}{W_t} \times 100 = \frac{360}{3{,}000} \times 100 = 12\%
$$

**Step-by-step arithmetic:**
- $240 \div 2{,}000 = 0.12$; $\times 100 = 12$
- $360 \div 3{,}000 = 0.12$; $\times 100 = 12$

**Comment:** Both FI and EI are **12%**, which is **less than 15%** (the permissible MoRTH limit). Hence, the aggregate is **suitable for use in bituminous and granular base layers** of the pavement.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Layer Functions vs Aggregate Properties

| Pavement Layer | Stress Condition | Critical Aggregate Property | Required Test |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Surface Course** | High shear, high impact, abrasion | Toughness, Hardness, Shape | LAAV ≤ 30%, AIV ≤ 24%, FI ≤ 15% |
| **Base Course** | High compression, moderate shear | Strength, Shape, Gradation | ACV ≤ 30%, FI ≤ 15% |
| **Subbase Course** | Moderate compression | Gradation, Drainage | Sieve analysis, Permeability |
| **Subgrade** | Low stress (after distribution) | In-situ density, CBR | Field density (≥97% MPMDD), CBR ≥ 8% |

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    D["Compacted Subgrade - CBR greater than 8 percent"]

    A --> B

    C --> D

    E -.-> A

    style E fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#888888,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The top **PQC (Pavement Quality Concrete)** layer is a **rigid slab** that distributes load over a wide area through **beam/slab action**.
- Below it lies a **DLC (Dry Lean Concrete)** acting as a working platform and bonding layer.
- **GSB (Granular Subbase)** provides drainage and frost protection.
- Note the contrast with flexible pavement: the load distribution is **horizontal (slab action)** rather than **conical (grain-to-grain)**.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Stress Distribution in Flexible vs. Rigid Pavement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Wheel Load P - Tyre Contact Area"]
    B["Flexible Pavement - Conical Spread - 45 degree angle"]
    C["Rigid Pavement - Slab Action - Wide Horizontal Spread"]
    D["Subgrade Stress - High - Concentrated"]
    E["Subgrade Stress - Low - Distributed"]

    A --> B
    A --> C
    B --> D
    C --> E

    style A fill:#D32F2F,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style B fill:#FFB300,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style C fill:#9E9E9E,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style D fill:#D32F2F,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style E fill:#388E3C,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- A wheel load $P$ is shown as a red block.
- The **flexible pavement** (yellow) has a conical stress distribution, leading to a **high concentrated stress** on the subgrade (red).
- The **rigid pavement** (grey) spreads the load **horizontally** through slab action, leading to a **low distributed stress** on the subgrade (green).
- This is the **fundamental reason** why rigid pavements can be built over weaker subgrades than flexible pavements.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three desirable properties of aggregates used in the surface course of a flexible pavement.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **High Abrasion Resistance** — Los Angeles Abrasion Value (LAAV) should be ≤ **30%** to resist tyre wear.
2. **High Toughness/Impact Resistance** — Aggregate Impact Value (AIV) ≤ **24%** to withstand shock loads.
3. **Angular Particle Shape** — Flakiness Index ≤ **15%** to ensure good interlocking and high internal friction.
*(Other acceptable answers: high skid resistance, low water absorption ≤ 2%, good bitumen adhesion.)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Subgrade. What is its primary function in a pavement structure?*

**Model Answer:**
The **subgrade** is the prepared and compacted natural soil layer that forms the **foundation** of the pavement.
**Primary Function:** To receive the **distributed traffic loads** from the upper pavement layers and to provide **uniform support** without excessive deformation. It is characterised by its **California Bearing Ratio (CBR)** — typically **≥ 8%** for rural roads and **≥ 10%** for expressways (MoRTH).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Describe, with neat sketches, the functions of each layer in a flexible pavement. State the typical materials used in each layer.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *An aggregate sample weighs 3,250 g. After an Aggregate Crushing Value test, the weight of fines passing the 2.36 mm IS sieve is 845 g. Compute the ACV. Comment on its suitability for WMM base course construction as per MoRTH specifications.*

**Model Solutions:**

**Part (a) — Layer Functions (7 marks):**

| Layer (Top→Bottom) | Function | Typical Material | Approx. Thickness |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Surface Course** | Direct contact with traffic; provides skid resistance, smoothness, impermeability | Bituminous Concrete (BC), Mastic Asphalt | 40–50 mm |
| **Binder Course** | Secondary structural layer below BC; binds layers together | Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM) | 50–100 mm |
| **Base Course** | Principal load-distributing layer; resists highest compression | Wet Mix Macadam (WMM), WBM, CTB | 100–300 mm |
| **Subbase Course** | Additional load distribution; drainage; frost protection | Granular Subbase (GSB), Stabilized Soil | 100–200 mm |
| **Subgrade** | Foundation; bears the distributed load | Compacted Natural Soil (CBR ≥ 8%) | ≥ 500 mm |

*Sketch: Draw a vertical cross-section showing the 5 layers in order, with arrows representing load distribution cone.*

**Part (b) — ACV Calculation (7 marks):**

Given: $W_1 = 3{,}250$ g, $W_2 = 845$ g

$$
ACV = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

$$
ACV = \frac{845}{3{,}250} \times 100
$$

**Step-by-step arithmetic:**
- $845 \div 3{,}250 = 0.26$
- $0.26 \times 100 = 26\%$

**Result:** $\text{ACV} = 26\%$

**Comment:** As per MoRTH, the permissible limit of ACV for WMM base course is **≤ 30%**. Since the calculated value (26%) is **less than 30%**, the aggregate is **suitable for WMM base course construction**.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the following properties of road aggregates: (i) Los Angeles Abrasion Value, (ii) Aggregate Impact Value, (iii) Soundness.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *The Los Angeles Abrasion test on a sample of 5,000 g yielded a retained weight of 3,400 g on the 1.70 mm sieve. Calculate the LAAV and state its suitability for a bituminous surface course. Also explain why angular aggregates are preferred over rounded ones in bituminous mixes.*

**Model Solutions:**

**Part (a) — Property Explanations (7 marks):**

**(i) Los Angeles Abrasion Value (LAAV):**
- **Test code:** IS 2386 Part IV.
- **Principle:** A sample of aggregate is rotated in a **drum** with **steel spheres** for 500 revolutions at 30–33 rpm.
- **What it measures:** Combined resistance to **abrasion, impact, and grinding** under traffic.
- **Formula:** $\text{LAAV} = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$
- **Permissible limit:** ≤ 30% for surface course.

**(ii) Aggregate Impact Value (AIV):**
- **Test code:** IS 2386 Part IV.
- **Principle:** Aggregate is placed in a mould and subjected to **15 blows of a 14 kg hammer** falling from a height of **380 mm**.
- **What it measures:** Resistance to **sudden impact / shock loading**.
- **Formula:** $\text{AIV} = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$
- **Permissible limit:** ≤ 24% for wearing course.

**(iii) Soundness Test:**
- **Test code:** IS 2386 Part V.
- **Principle:** Alternate **soaking and drying cycles** (5 or 10 cycles) in **sodium sulphate (Na₂SO₄) or magnesium sulphate (MgSO₄)** solutions.
- **What it measures:** Resistance to **weathering action** in the pavement environment.
- **Formula:** $\text{Soundness Loss} = \dfrac{W_{loss}}{W_{initial}} \times 100$
- **Permissible limit:** ≤ 12% (Na₂SO₄, 5 cycles).

**Part (b) — LAAV Calculation (7 marks):**

Given: $W_1 = 5{,}000$ g, $W_2 = 3{,}400$ g (retained on 1.70 mm)

$$
LAAV = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

$$
LAAV = \frac{5{,}000 - 3{,}400}{5{,}000} \times 100
$$

**Step-by-step arithmetic:**
- $5{,}000 - 3{,}400 = 1{,}600$
- $1{,}600 \div 5{,}000 = 0.32$
- $0.32 \times 100 = 32\%$

**Result:** $\text{LAAV} = 32\%$

**Suitability Comment:** The permissible limit of LAAV for a bituminous surface course is **≤ 30%** (MoRTH). Since the calculated LAAV (32%) is **greater than 30%**, this aggregate is **NOT suitable for use in the bituminous surface course**. It may be considered for subbase or lower-quality layers.

**Why angular aggregates are preferred over rounded ones:**

Angular aggregates have **sharp, well-defined edges** that allow them to **interlock** with each other, generating high **internal friction (φ)**. This results in:
- Higher **shear strength** of the bituminous mix.
- Greater **resistance to rutting** and permanent deformation.
- Higher **stability** under heavy traffic loads.

In contrast, rounded aggregates **roll and slide** over each other due to their smooth surfaces, leading to **lower shear strength**, more **rutting**, and a tendency to **displace** under braking and acceleration. Hence, angular crushed stone is **mandatory** for high-quality bituminous mixes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in this topic (KTU Examiner Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Wrong Formula for LAAV:** Many students write $\text{LAAV} = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ (which is for ACV). The correct LAAV formula is $\dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ because $W_2$ is the **retained** weight, and the **abraded portion** is $W_1 - W_2$. **2-mark penalty** for this mistake.
>
> 2. **Confusing ACV and AIV:** ACV uses **gradual compressive load (400 kN in 10 minutes)**; AIV uses **sudden impact (15 hammer blows)**. Mixing up the loading type is a **common 1-mark loss**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting IS Code:** Examiners expect you to **cite the IS code** (e.g., "IS 2386 Part IV" for strength tests). Omission costs **1 mark**.
>
> 4. **No Comment on Suitability:** Numerical problems on ACV/LAAV/AIV require a **"comment"** on whether the aggregate meets MoRTH/IS specifications. Just computing the value is **incomplete** — **2 marks lost** if comment is missing.
>
> 5. **Mixing up Specific Gravity Definitions:** Bulk SG ≠ SSD SG ≠ Apparent SG. **Know the difference** between $W_a$, $W_w$, $W_s$, and $W_{ssd}$. **1.5-mark loss** for wrong definition.
>
> 6. **No Diagram for Layer Functions:** For 7-mark questions on layer functions, **a neat cross-section diagram is COMPULSORY**. Skipping it = **2-mark loss** even if the text description is perfect.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 – Topic 1):**

**Pavement Layers:**
- 4 main layers in flexible pavement: **Surface → Base → Subbase → Subgrade** (plus binder course in modern practice).
- **Surface course** = BC; **Binder course** = DBM; **Base course** = WMM/WBM; **Subbase** = GSB; **Subgrade** = compacted natural soil.
- Stress **decreases with depth** → material quality can also **decrease with depth** (economy principle).
- Subgrade CBR ≥ 8% (rural) or ≥ 10% (expressway).
- Rigid pavement has a **PQC slab** that distributes load via **slab action** (horizontal spread) rather than **conical spread** (flexible pavement).

**Aggregate Properties (Memorize the test codes, formulas, and limits):**
- **ACV** — IS 2386 Part IV, formula $W_2 / W_1 \times 100$, limit **≤ 30%**.
- **AIV** — IS 2386 Part IV, formula $W_2 / W_1 \times 100$, limit **≤ 24%**.
- **LAAV** — IS 2386 Part IV, formula $(W_1 - W_2) / W_1 \times 100$, limit **≤ 30%**.
- **Flakiness Index** — IS 2386 Part I, formula $W_p / W_t \times 100$, limit **≤ 15%**.
- **Elongation Index** — IS 2386 Part I, formula $W_e / W_t \times 100$, limit **≤ 15%**.
- **Specific Gravity (Bulk)** — IS 2386 Part III, formula $W_a / (W_a - W_w)$, range **2.6 – 2.8**.
- **Water Absorption** — IS 2386 Part III, formula $(W_{ssd} - W_a) / W_a \times 100$, limit **≤ 2%**.
- **Soundness** — IS 2386 Part V, limit **≤ 12% (Na₂SO₄)**, **≤ 18% (MgSO₄)**.

**Key Distinctions:**
- **ACV** = gradual compression; **AIV** = sudden impact; **LAAV** = combined abrasion + impact + grinding.
- **Flakiness** = thickness < 0.6 × mean sieve size; **Elongation** = length > 1.8 × mean sieve size.
- **Angular aggregates** = high internal friction → preferred in bituminous mixes.
- **Hydrophobic aggregates** (limestone, basalt) = good bitumen adhesion; **hydrophilic** (quartzite) = poor adhesion, prone to stripping.
- **Aggregate Loading Hierarchy:** Surface Course needs toughest aggregates (LAAV ≤ 30%, AIV ≤ 24%); Subbase accepts weaker (LAAV ≤ 40%).

**Common KTU Exam Traps:**
- ACV and AIV have **identical formulas** — distinguish by **load type**, not by formula.
- LAAV's formula uses **$W_1 - W_2$** in the numerator, not $W_2$.
- The permissible ACV for WMM is **30%** but for DBM base course (IRC 111), it is also **≤ 30%**; the difference comes in AIV (≤ 24% for surface, ≤ 27% for base).
- Soundness test uses **sulphate solutions**, not **water**, to simulate weathering cycles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET745 - PAVEMENT CONSTRUCTION AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bitumen and Subgrade Soil; Requirements and Tests on Aggregates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Bitumen

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> Bitumen is a viscous, black, sticky, semi-solid petroleum product obtained either by the refining of crude petroleum (petroleum bitumen) or from natural sources (natural asphalt). It is essentially a mixture of hydrocarbons and is widely used as a binder in flexible pavement construction due to its excellent adhesive, waterproofing, and viscoelastic properties.

### Composition of Bitumen
Bitumen is primarily composed of the following chemical fractions:

- **Asphaltenes** – High molecular weight, polar, insoluble in n-heptane; provide hardness and stiffness.
- **Resins** – Adhesive, dark brown, semi-solid; provide ductility and adhesion to aggregates.
- **Aromatic oils (Maltenes)** – Low molecular weight, dark brown; provide viscosity and fluidity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The property of bitumen changes significantly with temperature. At high temperatures it behaves like a viscous liquid, while at low temperatures it becomes a brittle solid. This **thermo-viscous (viscoelastic) behavior** is critical for pavement performance.

### Classification of Bitumen (Based on Source)

| Type | Source | Key Property |
|------|--------|--------------|
| **Petroleum Bitumen** | Residue from crude oil distillation | Most commonly used; graded by penetration/viscosity |
| **Natural Asphalt** | Naturally occurring (e.g., Trinidad Lake Asphalt) | Contains mineral matter, very hard |
| **Cutback Bitumen** | Bitumen + Solvent (kerosene/diesel) | Used for prime/tack coats in cold regions |
| **Bitumen Emulsion** | Bitumen + Water + Emulsifier | Cold-applied, eco-friendly binder |
| **Modified Bitumen** | Bitumen + Polymer (SBS, EVA, Crumb Rubber) | Improved rutting & fatigue resistance |

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of bitumen as the **"mortar of the road"** — just as cement binds bricks in a wall to form a strong, monolithic structure, bitumen binds together loose aggregates (stones, sand, filler) in a flexible pavement. The difference is that bitumen is flexible (like chewing gum that softens on a hot day and hardens in winter), allowing the pavement to bend under traffic loads without cracking.

---

## 1.2 Subgrade Soil

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The subgrade is the natural or prepared soil layer (existing ground surface) that forms the foundation of the pavement structure. It must be capable of supporting the loads transmitted from the upper pavement layers without undergoing excessive deformation (rutting) or failure.

### Functions of Subgrade
- Serves as the **working platform** for construction equipment.
- Provides **uniform support** to the upper pavement layers.
- Resists **vertical compressive stresses** transmitted from traffic.
- Must be **stable** under the influence of moisture and climate changes.

### Conceptual Analogy
The subgrade is the **"foundation floor"** of a building — the pavement structure (gravel base + bituminous layers) is the actual building, and the subgrade is the soil below. If the floor is weak (e.g., black cotton soil or clay), the entire structure above will settle and crack. Hence, evaluating the strength of the subgrade soil is the **first step** in pavement design.

---

## 1.3 Aggregates

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> Aggregates are inert, granular materials (sand, gravel, crushed stone, slag) that form the structural skeleton of the bituminous pavement. They typically constitute **90–95% of the total pavement mass** and provide the load-bearing capacity, skid resistance, and durability to the mix.

### Desired Properties of Aggregates
1. **Strength & Toughness** – Resistance to crushing, impact, and abrasion under traffic.
2. **Shape & Surface Texture** – Angular, cubical particles with rough texture give better interlock and skid resistance.
3. **Affinity to Bitumen** – Should have good adhesion with bitumen in the presence of water.
4. **Durability** – Resistance to weathering and chemical attack.
5. **Cleanliness** – Free from dust, clay, organic matter, and deleterious materials.
6. **Gradation** – Well-graded aggregates provide a dense, stable mix.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of aggregates as the **"bricks and stones of a wall"** and bitumen as the **"mortar"**. The strength of the wall depends primarily on the strength of the bricks (aggregates), not the mortar. Similarly, the strength of a flexible pavement depends largely on the quality of aggregates used.

---

## 1.4 Quick Visual Summary

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│            FLEXIBLE PAVEMENT STRUCTURE           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Bituminous Surface Course (BC / DBM / SDBC)    │ ← BINDER + AGGREGATE
│  Base Course (WMM / WBM / CTB)                 │ ← PRIMARILY AGGREGATE
│  Subbase Course (Granular / Moorum)             │ ← AGGREGATE
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  PREPARED SUBGRADE (SOIL)                       │ ← FOUNDATION
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Requirements and Tests on Bitumen

### 2.1.1 Engineering Properties Required
A good bituminous binder should possess:

- Sufficient **viscosity** at high service temperatures to resist rutting.
- Sufficient **flexibility** (low temperature ductility) to resist cracking.
- Good **adhesion** to aggregates, even in the presence of water.
- **Aging resistance** against oxidation during mixing, laying, and service life.
- **Cohesion** — internal strength to resist tensile and shear stresses.

### 2.1.2 Standard Tests on Bitumen (IS 73, IS 1201–1220 Series)

| # | Test Name | Property Evaluated | Standard (IS Code) | Typical Test Value for VG-30 |
|---|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Penetration Test** | Hardness/Consistency | IS 1203 | 30–35 (0.1 mm units) |
| 2 | **Ductility Test** | Adhesion/Flexibility | IS 1208 | ≥ 75 cm (for paving grade) |
| 3 | **Softening Point (Ring & Ball)** | Temperature susceptibility | IS 1205 | ≥ 47 °C |
| 4 | **Viscosity Test** | Flow behavior | IS 1206 | 2400–3600 Poise at 60 °C |
| 5 | **Specific Gravity Test** | Density/Quality check | IS 1202 | ≥ 0.99 |
| 6 | **Flash & Fire Point Test** | Safety against fire | IS 1448 P:21 | Flash ≥ 220 °C |
| 7 | **Loss on Heating (Aging) Test** | Durability against hardening | IS 1212 | Loss ≤ 0.5% |
| 8 | **Solubility in Trichloroethylene** | Purity of bitumen | IS 1216 | ≥ 99% |
| 9 | **Water Content Test** | Moisture content | IS 1211 | ≤ 0.2% |

### 2.1.3 Penetration Test — Concept
The **penetration value** of bitumen is the distance in **tenths of a millimetre (0.1 mm)** that a standard needle vertically penetrates into a bitumen sample under:
- **Load:** 100 g
- **Temperature:** 25 °C
- **Time:** 5 seconds

A **lower penetration** indicates **harder bitumen** (suitable for warmer climates).
A **higher penetration** indicates **softer bitumen** (suitable for colder climates).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The Penetration Index (PI), proposed by Pfeiffer and Van Doormaal, is a measure of **temperature susceptibility** of bitumen. A higher PI (closer to +2 to +7) indicates **lower temperature susceptibility** (better quality bitumen for roads).

### 2.1.4 Ductility Test — Concept
The **ductility** of bitumen is the **distance (in cm)** to which a standard briquette-shaped specimen can be stretched before breaking, at a temperature of **27 ± 0.5 °C** and a pulling rate of **50 mm/min**.

> **Engineering Significance:** A minimum ductility of **75 cm** is required for paving grade bitumen as per IS 73:2013, ensuring the bitumen film does not crack under traffic-induced tensile stresses.

### 2.1.5 Softening Point (Ring & Ball) Test
The **softening point** is the temperature at which a standard bitumen sample softens sufficiently to allow a steel ball (3.5 g) to fall a distance of **25 mm** through a ring of bitumen, under a heating rate of **5 °C/min**.

### 2.1.6 Viscosity Grading (IS 73:2013 — Modern Indian Practice)
The Indian Roads Congress (IRC) and BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) have shifted from **penetration grading** to **viscosity grading** for paving bitumen, namely **VG-10, VG-20, VG-30, and VG-40**. The number represents the **absolute viscosity in Poise at 60 °C**.

| Grade | Viscosity at 60 °C (Poise) | Recommended Use |
|-------|---------------------------|-----------------|
| **VG-10** | 800–1200 | Cold regions / spraying (surface dressing) |
| **VG-20** | 1600–2400 | Cold-mix asphalt, light traffic |
| **VG-30** | 2400–3600 | **Most commonly used in India** for DBM, BC |
| **VG-40** | 3200–4800 | Heavy-duty / high-temperature regions |

### 2.1.7 Modified Bitumen
Polymer-modified bitumen (PMB) is used for heavily trafficked corridors and extreme climates. Common modifiers:
- **SBS (Styrene-Butadiene-Styrene)** – Improves elasticity & fatigue life.
- **EVA (Ethylene-Vinyl-Acetate)** – Improves rutting resistance.
- **Crumb Rubber** – Recycled tire rubber; cost-effective, eco-friendly.

---

## 2.2 Subgrade Soil — Requirements and Tests

### 2.2.1 Requirements of Subgrade
1. **Adequate bearing capacity** — to support pavement and traffic loads.
2. **Stability under moisture** — particularly important for black cotton (expansive) soils.
3. **Low compressibility** — to prevent excessive settlement.
4. **Resistance to volume change** — due to seasonal moisture variation.
5. **Workability** — should be able to be compacted to desired density.

### 2.2.2 California Bearing Ratio (CBR) Test — The Most Important Test

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The California Bearing Ratio (CBR) is the ratio of the load required to cause a standard plunger (49.65 mm dia.) to penetrate the soil at a rate of 1.25 mm/min to a depth of **2.5 mm (or 5.0 mm)** to the **standard load required for the same penetration in a standard crushed stone** (taken as 1370 kg at 2.5 mm and 2055 kg at 5.0 mm).

### 2.2.3 Properties & Tests on Subgrade Soil

| Property | Test | Standard | Acceptance Criterion |
|----------|------|----------|----------------------|
| **Bearing Capacity** | California Bearing Ratio (CBR) | IS 2720 Part 16 | ≥ 8% (IRC:37 recommended) |
| **Compaction** | Proctor / Modified Proctor | IS 2720 Part 7/8 | 95–98% of MDD |
| **Consistency Limits** | Atterberg Limits (LL, PL, SL) | IS 2720 Part 5/6 | LL ≤ 70% for subgrade |
| **Free Swell** | Free Swell Index Test | IS 2720 Part 40 | ≤ 50% preferred |
| **Particle Size** | Wet Sieve / Hydrometer | IS 2720 Part 4 | Grading distribution |
| **Moisture Content** | Oven Drying | IS 2720 Part 2 | OMC ± 2% |
| **Permeability** | Falling/Constant Head | IS 2720 Part 17/36 | Variable |

### 2.2.4 Field Tests for Subgrade Evaluation
- **Plate Bearing Test (PBT)** — In-situ modulus of subgrade reaction.
- **Dynamic Cone Penetrometer (DCP)** — Quick in-situ CBR estimation.
- **Vane Shear Test** — For soft clays (undrained shear strength).

---

## 2.3 Requirements and Tests on Aggregates

### 2.3.1 Physical Requirements (As per MoRTH/IRC)

| Property | Test | Standard (IS) | Recommended Value (for wearing course) |
|----------|------|---------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Strength** | Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV) | IS 2386 Part 4 | ≤ 30% (Wearing), ≤ 35% (Base) |
| **Toughness** | Aggregate Impact Value (AIV) | IS 2386 Part 4 | ≤ 24% (Wearing), ≤ 30% (Base) |
| **Hardness** | Los Angeles Abrasion Value (LAAV) | IS 2386 Part 4 | ≤ 30% (Wearing), ≤ 40% (Base) |
| **Durability** | Soundness (5 cycles of Na₂SO₄) | IS 2386 Part 5 | ≤ 12% (fine), ≤ 18% (coarse) |
| **Particle Shape** | Flakiness Index (FI) & Elongation Index (EI) | IS 2386 Part 1 | Combined ≤ 30% |
| **Cleanliness** | Water Absorption | IS 2386 Part 3 | ≤ 2% |
| **Bitumen Affinity** | Stripping Value (Retained Coating) | IS 6241 | ≥ 95% |
| **Specific Gravity** | Pycnometer | IS 2386 Part 3 | 2.5–2.9 |
| **Gradation** | Sieve Analysis | IS 2386 Part 1 | As per IRC/MoRTH specs |

### 2.3.2 Detailed Explanation of Key Aggregate Tests

#### (a) Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV) Test
- **Apparatus:** Compression testing machine, cylindrical mould (150 mm dia).
- **Procedure:** Aggregate passing 12.5 mm and retained on 10 mm is filled in mould in **3 layers**, each compacted with **25 blows** of tamping rod. Load is applied at a uniform rate to reach **400 kN in 10 minutes**.
- **Formula:**

$$ACV = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100 \text{ (in \%)}$$

Where:
- $W_1$ = Weight of dry aggregate sample (g)
- $W_2$ = Weight of fines passing 2.36 mm sieve (g)

#### (b) Aggregate Impact Value (AIV) Test
- **Procedure:** Aggregate (12.5–10 mm) is placed in the impact test mould and subjected to **15 blows** of a hammer of **13.5–14.0 kg** falling from a height of **380 mm**.
- **Formula:**

$$AIV = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100 \text{ (in \%)}$$

Where:
- $W_1$ = Weight of dry aggregate
- $W_2$ = Weight of fines passing 2.36 mm sieve

#### (c) Los Angeles Abrasion Test
- **Apparatus:** LA machine — a rotating drum (710 mm dia) fitted with a steel shelf. Steel balls (charge) are added with aggregate.
- **Procedure:** Aggregate sample (graded as per grading A/B/C/D) is rotated with **11–12 steel balls (total ~4580 g)** for **500 revolutions at 30–33 rpm**.
- **Formula:**

$$LAAV = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100 \text{ (in \%)}$$

Where:
- $W_1$ = Original weight of aggregate
- $W_2$ = Weight retained on 1.70 mm sieve (after test)

#### (d) Flakiness Index (FI) & Elongation Index (EI)
- **FI** is the % by weight of particles whose **thickness is less than 0.6 × mean sieve size** (passing through the corresponding slot).
- **EI** is the % by weight of particles whose **length is greater than 1.8 × mean sieve size** (retained on the corresponding gauge).
- **Combined FI+EI** should be **≤ 30%** for surface course.

#### (e) Stripping Value Test
- Determines the **affinity of bitumen to aggregate** in the presence of water.
- Aggregate is coated with bitumen, immersed in distilled water at 40 °C for 24 hours.
- **Stripping value** = % area uncoated (retained coating ≥ 95% preferred).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units | Boundary / Note |
|---------|---------|-------|----------------|
| **Penetration Index (PI)** | $\frac{20 \cdot (1 + 25A)}{1 + 50A}$ where $A = \frac{\log 800 - \log \text{Pen at 25°C}}{T_{SP} - 25}$ | Dimensionless | -3 (high temp susceptible) to +7 |
| **Softening-Penetration Relation** | $\log P = A T_{SP} + C$ | 0.1 mm | Pfeiffer & Van Doormaal |
| **CBR** | $\frac{P_T}{P_S} \times 100$ | % | $P_T$ = test load, $P_S$ = standard load |
| **ACV** | $\frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | % | $W_2$ = crushed fines |
| **AIV** | $\frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | % | $W_2$ = impacted fines |
| **LAAV** | $\frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | % | $W_2$ = retained on 1.70 mm |
| **Flakiness Index** | $\frac{W_{thin}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Thickness < 0.6 × mean size |
| **Elongation Index** | $\frac{W_{long}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Length > 1.8 × mean size |
| **Free Swell Index** | $\frac{V_2 - V_1}{V_1} \times 100$ | % | Volume change in water |
| **Moisture Content** | $\frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | % | $W_w$ = wet, $W_d$ = dry |
| **Specific Gravity** | $\frac{W_a}{W_a - W_w}$ | – | Pycnometer method |
| **Compaction MDD/OMC** | Proctor curves | – | Modified = 5.5 kg rammer, 5 layers |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bitumen grading** ensures the right binder is selected for the **climatic and traffic conditions** of a project (e.g., VG-30 for Kerala's tropical climate, VG-40 for high-temperature desert regions like Rajasthan).
- **CBR testing** of subgrade directly determines the **thickness of the pavement** as per IRC:37 (Flexible Pavement Design) or IRC:58 (Rigid Pavement Design).
- **Aggregate tests** like ACV, AIV, and LAAV are the **first quality checks** at the source quarry and batching plant — rejecting non-conforming aggregates prevents premature pavement failure (rutting, ravelling, potholes).
- **Stripping value** is critical in **coastal/high-rainfall regions** (Kerala, Goa) where water-induced stripping is the leading cause of pavement distress.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Penetration Index (PI) Calculation

**Problem:** A bitumen sample has a penetration of **80 at 25 °C** and a softening point of **52 °C**. Compute the **Penetration Index (PI)** and comment on the temperature susceptibility.

### Step 1 — Write down known values
- Penetration at 25 °C, $P_{25} = 80$ (in 0.1 mm)
- Softening point, $T_{SP} = 52$ °C

### Step 2 — Compute the temperature susceptibility factor A

$$A = \frac{\log 800 - \log P_{25}}{T_{SP} - 25}$$

$$\log 800 = \log(8 \times 100) = \log 8 + \log 100 = 0.9031 + 2.0000 = 2.9031$$

$$\log 80 = \log(8 \times 10) = \log 8 + \log 10 = 0.9031 + 1.0000 = 1.9031$$

$$A = \frac{2.9031 - 1.9031}{52 - 25} = \frac{1.0000}{27} = 0.03704$$

### Step 3 — Compute Penetration Index (PI)

$$PI = \frac{20 \cdot (1 + 25A)}{1 + 50A}$$

$$PI = \frac{20 \cdot (1 + 25 \times 0.03704)}{1 + 50 \times 0.03704}$$

$$PI = \frac{20 \cdot (1 + 0.9260)}{1 + 1.8518}$$

$$PI = \frac{20 \cdot 1.9260}{2.8518} = \frac{38.520}{2.8518} \approx +1.36$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
- **PI = +1.36** (closer to +2 range).
- Bitumen is **moderately temperature-susceptible** — acceptable for paving.
- For comparison: **PI = 0** for standard paving bitumen, **PI = +2 to +7** for oxidized (low susceptibility), **PI < 0** for highly temperature-susceptible bitumens.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: California Bearing Ratio (CBR) Calculation

**Problem:** A soaked CBR test on subgrade soil gave the following data. Compute CBR at 2.5 mm and 5.0 mm penetration.

| Penetration (mm) | Load (kg) |
|------------------|-----------|
| 0.0 | 0 |
| 0.5 | 14 |
| 1.0 | 28 |
| 1.5 | 42 |
| 2.0 | 54 |
| 2.5 | 62 |
| 3.0 | 70 |
| 4.0 | 80 |
| 5.0 | 88 |

**Standard Loads:** 1370 kg at 2.5 mm, 2055 kg at 5.0 mm.

### Step 1 — Verify Penetration Curve
The load-penetration curve must be **concave upward (no dips)**. Here, the data is smooth, so we use the readings directly.

### Step 2 — Compute CBR at 2.5 mm penetration
Load at 2.5 mm = 62 kg (read directly from data).

$$CBR_{2.5} = \frac{62}{1370} \times 100 = 4.53\%$$

### Step 3 — Compute CBR at 5.0 mm penetration
Load at 5.0 mm = 88 kg (read directly from data).

$$CBR_{5.0} = \frac{88}{2055} \times 100 = 4.28\%$$

### Step 4 — Adopt Higher CBR (Per IS 2720:16)
As per the standard, the **higher of the two** CBR values is adopted (provided the load at 5.0 mm is greater than at 2.5 mm and curve is valid).

$$\boxed{CBR_{adopted} = 4.53\% \text{ (at 2.5 mm penetration)}}$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation
- **CBR ≈ 4.5%** indicates **weak subgrade** (poor to fair quality).
- **Recommendation:** Either **improve the subgrade** (stabilization with lime/cement) or **increase pavement thickness** as per IRC:37 design charts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction Note (Load-Penetration Curve Correction):** If the curve shows an initial concavity (a "toe" or dip at low penetration), the origin must be shifted so that the curve is corrected. The corrected load = (load at given penetration) − (load at 0.25 × that penetration). This step is critical for accurate CBR reporting.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV)

**Problem:** In an ACV test, the weight of the dry aggregate sample is **W₁ = 2500 g**. After applying a 400 kN load in 10 minutes, the weight of fines passing 2.36 mm sieve is **W₂ = 600 g**. Determine the ACV.

### Solution
$$ACV = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100 = \frac{600}{2500} \times 100 = 24.0\%$$

**Interpretation:** Since ACV (24%) < 30% (MoRTH limit for wearing course), the aggregate is **acceptable** for use in bituminous surface course.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Los Angeles Abrasion Value (LAAV)

**Problem:** An aggregate sample weighing **W₁ = 5000 g** is tested in the LA Abrasion machine (Grading A) with 12 steel balls. After 500 revolutions, the material retained on the 1.70 mm sieve is **W₂ = 3650 g**. Compute the LAAV.

### Solution
$$LAAV = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100 = \frac{5000 - 3650}{5000} \times 100$$

$$LAAV = \frac{1350}{5000} \times 100 = 27.0\%$$

**Interpretation:** LAAV = 27% < 30% (limit for wearing course). Aggregate is **acceptable** for surface course construction.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5: Penetration Grade → Temperature Suitability

A bitumen has penetration values:
- At 25 °C = 60
- At 4 °C = 18
- Softening Point = 50 °C

**Identify the grade and climatic suitability.**

Since penetration at 25 °C = 60 → corresponds to **60/70 grade** (penetration grading system).

Such a grade is suitable for **moderate-temperature Indian conditions (30–35 °C pavement temperature)**, typically used in **DBC, BC, and DBM** mixes for highways.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting Materials for Pavement

| Material Property | Excellent (Premium) | Acceptable (Standard) | Reject |
|-------------------|---------------------|----------------------|--------|
| **CBR (Subgrade)** | > 10% | 5–10% | < 5% |
| **ACV** | < 20% | 20–30% | > 30% |
| **AIV** | < 18% | 18–24% | > 24% |
| **LAAV** | < 25% | 25–30% | > 30% |
| **FI + EI** | < 20% | 20–30% | > 30% |
| **Water Absorption** | < 1% | 1–2% | > 2% |
| **Stripping** | > 95% | 90–95% | < 90% |
| **Bitumen Ductility** | > 100 cm | 75–100 cm | < 75 cm |
| **PI** | +2 to +7 | 0 to +2 | < 0 |

---

## 3.7 Summary of Bitumen Grades — Decision Logic

```
                  ┌─ VG-10: Cold regions, surface dressing
                  │
   PAVING   ──────┼─ VG-20: Cold mix, light traffic
   BITUMEN        │
                  ├─ VG-30: ★ Standard for DBM, BC in India
                  │
                  └─ VG-40: Hot climates, heavy traffic
```
