# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**Q1. Define bitumen and explain the penetration test in detail.**

**Model Answer:**
Bitumen is a viscous, black, semi-solid petroleum product obtained from the refining of crude petroleum, used as a binder in flexible pavement construction. It consists of asphaltenes (provide hardness), resins (provide adhesion), and aromatic oils (provide fluidity).

The **penetration test** is conducted as per IS 1203 to determine the hardness/consistency of bitumen. A standard needle of 100 g load is allowed to penetrate a bitumen sample at **25 °C for 5 seconds**. The penetration is measured in **tenths of a millimetre (0.1 mm)**. A lower penetration value indicates harder bitumen. The result is used to grade bitumen (e.g., 60/70, 80/100 grade).

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Q2. What is the California Bearing Ratio (CBR) test? Write its significance in pavement design.**

**Model Answer:**
The **California Bearing Ratio (CBR) test** (IS 2720: Part 16) is a penetration test for evaluating the mechanical strength of subgrade soil. It is defined as the ratio of the load required to cause a standard plunger to penetrate a soil sample to a specified depth (2.5 mm or 5.0 mm) to the standard load required for the same penetration in standard crushed stone, expressed as a percentage.

**Significance:**
- It is the **primary input** in flexible pavement thickness design as per **IRC:37**.
- Higher CBR → thinner pavement, lower CBR → thicker pavement required.
- A minimum **CBR of 8%** is recommended for subgrade in India.
- It is also used to evaluate **in-situ subgrade** using the **DCP test**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Q (a)** Discuss the various properties and grades of bitumen used in flexible pavement construction. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition of Bitumen:**
A viscous, semi-solid, black, adhesive petroleum derivative used as a binder in bituminous pavements.

**2. Key Properties of Bitumen:**

- **Consistency/Hardness** — Measured by penetration test (IS 1203). Lower penetration → harder bitumen.
- **Ductility** — Ability to deform without breaking under tensile stress. Measured by ductility test at 27 °C (IS 1208). Minimum 75 cm for paving grade.
- **Softening Point** — Temperature at which bitumen softens (IS 1205). Indicates heat resistance.
- **Viscosity** — Resistance to flow at high temperature. Viscosity grading (VG-10, 20, 30, 40) used in India.
- **Adhesion to Aggregate** — Stripping value (IS 6241). High importance in wet/coastal regions.
- **Aging Resistance** — Loss on heating test (IS 1212). Loss ≤ 0.5%.
- **Specific Gravity** — Pycnometer test (IS 1202). Typical 0.99 to 1.02.
- **Purity** — Solubility in trichloroethylene (IS 1216). Purity ≥ 99%.
- **Flash & Fire Point** — Safety criteria, ≥ 220 °C.
- **Temperature Susceptibility** — Penetration Index (PI). PI = 0 (normal) to +2 to +7 (low susceptibility).

**3. Grades of Bitumen:**

- **Penetration Grades** (older system): 30/40, 60/70, 80/100.
- **Viscosity Grades** (IS 73:2013, current): VG-10, VG-20, VG-30, VG-40.
- **Modified Grades**: PMB (Polymer Modified Bitumen) — Crumb Rubber, SBS, EVA.
- **Cutback Bitumens**: RC (Rapid Curing), MC (Medium Curing), SC (Slow Curing).
- **Bitumen Emulsions**: RS (Rapid Setting), MS (Medium Setting), SS (Slow Setting).

**4. Selection of Grade for India:**
**VG-30** is the **most widely used** grade for DBM and BC layers of bituminous pavement in moderate to high traffic conditions. VG-10 is used for surface dressing in cold regions; VG-40 is used in hot regions like Rajasthan.

---

**Q (b)** Explain the California Bearing Ratio (CBR) test for subgrade soil evaluation. Describe the apparatus, procedure, and significance of the test in pavement design. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition:**
The CBR (California Bearing Ratio) is defined as the ratio of the load required to cause a standard plunger to penetrate a soil sample to a depth of 2.5 mm (or 5.0 mm) at a rate of 1.25 mm/min, expressed as a percentage of the standard load (1370 kg at 2.5 mm and 2055 kg at 5.0 mm).

**2. Apparatus:**
- Cylindrical mould (150 mm dia, 175 mm height).
- Plunger (49.65 mm dia).
- Loading machine (mechanical/hydraulic) of 50 kN capacity.
- Dial gauges (2 nos, 0.01 mm accuracy).
- Surcharge weights (4.5 kg, lead shot).
- Soaking tank.

**3. Procedure:**
1. Compact soil sample in mould at **MDD/OMC** in 3 layers using Modified Proctor.
2. **Soak the sample for 96 hours (4 days)** with surcharge weights — simulates worst field moisture conditions.
3. Place the mould under the loading machine, position the plunger, and apply seating load.
4. Apply load at **1.25 mm/min** uniform rate.
5. Record load readings at penetrations of 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 7.5, 10.0 mm.
6. Plot **load vs penetration curve**.
7. Apply curve correction if needed (shift origin).
8. Compute CBR at 2.5 mm and 5.0 mm; adopt the higher value.

**4. Formula:**
$$CBR = \frac{\text{Test Load}}{\text{Standard Load}} \times 100 \text{ (at 2.5 or 5.0 mm)}$$

**5. Significance in Pavement Design:**
- **Primary input** in IRC:37 flexible pavement design charts.
- Soaked CBR ≥ 8% recommended for new subgrades.
- Used in the design of all pavement layers — sub-base, base, and bituminous.
- Quick in-situ CBR can be estimated using the **Dynamic Cone Penetrometer (DCP)**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**

**Q (a)** List the engineering requirements of aggregates in flexible pavement. Explain the Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV) and Los Angeles Abrasion Value (LAAV) tests in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Engineering Requirements of Aggregates:**
- **Strength** — Must resist crushing under heavy loads (ACV test).
- **Toughness** — Must resist impact from moving wheels (AIV test).
- **Hardness** — Must resist wear/abrasion due to tyre wear (LAAV test).
- **Shape** — Angular, cubical particles with low FI+EI for good interlock.
- **Surface Texture** — Rough surface → better bitumen adhesion.
- **Cleanliness** — Free from clay, silt, organic matter, dust.
- **Durability** — Resistance to weathering (Soundness test).
- **Affinity to Bitumen** — High stripping resistance (≥ 95% retained coating).
- **Specific Gravity** — Typically 2.5–2.9; affects mix design.
- **Gradation** — Proper particle size distribution as per IRC/MoRTH.

**2. Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV) Test:**

- **Standard:** IS 2386 (Part 4).
- **Sample:** Aggregates passing 12.5 mm and retained on 10 mm.
- **Procedure:**
   1. Fill mould in **3 layers**, each layer tamped with **25 blows** of 16 mm dia rod.
   2. Place the mould in compression testing machine.
   3. Apply load **uniformly to reach 400 kN in 10 minutes**.
   4. After load, sieve the crushed material through **2.36 mm sieve**.
   5. Weight the fines passing (W₂).
- **Formula:** $ACV = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$
- **Result:** Typically 10–35%. Lower is better. Limit ≤ 30% (wearing course), ≤ 35% (base course).

**3. Los Angeles Abrasion Value (LAAV) Test:**

- **Standard:** IS 2386 (Part 4).
- **Apparatus:** LA machine — rotating drum (710 mm dia) with steel shelf.
- **Procedure:**
   1. Aggregate sample (typically 5000 g, Grading A) is placed in the drum.
   2. Add **12 steel balls** (total ~4580 g) as abrasive charge.
   3. Rotate drum at **30–33 rpm for 500 revolutions**.
   4. Sieve the material through **1.70 mm sieve**.
   5. Weight the material retained (W₂).
- **Formula:** $LAAV = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$
- **Result:** Limit ≤ 30% (wearing course), ≤ 40% (base course).

---

**Q (b)** Differentiate between Penetration Grading and Viscosity Grading of bitumen. State the advantages of viscosity grading over penetration grading. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Penetration Grading System:**

- **Basis:** Depth of penetration of standard needle (100 g, 25 °C, 5 sec).
- **Grades:** 30/40, 60/70, 80/100, 120/150.
- **Indicator:** Empirical hardness, measured at **one temperature (25 °C)**.
- **Limitation:** Does **not directly relate to pavement performance**; only at intermediate temperature.

**2. Viscosity Grading System:**

- **Basis:** Absolute viscosity at **60 °C** (in Poise).
- **Grades:** VG-10, VG-20, VG-30, VG-40.
- **Indicator:** Fundamental engineering property (viscosity) related to **rutting resistance** at high service temperature.
- **IS Code:** IS 73:2013.

**3. Comparative Table:**

| Feature | Penetration Grading | Viscosity Grading |
|---------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Test temperature | 25 °C | 60 °C (kinematic & dynamic) |
| Property | Empirical | Fundamental |
| Performance link | Indirect | Direct (rutting) |
| Current status | Phased out | **Current IS 73:2013** |
| Suitability | Decks of bridges, waterproofing | Roads — DBM, BC, SDBC |

**4. Advantages of Viscosity Grading:**

1. **Directly related to pavement performance** (viscosity at 60 °C is critical for rutting).
2. Measured at **two temperatures** — gives better indication of behavior across climate range.
3. More **scientific and rational**; based on fundamental fluid property.
4. Eliminates ambiguity in bitumen selection for varied climates.
5. Adopted worldwide (ASTM D3381, AASHTO M226).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Bitumen/Aggregate/Subgrade Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the load/pressure correction** in CBR when the load-penetration curve has an initial concave-up "toe" — students lose 2-3 marks for missing the curve correction.
> 2. **Confusing ACV and AIV tests** — both are similar in formula but differ in **load application method** (static compression vs impact hammer). Examiner will deduct marks if apparatus/procedure is mixed up.
> 3. **Not stating IS Codes** — Always cite the relevant IS code (IS 2386 Part 4 for aggregate tests, IS 1203 for penetration, IS 2720:16 for CBR, etc.).
> 4. **Penetration Index formula** is often given incorrectly — students forget that 800 in the numerator refers to a specific penetration reference value from Pfeiffer's work. Practice the formula carefully.
> 5. **Stating "CBR for design is the smaller value"** — this is WRONG. The **higher** of the two CBR values (2.5 mm and 5.0 mm) is adopted, provided the curve is valid.
> 6. **Stripping value misconception** — students often describe it as a "coating" test, but forget to mention the **24-hour immersion at 40 °C** specification.
> 7. **Units in penetration test** — must always be stated as "**0.1 mm**" or "dmm"; never just "mm". This is a frequently lost mark.
> 8. **Forgetting the difference between Soaked and Unsoaked CBR** — for design purposes in India, **soaked CBR is always used** (simulating worst monsoon conditions).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### ★ Bitumen
- ☐ **Penetration test** — needle 100 g, 25 °C, 5 sec; result in **dmm (0.1 mm)**.
- ☐ **Ductility test** — 27 °C, 50 mm/min; minimum **75 cm** for paving grade.
- ☐ **Softening point (Ring & Ball)** — 5 °C/min heating; min **47 °C** for VG-30.
- ☐ **VG-30 is the most common** grade in India.
- ☐ **Penetration Index (PI)** — +2 to +7 = low temperature susceptibility.
- ☐ **Cutback**: RC, MC, SC (solvent-based). **Emulsion**: RS, MS, SS (water-based).
- ☐ **Modified bitumen**: SBS, EVA, Crumb Rubber for high performance.

### ★ Subgrade
- ☐ **CBR test** is the **most important** subgrade test (IS 2720:16).
- ☐ **CBR formula**: (Test Load / Standard Load) × 100.
- ☐ **Soaked CBR** (96 hr) used for design.
- ☐ **CBR ≥ 8%** recommended for subgrade (IRC:37).
- ☐ **Atterberg limits** (LL, PL, PI) — IS 2720 Parts 5 & 6.
- ☐ **Free Swell Index** — important for expansive (black cotton) soils.
- ☐ **Modified Proctor** uses 5.5 kg rammer, 5 layers, 25 blows.

### ★ Aggregates
- ☐ **ACV** — Static compression load of **400 kN in 10 min**; sample 12.5–10 mm; limit **≤ 30%** (wearing course).
- ☐ **AIV** — 15 hammer blows from **380 mm height** (13.5 kg hammer); limit **≤ 24%** (wearing course).
- ☐ **LAAV** — 500 revolutions with steel balls; sieve through **1.70 mm**; limit **≤ 30%** (wearing course).
- ☐ **FI** — thickness < 0.6 × mean sieve size.
- ☐ **EI** — length > 1.8 × mean sieve size.
- ☐ **Combined FI + EI ≤ 30%** for surface course.
- ☐ **Stripping value ≥ 95%** (24 hr water immersion at 40 °C).
- ☐ **Soundness test** — 5 cycles of Na₂SO₄ or MgSO₄.
- ☐ **Water absorption ≤ 2%** for road aggregates.

### ★ IS Codes to Remember
| Test | IS Code |
|------|---------|
| Penetration | IS 1203 |
| Ductility | IS 1208 |
| Softening Point | IS 1205 |
| Viscosity | IS 1206 |
| ACV, AIV, LAAV | IS 2386 (Part 4) |
| FI, EI | IS 2386 (Part 1) |
| Soundness | IS 2386 (Part 5) |
| Specific Gravity | IS 2386 (Part 3) |
| CBR | IS 2720 (Part 16) |
| Atterberg Limits | IS 2720 (Parts 5, 6) |
| Proctor | IS 2720 (Parts 7, 8) |
| Free Swell | IS 2720 (Part 40) |
| Paving Bitumen | IS 73 (2013) |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bitumen and Subgrade Soil (CBR Value); Types of Bitumen and Uses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET745 - PAVEMENT CONSTRUCTION AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bitumen and Subgrade Soil (CBR Value); Types of Bitumen and Uses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Bitumen — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bitumen** is a dark brown to black, viscous, viscoelastic, thermoplastic, and adhesive material derived primarily from the fractional distillation of crude petroleum, or found in natural deposits (like Trinidad Lake Asphalt). It is **completely soluble in carbon disulphide (CS₂)** and is composed essentially of high molecular weight hydrocarbons.

**IS 73 (2013) and IRC SP 53 Classification Basis:**
Bitumen is graded based on:
- **Penetration** (for penetration grade)
- **Absolute Viscosity at 60°C** (for viscosity grade)

### 1.2 Subgrade Soil — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Subgrade** is the prepared and compacted natural soil layer (or imported fill) immediately below the pavement structure, which ultimately bears the entire traffic load transferred through the pavement layers. It is the **foundation of the pavement system**.

### 1.3 California Bearing Ratio (CBR) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **California Bearing Ratio (CBR)** is the ratio of the test load required to cause a plunger of standard cross-sectional area to penetrate the soil at a specific rate to the standard load required to produce the same penetration in a standard sample of crushed stone, expressed as a percentage. It is governed by **IS 2720 (Part 16) — 1987**.

$$\text{CBR (\%)} = \frac{P_t}{P_s} \times 100$$

Where:
- $P_t$ = Test load at specified penetration (kg)
- $P_s$ = Standard load at the same penetration (kg)

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Bitumen as "Black Glue" of Roads:**
Imagine you are making a cake. You have flour (aggregates), sugar (filler), and the binding agent — in this case, **bitumen** acts like the wet chocolate syrup that coats every dry ingredient and, upon cooling (compaction), locks the whole structure into a rigid, durable, and water-resistant mass. Without bitumen, aggregates would simply roll apart under vehicle loads.

**Subgrade as the "Building's Foundation":**
Just as a multi-storey building rests on a strong foundation, the **subgrade** is the bedrock (literally) of any road. A weak subgrade (soft clay) will cause the road to settle, crack, and fail prematurely — no matter how good the surface is.

**CBR as a "Soil Strength Scorecard":**
CBR is like a fitness test for soil. We push a small piston (plunger) into the soil and measure how much load it takes. A stronger soil resists more — like a fit athlete lifting heavier weights. The result is expressed as a percentage compared to a "champion" standard crushed stone sample.

### 1.5 Standard Reference Loads (CBR Test)

| Penetration Depth | Standard Load (kg) | Standard Load (kN) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **2.5 mm** | **1370 kg** | **13.44 kN** |
| **5.0 mm** | **2055 kg** | **20.16 kN** |

> [!NOTE]
> As per IS 2720 (Part 16), the CBR value is usually reported at **2.5 mm penetration**. If the CBR at 5.0 mm is higher, the test must be repeated. If the result persists, the **higher value** is reported.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of CBR

The CBR test is an **empirical penetration test** developed by the California State Highway Department in **1929**, later adopted worldwide. The logic is:

1. A cylindrical plunger of **50 mm diameter** is pushed into a compacted soil sample at a uniform rate of **1.25 mm/min**.
2. Loads are recorded at penetration depths of **0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 7.5, 10.0, and 12.5 mm**.
3. The loads at 2.5 mm and 5.0 mm are compared with standard crushed stone values.
4. The result gives an **index of shear strength** of the subgrade.

**Step-by-Step Logic of Why CBR is Used:**

- **Why CBR?** → Pavement thickness design requires a numerical measure of subgrade strength. CBR provides this directly.
- **Why plunger penetration?** → Simulates wheel load penetration into the subgrade under moving traffic.
- **Why standard crushed stone comparison?** → Provides a universal, repeatable benchmark.
- **Why three days soaking?** → Simulates worst-case field saturation (e.g., monsoon in Kerala).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CBR Value** | $\text{CBR} = \dfrac{P_t}{P_s} \times 100$ | % | Use higher of 2.5 mm or 5.0 mm |
| **Standard Load (2.5 mm)** | $P_s = 1370$ | kg | 13.44 kN |
| **Standard Load (5.0 mm)** | $P_s = 2055$ | kg | 20.16 kN |
| **Soaking Condition** | Soaked CBR (4 days) | — | Simulates monsoon saturation |
| **Pavement Thickness (IRC 37)** | $h \propto \left(\dfrac{1}{\text{CBR}}\right)^{0.2 \text{ to } 0.3}$ | mm | Empirical relation |
| **Penetration Test (Bitumen)** | Penetration in **0.1 mm units** under 100 g load at 25°C for 5 s | dmm | IS 1203 |
| **Softening Point** | Temperature at which bitumen touches a plate 25 mm below a steel ball (IS 1205) | °C | Indicates temperature susceptibility |
| **Ductility** | Distance (cm) to which bitumen elongates before breaking at 27°C (IS 1208) | cm | Indicates flexibility |
| **Viscosity Grade** | Absolute viscosity at 60°C (Poise) | Poise | VG 10, VG 20, VG 30, VG 40 |
| **Specific Gravity** | Ratio of weight of bitumen to weight of equal volume of water at 27°C | — | Typically 0.97 to 1.02 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Table Syntax:** In markdown tables, the vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is written using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the table grid. For example, the formula above uses `\dfrac` instead of inline pipes.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CBR** is the most widely used subgrade strength parameter for **flexible pavement design** in India (as per **IRC:37 — 2018**).
- **Bitumen** is the most consumed hydrocarbon binder for road construction globally — over **100 million tonnes/year** worldwide.
- Selection of bitumen grade directly affects **rutting resistance** in hot climates (Kerala, Tamil Nadu) and **thermal cracking** in cold climates (Himalayan states).

### 2.4 Types of Bitumen — Comprehensive Classification

**A. Based on Source:**
1. **Petroleum Bitumen** — Obtained from crude oil refining.
2. **Natural Bitumen** — Found in nature (e.g., Gilsonite, Trinidad Lake Asphalt).

**B. Based on Grade (IS 73:2013 — Viscosity Grade):**
1. **VG 10** — Very soft; cold climates; priming and surface dressing.
2. **VG 20** — Soft; cold climates; premix carpet and surface dressing.
3. **VG 30** — Most commonly used; moderate climates; hot mix asphalt (HMA) for Indian roads.
4. **VG 40** — Hard; hot climates; heavy traffic corridors.

**C. Based on Penetration Grade (Older IS 73):**
- 30/40, 50/60, 60/70, 80/100 (now largely replaced by VG system).

**D. Cutback Bitumen (IS 217):**
Bitumen blended with a solvent (kerosene/diesel/naphtha) to reduce viscosity for cold application.
- **RC (Rapid Curing)** → Naphtha solvent; dries in minutes.
- **MC (Medium Curing)** → Kerosene solvent; dries in hours.
- **SC (Slow Curing)** → Diesel/heavy oil; very slow drying.

**E. Bitumen Emulsion (IS 3117):**
Bitumen droplets dispersed in water with an emulsifier.
- **RS (Rapid Setting)** → Surface dressing, tack coat.
- **MS (Medium Setting)** → Premix with coarse aggregate.
- **SS (Slow Setting)** → Premix with fine aggregate, fog seal.

**F. Modified Bitumen (IS 15462, IRC SP 53):**
- **PMB (Polymer Modified Bitumen)** — SBS, EVA additives for high-temperature stability.
- **CRMB (Crumb Rubber Modified Bitumen)** — Used with shredded tyre rubber for flexible, durable pavements.

**G. Oxidized / Blown Bitumen:**
Blown with hot air at 200–300°C to make it more rubbery; used in roofing, waterproofing, and pipe coating (not road construction).

### 2.5 Uses of Different Bitumen Types

| Bitumen Type | Specific Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **VG 10** | Spray application, prime coat, tack coat, cold regions |
| **VG 20** | Surface dressing, premix carpet, light traffic roads |
| **VG 30** | DBM, BC, SDBC — main roads, NH, SH (most common) |
| **VG 40** | Heavy traffic, intersections, hot climates |
| **RC (Cutback)** | Surface dressing in cold/wet weather |
| **MC (Cutback)** | Premix in cold weather patching |
| **Emulsion RS** | Tack coat, fog seal, surface dressing |
| **Emulsion SS** | Slurry seal, cold mix, patch repair |
| **PMB/CRMB** | Heavy traffic, expressways, bridge decks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IRC SP 53 Recommendation:** Polymer Modified Bitumen (PMB) is recommended for **intersections, bus stops, toll plazas, and bridge decks** where conventional bitumen fails due to high stress and temperature.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive CBR Calculation — Solved Example

**Problem Statement:**
A soaked CBR test was conducted on a subgrade soil sample. The following loads were recorded:

| Penetration (mm) | Load (kg) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0.5 | 18 |
| 1.0 | 32 |
| 1.5 | 42 |
| 2.0 | 50 |
| **2.5** | **60** |
| 3.0 | 72 |
| 4.0 | 95 |
| **5.0** | **110** |
| 7.5 | 145 |

**Determine the CBR value of the soil.**

#### Step 1: Recall the Standard Loads (IS 2720 Part 16)

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{s,\,2.5} &= 1370 \text{ kg} \\
P_{s,\,5.0} &= 2055 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 2: Calculate CBR at 2.5 mm Penetration

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CBR}_{2.5} &= \frac{P_{t,\,2.5}}{P_{s,\,2.5}} \times 100 \\
\text{CBR}_{2.5} &= \frac{60}{1370} \times 100 \\
\text{CBR}_{2.5} &= 4.379\% \approx 4.38\%
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 3: Calculate CBR at 5.0 mm Penetration

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CBR}_{5.0} &= \frac{P_{t,\,5.0}}{P_{s,\,5.0}} \times 100 \\
\text{CBR}_{5.0} &= \frac{110}{2055} \times 100 \\
\text{CBR}_{5.0} &= 5.352\% \approx 5.35\%
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 4: Compare and Select CBR Value

Since $\text{CBR}_{5.0} > \text{CBR}_{2.5}$, as per IS code, the test should ideally be repeated. However, if the trend persists, the higher value is reported.

$$\boxed{\text{CBR of Soil} = 5.35\% \text{ (at 5.0 mm penetration)}}$$

#### Step 5: Engineering Interpretation

| CBR Range | Soil Classification | Pavement Suitability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 – 3 % | Very poor (soft clay) | Sub-base required, thick pavement |
| 3 – 7 % | Poor to fair (silty clay) | Moderate pavement thickness |
| 7 – 20 % | Good (sandy soil, gravel) | Standard pavement design |
| > 20 % | Excellent (gravel, rock) | Thin pavement possible |

For our soil (CBR = 5.35%), the subgrade is **fair to poor**, requiring improved pavement thickness as per IRC:37.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Bitumen Grade Selection

**Decision Logic for Selecting Bitumen Grade:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Climate Factor} &= \text{Mean Annual Air Temperature (MAAT)} \\
\text{Traffic Factor} &= \text{Cumulative Million Standard Axles (MSA)} \\
\text{Speed Factor} &= \text{Heavy/Slow vs Light/Fast traffic}
\end{aligned}$$

**Selection Table (As Per IS 73:2013 & IRC:111):**

| Bitumen Grade | Climate (Max Temp) | Traffic (MSA) | Common Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **VG 10** | Cold (< 30°C) | < 1.5 | Prime/tack coat, surface dressing |
| **VG 20** | Moderate (30–35°C) | 1.5 – 5 | Premix carpet, light roads |
| **VG 30** | Hot (35–40°C) | 5 – 30 | DBM, BC, NH, SH (Standard) |
| **VG 40** | Very Hot (> 40°C) | > 30 | Expressways, intersections |

**For Kerala State Context:**
Kerala's mean maximum temperature ranges from **29°C to 33°C**, classifying it as a **moderate climate**. Hence, **VG 30** is the most appropriate grade for general road construction. However, coastal areas with heavy truck movement may justify **VG 40** or **PMB**.

---

### 3.3 Relationship Between Subgrade CBR and Pavement Thickness

**IRC:37 Empirical Equation (Simplified):**

$$h = \left[ \frac{p \cdot r}{a \cdot \text{CBR}^b} \right]$$

Where:
- $h$ = total pavement thickness (mm)
- $p$ = contact pressure (tyre inflation pressure)
- $r$ = radius of contact area
- $a$, $b$ = empirical constants (depend on traffic and material)

**Key Insight:** As CBR increases, the required pavement thickness decreases **non-linearly** (power-law relationship). Doubling the CBR does NOT halve the thickness.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Bitumen Quantity Estimation

**Problem:** Estimate the bitumen requirement for a 1 km stretch of 3.75 m wide road with 50 mm thick DBM layer, given mix bitumen content = 5% by weight of total mix. Bulk density of compacted mix = 2.3 g/cc.

#### Step 1: Calculate Volume of DBM Layer

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= L \times W \times t \\
V &= 1000 \text{ m} \times 3.75 \text{ m} \times 0.050 \text{ m} \\
V &= 187.5 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 2: Calculate Weight of Compacted Mix

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{mix}} &= V \times \rho \\
W_{\text{mix}} &= 187.5 \text{ m}^3 \times 2300 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
W_{\text{mix}} &= 431,250 \text{ kg} = 431.25 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 3: Calculate Bitumen Weight (5% by weight)

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{bitumen}} &= 0.05 \times 431,250 \\
W_{\text{bitumen}} &= 21,562.5 \text{ kg} \approx 21.56 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Approximately **21.56 tonnes of bitumen** is required for this stretch.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define California Bearing Ratio (CBR) and state its significance in flexible pavement design. Mention the standard loads used in the test as per IS 2720 (Part 16).

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
California Bearing Ratio (CBR) is defined as the ratio of the test load required to cause a plunger of 50 mm diameter to penetrate a compacted soil sample at a rate of 1.25 mm/min to the standard load required to produce the same penetration in a standard sample of crushed stone, expressed as a percentage. **Significance:** It is a primary input parameter for the design of flexible pavement thickness as per IRC:37. The higher the CBR, the stronger the subgrade and the thinner the required pavement. **Standard loads:** 1370 kg (13.44 kN) at 2.5 mm penetration and 2055 kg (20.16 kN) at 5.0 mm penetration. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** List the four viscosity grades of bitumen as per IS 73:2013 and state one specific use of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
As per IS 73:2013, the four viscosity grades are:
1. **VG 10** — Used for priming and surface dressing in cold climates.
2. **VG 20** — Used for premix carpet and light traffic roads in cold regions.
3. **VG 30** — Used for DBM, BC, and standard road construction in moderate climates (most common).
4. **VG 40** — Used for heavy traffic corridors, intersections, and hot climates. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 1 Internal Choice Question]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain the procedure of the California Bearing Ratio (CBR) test in detail with a neat sketch. State the apparatus used, soil preparation steps, and the standard values adopted as per IS 2720 (Part 16). **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Apparatus Required:**
- CBR mould (150 mm diameter × 175 mm height with collar and base plate)
- Spacer disc (148 mm diameter × 47.7 mm height)
- Compaction rammer (2.6 kg or 4.9 kg)
- CBR penetration plunger (50 mm diameter)
- Loading machine with proving ring (capacity 50 kN, dial gauge sensitivity 0.01 mm)
- Surcharge weights (2.5 kg discs)
- Soaking tank

**Procedure:**

1. **Soil Preparation:** Take about 4.5–5.0 kg of air-dried soil passing through 20 mm IS sieve. Add water to reach **optimum moisture content (OMC)** as obtained from the Standard Proctor / Modified Proctor test.

2. **Compaction:** Fix the spacer disc at the bottom of the mould. Compact the soil in **five equal layers** using a **4.9 kg rammer** with **56 blows per layer** (for Modified Proctor) or **2.6 kg rammer with 55 blows** (for Standard Proctor). Remove the collar and trim the top surface flush.

3. **Soaking:** Place a filter paper on top, position the perforated plate, apply **2.5 kg surcharge weights** (to simulate pavement surcharge), and immerse the mould in a water tank for **96 hours (4 days)**. This simulates worst-case field saturation.

4. **Penetration Test:** After soaking, remove the mould, allow it to drain for 15 minutes, and place it on the loading machine. Position the **50 mm plunger** to make full contact with the soil surface. Apply a **seating load of 4 kg** to ensure proper contact. Set the dial gauges for penetration and load to zero.

5. **Loading:** Apply load such that the plunger penetrates the soil at a **uniform rate of 1.25 mm/minute**. Record the load readings at penetration depths of **0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 7.5, 10.0, and 12.5 mm**.

6. **Plotting:** Plot a **load vs. penetration curve**. Correct the curve by drawing a tangent from the steepest portion back to the y-axis to account for surface irregularities, swelling, and plunger seating errors.

7. **Calculation:** Read the corrected loads at **2.5 mm and 5.0 mm** penetration. Calculate CBR using:
   $$\text{CBR} = \frac{\text{Test Load}}{\text{Standard Load}} \times 100$$
   Report the higher CBR value (after repeat test verification). **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** In a soaked CBR test conducted on a subgrade soil, the corrected loads at 2.5 mm and 5.0 mm penetrations were observed as **45 kg** and **75 kg** respectively. Calculate the CBR value of the soil. State the suitability of the soil as a subgrade material. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Test load at 2.5 mm, $P_{t,\,2.5} = 45$ kg
- Test load at 5.0 mm, $P_{t,\,5.0} = 75$ kg
- Standard load at 2.5 mm, $P_{s,\,2.5} = 1370$ kg
- Standard load at 5.0 mm, $P_{s,\,5.0} = 2055$ kg

**Step 1: Calculate CBR at 2.5 mm Penetration**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CBR}_{2.5} &= \frac{P_{t,\,2.5}}{P_{s,\,2.5}} \times 100 \\
\text{CBR}_{2.5} &= \frac{45}{1370} \times 100 \\
\text{CBR}_{2.5} &= 3.28\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate CBR at 5.0 mm Penetration**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CBR}_{5.0} &= \frac{P_{t,\,5.0}}{P_{s,\,5.0}} \times 100 \\
\text{CBR}_{5.0} &= \frac{75}{2055} \times 100 \\
\text{CBR}_{5.0} &= 3.65\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Comparison and Final CBR Value**

Since $\text{CBR}_{5.0} = 3.65\% > \text{CBR}_{2.5} = 3.28\%$, the test should be repeated. Assuming the trend persists:

$$\boxed{\text{CBR of Soil} = 3.65\% \text{ (governed at 5.0 mm penetration)}}$$

**Step 4: Suitability Assessment**

A CBR of 3.65% classifies the soil as **POOR** (silty clay or clayey silt). As per IRC:37, such a subgrade requires:
- **Improved subgrade** treatment (lime/cement stabilization)
- **Thick pavement** design (approximately 700–800 mm total for moderate traffic)
- A minimum **capping layer** of 150–200 mm granular material

The soil is **NOT suitable as a direct subgrade** for major roads without stabilization. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**

**(a)** Classify the different types of bitumen used in pavement construction. Explain viscosity-graded bitumen and cutback bitumen in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Classification of Bitumen (Comprehensive):**

I. **Petroleum Bitumen** (refined from crude oil)
II. **Natural Bitumen** (Gilsonite, Trinidad Lake Asphalt)
III. **Modified Bitumen** (PMB, CRMB)
IV. **Cutback Bitumen** (RC, MC, SC)
V. **Bitumen Emulsion** (RS, MS, SS)
VI. **Oxidized Bitumen** (industrial use, not paving)

**A. Viscosity-Graded Bitumen (IS 73:2013):**

This is the **current Indian standard** for paving bitumen, replacing the older penetration grade. The grades are designated **VG 10, VG 20, VG 30, and VG 40**, based on **absolute viscosity at 60°C** (in Poise). Higher VG number indicates stiffer, harder bitumen suitable for hotter climates and heavier traffic.

**Advantages of VG system over Penetration Grade:**
- Directly related to **rheological (flow) properties** of bitumen
- Better correlation with **rutting performance** at high temperatures
- More reliable for **performance-based specifications**

**Selection Criteria:**

| Grade | Viscosity at 60°C (Poise) | Climate | Traffic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| VG 10 | 800–1200 | Cold | Light |
| VG 20 | 1600–2400 | Moderate | Medium |
| VG 30 | 2400–3600 | Hot | Heavy |
| VG 40 | 3200–4800 | Very Hot | Very Heavy |

**B. Cutback Bitumen (IS 217):**

Cutback bitumen is a **solution of bitumen in a volatile solvent** (kerosene, diesel, or naphtha). The solvent reduces viscosity, allowing **cold-weather application** without heating. After application, the solvent evaporates, leaving behind the bitumen binder.

**Three Sub-Classes:**
1. **Rapid Curing (RC):** Naphtha solvent — evaporates in **minutes**; used for surface dressing in emergencies or cold/wet weather.
2. **Medium Curing (MC):** Kerosene solvent — evaporates in **hours**; used for premix patching and patch repair.
3. **Slow Curing (SC):** Heavy oil/diesel solvent — evaporates in **days**; used as a primer or for stockpile patching.

**Drawback:** Environmental pollution due to volatile solvent emission; hence, **bitumen emulsion** is increasingly preferred as a green alternative. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Discuss the important engineering properties of bitumen that are routinely tested in the laboratory as per relevant IS codes. State the significance of each test. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Property | Test Method | IS Code | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Penetration** | Depth of needle penetration (0.1 mm) under 100 g load at 25°C for 5 s | **IS 1203** | Indicates **consistency/hardness** of bitumen; lower penetration = harder bitumen |
| **Softening Point** | Ring and ball test — temperature at which bitumen deforms and touches a plate 25 mm below | **IS 1205** | Indicates **temperature susceptibility**; higher value = better high-temperature performance |
| **Ductility** | Distance (cm) to which a standard briquette stretches before breaking at 27°C | **IS 1208** | Indicates **flexibility and adhesive properties**; minimum 75 cm required for paving grade |
| **Viscosity** | Absolute viscosity at 60°C (poise) using rotational viscometer | **IS 1206** | Direct measure of **flow resistance** at pavement service temperature |
| **Flash & Fire Point** | Cleveland open cup / Pensky-Martens | **IS 1209** | Indicates **safety during heating**; bitumen must not be heated above flash point at the plant |
| **Specific Gravity** | Pycnometer method at 27°C | **IS 1202** | Used for **volumetric calculations** of bitumen quantity in mix design |
| **Solubility** | Solubility in trichloroethylene or carbon disulphide | **IS 1216** | Indicates **purity** of bitumen; should be ≥ 99% soluble |
| **Loss on Heating** | Thin film oven test, 163°C for 5 hours | **IS 1212** | Indicates **ageing resistance** of bitumen during mixing and laying |
| **Water Content** | Dean and Stark apparatus | **IS 1211** | Should be ≤ 0.2% to prevent **foaming** during hot mixing |

**Engineering Significance:** These tests collectively ensure that bitumen performs satisfactorily under:
- **High-temperature rutting** (softening point, viscosity)
- **Low-temperature cracking** (ductility, penetration at lower temperatures)
- **Ageing durability** (loss on heating, solubility)
- **Handling safety** (flash and fire point) **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong Standard Load:** Many students incorrectly use 1370 kg for both 2.5 mm AND 5.0 mm. The standard load for 5.0 mm is **2055 kg**, not 1370 kg. (**Loss: 1–2 marks**)
> 2. **Forgetting Units:** CBR is expressed in **%**, not decimals. A CBR of 5 means 5%, not 0.05. (**Loss: 0.5 mark**)
> 3. **Skipping Soaking Justification:** For soaked CBR (4 days), always mention the **purpose of soaking** (monsoon saturation simulation). (**Loss: 1 mark**)
> 4. **Confusing Penetration Grade vs Viscosity Grade:** VG system is the current IS 73:2013 standard. Penetration grade is **obsolete** in India. Examiners may deduct marks for outdated classification. (**Loss: 1 mark**)
> 5. **Not Drawing the Load-Penetration Curve:** For 7-mark questions on CBR test, the **load-penetration curve** is mandatory. Skipping it costs **1 mark** minimum.
> 6. **Forgetting to Specify Penetrations in the CBR Formula:** Always state "CBR at 2.5 mm = X% and CBR at 5.0 mm = Y%". Reporting only the final value loses the **process marks** (2 marks).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CBR** is a measure of subgrade strength; the higher the CBR, the thinner the pavement required.
- **Standard plunger diameter:** 50 mm; **Penetration rate:** 1.25 mm/min; **Standard loads:** 1370 kg (2.5 mm) and 2055 kg (5.0 mm).
- **Soaking duration:** 4 days (96 hours) for soaked CBR to simulate monsoon saturation.
- **CBR is reported at 2.5 mm** normally; if 5.0 mm gives a higher value, **repeat the test** and report the higher value.
- **Bitumen** is a viscoelastic, thermoplastic, and adhesive material derived from crude oil fractional distillation.
- **IS 73:2013** specifies **Viscosity Grade (VG)** system: VG 10, VG 20, VG 30, VG 40 (stiffest to softest, in increasing grade).
- **VG 30** is the most widely used grade in India for standard road construction in moderate climates (e.g., Kerala).
- **Penetration test** measures hardness (IS 1203); **Softening point** measures temperature susceptibility (IS 1205); **Ductility** measures flexibility (IS 1208).
- **Cutback bitumen** (RC, MC, SC) is bitumen + solvent for cold application; **emulsion** is bitumen + water + emulsifier (environmentally friendlier alternative).
- **Modified bitumens** (PMB, CRMB) are used for heavy-traffic, high-stress locations like intersections, bridges, and expressways.
- **Minimum subgrade CBR** for major roads in India is **8%** (as per IRC:37).
- **CBR of clay** = 2–5%, **silty soil** = 5–10%, **sandy soil** = 10–20%, **gravel** = 20–80%.
- **Kerala Context:** Mean max temperature 29–33°C → Use **VG 30** for most projects; **VG 40** for heavy traffic coastal corridors.
- **Specific gravity** of bitumen is typically 0.97–1.02.
- **Ductility** minimum requirement: **75 cm** for paving grade bitumen.
- **Penetration value** for **60/70 grade** (old system) was 60–70 (in 0.1 mm units) — still used in some older specifications.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bituminous Emulsion and Cutback; Methods of Grading of Bitumen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET745 - PAVEMENT CONSTRUCTION AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bituminous Emulsion and Cutback; Methods of Grading of Bitumen

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Bituminous Emulsion

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (As per IS 8887 & IRC SP: 100):**
> A **bituminous emulsion** is a heterogeneous mixture of two immiscible liquids — **bitumen (the dispersed phase)** and **water (the continuous/dispersion medium)** — stabilized by a third agent called an **emulsifier (surfactant)**, which reduces the interfacial tension between the two phases and prevents coalescence.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Mayonnaise Principle:**
Think of mayonnaise, where you cannot normally mix oil and water. By adding **egg yolk (lecithin — a natural emulsifier)**, you get a stable creamy mixture. A bituminous emulsion works on the same principle: bitumen (oil-like, **highly viscous at 25 °C, typically 10⁵ to 10⁹ Pa·s**) is broken into microscopic droplets (**1 µm to 10 µm**) and suspended in water using a chemical emulsifier. When sprayed on an aggregate, the water evaporates, the emulsion "breaks," and the bitumen bonds to the stone — all at **ambient temperature (no heating required)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Emulsions Matter in KTU Context:** They enable **Cold Mix Technology**, reducing energy consumption by **~30–40 %** and eliminating fire hazards during laying.

### 1.2 Cutback Bitumen

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (As per IS 217 & IS 454):**
> **Cutback bitumen** is a free-flowing liquid binder obtained by blending penetration-grade bitumen with a **volatile petroleum solvent (cutter / flux)** to temporarily reduce its viscosity for ease of application. The solvent evaporates over time, leaving behind the original bitumen.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Turpentine-Paint Analogy:**
If thick paint is hard to brush, you thin it with **turpentine (the "cutter")**. The paint flows easily, spreads thin on the wall, and once the turpentine evaporates, the paint hardens. Cutback bitumen behaves identically: **kerosene / naphtha / diesel** is the "turpentine," the **bitumen is the "paint,"** and the road surface is the "wall."

### 1.3 Grading of Bitumen — The Three Pillars

Bitumen is graded (classified) to standardize its properties for different climatic and traffic conditions. KTU 2024 recognizes three grading systems:

| Grading System | Key Parameter | Test Temperature | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Penetration Grading** | Penetration depth (in 1/10 mm) | 25 °C | IS 73 |
| **Viscosity Grading** | Absolute / Kinematic Viscosity | 60 °C (and 135 °C) | IS 1701, ASTM D3381 |
| **Performance Grading (PG)** | Max & Min service temperature range | -10 °C to +76 °C range | ASTM D6373, AASHTO M320 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** India is in a **transitional phase** — penetration grading (IS 73: 2013) is still dominant, but **Viscosity Grade (VG) classification** is preferred for major National Highways, and **PG grading** is mandatory for expressways under MoRTH specifications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Bituminous Emulsion

A typical emulsion consists of:

1. **Bitumen (50–70 % by mass)** — the binder
2. **Water (30–50 %)** — the carrier
3. **Emulsifier (0.1–3.0 %)** — the stabilizer
4. **Additives (0.05–1.0 %)** — stabilizers, anti-stripping agents, break-control agents
5. **HCl or CaCl₂ (traces)** — pH control (acidic for cationic, alkaline for anionic)

### 2.2 Classification of Emulsions

**A. Based on Surface Charge (Most Important for KTU)**

$$\text{Emulsion Types} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\textbf{Anionic} \rightarrow \text{Bitumen droplets carry NEGATIVE charge} \rightarrow \text{Preferred with ALKALINE aggregates (limestone)} \\
\textbf{Cationic} \rightarrow \text{Bitumen droplets carry POSITIVE charge} \rightarrow \text{Preferred with ACIDIC aggregates (granite, quartzite)} \\
\textbf{Non-ionic} \rightarrow \text{Neutral charge} \rightarrow \text{Used in specialized applications}
\end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** **C**ationic = **C**hemically attractive to most Indian aggregates (which are acidic — granite, basalt). Hence cationic emulsions dominate the Indian market (~80 % share).

**B. Based on Setting (Breaking) Speed — IS 8887 Designation**

| Designation | Symbol | Setting Time | Common Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rapid Setting** | RS | 1–5 min | Tack coat, surface dressing, patch repair |
| **Medium Setting** | MS | 15–60 min | Premix cold patching, slurry seal |
| **Slow Setting** | SS | Several hours | Cold mix asphalt, soil stabilization, prime coat |
| **Quick Setting** | QS | 5–30 min | Microsurfacing, cape seal |

**C. Based on Stability**
- **Stable emulsions** — long storage life (CSS-1, SS-1 grades)
- **Unstable emulsions** — break quickly on contact with aggregate

### 2.3 Manufacturing of Emulsion — The Colloidal Mill Process

1. Bitumen is heated to **120–140 °C** to reduce viscosity.
2. Water is heated to **40–60 °C** with emulsifier and additives dissolved in it.
3. Both streams are fed into a **colloidal mill** rotating at **3000–8000 rpm**.
4. High shear forces break bitumen into micron-sized droplets (**1–10 µm**).
5. The freshly formed emulsion is cooled and stored in sealed tanks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature Control:** Bitumen stream must be at **>80 °C** above water stream temperature to prevent premature breaking inside the mill.

### 2.4 Critical Tests on Emulsion (IS 8887 & ASTM D244)

| Test | Purpose | Acceptable Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Viscosity (Saybolt Furol, 50 °C)** | Flowability check | RS1: 20–100 s, MS: ≥100 s |
| **Settlement (5-day)** | Storage stability | ≤ 5 % |
| **Storage Stability (24 h)** | Short-term stability | ≤ 1 % |
| **Particle Charge Test** | Identify anionic/cationic | Pass/Fail |
| **Sieve Test (No. 20, 850 µm)** | Detect coarse particles | ≤ 0.10 % retained |
| **Residue by Distillation** | Actual bitumen content | RS: ≥ 60 %, MS: ≥ 57 %, SS: ≥ 57 % |
| **Residue by Evaporation** | Alternative to distillation | Comparable values |
| **Demulsibility** | Breaking tendency (cationic) | RS: ≥ 40 %, MS: ≥ 30 % |
| **Penetration of Residue** | Hardness after breaking | 40–120 dmm (typical) |
| **Ductility of Residue** | Flexibility | ≥ 50 cm |

### 2.5 Anatomy of Cutback Bitumen

Cutbacks are classified by the **type of solvent** used (which controls evaporation rate = curing rate):

| Type | Solvent Used | Flash Point | Curing Time | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rapid Curing (RC)** | **Naphtha / Gasoline** (highly volatile) | < 27 °C | Hours | Surface dressing, tack coat in cold weather |
| **Medium Curing (MC)** | **Kerosene** (intermediate volatility) | 27–65 °C | Days | Cold mix patching, priming granular bases |
| **Slow Curing (SC)** | **Diesel / Light fuel oil** (low volatility) | > 65 °C | Weeks–Months | Prime coat, dust laying, road mixes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Environmental Concern (KTU 2024 NEP-2020 Sustainability Module):** Cutbacks release **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)**. MoRTH and IRC increasingly restrict MC and SC use in urban zones. Emulsions are the eco-friendlier alternative.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Penetration Index (PI)** — Pfeiffer's Equation | $PI = \dfrac{20 - 500A}{1 + 50A}$ , where $A = \dfrac{\log\, \text{pen at } T_1 - \log\, \text{pen at } T_2}{T_1 - T_2}$ | Dimensionless; $T$ in °C, pen in dmm |
| 2 | **Softening Point (Ring & Ball)** | Empirical; R&B apparatus | °C; 1 cm ball, 3.5 g load |
| 3 | **Viscosity Conversion (kinematic → Saybolt Furol)** | $SF\,(s) = 0.226 \cdot \nu\,(cSt) + \dfrac{195}{\nu} \text{ (approx for } \nu > 70 \text{ cSt)}$ | s and cSt |
| 4 | **Residue Content of Emulsion** | $\%\text{ Residue} = \dfrac{W_r}{W_s} \times 100$ | Mass fraction |
| 5 | **Settlement Test (5-day)** | $\%\text{ Sediment} = \dfrac{\text{Volume of sediment}}{\text{Total volume}} \times 100$ | % |
| 6 | **Spray Temperature (Emulsion)** | RS: 50–85 °C; MS: 50–85 °C; SS: 20–70 °C | °C |
| 7 | **Ductility – Penetration Empirical Link** | $\log\,D = 1.4 + 0.7\,\log\,P$ (approx, where $D$ in cm, $P$ in dmm) | cm & dmm |
| 8 | **PG Grade Nomenclature** | PG $T_{high}-T_{low}$ e.g., PG 64-22 | °C; binder usable from $-22$ to $+64$ °C |
| 9 | **Penetration – Viscosity (Saal formula)** | $\eta = \dfrac{1.58 \times 10^9}{P^{1.56}}$ at 25 °C (P in dmm) | Pa·s |
| 10 | **Setting Time Approximation** | Setting $\propto \dfrac{1}{\text{Temp}}$ and Setting $\propto \dfrac{1}{\text{Emulsifier dose}}$ | Qualitative |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Revision Anchor:** For KTU numericals, remember the **Pfeiffer PI equation** and the **Saal viscosity-penetration relation** — these appear in Part B questions almost every cycle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pfeiffer's Penetration Index (PI)

The Penetration Index is a fundamental rheological parameter that quantifies the **temperature susceptibility** of bitumen.

**Step 1: Penetration is logarithmically related to temperature (Log-Pen-Temp linearity).**

$$\log\,P = A \cdot T + C$$

where $P$ = penetration (dmm), $T$ = temperature (°C), $A$ = slope (negative), $C$ = intercept.

**Step 2: Compute the slope $A$ from two penetration values at two temperatures.**

Given $P_1$ at $T_1$ and $P_2$ at $T_2$:

$$A = \dfrac{\log P_1 - \log P_2}{T_1 - T_2}$$

**Step 3: Relate $A$ to PI by the empirical reference (Pfeiffer & Van Doormaal, 1936).**
A standard bitumen (PI = 0) has a slope $A_0 = -1/50 = -0.02$ per °C when penetration at 25 °C = 100.

The general relationship is:

$$A = \dfrac{1}{50}\left[\dfrac{1 - PI/20}{1 + PI/10}\right]$$

**Step 4: Invert the relationship to solve for PI.**

$$\boxed{PI = \dfrac{20 - 500A}{1 + 50A}}$$

**Step 5: Worked Example (KTU-style calculation).**

A bitumen has penetration **120 dmm at 25 °C** and **45 dmm at 40 °C**. Calculate PI.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\log 120 - \log 45}{25 - 40} \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{2.0792 - 1.6532}{-15} \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{0.4260}{-15} \\[4pt]
  &= -0.02840 \text{ per °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now substitute into Pfeiffer's formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PI &= \dfrac{20 - 500 \times (-0.02840)}{1 + 50 \times (-0.02840)} \\[4pt]
   &= \dfrac{20 + 14.20}{1 - 1.42} \\[4pt]
   &= \dfrac{34.20}{-0.42} \\[4pt]
   &= -81.43
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The negative PI seems odd** — this indicates a calculation error in the sign convention. In KTU problems, **always** ensure $T_1 < T_2$ and $P_1 > P_2$ (penetration decreases with temperature). The correct sign convention is:

$$A = \dfrac{\log P_2 - \log P_1}{T_2 - T_1} \text{ (both numerator and denominator negative, giving positive } A \text{ as magnitude) — use } \vert A\vert$$

**Corrected Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert A \vert &= \dfrac{\log 120 - \log 45}{40 - 25} = \dfrac{0.4260}{15} = 0.02840
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
PI &= \dfrac{20 - 500 \times 0.02840}{1 + 50 \times 0.02840} \\[4pt]
   &= \dfrac{20 - 14.20}{1 + 1.42} \\[4pt]
   &= \dfrac{5.80}{2.42} \\[4pt]
   &= +2.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $PI = +2.40$ indicates a **low temperature-susceptibility** (oxidation-resistant, blown-type bitumen, ideal for roofing rather than pavements). For paving grade, $PI$ is ideally between **-1.5 and +1.0**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Grading System

| Parameter | Penetration Grading | Viscosity Grading | Performance Grading |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Test Property** | Penetration at 25 °C | Viscosity at 60 °C | $G^{*}/\sin\delta$ (rutting) and $S(t)$ (fatigue/cracking) |
| **Basis of Classification** | Consistency at intermediate temperature | Consistency at high service temperature | Performance at actual pavement temperatures |
| **Number of Grades in IS 73 / AASHTO** | 6 (S 35 to S 200) | 4 (VG 10, 20, 30, 40) | Multiple PG XX-YY grades |
| **Worst Drawback** | Tested at 25 °C (no relation to field) | Ignores low-temperature cracking | Requires expensive DSR & BBR equipment |
| **Adoption Status (India)** | Still used for low-volume roads | **Preferred for NHs and SHs** | Mandatory for expressways and high-altitude roads |
| **Reproducibility** | Poor (operator-dependent) | Moderate | Excellent (rheological measurement) |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Residue by Distillation (Emulsion)

> A **200 g sample** of cationic rapid-setting emulsion is subjected to distillation per ASTM D244. The residue collected in the flask weighs **128 g**. Calculate the **percentage residue** and **water content**.

**Step 1: Apply the mass-balance equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{Residue} &= \dfrac{W_r}{W_s} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{128}{200} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 64.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute water + solvent (distillate) content.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{Distillate} &= 100 - \%\,\text{Residue} = 100 - 64.0 = 36.0\% \\[4pt]
\text{Mass of distillate} &= 200 - 128 = 72\,\text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Quality Check against IS 8887 specification for RS-1 cationic.**

Required residue ≥ 60 % → **Passes (64.0 %)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Penetration test on residue (at 25 °C, 100 g, 5 s)** must yield **100–250 dmm** for RS-1. This is a common KTU Part A sub-question.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Emulsion vs. Cutback

| Property | Bituminous Emulsion | Cutback Bitumen |
|---|---|---|
| **Diluent** | Water + emulsifier | Petroleum solvent (kerosene, naphtha, diesel) |
| **Curing Mechanism** | Water evaporation + emulsion break | Solvent evaporation |
| **Application Temperature** | Ambient (or warm: 50–85 °C) | Ambient (or warm: 30–80 °C depending on grade) |
| **Energy Required** | Low (no bitumen heating mandatory) | Low (some heating may help) |
| **Fire Hazard** | None (water-based) | High (naphtha flash point <27 °C) |
| **Environmental Impact** | Low (water is harmless) | High (VOCs, air pollution) |
| **Storage Stability** | Limited (1–3 months, may settle) | Excellent (months to a year) |
| **Aggregate Moisture Tolerance** | High (can be applied on damp aggregate) | Low (aggregate must be dry) |
| **Cost** | Slightly higher binder cost, lower handling cost | Lower binder cost, higher handling/safety cost |
| **Best Suited For** | Dense traffic, urban, eco-sensitive zones | Remote areas, dust control, prime coat |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Emulsion Manufacturing Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bitumen Storage Tank\n120 to 140 C] --> B[Bitumen Heater]
    B --> C[Colloidal Mill\n3000 to 8000 rpm]
    D[Water Tank\n40 to 60 C] --> E[Emulsifier + Acid\nMixing Vessel]
    E --> C
    C --> F[Cooling Chamber]
    F --> G[Storage Tank\nSealed, Ambient]
    G --> H[Quality Lab\nIS 8887 Tests]
    H --> I[Dispatch to Site\nSprayer or Mixer]

    classDef hot fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cool fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px
    class A,B hot fill
    class D,E cool fill
    class C,F,G,H,I proc fill
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree — Cutback Bitumen

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cutback Bitumen\nBitumen + Solvent]
    A --> B[RC - Rapid Curing\nSolvent: Naphtha\nFlash Point: below 27 C\nCure: Hours]
    A --> C[MC - Medium Curing\nSolvent: Kerosene\nFlash Point: 27 to 65 C\nCure: Days]
    A --> D[SC - Slow Curing\nSolvent: Diesel or Light Fuel Oil\nFlash Point: above 65 C\nCure: Weeks to Months]

    B --> B1[RC 70\nRC 250\nRC 800\nRC 3000]
    C --> C1[MC 30\nMC 70\nMC 250\nMC 800\nMC 3000]
    D --> D1[SC 70\nSC 250\nSC 800\nSC 3000]

    B1 --> APP1[Surface Dressing\nTack Coat\nPatch Repair in Cold Weather]
    C1 --> APP2[Premix Cold Patching\nPrime Coat\nGranular Base Treatment]
    D1 --> APP3[Prime Coat\nDust Palliation\nSlow-Traffic Road Mixes]

    classDef root fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef type fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef grade fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,stroke-width:2px
    classDef app fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,stroke-width:2px
    class A root fill
    class B,C,D type fill
    class B1,C1,D1 grade fill
    class APP1,APP2,APP3 app fill
```

### 4.3 Comparative Architecture — Three Grading Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    START[Bitumen Binder\nUnclassified] --> Q1{Tested Property?}
    Q1 -->|Penetration at 25 C| P[Penetration Grading\nIS 73 - 2013]
    Q1 -->|Viscosity at 60 C| V[Viscosity Grading\nIS 1701 - 1988]
    Q1 -->|Performance at\nMax and Min Temp| PG[Performance Grading\nASTM D6373]

    P --> P1[Grades: S 35 to S 200\nStiffness at 25 C basis]
    V --> V1[Grades: VG 10 VG 20 VG 30 VG 40\nViscosity at 60 C basis]
    PG --> PG1[Grades: PG 58 - 22 etc.\nRutting and Cracking basis]

    P1 --> P2[Use: Low volume roads\nPWD rural roads]
    V1 --> V2[Use: NH and SH\nMost MoRTH works]
    PG1 --> PG3[Use: Expressways\nHigh altitude and Extreme climate]

    classDef start fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,stroke-width:2px
    classDef grad fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef grade fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,stroke-width:2px
    classDef app fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,stroke-width:2px
    class START start fill
    class P,V,PG grad fill
    class P1,V1,PG1 grade fill
    class P2,V2,PG3 app fill
```

### 4.4 Emulsion Setting Mechanism — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Emulsion Spray on Aggregate] --> Y[Step 1: Water Evaporation\nfrom thin film]
    Y --> Z[Step 2: Particle Approach\nElectrostatic attraction]
    Z --> W[Step 3: Emulsion Break\nCoalescence of bitumen droplets]
    W --> U[Step 4: Bitumen Film Formation\nAdhesion to aggregate]
    U --> R[Step 5: Curing and Strength Gain\nFinal bond development]

    X -->|Rapid Setting RS| RST[Time: 1 to 5 min]
    X -->|Medium Setting MS| MST[Time: 15 to 60 min]
    X -->|Slow Setting SS| SST[Time: Several hours]

    RST --> R
    MST --> R
    SST --> R

    classDef step fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,stroke-width:2px
    classDef time fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,stroke-width:2px
    classDef end fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px
    class X,Y,Z,W,U,R step fill
    class RST,MST,SST time fill
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define bituminous emulsion. List any four tests performed on emulsions as per IS 8887.**

**Model Answer:**
A bituminous emulsion is a **heterogeneous mixture of bitumen and water**, stabilized by an emulsifying agent, used primarily for cold-mix pavement construction. The bitumen is dispersed as fine droplets (1–10 µm) in the continuous water phase.

Four tests as per IS 8887:

1. **Viscosity test** (Saybolt Furol viscometer at 50 °C)
2. **Settlement test** (5-day storage stability)
3. **Sieve test** (No. 20, 850 µm) — detects coarse particles
4. **Residue by distillation** — confirms minimum bitumen content (≥60 % for RS, ≥57 % for MS/SS)

> [!TIP]
> **3-mark Pro-Tip:** For full marks, always mention the **Indian Standard code (IS 8887: 2018)** along with the test name. Examiners reward specificity.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between rapid-setting (RS) and slow-setting (SS) bituminous emulsions in terms of setting time and typical applications.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | RS Emulsion | SS Emulsion |
|---|---|---|
| **Setting Time** | 1–5 minutes (fast break) | Several hours (slow break) |
| **Emulsifier Dose** | Low (0.1–0.5 %) | High (0.5–1.5 %) |
| **Mixing Stability** | Poor (cannot be mixed with aggregate for long) | Excellent (stable during mixing) |
| **Typical Application** | Tack coat, surface dressing, fog seal | Cold mix asphalt, soil stabilization, prime coat |
| **Particle Size** | Coarser (5–10 µm) | Finer (1–3 µm) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the manufacturing process of bituminous emulsion with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the role of emulsifier and differentiate between anionic and cationic emulsions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is cutback bitumen? Classify cutbacks into RC, MC, and SC with their solvents, flash points, and applications. Why is the use of cutback bitumen discouraged in modern pavement construction? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Manufacturing of Emulsion:**

The manufacturing process follows these sequential steps:

1. **Heating the bitumen:** Penetration-grade bitumen is heated in a jacketed tank to **120–140 °C** to bring it to a pumpable viscosity (~0.2 Pa·s).
2. **Preparation of soap solution:** Water is heated to **40–60 °C** in a separate tank. The emulsifier (fatty amine for cationic / fatty acid for anionic) and stabilizers (CaCl₂ or HCl) are dissolved to form the "soap."
3. **Metering & feeding:** The hot bitumen stream and the soap solution are metered in the desired proportion (typically **60:40 bitumen:water** by mass) and pumped into the colloidal mill.
4. **Colloidal milling:** A high-speed rotor (3000–8000 rpm) inside a close-tolerance stator generates intense shear that **disperses bitumen into micron-sized droplets** (1–10 µm).
5. **Cooling & storage:** The hot emulsion is cooled to below 60 °C, then transferred to sealed storage tanks to prevent skin formation and water evaporation.
6. **Quality control:** Samples are tested per IS 8887 (viscosity, residue, particle charge, sieve test, settlement).

**Role of Emulsifier:** The emulsifier is a **surfactant molecule** with a hydrophilic (water-loving) head and a lipophilic (oil-loving) tail. It positions itself at the bitumen-water interface, **reducing interfacial tension** (from ~30 mN/m to ~1–2 mN/m) and imparting an **electrostatic charge** to the bitumen droplets, preventing coalescence.

**Anionic vs Cationic:**

| Property | Anionic | Cationic |
|---|---|---|
| **Emulsifier type** | Fatty acid + NaOH / KOH (alkaline soap) | Fatty amine + HCl (acidic salt) |
| **Bitumen droplet charge** | Negative | Positive |
| **pH of emulsion** | 9–12 (alkaline) | 2–5 (acidic) |
| **Compatible aggregate** | Alkaline (limestone, dolomite) | Acidic (granite, basalt, quartzite) |
| **Dominant in India** | Historical (declining) | Current (~80 % market share) |
| **Setting mechanism** | Water evaporation only | Chemical reaction with aggregate + evaporation (faster break) |

**(b) Cutback Bitumen:**

Cutback bitumen is bitumen whose viscosity has been temporarily reduced by blending with a **volatile petroleum cutter**. After application, the cutter evaporates, restoring the original bitumen properties.

**Classification:**

| Type | Solvent | Flash Point (°C) | Curing Time | Applications |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RC (Rapid Curing)** | Naphtha / Gasoline | < 27 | Hours | Surface dressing in cold weather, tack coat, patch repair |
| **MC (Medium Curing)** | Kerosene | 27–65 | Days | Cold mix patching, prime coat on granular base |
| **SC (Slow Curing)** | Diesel oil | > 65 | Weeks to months | Dust control, slow-traffic road mix, prime coat |

**Why cutbacks are discouraged:**

1. **Environmental hazard:** Solvents are **VOCs** contributing to urban air pollution and smog formation.
2. **Fire risk:** RC-grade naphtha has flash point below ambient temperature, posing **storage and handling hazards**.
3. **Higher total VOC emissions:** Approximately **50–80 g of VOC per kg of cutback** applied, violating CPCB norms.
4. **Cost over lifecycle:** Energy spent on solvent recovery or environmental mitigation exceeds the savings in heating.
5. **MoRTH/IRC policy:** Specifications (MORT&H 5th revision) restrict cutbacks to **non-urban, low-volume roads only** and recommend **emulsions as the preferred alternative** for prime/tack coats.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **three methods of grading of bitumen** — penetration, viscosity, and performance grading. Compare their merits and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bitumen sample has penetration **90 dmm at 25 °C** and **32 dmm at 40 °C**. Calculate the **Penetration Index (PI)** using Pfeiffer's equation and comment on its temperature susceptibility. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Methods of Grading:**

**1. Penetration Grading (IS 73: 2013):**
The oldest and most widely used method in India. Bitumen is classified based on the **depth a standard needle penetrates the binder under a 100 g load at 25 °C for 5 seconds**, expressed in units of 1/10 mm. Grades: **S 35, S 45, S 55, S 65, S 90, S 200** (numerical value = penetration range).

* **Merits:** Simple apparatus, low cost, decades of field correlation.
* **Limitations:** Tests at a single arbitrary temperature (25 °C) that does **not represent the in-service pavement temperature range** (5–70 °C). Two bitumens with the same penetration can behave very differently on the road.

**2. Viscosity Grading (IS 1701: 1988, ASTM D3381):**
Bitumen is graded by its **absolute viscosity at 60 °C** (the maximum pavement service temperature) and **kinematic viscosity at 135 °C** (the mixing/compaction temperature). Grades in India: **VG 10, VG 20, VG 30, VG 40** (number = viscosity range in poise × 100, at 60 °C).

* **Merits:** Directly related to **rutting resistance** (a high-temperature failure).
* **Limitations:** Ignores **low-temperature cracking** performance. VG 30 is the most common choice for Indian NHs.

**3. Performance Grading (PG) (ASTM D6373, AASHTO M320):**
Bitumen is classified as **PG XX-YY**, where XX = maximum design pavement temperature (high-temp rutting criterion) and YY = minimum design pavement temperature (low-temp cracking criterion). Properties are measured using **Dynamic Shear Rheometer (DSR)** for high-temp ($G^{*}/\sin\delta \geq 1.0$ kPa original, $\geq 2.2$ kPa RTFO-aged) and **Bending Beam Rheometer (BBR)** for low-temp ($S(t) \leq 300$ MPa, $m \geq 0.300$).

* **Merits:** Directly tied to **field performance** across the full service temperature range. Mandatory for **SHRP-classified** high-grade projects.
* **Limitations:** Requires **expensive equipment** (DSR ≈ ₹30 lakh, BBR ≈ ₹40 lakh) and skilled operators.

**Comparative Table:**

| Aspect | Penetration | Viscosity | Performance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Performance correlation | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| Equipment cost | Very low | Low | High |
| Temperature range covered | Single (25 °C) | Two (60 °C, 135 °C) | Wide (e.g., -22 to +64 °C for PG 64-22) |
| India adoption | Universal historically | Current NH standard | Future / expressways |
| Rutting indicator | Indirect | Direct (60 °C viscosity) | Direct ($G^{*}$) |
| Cracking indicator | None | None | Direct ($S$, $m$-value) |

**(b) Penetration Index Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 90$ dmm at $T_1 = 25$ °C
- $P_2 = 32$ dmm at $T_2 = 40$ °C

**Step 1: Compute the slope $A$ of the log-Pen-Temperature line.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\log P_1 - \log P_2}{T_1 - T_2} \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{\log 90 - \log 32}{25 - 40} \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{1.9542 - 1.5051}{-15} \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{0.4491}{-15} \\[4pt]
  &= -0.02994 \text{ per °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Pfeiffer's equation using the magnitude (since slope is conventionally negative).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PI &= \dfrac{20 - 500 \times 0.02994}{1 + 50 \times 0.02994} \\[4pt]
   &= \dfrac{20 - 14.97}{1 + 1.497} \\[4pt]
   &= \dfrac{5.03}{2.497} \\[4pt]
   &= 2.01
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.**

A **PI of +2.0** indicates that the bitumen has **low temperature susceptibility** (it hardens and softens more slowly with temperature changes). Such bitumens are **more oxidized / blown** and are typically used for **roofing, waterproofing, and industrial applications** rather than for flexible pavements.

For **pavement construction**, the desired PI range is **−1.5 to +1.0**, with most paving-grade bitumens falling in **−1.0 to +0.5** (so-called "normal" temperature susceptibility).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Sign error in $A$:** The most common mistake is treating $A$ as positive. Always remember: as temperature increases, penetration **decreases**, so $A$ is **negative**. Use $|A|$ in the formula.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** Some students write $\log$ in base 10 but compute $A$ in natural log. **Always use base 10** for Pfeiffer's equation (IS convention).
> 3. **Range misinterpretation:** Don't conclude "PI = +2 is best" — in pavement engineering, **PI within −1.5 to +1.0 is preferred**. Outside this range = unsuitable for paving.
> 4. **No unit on PI:** PI is **dimensionless**, but mention this explicitly to avoid 0.5 mark deductions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Emulsion** = Bitumen + Water + Emulsifier (a cold-mix binder; environmentally safe, fire-safe).
- **Cutback** = Bitumen + Petroleum Solvent (declining use due to VOC and fire hazards).
- **Cationic emulsions dominate India** because most Indian aggregates (granite, basalt) are acidic.
- **Emulsifier dose** controls setting time: **Low dose → RS**, **High dose → SS**.
- **RC, MC, SC cutbacks** differ in **solvent type and volatility**, not in bitumen grade.
- **Penetration grading (IS 73)** is the oldest; **Viscosity grading (VG 10/20/30/40)** is the current MoRTH standard; **Performance grading (PG XX-YY)** is the future for expressways.
- **Pfeiffer's PI** quantifies temperature susceptibility; paving-grade bitumen needs **PI between −1.5 and +1.0**.
- **Particle charge test** distinguishes anionic from cationic emulsions in the field.
- **Residue by distillation ≥ 60 % (RS) / 57 % (MS, SS)** — these are the KTU-mandated minimums.
- **PG grading** uses **DSR** ($G^{*}/\sin\delta$) for high-temp and **BBR** ($S$, $m$-value) for low-temp performance.
- **Saal's formula** $\eta = 1.58 \times 10^9 / P^{1.56}$ links penetration to viscosity at 25 °C — useful for converting data across grading systems.
- **Saybolt Furol** viscosity at 50 °C is the standard field test for emulsion consistency.
- **Naphtha** (RC) has flash point **< 27 °C** — most hazardous; **Diesel** (SC) has flash point **> 65 °C** — least hazardous.
- **MoRTH restricts MC and SC cutbacks** in urban / environmentally sensitive zones; **emulsions are the preferred alternative**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Penetration Layer System and Premixed System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET745 - PAVEMENT CONSTRUCTION AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Penetration Layer System and Premixed System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Penetration Layer System (Penetration Macadam)

### Formal KTU Definition
The **Penetration Layer System**, technically called **Penetration Macadam** (or **Tarmacadam** when tar was historically used), is a pavement construction technique in which a compacted layer of **single-sized coarse aggregates** is first laid, after which a **bituminous binder** is sprayed and allowed to **penetrate (percolate) downward by gravity** into the open voids of the aggregate skeleton. This is followed by the application of **finer choke aggregates** to fill the residual voids and lock the structure. The result is a dense, stable, and water-resistant bituminous bound layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Origin (KTU Board Favourite):**
> The method was pioneered by **Edgar Purnell Hooley** in **1901** and named after **John Loudon McAdam** (the 19th-century Scottish road engineer). Hence the term "**Macadam**."

### Types of Penetration Macadam (as per IRC / MoRTH)
1. **Single Coat Penetration Macadam** – one layer of coarse aggregate + one application of binder + one layer of choke aggregate.
2. **Double Coat Penetration Macadam** – two successive aggregate + binder cycles.
3. **Triple Coat Penetration Macadam** – three successive cycles (used for very heavy traffic / heavy compaction).

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) 🍰
> Imagine building a **layered chocolate cake with a poured syrup**:
> 1. First, you place a layer of **large chocolate chunks** (coarse aggregate).
> 2. Then you pour **warm chocolate syrup** (bitumen) over it. The syrup **seeps down** through the gaps by gravity (this is the "**penetration**" part).
> 3. Finally, you sprinkle **small chocolate chips** (choke aggregate) on top to fill the remaining gaps, then press the cake firmly.
>
> That is exactly how a penetration macadam road is built — a **two-phase system** where the binder is **poured on top** and **allowed to percolate**, rather than being pre-mixed.

### Key Physical Metrics
- **Coarse Aggregate Size (Single Coat):** 50 mm to 63 mm (nominal), with stone chips passing 75 mm and retained on 50 mm.
- **Key Aggregate (Choke):** 12 mm to 20 mm nominal size.
- **Layer Thickness (Single Coat):** **50 mm to 75 mm** compacted.
- **Bitumen Grade:** Viscosity Grade **VG-30** or **VG-40** (as per IS 73).
- **Bitumen Quantity:** Roughly **50 to 55 kg per 10 m²** area for single coat (≈ 5–5.5 kg/m²).
- **Rolling Equipment:** **8–10 tonne smooth-wheeled static roller** (NOT pneumatic, to avoid sucking out the binder).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Keyword Trap:** A common mistake is calling penetration macadam a "**pre-mixed**" layer. They are **fundamentally opposite processes**. In penetration macadam, the binder is **NOT pre-mixed** with aggregate — it is **sprayed over the laid aggregate** and allowed to penetrate.

---

## 1.2 Premixed System (Premix Macadam)

### Formal KTU Definition
The **Premixed System (Premix Macadam / Premixed Bituminous Macadam)** is a pavement construction technique in which the **coarse aggregates, fine aggregates, filler, and bituminous binder are proportioned and mixed together in a controlled plant** (either a **Hot Mix Plant** or a **Cold Mixing Plant**) **before being transported, laid, and compacted** on the prepared road surface. The mix is **homogeneous**, and the binder coats every single particle at the plant itself.

### Types of Premix (KTU Syllabus Classification)
1. **Open-Graded Premix (Premix Carpet / PMC):** Air voids 15–20%, used as a **wearing course** for low–medium traffic. Followed by a **seal coat**.
2. **Dense-Graded Premix (Bituminous Macadam / BM, Dense Bituminous Macadam / DBM):** Air voids 3–5%, used as a **base / binder course** for medium–heavy traffic.
3. **Semi-Dense Bituminous Concrete (SDBC):** Intermediate grading.
4. **Bituminous Concrete (BC):** Dense, used as a wearing course for heavy traffic.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) 🍪
> Compare it to **baking cookies at home**:
> - You first **mix the flour, butter, sugar, and chocolate chips together in a bowl** (this is the **premix** — binder + aggregates combined in plant).
> - You then **shape and place the cookie dough on a tray** (this is the **laying**).
> - Finally, you **press and bake** it (this is the **compaction**).
>
> Unlike the chocolate cake analogy (penetration), **everything is pre-blended before placement** — uniform, controlled, and modern.

### Key Physical Metrics
- **Mixing Temperature (HMA):** **150 °C to 165 °C** (for VG-30 bitumen).
- **Laying Temperature:** **120 °C to 150 °C** (must not fall below 115 °C before compaction).
- **Compaction Temperature:** **≥ 90 °C**.
- **Layer Thickness:** 50–100 mm (BM), 40–50 mm (BC), 20–25 mm (PMC).
- **Bitumen Content:** **3.5% to 5.0%** by weight of mix (typical for BM/BC).
- **Air Voids (Design):** 3–5% (dense-graded), 15–20% (open-graded).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Reality (KTU Current Practice):** Penetration macadam is now **obsolete for national highways and expressways** due to its inconsistency and labour-intensive nature. **Premixed systems (HMA)** dominate modern pavement construction. However, penetration macadam is still in your syllabus and is asked in exams as a **conceptual/evolutionary question**.

---

## 1.3 Why Study Both? (Module Context)

| Aspect | Penetration Macadam | Premixed System |
|---|---|---|
| **Era of Dominance** | Pre-1960s, rural roads | Modern era (post-1970) |
| **Quality Control** | Poor (field-dependent) | Excellent (plant-controlled) |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate–High |
| **Skill Required** | Field labour | Plant operator + paver |
| **Traffic Suitability** | Low–Medium | Low → Very Heavy |
| **KTU Relevance** | Historical + Conceptual | Industry Standard |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for exams — "PEM PRE":** **P**enetration = **E**mpty voids filled **M**anually; **PRE**mix = **PRE**-blended at plant. The word "PRE" itself is the trick!
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Penetration Macadam — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

The construction logic follows a strict **5-stage sequence** per coat. Let us break each stage:

### Stage 1: Preparation of Base
- The **sub-base / WBM base** is prepared, cleaned, and lightly watered.
- A **tack coat** is applied (if over an existing bituminous layer) at **2.5 to 3.0 kg per 10 m²**.
- Purpose: To ensure adhesion between the existing surface and the new macadam layer.

### Stage 2: Spreading Coarse Aggregate
- **Clean, dry, angular, single-sized coarse aggregate** (50–63 mm for single coat) is spread uniformly.
- Layer is **dry-rolled** using an 8–10 tonne roller to achieve initial keying.
- Quantity: Approximately **0.6 to 0.75 m³ per 10 m²** (depends on nominal size).

### Stage 3: Application of Bituminous Binder (1st Application)
- **Heated bitumen** (VG-30, at 150–180 °C) is sprayed uniformly using a **mechanical bitumen sprayer / pressure distributor**.
- **Rate:** **50 kg per 10 m²** (≈ 5 kg/m²) for the first coat in single-coat macadam.
- The binder **penetrates downward** by gravity into the open voids — this is the **defining characteristic**.

### Stage 4: Spreading of Key Aggregate (Choking)
- Before the binder cools, **12 mm to 20 mm nominal key aggregate** is spread evenly.
- This **chokes** the surface voids, locking the coarse aggregate and preventing the binder from being picked up by the roller.

### Stage 5: Final Rolling & Curing
- Rolling is done with a **smooth-wheeled static roller** (8–10 tonne) starting from edges towards centre.
- **No vibratory roller** and **no pneumatic roller** (pneumatic would suck up the fresh binder).
- **Curing period:** 24 hours before opening to traffic.

### Double Coat Logic (Why Two Coats?)
- The **2nd coat** uses a **smaller nominal size** aggregate (e.g., 25–40 mm), sprayed with binder, and choked with even finer material.
- Total thickness: **75 mm to 100 mm**.
- Improves **impermeability** and **load distribution** for heavier traffic.

### Triple Coat Logic
- Used for **very heavy commercial traffic** or on weak subgrades.
- Total thickness: **100 mm to 125 mm**.
- Aggregate sizes step down: 50–63 mm → 25–40 mm → 12–20 mm.
- **Heaviest bitumen application** — total binder can reach 12–15 kg/m².

> [!NOTE]
