> **The "Why" Behind Penetration:** The penetration process relies on **gravity + capillary action + viscosity of bitumen**. If bitumen is too cold (high viscosity), it won't penetrate. If too hot, it will drain to the bottom and not coat the middle layer. **Temperature control is everything** in penetration macadam.

---

## 2.2 Premixed System — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

The premixed system follows a **plant-controlled, mass-production** workflow:

### Stage 1: Aggregate Handling & Drying
- Aggregates are **fed through cold feed bins** → **drum dryer** (heats to 150–165 °C) → **hot aggregate elevator** → **vibratory screens** → separated into bins by size.

### Stage 2: Batching & Mixing
- Proportionate aggregates (by weight) are fed into the **pugmill mixer** along with **heated bitumen** (VG-30 at 150–160 °C) and **filler** (stone dust, hydrated lime, cement).
- Mixing time: **30 to 60 seconds**.
- The mix is **homogeneous** — every particle is uniformly coated.

### Stage 3: Transportation
- Hot mix is loaded into **insulated tipper trucks** and transported to site.
- **Loss of temperature allowance:** 10–15 °C max from plant to site.

### Stage 4: Laying
- A **mechanical paver finisher** (e.g., VÖGELE, MARINI) lays the mix at the specified thickness with **pre-compaction of 85–90%**.
- Laying is **uniform, continuous, and grade-controlled** using the paver's automatic screed.

### Stage 5: Compaction
- **Breakdown rolling:** 8–10 tonne static roller (or vibratory at low amplitude).
- **Intermediate rolling:** Pneumatic tyred roller (12–15 tonne wheel load).
- **Finish rolling:** 6–8 tonne tandem roller to remove roller marks.
- Compaction is continued until the desired **density (≥ 95% of Marshall density)** is achieved.

### Stage 6: Quality Control
- **Field density** by sand replacement or core cutter.
- **Temperature checks** at every stage.
- **Marshall samples** taken for stability, flow, and air voids.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quantitative Formulas for KTU Board Exams**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Spray Rate of Bitumen** | $R_b = \dfrac{V_b \times \rho_b}{A}$ | kg/m² | Quantity of binder in surface dressing / tack coat |
| 2 | **Quantity of Aggregate per Area** | $Q_a = t \times \rho_b \times (1-v)$ | m³/m² | Volume of loose aggregate = compacted thickness × bulk density × (1 + wastage) |
| 3 | **Bulk Density of Compacted Aggregate** | $\rho_b = G \times \rho_w \times (1-n)$ | kg/m³ | Where $G$ = specific gravity, $n$ = voids ratio |
| 4 | **Compacted Thickness ↔ Loose Thickness** | $T_c = T_l \times (1 - s)$ | mm | $s$ = shrinkage factor (≈ 0.15–0.20 for macadam) |
| 5 | **Optimum Bitumen Content (OBC)** | $P_b = \dfrac{W_b}{W_{mix}} \times 100$ | % | By weight of total mix |
| 6 | **Air Voids (VMA-based)** | $V_a = 100 - \dfrac{G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100$ | % | $G_{mb}$ = bulk specific gravity, $G_{mm}$ = max theoretical specific gravity |
| 7 | **Marshall Stability Requirement (BM)** | $S \geq 540$ kg (at 60 °C) | kg | IRC / MoRTH specification |
| 8 | **Marshall Flow Requirement (BM)** | $2 \text{ mm} \leq F \leq 4 \text{ mm}$ | mm | Indicates flexibility |
| 9 | **Aggregate Spreading Rate** | $R_a = \dfrac{1.0 \times t_c \times G_a}{C}$ | m³/10 m² | $C$ = compaction factor |
| 10 | **Tack Coat Quantity** | $Q_t = 2.5 \text{ to } 3.0$ | kg / 10 m² | Over bituminous surface |
| 11 | **Penetration Macadam — Single Coat Bitumen** | $B = 50$ | kg / 10 m² | First binder application |
| 12 | **Penetration Macadam — Double Coat Bitumen** | $B = 68 \text{ to } 75$ | kg / 10 m² | Sum of both applications |
| 13 | **Roller Speed for Compaction** | $v \leq 5$ | km/h | Static; ≤ 3 km/h for vibratory |
| 14 | **Paving Temperature Drop Limit** | $\Delta T \leq 15$ | °C | Plant to site |
| 15 | **Minimum Compaction Effort** | $\rho_{field} \geq 0.95 \times \rho_{lab}$ | — | IRC:SP:11 / MoRTH |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Formulas:** **"S-T-B-V-M"** → **S**pray rate, **T**hickness conversion, **B**itumen content, **V**oids, **M**arshall. Five formulas cover 90% of numerical questions.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Penetration Macadam Use Today | Premix System Use Today |
|---|---|---|
| **National Highways (NH/SH)** | ❌ Not used (obsolete) | ✅ Dense Bituminous Macadam + Bituminous Concrete |
| **Rural Roads (PMGSY)** | ⚠️ Limited use (cost-driven) | ✅ DBM + BC |
| **Urban Arterials** | ❌ | ✅ BC / SMA |
| **Airfield Runways** | ❌ | ✅ Modified BM with polymer-modified bitumen |
| **Parking Lots / Light Traffic** | ✅ Sometimes (cost-effective) | ✅ Cold mix or HMA |
| **Bridge Decks** | ❌ | ✅ Mastic asphalt or premix BC |

> **Engineer's Real-World Takeaway:** A civil engineer today *designs* premixed systems (Marshall / Superpave) and *understands* penetration macadam only as a historical baseline. The transition from penetration → premix represents the **industrialisation of road construction** in the 20th century.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantity of Bitumen and Aggregate for Single Coat Penetration Macadam

**Given (Typical KTU Problem):**
Construct a single coat penetration macadam road of length **1 km** and width **3.75 m** using:
- Coarse aggregate nominal size = **50 mm** (loose thickness = 75 mm)
- Bitumen application rate = **50 kg per 10 m²**
- Specific gravity of aggregate $G = 2.7$
- Shrinkage factor $s = 0.20$ (after rolling)
- Bulk density of compacted aggregate $\rho_c = 1700 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Find:** Quantity (weight and volume) of coarse aggregate, key aggregate, and bitumen.

---

### Step 1: Area of the Road

$$
A = L \times W = 1000 \text{ m} \times 3.75 \text{ m} = 3750 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Logic:** Standard 2-lane rural road; each lane = 3.75 m (per IRC:SP:73).

---

### Step 2: Compacted Thickness of Coarse Aggregate Layer

Given loose thickness $T_l = 75$ mm. The shrinkage factor (compaction reduction) is $s = 0.20$.

$$
T_c = T_l \times (1 - s) = 75 \times (1 - 0.20) = 75 \times 0.80 = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.060 \text{ m}
$$

**Logic:** After rolling, the layer reduces by 20% in thickness. So 75 mm loose becomes 60 mm compacted.

---

### Step 3: Volume of Compacted Coarse Aggregate

$$
V_c = A \times T_c = 3750 \text{ m}^2 \times 0.060 \text{ m} = 225 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Logic:** Volume = Area × compacted thickness (simple geometry of a slab).

---

### Step 4: Weight of Coarse Aggregate (Compacted)

$$
W_c = V_c \times \rho_c = 225 \text{ m}^3 \times 1700 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 382{,}500 \text{ kg} = 382.5 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Logic:** Bulk density of compacted aggregate converts the volume to weight.

---

### Step 5: Quantity of Bitumen (1st Application)

$$
\text{Bitumen Quantity} = \frac{A}{10} \times R_b = \frac{3750}{10} \times 50 = 1875 \text{ kg} = 1.875 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Logic:** The standard rate of 50 kg per 10 m² is multiplied by the number of 10 m² units in the total area.

---

### Step 6: Quantity of Key (Choke) Aggregate

Typical key aggregate quantity ≈ **0.30 m³ per 10 m²** (about 40% of the coarse aggregate volume).

$$
V_{key} = \frac{A}{10} \times 0.30 = 375 \times 0.30 = 112.5 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
W_{key} = 112.5 \times 1500 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 168{,}750 \text{ kg} \approx 168.75 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Logic:** The key aggregate fills the upper surface voids; its quantity is empirical but standardised in MoRTH specifications.

---

### Step 7: Summary Table for the Solution

| Material | Quantity |
|---|---|
| **Coarse Aggregate (50 mm)** | 225 m³ / 382.5 tonnes |
| **Bitumen (VG-30)** | 1875 kg / 1.875 tonnes |
| **Key Aggregate (12–20 mm)** | 112.5 m³ / 168.75 tonnes |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Marshall Mix Design — Optimum Bitumen Content (OBC) for Premix

**Given (Typical KTU Numerical):**
A premix asphalt design produced the following test data at varying bitumen contents (5 samples):

| Trial | Bitumen Content $P_b$ (%) | Stability $S$ (kg) | Flow $F$ (mm) | Unit Weight (g/cm³) | Voids $V_a$ (%) | VMA (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4.0 | 820 | 2.5 | 2.35 | 6.0 | 17.0 |
| 2 | 4.5 | 940 | 2.8 | 2.38 | 4.5 | 17.2 |
| 3 | **5.0** | **1020** | **3.2** | **2.40** | **3.5** | **17.5** |
| 4 | 5.5 | 980 | 3.8 | 2.41 | 2.8 | 18.0 |
| 5 | 6.0 | 880 | 4.5 | 2.40 | 2.5 | 18.5 |

**Find:** Optimum Bitumen Content (OBC).

---

### Step 1: Identify Peak Properties

Plot all five properties vs. bitumen content. The **OBC** is determined as the bitumen content that best satisfies all the criteria simultaneously:

- **Peak Stability** → occurs at **5.0%** (1020 kg)
- **Maximum Unit Weight** → occurs at **5.0%** (2.40 g/cm³)
- **Target Air Voids (3–5% range)** → satisfied at **5.0%** (3.5%)
- **VMA ≥ 14%** → satisfied at all values; closest to 5.0% with 17.5%
- **Flow in 2–4 mm range** → satisfied at 5.0% (3.2 mm)

**Logic:** The OBC is the intersection of the peak of stability, peak of unit weight, and the midpoint of the air-voids curve (4% target).

---

### Step 2: Average the Three Indicating Values

$$
OBC_{avg} = \frac{P_{b,\,peak\,S} + P_{b,\,peak\,\rho} + P_{b,\,V_a=4\%}}{3} = \frac{5.0 + 5.0 + 5.0}{3} = 5.0\%
$$

**Logic:** Marshall's method prescribes averaging the three indicating values for robustness.

---

### Step 3: Verify Design Properties at OBC = 5.0%

| Property | Value | Specification (BM) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stability | 1020 kg | ≥ 540 kg | ✅ Pass |
| Flow | 3.2 mm | 2–4 mm | ✅ Pass |
| Air Voids | 3.5% | 3–5% | ✅ Pass |
| VMA | 17.5% | ≥ 14% | ✅ Pass |
| VFB | 80% | 65–75% | ✅ Pass |

---

### Step 4: Final Answer

$$
\boxed{OBC = 5.0\% \text{ by weight of total mix}}
$$

**Logic:** Since all properties pass specification at 5.0% bitumen, no adjustment is needed. The OBC is confirmed.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Penetration vs. Premixed

| Parameter | Penetration Macadam | Premixed System |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Location** | In-situ (on the road) | Off-site (in plant) |
| **Mixing of Binder** | NO (gravity penetration) | YES (pugmill / drum) |
| **Quality Uniformity** | Variable (depends on operator) | Excellent (automatic batching) |
| **Layer Thickness Range** | 50–125 mm (multi-coat) | 25–100 mm (single lift) |
| **Aggregate Requirement** | Single-sized, open-graded | Well-graded (dense) or gap-graded |
| **Bitumen Content Control** | Approximate (spray rate) | Precise (weigh batching) |
| **Construction Speed** | Slow (multi-step per coat) | Fast (continuous paver) |
| **Labour Intensity** | High (manual choking) | Low (mechanised) |
| **Cost (per m²)** | ₹ 80–120 (approx.) | ₹ 180–350 (approx.) |
| **Suitable Traffic (CVPD)** | < 500 | Up to > 10,000 |
| **Cold Weather Performance** | Poor (viscosity issue) | Better (controlled mix) |
| **Lifespan (Design)** | 5–7 years | 10–15 years (BC) / 15–20 (SMA) |
| **Equipment Required** | Sprayer + roller | Plant + paver + rollers |
| **Environmental Impact** | Higher (spray losses) | Lower (enclosed mixing) |
| **IS Code Reference** | IS 215, IRC:14 | IS 73, IS 1203–1220, IRC:111 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Question Pattern:** "Compare penetration macadam and premix macadam in terms of (i) process, (ii) quality, (iii) suitability." Use the above matrix as your one-page answer cheat.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Single Coat Penetration Macadam

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Prepare Base Sub-Base Cleaning and Minor Repairs] --> B[Step2: Apply Tack Coat 2.5 kg per 10 m2 if on existing bitumen]
    B --> C[Step3: Spread Coarse Aggregate 50 to 63 mm nominal size]
    C --> D[Step4: Dry Roll with 8 to 10 tonne Static Roller]
    D --> E[Step5: Spray Heated Bitumen VG30 at 50 kg per 10 m2]
    E --> F[Step6: Wait for Penetration 15 to 30 minutes for binder to seep in]
    F --> G[Step7: Spread Key Aggregate 12 to 20 mm Choke Material]
    G --> H[Step8: Final Rolling Static Roller 8 to 10 tonne]
    H --> I[Step9: Curing 24 hours before opening to traffic]
    I --> J[Completed Single Coat Penetration Macadam Layer]

    style A fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The pink node is the **binder application step** (the defining moment of "penetration"), and the blue node is the **choking step** (sealing the surface). The flow is strictly **sequential** — you cannot skip steps.

---

## 4.2 Process Flow — Double Coat Penetration Macadam

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Base Preparation and Tack Coat] --> B[1st Coat: Spread Coarse Agg 50 to 63 mm]
    B --> C[1st Coat: Dry Rolling]
    C --> D[1st Coat: Spray Bitumen 50 kg per 10 m2]
    D --> E[1st Coat: Spread Key Agg 25 to 40 mm]
    E --> F[1st Coat: Rolling]
    F --> G[2nd Coat: Spray Bitumen 18 to 25 kg per 10 m2]
    G --> H[2nd Coat: Spread Finer Agg 12 to 20 mm]
    H --> I[2nd Coat: Final Rolling]
    I --> J[Completed Double Coat Penetration Macadam]

    style B fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The **second binder application** (lighter, 18–25 kg/10 m²) acts both as a binder for the new aggregate and as a **tack coat** for the previous layer — a clever double-purpose design.

---

## 4.3 Process Flow — Premixed Bituminous Macadam (BM / BC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Aggregate Stockpile Cold Feed Bins] --> B1[Drying Drum 150 to 165 degree Celsius]
    B1 --> C1[Hot Elevator]
    C1 --> D1[Vibratory Screening and Hot Bins]
    D1 --> E1[Weigh Batching of Aggregates]
    D1 --> E2[Filler Storage Stone Dust or Lime]
    E1 --> F1[Pugmill Mixer]
    E2 --> F1
    E3[Bitumen Storage Heated to 150 to 160 degree Celsius] --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Hot Mix Storage Silo]
    G1 --> H1[Tipper Truck Transport to Site]
    H1 --> I1[Paver Finisher Lay and Pre Compact]
    I1 --> J1[Breakdown Roller Static 8 to 10 tonne]
    J1 --> K1[Intermediate Roller Pneumatic 12 to 15 tonne]
    K1 --> L1[Finish Roller Tandem 6 to 8 tonne]
    L1 --> M1[Quality Control Tests Density and Temperature]
    M1 --> N1[Completed Premix Layer Ready for Next Course]

    style F1 fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I1 fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M1 fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N1 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow is **linear and continuous** (left to right). The pink node is the **mixing** (defining step of premix), the blue node is the **laying**, and the orange node is the **QC checkpoint**. Note the strict **temperature cascade** from 165 °C → 150 °C → 120 °C → 90 °C.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Comparative Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PEN["PENETRATION MACADAM - In Situ Process"]
        P1[Aggregate Laying on Road] --> P2[Bitumen Spraying from Top]
        P2 --> P3[Gravity Penetration of Binder]
        P3 --> P4[Choke Aggregate Filling]
        P4 --> P5[Compaction by Static Roller]
    end

    subgraph PRE["PREMIXED SYSTEM - Plant Controlled Process"]
        Q1[Proportioning in Plant] --> Q2[Hot Mixing in Pugmill]
        Q2 --> Q3[Homogeneous Bitumen Coated Mix]
        Q3 --> Q4[Transport and Lay by Paver]
        Q4 --> Q5[Multi-Stage Compaction]
    end

    P5 --> OUT[Completed Pavement Layer]
    Q5 --> OUT

    style P3 fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Q2 fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The **two parallel subgraphs** compare the two systems side-by-side. The **pink-highlighted step** in each is the **defining moment**: gravity penetration vs. mechanical mixing. The final green node represents the **identical end product** — a bound pavement layer.

---

## 4.5 Cross-Section Schematic — Penetration Macadam (ASCII)

```
            <------ 3.75 m (Lane Width) ------>
            +-----------------------------------+
            |  KEY AGGREGATE   (12-20 mm)       |  ← 10-15 mm thick
            +-----------------------------------+
            |  BITUMEN-BOUND  COARSE AGGREGATE  |  ← 50-75 mm thick
            |  (50-63 mm aggregate + bitumen)   |     (penetration zone)
            +-----------------------------------+
            |  TACK COAT                       |  ← Thin (2.5 kg/10 m²)
            +-----------------------------------+
            |  EXISTING WBM / SUB-BASE          |
            +-----------------------------------+
            |  SUBGRADE (Compacted)             |
            +-----------------------------------+
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** This is a **vertical section** through the pavement. Notice the **two distinct zones** in penetration macadam: the **top key aggregate layer** and the **bitumen-bound coarse aggregate** layer beneath. The **bitumen** is **NOT uniformly distributed** — it is **concentrated near the top**, having penetrated only partially downward.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**Define penetration macadam. List the steps involved in the construction of single coat penetration macadam.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Penetration macadam is a method of road construction in which a layer of clean, single-sized coarse aggregate is spread, dry-rolled, and then sprayed with hot bituminous binder. The binder **percolates downward by gravity** into the open voids of the aggregate skeleton. Finer "key" aggregate is then applied and rolled to fill surface voids and lock the structure.

**Steps (2 Marks):**
1. **Base Preparation:** Clean the WBM / sub-base; apply tack coat @ **2.5 kg / 10 m²** if on existing bitumen surface.
2. **Spreading Coarse Aggregate:** Spread 50–63 mm nominal size aggregate uniformly; dry-roll with 8–10 tonne static roller.
3. **Spraying Bitumen:** Apply heated VG-30 bitumen @ **50 kg / 10 m²** at 150–180 °C using a pressure distributor.
4. **Spreading Key Aggregate:** Apply 12–20 mm nominal key aggregate before the binder cools to choke surface voids.
5. **Final Rolling and Curing:** Roll with 8–10 tonne static roller; cure for 24 hours before opening to traffic.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "penetration macadam" with "premix macadam." If you write "binder is mixed with aggregate in a plant," you **lose 1 mark** immediately — the definition is fundamentally wrong.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**
**Differentiate between penetration macadam and premixed macadam in terms of (i) process, (ii) quality of construction, and (iii) suitability.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Criterion | Penetration Macadam | Premixed Macadam |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Process** (1 Mark) | Binder is **sprayed on the laid aggregate** and allowed to **penetrate by gravity** — no pre-mixing | Aggregates and binder are **mixed in a plant** (pugmill) and laid as a **homogeneous mix** |
| **(ii) Quality** (1 Mark) | **Variable, depends on operator skill** and temperature control; non-uniform binder distribution | **Consistent, plant-controlled**; uniform coating of every aggregate particle |
| **(iii) Suitability** (1 Mark) | **Low-volume rural roads** (< 500 CVPD); obsolete for modern highways | **All traffic levels**, including expressways, airfields, urban arterials (uses HMA, BC, DBM) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing **only one or two points** without specifying the **criterion** (process / quality / suitability). KTU examiners award marks **per criterion** — if you write all three points but don't label them, you may lose 0.5–1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

### 📌 Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper (14 Marks)]**

**(a) Explain in detail the construction procedure of double coat penetration macadam with a neat sketch. List the materials required and their quantities per 10 m² area. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Describe the construction procedure of premix carpet with seal coat. State its advantages and limitations. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Double Coat Penetration Macadam (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Double coat penetration macadam is an extension of single coat macadam, used for medium-traffic roads where a thicker (75–100 mm) bound layer is required. Two successive applications of binder and aggregate are done, with the second layer using a **smaller nominal size** aggregate.

**Construction Steps (4 Marks):**
1. **Base Preparation:** Clean the prepared WBM / sub-base surface. Apply tack coat @ **2.5–3.0 kg/10 m²** if over existing bituminous surface.
2. **First Coat – Coarse Aggregate Laying:** Spread 50–63 mm nominal size **clean, angular, single-sized** coarse aggregate uniformly. Dry roll using 8–10 tonne static roller.
3. **First Coat – Bitumen Application:** Spray heated VG-30 bitumen (150–180 °C) @ **50 kg/10 m²** using a mechanical pressure distributor. Allow 15–30 minutes for penetration.
4. **First Coat – Key Aggregate:** Spread 25–40 mm nominal size aggregate to choke surface voids. Roll to embed.
5. **Second Coat – Bitumen Application:** Spray bitumen @ **18–25 kg/10 m²** (this acts as both binder and tack coat).
6. **Second Coat – Finer Aggregate:** Spread 12–20 mm nominal key aggregate. Roll with 8–10 tonne static roller.
7. **Curing:** Allow 24 hours before opening to traffic.

**Materials Quantity Table (2 Marks):**

| Material | First Coat | Second Coat | Total per 10 m² |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coarse Aggregate (50–63 mm) | 0.6–0.75 m³ | — | 0.6–0.75 m³ |
| Key Aggregate (25–40 mm) | 0.15 m³ | — | 0.15 m³ |
| Bitumen (VG-30) | 50 kg | 18–25 kg | 68–75 kg |
| Key Aggregate (12–20 mm) | — | 0.12 m³ | 0.12 m³ |

**Neat Sketch (to be drawn in exam):**
Refer to Section 4.2 (Process Flow) of these notes for the construction sequence diagram. A cross-section showing the **two distinct aggregate layers** with binder concentrations between them is expected.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Premix Carpet with Seal Coat (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 Mark):**
**Premix Carpet (PMC)** is an **open-graded premix** (15–20% air voids) used as a thin (20–25 mm) wearing course over a WBM or BM base, sealed with a thin layer of bitumen and fine sand/chips.

**Materials (1 Mark):**
- **Aggregate (12.5 mm nominal):** 0.0275 m³/m² (≈ 0.275 m³ per 10 m²)
- **Bitumen (VG-30):** 14.6 kg per 10 m² for premix + 12.5 kg per 10 m² for seal coat
- **Seal Coat Aggregate:** Stone chips 6.7 mm size, 0.0091 m³/m²

**Construction Procedure (3 Marks):**
1. **Preparation:** Clean the base; apply tack coat @ 2.5–3.0 kg/10 m².
2. **Mixing:** Mix heated aggregate (150–165 °C) with heated bitumen (VG-30) in a hot mix plant. Premix typically uses 60/70 or VG-30 grade.
3. **Laying:** Lay the mix with a paver at 20–25 mm compacted thickness.
4. **Compaction:** Roll with a smooth-wheeled roller at 100 °C+; no vibratory.
5. **Seal Coat Application:** Apply bitumen @ 12.5 kg/10 m² uniformly, immediately followed by spreading of 6.7 mm chips @ 0.09 m³/10 m². Roll lightly.

**Advantages (1 Mark):**
- Economical for low-medium traffic.
- Quick construction; minimum equipment.
- Provides a skid-resistant surface (due to surface texture of open-graded mix).
- Waterproof surface (with seal coat).

**Limitations (1 Mark):**
- **NOT suitable** for heavy commercial traffic.
- Open-graded structure is **porous**; if seal coat fails, water ingress causes **ravelling and potholes**.
- Shorter service life (5–7 years) compared to BC.
- Cannot be used on high-speed corridors.

---

### 📌 Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper (14 Marks)]**

**(a) Discuss the various types of penetration macadam construction. Explain their suitability based on traffic and subgrade conditions. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Explain the premixed bituminous macadam system. Discuss open-graded and dense-graded premix with examples. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Types of Penetration Macadam (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Penetration macadam is classified based on the **number of binder + aggregate cycles (coats)** applied. The selection depends on traffic intensity and subgrade strength.

**Type 1: Single Coat Penetration Macadam (2 Marks)**
- **Construction:** One layer of 50–63 mm coarse aggregate + one bitumen spray (50 kg/10 m²) + one layer of 12–20 mm key aggregate.
- **Compacted Thickness:** 50–75 mm.
- **Suitability:** Low-volume rural roads, village roads, light traffic (< 500 CVPD), strong subgrade (CBR ≥ 8%).
- **Limitations:** Insufficient strength for heavy vehicles; prone to surface deformation.

**Type 2: Double Coat Penetration Macadam (2 Marks)**
- **Construction:** Two cycles of binder and aggregate with stepped-down aggregate size.
- **Compacted Thickness:** 75–100 mm.
- **Suitability:** Medium-traffic roads (500–2000 CVPD), ODRs (Other District Roads), and VRs with moderate subgrade (CBR 5–8%).
- **Better Impermeability:** Two binder layers reduce water infiltration.

**Type 3: Triple Coat Penetration Macadam (2 Marks)**
- **Construction:** Three cycles; aggregate sizes 50–63 mm → 25–40 mm → 12–20 mm.
- **Compacted Thickness:** 100–125 mm.
- **Bitumen Application:** Total ≈ 12–15 kg/m².
- **Suitability:** Heavy commercial traffic corridors, weak subgrades (CBR 3–5%), industrial access roads.
- **Modern Status:** Even triple coat is **now considered obsolete** for national highways — replaced by DBM + BC premix system.

**Conclusion (–):**
The progression from single → double → triple coat mirrors the historical evolution of bituminous road construction in India (pre-1970s era), reflecting the increasing demand for stronger, more durable, and more impermeable pavements.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Premixed Bituminous Macadam System (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The premixed system is a **plant-controlled, mechanised** bituminous pavement construction method where all constituents (coarse aggregate, fine aggregate, filler, and bitumen) are **proportionately mixed in a hot mix plant** before being laid and compacted.

**Classification Based on Grading (3 Marks):**

**1. Open-Graded Premix:**
- **Air Voids:** 15–20% (high).
- **Aggregate Structure:** Mostly coarse aggregate with little fines.
- **Examples:** **Premix Carpet (PMC), Open-graded Bituminous Concrete (OGBC), Open-graded Friction Course (OGFC).**
- **Application:** **Wearing course** for low-medium traffic; provides **skid resistance** and **drainage** of surface water.
- **Bitumen Content:** 4.5–5.5% by weight.
- **Limitation:** Porous; requires **seal coat** to prevent water ingress.

**2. Dense-Graded Premix:**
- **Air Voids:** 3–5% (low).
- **Aggregate Structure:** Well-graded with balanced coarse, fine, and filler proportions.
- **Examples:** **Bituminous Macadam (BM), Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM), Bituminous Concrete (BC), Semi-Dense Bituminous Concrete (SDBC).**
- **Application:** **Base / binder course (BM, DBM)** and **wearing course (BC)** for all traffic levels.
- **Bitumen Content:** 4.0–5.0% (BM/DBM), 5.0–6.0% (BC).
- **Advantage:** **Impermeable, durable, high strength.**

**3. Gap-Graded Premix (Bonus – 1 Mark):**
- **Air Voids:** 2–4%.
- **Examples:** **Stone Matrix Asphalt (SMA), Mastic Asphalt.**
- **Application:** Heavy traffic, high-temperature regions, premium expressways.

**Process Flow (1 Mark):**
Aggregate drying → Screening → Weigh batching → Mixing in pugmill → Storage in silo → Transport in insulated trucks → Laying by paver → Rolling (breakdown → intermediate → finish) → QC.

**Comparison Summary (1 Mark):**

| Property | Open-Graded | Dense-Graded |
|---|---|---|
| Air Voids | 15–20% | 3–5% |
| Use | Wearing course (low traffic) | Base + wearing course (all traffic) |
| Durability | Lower | Higher |
| Impermeability | Poor (needs seal) | Excellent |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls for Part B:**
> 1. **Skipping the diagram/schematic** in 14-mark questions costs **1–2 marks** always. Even a rough hand-drawn flowchart or cross-section is mandatory.
> 2. **Confusing Bituminous Macadam (BM) with Bituminous Concrete (BC):** BM is a **base/binder course** with **larger aggregate (25–40 mm)**; BC is a **wearing course** with **smaller aggregate (12.5–20 mm)** and higher bitumen. Mixing them up is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention temperatures** (mixing, laying, compaction) — the examiner expects at least **one temperature mentioned** in a 14-mark answer on premix.
> 4. **Not stating quantities** in penetration macadam questions — examiners expect **kg/10 m²** for bitumen and **m³/10 m²** for aggregate, not just descriptive answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Read Before Every KTU Exam**

- [ ] **Penetration Macadam** = binder is **NOT pre-mixed**; it is **sprayed and allowed to penetrate by gravity**.
- [ ] **Premixed System** = binder is **pre-mixed with aggregate in a plant** (hot mix plant for HMA).
- [ ] **Single coat** = 50 kg bitumen / 10 m², 50–63 mm aggregate.
- [ ] **Double coat** = 68–75 kg bitumen / 10 m², 75–100 mm total thickness.
- [ ] **Triple coat** = 100–125 mm, used for heavy traffic, total binder ~12–15 kg/m².
- [ ] **Aggregate step-down principle:** 50–63 mm → 25–40 mm → 12–20 mm for multi-coat macadam.
- [ ] **Choke aggregate** fills surface voids; it is **NOT** a separate binder step.
- [ ] **Use 8–10 tonne static roller** for penetration macadam — **NO vibratory, NO pneumatic** (pneumatic sucks fresh binder).
- [ ] **Tack coat** = 2.5–3.0 kg / 10 m² on existing bituminous surface; 5.0 kg / 10 m² on dry / hungry surface.
- [ ] **Premix temperatures:** Mixing 150–165 °C → Laying 120–150 °C → Compaction ≥ 90 °C.
- [ ] **Open-graded premix** = 15–20% voids; needs **seal coat**; example = **Premix Carpet (PMC)**.
- [ ] **Dense-graded premix** = 3–5% voids; example = **DBM, BC**; main highway material.
- [ ] **BM** is a **base course** (large aggregate, ~40 mm); **BC** is a **wearing course** (small aggregate, ~13 mm).
- [ ] **Marshall Stability** for BM ≥ 540 kg; **Flow** 2–4 mm; **Air Voids** 3–5%; **VMA** ≥ 14%.
- [ ] **Penetration macadam is OBSOLETE** for NH/SH — only used for low-volume rural roads in India (PMGSY).
- [ ] **Historical name origin:** "Tarmacadam" = **Tar** + **McAdam** (1901, Hooley).
- [ ] **Quality control shift:** Penetration macadam is **field-dependent**; premix is **plant-dependent** — this is the **key reason** premix has replaced penetration in modern practice.
- [ ] **Memory hook:** "**Penetration = Pouring**" (binder poured from top); "**Premix = Pre-mixing**" (pre-blended at plant).
- [ ] **Standard IS codes to cite:** IS 73 (bitumen), IS 215 (penetration grade), IS 1201–1220 (bitumen tests), IRC:14 (penetration macadam), IRC:111 (dense-graded premix).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types and Specification of Materials Used; Special Types of Bituminous Layers (Stone Mastic Asphalt and Mastic Asphalt); Mix Design: Physical and Volumetric Properties of Bituminous Mix, Marshall Method of Mix Design, Superpave Mix Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET745 - PAVEMENT CONSTRUCTION AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types and Specification of Materials Used; Special Types of Bituminous Layers (Stone Mastic Asphalt and Mastic Asphalt); Mix Design: Physical and Volumetric Properties of Bituminous Mix, Marshall Method of Mix Design, Superpave Mix Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Types and Specifications of Bituminous Materials

**Bituminous materials** are hydrocarbons (soluble in carbon disulphide CS₂) derived from petroleum refining or naturally occurring deposits, used as binders in flexible pavements. They exhibit **viscoelastic** behaviour—behaving as an elastic solid at low temperatures/high loading rates and as a viscous liquid at high temperatures/low loading rates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The examiner frequently tests the *classification* of binders, the *penetration grade system*, and the *IRC/MoRTH specifications* for viscosity-grade (VG) binders.

#### A. Asphalt Cement (Bitumen)
- A dense, highly viscous, semi-solid petroleum residue obtained by refining crude oil.
- Classified by **penetration test** (IS 73) into grades such as **30/40, 40/50, 60/70, 80/100** (penetration in 1/10 mm at 25 °C, 100 g, 5 s).
- Classified by **viscosity test** (IS 73 – Viscosity Grade) as **VG-10, VG-20, VG-30, VG-40** (absolute viscosity at 60 °C in poises × 10²).
- **Analogy:** Think of bitumen as the "glue" that holds the entire pavement together—similar to how chocolate acts as a binder in a chocolate-chip cookie, holding the nuts/chips (aggregates) intact.

#### B. Cutback Bitumen
- Bitumen temporarily liquefied by blending with **petroleum solvents (cutters)** like naphtha, kerosene, or diesel.
- Three types based on curing rate:
  - **Rapid Curing (RC):** Bitumen + Naphtha (e.g., RC-70, RC-250, RC-800, RC-3000)
  - **Medium Curing (MC):** Bitumen + Kerosene (e.g., MC-30, MC-70, MC-250, MC-800)
  - **Slow Curing (SC):** Bitumen + Diesel/heavy oil (e.g., SC-70, SC-250, SC-800)
- The number denotes the kinematic viscosity of the cutback in centistokes at 60 °C.
- **Analogy:** Cutback bitumen is like "diluted honey"—once the water/solvent evaporates, the honey regains its original thick, sticky consistency.

#### C. Bitumen Emulsion
- A two-phase system consisting of **bitumen droplets (60–70%)** dispersed in **water (30–40%)** with an emulsifying agent (cationic/anionic surfactant).
- Three classes:
  - **RS (Rapid Setting):** RS-1, RS-2 — for surface dressing, tack coat
  - **MS (Medium Setting):** MS-1, MS-2 — for cold mix, patch mix
  - **SS (Slow Setting):** SS-1, SS-2 — for prime coat, slurry seal
- **Cationic emulsions** (e.g., CRS, CMS) — positively charged; preferred for siliceous aggregates.
- **Anionic emulsions** (e.g., RS-1) — negatively charged; suitable for limestone aggregates.
- **Analogy:** Emulsion is like "mayonnaise"—tiny oil droplets suspended in water with a stabilizer. When applied, water evaporates ("breaks"), leaving behind pure bitumen.

#### D. Modified Bitumen
- Bitumen whose rheological properties are enhanced by adding **polymers** (SBS, EVA, crumb rubber), **fibres**, **anti-stripping agents**, or **warm-mix additives**.
- **PMB (Polymer Modified Bitumen)** grades as per IS 15462: PMB-40, PMB-70, PMB-120.
- **CRMB (Crumb Rubber Modified Bitumen)** as per IRC SP-53.

> [!NOTE]
> **IRC/MoRTH Specification Highlights for Binders:**
> - VG-30: Most common binder grade for DBM and BC layers in India.
> - VG-40: Used in high-temperature zones (>45 °C) and heavy-traffic corridors.
> - Emulsion SS-1: Standard for prime coat on granular base.
> - Emulsion RS-1: Standard for tack coat between bituminous layers.

---

### 1.2 Aggregates for Bituminous Mixes

Aggregates constitute **90–95% by weight** of a bituminous mix and are the skeleton that resists traffic loads.

#### A. Types of Aggregates
- **Coarse aggregates:** Retained on 2.36 mm IS sieve (e.g., crushed stone, gravel, slag).
- **Fine aggregates:** Passing 2.36 mm and retained on 75-micron sieve (e.g., crusher dust, natural sand).
- **Mineral filler:** Passing 75-micron sieve (e.g., cement, hydrated lime, stone dust). Filler stiffens the mastic and reduces drain-down.

#### B. Key Aggregate Properties (MoRTH Specifications)

| Property | Test Standard | Specification (typical for BC) |
|---|---|---|
| Aggregate Impact Value (AIV) | IS 2386 Part IV | ≤ 24% |
| Los Angeles Abrasion Value | IS 2386 Part IV | ≤ 30% |
| Aggregate Crushing Value | IS 2386 Part IV | ≤ 30% |
| Flakiness Index | IS 2386 Part I | ≤ 25% (BC/DBM) |
| Elongation Index | IS 2386 Part I | ≤ 15% |
| Water Absorption | IS 2386 Part III | ≤ 2% |
| Soundness (Na₂SO₄/MgSO₄) | IS 2386 Part V | ≤ 12% / ≤ 18% |
| Stripping Value | IS 6241 | ≥ 95% retained coating |
| Polished Stone Value | BS 812 | ≥ 55 (for surface course) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stripping** is the detachment of bitumen film from aggregate surface due to water action. Anti-stripping agents (e.g., hydrated lime at 1–2% by weight of aggregate) are added when stripping value < 95%.

---

### 1.3 Special Bituminous Layers

#### A. Stone Matrix Asphalt (SMA)
- A gap-graded, stone-rich mix designed originally in **Germany (1968)** to resist studded tyre wear.
- Composition: **70–80% coarse aggregate (stone-on-stone contact)**, 6–12% filler, 6–7% binder, 0.3–0.5% cellulose/ mineral fibres (drain-down inhibitor).
- **Analogy:** SMA is like a **dry-stone wall**—large, jagged stones (coarse aggregate) interlock directly with each other, while the gaps are filled with a thick, mortar-like **mastic** (binder + filler + fibre). The stone-to-stone contact provides shear strength, and the mastic seals the mix against water and air.

#### B. Mastic Asphalt
- A dense, voidless, impermeable mixture of **well-graded fine aggregate, mineral filler (limestone powder), and bitumen** (often modified).
- Bitumen content is high (**14–17% by weight of total mix**), and the mix is **essentially zero air void** (truly impermeable).
- **Analogy:** Mastic asphalt is like **putty or thick tar**—it is so rich in binder and filler that there is no room for air voids. It is used in waterproofing bridge decks, car park decks, and tunnel linings where absolute impermeability is critical.

---

### 1.4 Mix Design — Conceptual Overview

**Mix design** is the laboratory process of selecting and proportioning aggregate and binder materials to produce a bituminous mix with:
- Adequate **stability** to resist deformation under traffic,
- Sufficient **durability** against weathering and aging,
- Adequate **flexibility** to resist cracking,
- Adequate **skid resistance** for safety,
- **Workability** for placement and compaction, and
- **Impermeability** to prevent water ingress.

**Two principal methods** are covered in this module:
- **Marshall Method** (empirical/performance-based, widely used in India).
- **Superpave Method** (volumetric-based, advanced SHRP method).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Mix design is like baking a cake—you must select the right ratio of flour (aggregate), sugar (filler), eggs (binder), and baking time (compaction) to get the desired texture (strength) and shelf life (durability). Too much binder → rutting; too little → cracking; wrong aggregate grading → segregation.

## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Physical and Volumetric Properties of Bituminous Mix

A compacted bituminous mix is treated as a **three-phase system**:

1. **Aggregate (solid phase)**
2. **Binder (asphalt cement)** — partially absorbed into aggregate pores
3. **Air voids (Vv)** — unfilled spaces

#### A. Bulk Specific Gravity of Mix (G_m or Gmb)
The ratio of the mass of a compacted mix specimen in air to the volume of the specimen (including impermeable and permeable voids, but excluding only those permeable to water).

$$G_{mb} = \frac{W_{air}}{W_{SSD} - W_{water}}$$

Where:
- $W_{air}$ = weight in air (g)
- $W_{SSD}$ = saturated surface-dry weight (g)
- $W_{water}$ = weight submerged in water (g)

#### B. Maximum Theoretical Specific Gravity (G_mm)
The specific gravity of a mix assuming **zero air voids** — i.e., binder fully fills all aggregate voids.

$$G_{mm} = \frac{100}{\frac{P_s}{G_{sb}} + \frac{P_b}{G_b}}$$

Where:
- $P_s$ = % aggregate by total mix weight
- $P_b$ = % binder by total mix weight
- $G_{sb}$ = bulk specific gravity of aggregate
- $G_b$ = specific gravity of binder

#### C. Air Voids (Vv or AV or Va)
Percentage of air voids by total mix volume:

$$V_a = \frac{G_{mm} - G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100 \quad (\%) $$

#### D. Voids in Mineral Aggregate (VMA)
Volume of inter-granular void space between aggregate particles, including air voids and effective binder content (but excluding aggregate itself).

$$VMA = 100 - \frac{G_{mb} \cdot P_s}{G_{sb}}$$

Alternative formula:
$$VMA = V_a + V_{be}$$

Where $V_{be}$ = volume of effective binder.

#### E. Voids Filled with Asphalt (VFA)
Portion of VMA occupied by effective binder:

$$VFA = \frac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100 \quad (\%) $$

#### F. Effective Specific Gravity of Aggregate (G_se)
Specific gravity of aggregate **excluding** permeable voids filled by absorbed binder:

$$G_{se} = \frac{100 - P_b}{\frac{100}{G_{mm}} - \frac{P_b}{G_b}}$$

#### G. Absorbed Binder (P_ba)
Binder absorbed into aggregate pores:

$$P_{ba} = 100 \times \frac{G_{se} - G_{sb}}{G_{se} \cdot G_{sb}} \times G_b$$

#### H. Effective Binder Content (P_be)
$$P_{be} = P_b - \frac{P_{ba} \times P_s}{100}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these formulas matter:** Volumetric properties (Va, VMA, VFA) form the **backbone of both Marshall and Superpave mix design**. Va ensures impermeability and oxidation resistance; VMA ensures room for binder; VFA ensures the mix is not over-asphalted (rutting-prone) or under-asphalted (durability-poor).

---

### 2.2 Marshall Method of Mix Design

Developed by **Bruce Marshall (1939)**; standardised by the **U.S. Army Corps of Engineers**. The Marshall test provides:
- **Marshall Stability (kN):** Maximum load the specimen resists at 60 °C under standard loading rate (50.8 mm/min).
- **Marshall Flow (mm):** Total deformation of the specimen at the point of maximum load.

#### A. Marshall Test Specimen Preparation
- Specimens of **101.6 mm diameter** and **63.5 mm height** (≈ 1200 g).
- Compaction: **75 blows per face** (heavy traffic) or **50 blows per face** (light traffic) using a hammer of **4.54 kg** falling **457 mm**.
- Tested in water bath at **60 ± 1 °C** for **30–40 minutes**.

#### B. Marshall Mix Design Procedure
1. **Select aggregate gradation** meeting MoRTH/IRC specifications for the layer type (BC, DBM, DBM+SMA).
2. **Estimate binder content** (typically 5–6% for BC) using empirical equations or past experience.
3. **Prepare specimens** at varying binder contents (e.g., 4.5, 5.0, 5.5, 6.0, 6.5%) with 3 replicates each = 15 specimens.
4. **Compact** specimens using Marshall hammer.
5. **Determine** bulk specific gravity (Gmb) of each compacted specimen.
6. **Determine** theoretical maximum specific gravity (Gmm) of loose mix (Rice test, ASTM D2041).
7. **Compute** Va, VMA, VFA, Vba for each specimen.
8. **Perform** Marshall stability and flow test.
9. **Plot** curves: Stability, Flow, Va, VMA, VFA, Gmb vs. % binder.
10. **Select OBC (Optimum Binder Content)** as the average of three values:
    - Binder content at **peak stability**
    - Binder content at **median Va (4% for BC)**
    - Binder content at **VFA = 65% (BC) or 70% (DBM)**
11. **Validate** properties of OBC specimen against Marshall criteria (Table below).

#### C. Marshall Mix Design Criteria (MoRTH Table 500-11 / IRC 111)

| Property | BC (Wearing Course) | DBM (Base Course) |
|---|---|---|
| Compaction (blows/face) | 75 | 75 |
| Stability (kN), min | **9.0** | **9.0** |
| Flow (mm) | 2–4 | 2–4 |
| Air Voids (%) | 3–5 | 3–5 |
| VMA (%) | **min 14** (12.5 mm NMAS) | **min 13** (26.5 mm NMAS) |
| VFA (%) | 65–75 | 65–75 |
| Gmb | To be reported | To be reported |
| OBC | By averaging | By averaging |

#### D. Key Formulas in Marshall Method

$$Stability \; corrected = Stability \; measured \times Correlation \; Ratio$$

The correlation ratio accounts for specimen thickness deviating from 63.5 mm (Table in ASTM D6927 / IS 1203).

$$Stiffness \; Ratio = \frac{Marshall \; Stability \; (kN)}{Marshall \; Flow \; (mm)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stiffness ratio (kN/mm)** indicates the mix's resistance to deformation per unit deformation. Higher stiffness = stiffer, more rut-resistant mix; but excessive stiffness leads to brittleness and cracking.

---

### 2.3 Superpave Mix Design

**SHRP (Strategic Highway Research Program, USA 1987–1993)** developed the **Superpave (Superior Performing Asphalt Pavements)** system to overcome the empirical limitations of Marshall/Hveem methods.

#### A. Superpave Key Innovations
1. **Performance-Graded (PG) asphalt binder** specification based on climate (temperature, traffic speed).
2. **Gyratory Shear Compactor (SGC)** — better simulates field compaction.
3. **Volumetric mix design** with restricted zone and control points.
4. **Performance testing** (rutting, fatigue, low-temperature cracking).

#### B. Gyratory Compactor Parameters
- **Compaction angle (gyration angle):** 1.16° internal angle
- **Pressure:** 600 kPa
- **Speed:** 30 gyrations/min
- **Specimen diameter:** 150 mm
- **Design gyration (N_des):** Selected based on traffic level and design ESALs

| Traffic (million ESALs) | N_init | N_des | N_max |
|---|---|---|---|
| < 0.3 | 6 | 50 | 75 |
| 0.3–1 | 7 | 75 | 115 |
| 1–3 | 8 | 100 | 160 |
| 3–10 | 9 | 125 | 205 |
| 10–30 | 10 | 150 | 245 |
| 30–100 | 11 | 175 | 285 |
| > 100 | 12 | 200 | 320 |

Where:
- **N_init:** Number of gyrations to achieve initial density (for estimating compactability).
- **N_des:** Number of gyrations at which mix is designed (≈ 4% air voids).
- **N_max:** Number of gyrations representing the densest condition (≈ 2% air voids).

#### C. Superpave Volumetric Criteria (12.5 mm NMAS, < 0.3 million ESALs)

| Property | Specification |
|---|---|
| Air Voids at N_des | 4.0% |
| VMA at N_des | ≥ 14.0% |
| VFA at N_des | 65–78% |
| Dust Proportion (DP = %passing 75 µm / effective binder content) | 0.6–1.2 |
| %G_mm at N_init | ≤ 89% (for design traffic) |
| %G_mm at N_max | ≤ 98% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dust Proportion (DP)** is unique to Superpave. It prevents both too little filler (mix is too open) and too much filler (mix is too stiff, prone to cracking).

#### D. Aggregate Properties in Superpave

| Property | Specification |
|---|---|
| Coarse Aggregate Angularity (CAA) | 75% minimum (1 or 2 faces fractured) |
| Fine Aggregate Angularity (FAA) | 40% minimum |
| Flat & Elongated Particles (F&E, 5:1 ratio) | ≤ 10% |
| Sand Equivalent (clay content) | ≥ 45% |
| Los Angeles Abrasion | ≤ 35% (surface) / ≤ 40% (base) |

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Equation | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Bulk Specific Gravity of Mix | $G_{mb} = \frac{W_{air}}{W_{SSD} - W_{water}}$ | Mix design & QC |
| Maximum Theoretical SG (Rice) | $G_{mm} = \frac{100}{\frac{P_s}{G_{sb}} + \frac{P_b}{G_b}}$ | Air void calculation |
| Air Voids | $V_a = \frac{G_{mm}-G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100$ | Durability check |
| VMA | $VMA = 100 - \frac{G_{mb} P_s}{G_{sb}}$ | Binder space check |
| VFA | $VFA = \frac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100$ | Binder effectiveness |
| Effective SG of Aggregate | $G_{se} = \frac{100 - P_b}{\frac{100}{G_{mm}} - \frac{P_b}{G_b}}$ | Absorption correction |
| Absorbed Binder | $P_{ba} = 100 \cdot \frac{G_{se}-G_{sb}}{G_{se} \cdot G_{sb}} \cdot G_b$ | Effective binder |
| Effective Binder | $P_{be} = P_b - \frac{P_{ba} P_s}{100}$ | Volumetric ratio |
| Marshall Stiffness Ratio | $S_R = \frac{Stability (kN)}{Flow (mm)}$ | Mix stiffness |
| % Density at N_gyr | $\%G_{mm,N} = \frac{h_d}{h_N} \times 100$ | Superpave densification curve |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in **highway design offices, bituminous mix plants (QC/QA), and research labs** to validate mix proportions before any construction begins. The volumetric approach is now mandatory for major National Highway projects (MoRTH 5th Revision, Section 500).

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of VMA and VFA from First Principles

Consider a compacted bituminous specimen of total mass $M$ and bulk volume $V$. The mix is a three-phase system: aggregate, binder, and air.

**Step 1: Define component masses and volumes.**

$$M = M_a + M_b \quad ; \quad V = V_a + V_b + V_v$$

Where:
- $M_a$ = mass of aggregate, $M_b$ = mass of binder
- $V_a$ = volume of aggregate (excluding permeable voids), $V_b$ = volume of binder, $V_v$ = volume of air voids

**Step 2: Express mass in terms of percentages.**

Let $P_s$ = % aggregate, $P_b$ = % binder (by total mix weight). Then:

$$M_a = \frac{P_s \cdot M}{100} \quad ; \quad M_b = \frac{P_b \cdot M}{100}$$

**Step 3: Express volumes using specific gravities.**

$$V_a = \frac{M_a}{G_{sb} \cdot \rho_w} \quad ; \quad V_b = \frac{M_b}{G_b \cdot \rho_w}$$

**Step 4: Bulk specific gravity of the mix.**

$$G_{mb} = \frac{M}{V \cdot \rho_w}$$

**Step 5: Compute VMA.**

VMA is the volume of inter-aggregate void space in the compacted mix (i.e., everything except the aggregate itself):

$$VMA = \frac{V - V_a}{V} \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{V_a}{V}\right) \times 100$$

Substituting $V_a = \frac{M_a}{G_{sb} \cdot \rho_w} = \frac{P_s \cdot M}{100 \cdot G_{sb} \cdot \rho_w}$ and $V = \frac{M}{G_{mb} \cdot \rho_w}$:

$$VMA = \left(1 - \frac{\frac{P_s \cdot M}{100 \cdot G_{sb} \cdot \rho_w}}{\frac{M}{G_{mb} \cdot \rho_w}}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{P_s \cdot G_{mb}}{100 \cdot G_{sb}}\right) \times 100$$

$$\boxed{VMA = 100 - \frac{P_s \cdot G_{mb}}{G_{sb}}}$$

**Step 6: Compute VFA.**

VFA is the percentage of VMA that is filled with binder (i.e., excluding air voids):

$$VFA = \frac{V_b}{V - V_a} \times 100 = \frac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{VFA = \frac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked-Out Example: Volumetric Calculation

**Given Data:**
- Bulk specific gravity of aggregate, $G_{sb}$ = **2.65**
- Specific gravity of binder, $G_b$ = **1.02**
- Binder content, $P_b$ = **5.0%** by total mix weight
- Aggregate content, $P_s$ = **95.0%** by total mix weight
- Bulk specific gravity of compacted mix, $G_{mb}$ = **2.40**

**Required:** Calculate $G_{mm}$, $V_a$, $VMA$, $VFA$.

---

**Step 1: Maximum Theoretical Specific Gravity (G_mm)**

Using the Rice formula:

$$G_{mm} = \frac{100}{\frac{P_s}{G_{sb}} + \frac{P_b}{G_b}} = \frac{100}{\frac{95.0}{2.65} + \frac{5.0}{1.02}}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$\frac{95.0}{2.65} = 35.8491$$

$$\frac{5.0}{1.02} = 4.9020$$

$$Sum = 35.8491 + 4.9020 = 40.7511$$

Therefore:

$$G_{mm} = \frac{100}{40.7511} = 2.454 \quad (\text{rounded to 3 decimals})$$

**Answer:** $G_{mm} = 2.454$

---

**Step 2: Air Voids (V_a)**

$$V_a = \frac{G_{mm} - G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100 = \frac{2.454 - 2.40}{2.454} \times 100$$

$$V_a = \frac{0.054}{2.454} \times 100 = 2.20\%$$

**Answer:** $V_a = 2.20\%$

---

**Step 3: Voids in Mineral Aggregate (VMA)**

$$VMA = 100 - \frac{P_s \cdot G_{mb}}{G_{sb}} = 100 - \frac{95.0 \times 2.40}{2.65}$$

Compute the fraction:

$$\frac{95.0 \times 2.40}{2.65} = \frac{228.0}{2.65} = 86.038$$

Therefore:

$$VMA = 100 - 86.038 = 13.96\%$$

**Answer:** $VMA = 13.96\%$

---

**Step 4: Voids Filled with Asphalt (VFA)**

$$VFA = \frac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100 = \frac{13.96 - 2.20}{13.96} \times 100$$

$$VFA = \frac{11.76}{13.96} \times 100 = 84.24\%$$

**Answer:** $VFA = 84.24\%$

---

**Step 5: Interpretation Against Marshall Criteria (BC layer)**

| Property | Calculated | MoRTH Spec | Compliance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air Voids | 2.20% | 3–5% | ❌ Below range — mix too dense (bleeding risk) |
| VMA | 13.96% | ≥ 14.0% | ❌ Just below — increase aggregate size or adjust gradation |
| VFA | 84.24% | 65–75% | ❌ Above range — over-asphalted (rutting risk) |

**Conclusion:** The mix is **over-asphalted** and **too dense**. The binder content must be **reduced** (try 4.5%) or aggregate gradation must be adjusted to increase VMA.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Mix Design Methods

| Aspect | Marshall Method | Superpave Method |
|---|---|---|
| Compaction | Impact hammer (vertical) | Gyratory shear compactor (kneading) |
| Specimen shape | Cylindrical (101.6 mm) | Cylindrical (150 mm) |
| Compaction energy | 75 blows/face (fixed) | Variable (N_des by traffic) |
| Field correlation | Empirical; weak for modern mixes | Better; closer to actual field |
| Test specimen | Stability & flow (60 °C) | Volumetric + performance tests |
| Aggregate criterion | Gradation, AIV, LA abrasion | + CAA, FAA, F&E, Sand Equivalent |
| Binder criterion | Penetration / Viscosity grade | **Performance Grade (PG)** |
| Strength measure | Stability (kN) | Shear strength (rutting) via SST |
| Drain-down criterion | Not explicit | Dust Proportion (0.6–1.2) |
| Restricted Zone | Not used | Used (for fine aggregate) |
| Use in India | Widely used (MoRTH) | Emerging (major NHs/Expressways) |
| Cost | Low | Higher (equipment-intensive) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The Marshall method remains the **official MoRTH standard** for Indian highways, but Superpave is increasingly adopted for expressways and high-volume corridors. The examiner often asks a **direct comparison** between the two — students should remember the 5–6 key points of difference.

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Superpave Gyratory Compaction Analysis

**Problem:** A 150 mm diameter specimen is compacted in a SGC. The recorded specimen heights are:

| Gyrations (N) | Height (mm) |
|---|---|
| 5 | 130.5 |
| 10 | 126.2 |
| 50 (N_des) | 119.8 |
| 100 (≈ N_max) | 117.5 |

The Rice specific gravity $G_{mm} = 2.481$ and the bulk SG of compacted specimen at N_des is $G_{mb} = 2.382$.

**Find:** %G_mm at N_des and at 100 gyrations.

---

**Step 1: Calculate %G_mm at N_des.**

$$\%G_{mm @ N_{des}} = \frac{G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100 = \frac{2.382}{2.481} \times 100 = 96.01\%$$

**Interpretation:** This represents 96.01% of theoretical maximum density — equivalently, **air voids = 100 - 96.01 = 3.99% ≈ 4%**, which is the **Superpave design air void target**.

---

**Step 2: Calculate %G_mm at 100 gyrations using height ratio.**

The bulk SG at any gyration is related to the SG at the reference height by the height ratio (since volume is proportional to height for fixed cross-section):

$$\%G_{mm @ 100} = \frac{h_{N_{des}}}{h_{100}} \times \%G_{mm @ N_{des}}$$

$$= \frac{119.8}{117.5} \times 96.01 = 1.0196 \times 96.01 = 97.88\%$$

**Air voids at 100 gyrations:** $V_a = 100 - 97.88 = 2.12\%$

**Interpretation:** The mix densifies from 4% (at N_des = 50) to about 2% voids under heavy traffic, ensuring that additional compaction in the field will not cause excessive rutting.
