## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 SMA — Stone-on-Stone Contact Schematic

The following block diagram illustrates the **internal architecture of Stone Matrix Asphalt (SMA)** and the role of each component in load transfer.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Coarse Aggregate 70-80%] --> B[Stone-on-Stone Contact]
    A --> C[Load Transfer Skeleton]
    D[Fine Aggregate 10-20%] --> E[Mortar Phase]
    F[Mineral Filler 8-12%] --> E
    G[Fibres 0.3-0.5%] --> E
    H[Bitumen 6-7%] --> E
    E --> I[Mastic Fills Voids]
    B --> J[Resistance to Rutting]
    I --> K[Impermeability and Durability]
    G --> L[Prevents Binder Drain-Down]
    C --> M[Interlocking = High Shear Strength]
    J --> N[Final SMA Mix Performance]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The coarse aggregate forms a **load-bearing skeleton** (stone-on-stone contact) — this is the *primary* contributor to rutting resistance. The mastic (binder + filler + fibre) **fills the voids** of this skeleton and provides *secondary* benefits of impermeability and durability. The fibre acts as a **3D-reinforcement** that holds the binder in place during transport and laying (preventing drain-down into the underlying layer).

---

### 4.2 Marshall Mix Design — Process Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Step 1: Select Aggregate Gradation per MoRTH] --> B[Step 2: Estimate Trial Binder Content]
    B --> C[Step 3: Prepare 15 Specimens at 5 Binder Levels]
    C --> D[Step 4: Compact Using Marshall Hammer 75 Blows per Face]
    D --> E[Step 5: Determine Bulk SG of Each Specimen]
    D --> F[Step 6: Determine Theoretical Max SG by Rice Test]
    E --> G[Step 7: Compute Va, VMA, VFA, Gmb]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Step 8: Perform Stability and Flow Test at 60 C]
    H --> I[Step 9: Plot Graphs Stability vs Pb Flow vs Pb Va vs Pb VMA vs Pb VFA vs Pb]
    I --> J[Step 10: Determine OBC from Average of 3 Values]
    J --> K[Step 11: Validate OBC Specimens Against MoRTH Criteria]
    K --> L{All Criteria Met?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Approve Mix Design]
    L -->|No| N[Adjust Gradation or Binder and Re-Test]
    N --> B
```

---

### 4.3 Superpave Mix Design — Volumetric Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Climate Analysis] --> B[PG Binder Selection]
    C[Traffic ESALs] --> D[Selection of N_init N_des N_max]
    E[Aggregate Properties] --> F[CAA FAA F and E Sand Equivalent LA]
    A --> G[Superpave Mix Design]
    B --> G
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    H[Restricted Zone Check] --> G
    I[Control Points Check] --> G
    G --> J[SGC Compaction]
    J --> K[Densification Curve]
    K --> L[Select Design Binder Content at 4 percent Air Voids]
    L --> M[Validate VMA VFA Dust Proportion]
    M --> N[Performance Testing Rutting Fatigue Cracking]
    N --> O[Approved Superpave Mix]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a flow diagram helps:** The Superpave design is more *systematic* than Marshall — it begins with **performance-graded binder selection based on climate** and **N_des selection based on traffic**, which is a fundamentally different starting point. The volumetric validation is followed by **performance testing** (rutting, fatigue, thermal cracking) — making it a *performance-based* rather than *empirical* design.

---

### 4.4 Volumetric Composition of Compacted Bituminous Mix

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Compact Bituminous Specimen] --> B[Total Volume V]
    B --> C[Volume of Aggregate Va]
    B --> D[VMA Voids in Mineral Aggregate]
    D --> E[Volume of Air Voids Vv]
    D --> F[Volume of Effective Binder Vbe]
    C --> G[Permeable Voids in Aggregate Filled with Absorbed Binder]
    E --> H[Va = 3 to 5 percent Target]
    F --> I[VFA = 65 to 75 percent Target]
    I --> J[Mix is Optimum Durable Stable and Impermeable]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A 100% compacted mix is divided as: **(Volume of aggregate + VMA) = 100%**, and VMA itself is split into **air voids (Va)** and **effective binder (Vbe)**. The ratio of Vbe to VMA is the VFA, the most important indicator of *binder economy* and *rutting resistance*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A: 3-Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define **Stone Matrix Asphalt (SMA)**. List any **three** advantages of SMA over conventional dense-graded bituminous concrete.

**Model Answer:**

**Stone Matrix Asphalt (SMA)** is a gap-graded, stone-rich bituminous mix designed for high rutting resistance and durability. It consists of a coarse aggregate skeleton (70–80%) in **stone-on-stone contact**, with the inter-particle voids filled by a rich **mastic** of binder (6–7%), mineral filler (8–12%), and stabilising fibre (0.3–0.5%).

**Three advantages of SMA over conventional BC:**
1. **Superior rutting resistance** due to coarse aggregate interlock (stone-on-stone contact).
2. **Better durability and aging resistance** because of higher binder film thickness and filler content.
3. **Improved skid resistance** due to the macro-texture created by exposed coarse aggregates.
4. *(Optional 4th)* Excellent impermeability as the mastic seals all voids.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **Mastic Asphalt** and **Stone Matrix Asphalt (SMA)** based on composition, gradation, and primary application.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Mastic Asphalt | Stone Matrix Asphalt (SMA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Gradation** | Dense-graded (no coarse aggregate skeleton) | Gap-graded (stone-rich skeleton) |
| **Binder content** | Very high (14–17%) | Moderate (6–7%) |
| **Air voids** | ~0% (essentially voidless) | 3–4% (designed voids) |
| **Filler content** | High (limestone powder 25–35% of aggregate) | High (8–12% of mix) |
| **Aggregate skeleton** | None (matrix-dominated) | Stone-on-stone contact |
| **Primary application** | Bridge deck waterproofing, tunnel linings, footpaths, car park decks | Heavy-traffic highways, expressways, intersections (rut-prone zones) |
| **Laying method** | Floated hot, no rolling required | Laid and compacted with rollers |

---

### PART B: 14-Mark Questions

#### QUESTION A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]

**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **Marshall Method of Bituminous Mix Design**. List the **Marshall criteria** for Bituminous Concrete (BC) as per MoRTH 5th Revision. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data were obtained from a Marshall mix design study on a BC specimen with 5.0% binder content by weight of mix. Compute the **volumetric properties** (Va, VMA, VFA) and comment on the suitability of the mix.

**Data:**
- Bulk specific gravity of aggregate, $G_{sb}$ = 2.68
- Specific gravity of binder, $G_b$ = 1.02
- Aggregate content, $P_s$ = 95.0%
- Bulk specific gravity of compacted mix, $G_{mb}$ = 2.41
- Theoretical maximum specific gravity (Rice), $G_{mm}$ = 2.478

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Marshall Method Flow Diagram:**

The Marshall method involves the following sequential steps (also shown in Section 4.2 diagram):

1. **Step 1 – Aggregate selection:** Select aggregate gradation conforming to MoRTH Table 500-5 (BC) for 13.2 mm nominal maximum size.
2. **Step 2 – Trial binder content:** Estimate initial binder content (typically 5.0–5.5% by weight of mix).
3. **Step 3 – Specimen preparation:** Prepare 3 replicate specimens each at 5 binder levels (e.g., 4.5, 5.0, 5.5, 6.0, 6.5%) → 15 specimens total.
4. **Step 4 – Compaction:** Compact specimens in a 101.6 mm mould using a 4.54 kg hammer falling 457 mm, with **75 blows per face** (heavy traffic).
5. **Step 5 – Bulk specific gravity:** Determine $G_{mb}$ by the saturated surface-dry (SSD) method.
6. **Step 6 – Maximum theoretical SG:** Determine $G_{mm}$ by the Rice test (ASTM D2041) on loose mix.
7. **Step 7 – Volumetrics:** Compute Va, VMA, VFA from formulas.
8. **Step 8 – Stability & flow test:** Heat specimens in a 60 °C water bath for 30–40 min; load in the Marshall testing machine at **50.8 mm/min** until failure; record maximum load (stability, kN) and deformation (flow, mm).
9. **Step 9 – Plot graphs:** Plot Stability, Flow, Va, VMA, VFA, Gmb versus binder content.
10. **Step 10 – Determine OBC:** OBC = average of (a) Pb at peak stability, (b) Pb at median target Va (4%), (c) Pb at target VFA.
11. **Step 11 – Validation:** Re-cast 3 specimens at OBC and verify all properties meet MoRTH criteria.

**Marshall Criteria for Bituminous Concrete (BC) — MoRTH Table 500-11:**

| Property | Specification |
|---|---|
| Compaction (blows/face) | 75 |
| Marshall Stability, min | **9.0 kN** |
| Flow value | 2 – 4 mm |
| Air Voids (Va) | 3 – 5% |
| VMA, min (12.5 mm NMAS) | **14.0%** |
| VFA | 65 – 75% |
| Gmb | To be reported |

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Air Voids (Va)**

$$V_a = \frac{G_{mm} - G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100 = \frac{2.478 - 2.41}{2.478} \times 100 = \frac{0.068}{2.478} \times 100 = 2.74\%$$

**Step 2: VMA**

$$VMA = 100 - \frac{P_s \cdot G_{mb}}{G_{sb}} = 100 - \frac{95.0 \times 2.41}{2.68} = 100 - \frac{228.95}{2.68}$$

$$VMA = 100 - 85.43 = 14.57\%$$

**Step 3: VFA**

$$VFA = \frac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100 = \frac{14.57 - 2.74}{14.57} \times 100 = \frac{11.83}{14.57} \times 100 = 81.19\%$$

**Step 4: Comparison with MoRTH Criteria**

| Property | Calculated | MoRTH Spec | Compliance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Va | 2.74% | 3 – 5% | ❌ Below range |
| VMA | 14.57% | ≥ 14.0% | ✅ Pass |
| VFA | 81.19% | 65 – 75% | ❌ Above range |

**Step 5: Comment on Suitability**

The mix is **NOT suitable** at 5.0% binder content. The Va is below the target range (mix is over-compacted/over-asphalted), and the VFA exceeds 75% (indicating the VMA is over-filled with binder, which makes the mix prone to **rutting and bleeding** under heavy traffic).

**Recommendation:** **Reduce the binder content** to approximately 4.5% (to lift Va to ~4% and lower VFA into the 65–75% range) and re-test.

---

#### QUESTION B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]

**(a)** Explain the **Superpave method of bituminous mix design**. What are the **key differences** between Superpave and Marshall methods? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data is given for a Superpave SGC-compacted mix at design gyration N_des = 100. Determine (i) the **effective specific gravity of aggregate** (G_se), (ii) the **absorbed binder** (P_ba), and (iii) the **effective binder content** (P_be). **(7 marks)**

**Data:**
- Theoretical maximum SG, $G_{mm}$ = 2.481
- Bulk SG of aggregate, $G_{sb}$ = 2.642
- SG of binder, $G_b$ = 1.025
- Total binder content, $P_b$ = 5.5%
- Aggregate content, $P_s$ = 94.5%

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Superpave Method of Mix Design:**

The **Superior Performing Asphalt Pavements (Superpave)** system was developed under SHRP (1987–1993) to address limitations in Marshall and Hveem methods. It has three components:

**1. Performance-Graded (PG) Binder Specification:**
The binder is selected based on the **7-day maximum pavement temperature** and **minimum pavement temperature** of the project location. For example, **PG 64-22** means the binder is graded for +64 °C high and -22 °C low pavement temperatures.

**2. Gyratory Shear Compactor (SGC):**
- Specimen: 150 mm diameter, ~115–120 mm height.
- Compaction angle: 1.16°, Pressure: 600 kPa, Speed: 30 gyrations/min.
- Compaction continues through N_init, N_des, N_max with continuous height recording.

**3. Volumetric Mix Design:**
- Specimens are compacted at **N_des** to achieve **4% air voids** — this is the design binder content.
- Mix is verified against Va (4%), VMA (≥ 14% for 12.5 mm), VFA (65–78%), Dust Proportion (0.6–1.2).
- The gradation is plotted against **control points** and must avoid the **restricted zone** (for fine aggregate).

**4. Performance Testing:**
- **Rutting:** Asphalt Shear Rutting Test (RSST-CH).
- **Fatigue cracking:** Flexural Beam Fatigue Test.
- **Low-temp cracking:** Indirect Tensile Creep & Strength Test.

**Key Differences — Superpave vs. Marshall:**

| Aspect | Marshall | Superpave |
|---|---|---|
| Compaction | Impact hammer (vertical) | Gyratory (kneading) |
| Compaction effort | Fixed (75 blows) | Variable (N_des by traffic) |
| Specimen | 101.6 mm | 150 mm |
| Binder spec | Penetration / Viscosity | Performance Grade (PG) |
| Aggregate property | AIV, LA, FI/EI | + CAA, FAA, F&E, Sand Eq. |
| Mix strength | Stability & Flow | Shear strength (rutting) |
| Restricted Zone | Not used | Used |
| Dust Proportion | Not used | Used (0.6–1.2) |
| Performance test | Not included | Included (rutting, fatigue, cracking) |
| Field correlation | Empirical | Performance-based |

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Effective Specific Gravity of Aggregate (G_se)**

$$G_{se} = \frac{100 - P_b}{\frac{100}{G_{mm}} - \frac{P_b}{G_b}} = \frac{100 - 5.5}{\frac{100}{2.481} - \frac{5.5}{1.025}}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$\frac{100}{2.481} = 40.3063$$

$$\frac{5.5}{1.025} = 5.3659$$

$$Denominator = 40.3063 - 5.3659 = 34.9404$$

$$G_{se} = \frac{94.5}{34.9404} = 2.704$$

**Answer:** $G_{se} = 2.704$

**Step 2: Absorbed Binder (P_ba)**

$$P_{ba} = 100 \times \frac{G_{se} - G_{sb}}{G_{se} \cdot G_{sb}} \times G_b$$

$$P_{ba} = 100 \times \frac{2.704 - 2.642}{2.704 \times 2.642} \times 1.025$$

Compute the numerator:

$$2.704 - 2.642 = 0.062$$

Compute the denominator:

$$2.704 \times 2.642 = 7.144$$

Therefore:

$$P_{ba} = 100 \times \frac{0.062}{7.144} \times 1.025 = 100 \times 0.00868 \times 1.025$$

$$P_{ba} = 100 \times 0.00889 = 0.889\%$$

**Answer:** $P_{ba} = 0.89\%$ (by weight of aggregate)

**Step 3: Effective Binder Content (P_be)**

$$P_{be} = P_b - \frac{P_{ba} \times P_s}{100}$$

$$P_{be} = 5.5 - \frac{0.889 \times 94.5}{100} = 5.5 - \frac{84.01}{100} = 5.5 - 0.840 = 4.66\%$$

**Answer:** $P_{be} = 4.66\%$ (by weight of total mix)

**Interpretation:** Out of 5.5% total binder added, 0.84% is absorbed into the aggregate pores, and only **4.66%** acts as the **effective binder** that coats aggregate and provides adhesion/impermeability. This value is used in computing Dust Proportion for Superpave validation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Rice Test in Marshall design** — $G_{mm}$ is required for Va calculation. Students often use 100% as $G_{mb}$ and get wrong Va.
> 2. **Mixing up VMA and VFA** — VMA is the total void space in the aggregate skeleton (including air and binder); VFA is only the *percentage of that void space* filled with binder.
> 3. **Using $G_{sb}$ instead of $G_{se}$** in Superpave $G_{mm}$ calculation — this gives erroneous $G_{se}$ in the iterative solution.
> 4. **Not reporting the units** of stability (kN) and flow (mm) — examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 5. **Confusing the role of fibre in SMA** — it is added to prevent **drain-down**, not for strength.
> 6. **Stating "Marshall is better than Superpave"** — both are valid; the question is **application-specific**. Always state the *context* of choice.
> 7. **Ignoring Marshall stability correlation ratio** — if specimen height deviates from 63.5 mm, a correlation ratio must be applied (loss of 1 mark if not mentioned).
> 8. **Mixing up Restricted Zone with Control Points** — Control Points are mandatory gradation boundaries; Restricted Zone is a "no-pass" zone for fine aggregate in Superpave (advisory).

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- [ ] **Bitumen grades in India:** VG-10, VG-20, VG-30, VG-40 (IS 73). VG-30 is the workhorse for DBM/BC.
- [ ] **Cutback bitumen** types: RC (naphtha), MC (kerosene), SC (diesel). The number = viscosity in cSt at 60 °C.
- [ ] **Emulsions:** RS, MS, SS based on setting rate. **Cationic** preferred for siliceous (acidic) aggregates.
- [ ] **Aggregate requirements (MoRTH):** AIV ≤ 24%, LAA ≤ 30%, FI ≤ 25%, water absorption ≤ 2%, stripping value ≥ 95%.
- [ ] **Stripping** is the water-induced separation of bitumen from aggregate; counter with **hydrated lime** (1–2%).
- [ ] **SMA = stone-rich, gap-graded, fibre-stabilised** mix; binder 6–7%, filler 8–12%, fibre 0.3–0.5%.
- [ ] **Mastic Asphalt = voidless, dense, very rich in binder (14–17%)**; used for waterproofing and bridges.
- [ ] **Three key volumetric properties:** Va (3–5%), VMA (≥14% for BC), VFA (65–75%).
- [ ] **Marshall test parameters:** 60 °C water bath, 50.8 mm/min loading rate, 75 blows/face.
- [ ] **Marshall criteria for BC:** Stability ≥ 9 kN, Flow 2–4 mm, Va 3–5%, VMA ≥ 14%, VFA 65–75%.
- [ ] **OBC = Average of** (Pb at peak stability, Pb at median Va=4%, Pb at target VFA).
- [ ] **Superpave N_des selection** depends on traffic ESALs (range 50–200 gyrations).
- [ ] **Superpave design target: 4% air voids at N_des** = 96% of $G_{mm}$.
- [ ] **Dust Proportion (DP)** = (% passing 75 µm) / (effective binder $P_{be}$). Range 0.6–1.2.
- [ ] **PG binder** is selected by project **7-day max pavement temperature** and **min pavement temperature**.
- [ ] **Fibre in SMA** is to prevent binder drain-down during transport and laying (NOT for strength).
- [ ] **Always cross-check G_se > G_sb** in Superpave calculations; otherwise the mix has anomalous properties.
- [ ] **Stone-on-stone contact** in SMA is what gives superior rutting resistance — verified by **VCA (Voids in Coarse Aggregate) ratio check**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction of Flexible Pavement - Construction Steps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET745 - PAVEMENT CONSTRUCTION AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Construction of Flexible Pavement - Construction Steps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Flexible Pavement** is a layered system of superior quality materials, built directly on the natural soil subgrade, whose flexural strength is negligible compared to the concrete rigid pavements. It transmits the vehicular wheel load through a mechanism of **grain-to-grain transfer of load** from the upper layers to the lower layers via the inter-particle friction and cohesion of the aggregate matrix. The vertical compressive stress dissipates as the load travels downward, distributing it over a wider area at the subgrade level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECET745 Module 3):** "The construction of a flexible pavement is a multi-stage, sequential operation involving preparation of the subgrade, construction of granular sub-base and base courses, application of bituminous binders (prime and tack coats), and laying of bituminous surfacing courses, all conforming to MoRTH (Ministry of Road Transport and Highways, India) specifications."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multi-layered sponge cake** resting on a soft kitchen counter. When you press your finger on the top frosting:
- The **frosting (surface course)** takes the direct pressure
- The **cream layer (binder course)** spreads the load sideways slightly
- The **sponge base (base course)** spreads it even more
- The **thin jam layer (sub-base)** continues the spreading
- Finally, the **counter (subgrade)** bears a much larger but much weaker distributed force

A flexible pavement works exactly like this cake. No single layer is "strong" by itself in bending — instead, each successive lower layer *spreads* the wheel load over a wider footprint, reducing stress. The pavement is "flexible" because the structure deflects (bends) under load without cracking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A flexible pavement is only as good as its weakest layer — usually the **subgrade**. If the subgrade fails, the entire pavement structure fails regardless of how thick the upper bituminous layer is.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Standard Lane Width:** **3.5 m** (single lane), **7.0 m** (two lanes without median)
- **Standard Design Speed (NH/SH):** **80–100 km/h**
- **Minimum CBR for Subgrade:** **8%** (Rural Roads), **10%** (Highways)
- **Compaction Standard Reference:** **IS 2720 (Part 8)** for Modified Proctor, **IS 2720 (Part 7)** for Light Compaction
- **Bitumen Grade (Viscosity):** **VG-30, VG-40** (as per IS 73)
- **Layer Thickness Tolerance:** **± 5 mm** for bituminous layers; **± 10 mm** for granular layers
- **Prime Coat Tackiness Reference Temperature:** **60–80 °C** (cutback bitumen application)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Sequential Logic of Construction (Step-by-Step Operations)

The construction of a flexible pavement is a **linear dependency chain** — every step depends on the successful completion of the previous one. The workflow is divided into **three major phases**:

#### PHASE A — Earthwork & Subgrade Preparation
1. **Site Clearance:** Removal of vegetation, topsoil, organic matter, debris (typically **150–300 mm** depth).
2. **Setting Out & Surveying:** Centerline marking, cross-section levels using Total Station/GPS.
3. **Excavation / Embankment Construction:** Cut or fill to reach formation level. Embankment filled in **loose layers of 200–300 mm** if the height exceeds 500 mm.
4. **Subgrade Compaction:** Achieved to **97% of Modified Proctor Maximum Dry Density (MDD)** as per IS 2720 (Part 8).
5. **Subgrade Strength Verification:** CBR test on the prepared subgrade (must be ≥ 8% for rural, ≥ 10% for highways).
6. **Drainage Installation:** Laying of side drains, catch-pits, and sub-drains BEFORE the pavement layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Subgrade:** *Never* lay a granular sub-base on a wet, soft, or uncompacted subgrade. Trapped moisture will cause **pumping** and **rutting** under traffic.

#### PHASE B — Granular Layer Construction
7. **Sub-Base Course (GSB - Granular Sub-Base):**
   - Material: Crushed stone, moorum, gravel, or stabilized soil.
   - Gradation: As per **MoRTH Section 400** (Table 400-1).
   - Compacted to **98% of MDD** (Modified Proctor).
   - Thickness: Typically **100–300 mm** in compacted layers (each layer ≤ 200 mm).
8. **Base Course (WBM/WMM):**
   - **WBM (Water Bound Macadam):** Coarse aggregates + screenings + binding material (moorum), compacted with **8–10 tonnes smooth-wheeled roller**, sprinkled with water for 3–4 days.
   - **WMM (Wet Mix Macadam):** Pre-mixed plant-mix of graded aggregates + water, laid by paver, compacted with **vibratory roller**. Preferred over WBM in modern construction.

#### PHASE C — Bituminous Layer Construction
9. **Prime Coat:** Application of **slow-curing cutback bitumen (MC-70 or SS-1)** at **0.6–0.9 kg/m²** over the granular base, to seal the voids and promote adhesion. **Curing time: 24–48 hours.**
10. **Tack Coat:** Application of **rapid-setting emulsion (RS-1) or bitumen emulsion** at **0.20–0.30 kg/m²** between successive bituminous layers. **Curing time: 2–4 hours.**
11. **Binder Course (DBM - Dense Bituminous Macadam):** Hot mix plant output (**150–165 °C**), transported in **insulated trucks**, laid by **paver finisher**, compacted to **96% of Marshall Density** (or **94% of Gmm** for Superpave).
12. **Surface Course (BC - Bituminous Concrete / SDBC - Semi-Dense Bituminous Concrete):** Final wearing course, **40–50 mm thick**, placed with maximum care for **surface regularity, skid resistance, and riding quality**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern (PECET745):** Examiners frequently ask students to list construction steps in *chronological order* with the *purpose* of each step. Memorizing the sequence is worth 7+ marks in a single question.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Compaction & Density)

The following equations are routinely used for **quality control of compaction** during flexible pavement construction.

$$
\rho_{d(\text{field})} = \frac{M_{\text{wet}}}{V_{\text{core}} \times (1 + w)}
$$

where $\rho_{d(\text{field})}$ = field dry density (g/cc), $M_{\text{wet}}$ = wet mass of core cutter sample, $V_{\text{core}}$ = volume of core cutter (cc), $w$ = in-situ moisture content (decimal).

$$
\text{Relative Compaction (\%)} = \frac{\rho_{d(\text{field})}}{\rho_{d(\text{max, lab})}} \times 100
$$

For Modified Proctor (IS 2720 Part 8): light compaction energy = **595 kJ/m³**, modified = **2,693 kJ/m³**.

$$
\text{Air Voids } (V_a) = \frac{G_m - \gamma_d}{G_m} \times 100 \;\; (\%)
$$

For bituminous mixes:

$$
\text{VMA} = 100 - \frac{G_{mb} \times P_s}{G_{sb}}
$$

$$
\text{VFB} = \frac{\text{VMA} - V_a}{\text{VMA}} \times 100
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VFB (Voids Filled with Bitumen) is the parameter most cited in KTU questions for bituminous mix design.** A typical BC mix must satisfy VFB = **65–75%**.

### 2.3 Compaction Standards Quick Reference Table

| Layer Type | Compaction Standard | Field Test Method | Required Density (%) | Reference Code |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Embankment / Subgrade | Modified Proctor | Sand Replacement / Core Cutter | 95–97 | IS 2720 Part 8 |
| Granular Sub-Base (GSB) | Modified Proctor | Sand Replacement | 98 | IS 2720 Part 8 |
| WMM Base Course | Modified Proctor | Sand Replacement | 98 | IS 2720 Part 8 |
| WBM Base Course | Modified Proctor | Sand Replacement | 98 | IS 2720 Part 8 |
| DBM (Binder Course) | Marshall / Gmm | Bulk Density of Cores | 96 / 94 | ASTM D2726 / D2041 |
| BC (Surface Course) | Marshall / Gmm | Bulk Density of Cores | 97 / 95 | ASTM D2726 / D2041 |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Pitfall Alert:** KTU valuation scripts often *trap* students who confuse **WBM** (Water Bound Macadam, uses water-bound screenings) with **WMM** (Wet Mix Macadam, plant-mixed, no separate screenings). Remember: WMM is the **modern, IRC-recommended** replacement for WBM.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The mastery of construction sequencing directly impacts:
- **Project Duration:** Poor scheduling of bituminous plant arrival can delay works by weeks.
- **Cost Overruns:** Rework on subgrade (recompaction, re-stabilization) accounts for **30–40%** of pavement construction disputes.
- **Service Life:** A pavement constructed with proper sequencing lasts **15–20 years** for NH/SH and **8–10 years** for rural roads; a poorly sequenced one fails in **3–5 years**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Compaction Quality Control (Relative Compaction %)

This derivation is critical because the **Relative Compaction** is the *primary acceptance criterion* for any flexible pavement layer in the field.

**Given:**
- Volume of core cutter, $V_{\text{core}}$ = **1,000 cm³**
- Wet mass of soil + core cutter, $M_2$ = **2,950 g**
- Mass of empty core cutter, $M_1$ = **1,500 g**
- Moisture content (from oven-dry method), $w$ = **12% = 0.12**
- Maximum dry density from Modified Proctor lab test, $\rho_{d(\text{max})}$ = **1.92 g/cc**

**Step 1 — Compute the wet mass of soil alone:**

$$
M_{\text{wet}} = M_2 - M_1
$$

$$
M_{\text{wet}} = 2950 \text{ g} - 1500 \text{ g} = 1450 \text{ g}
$$

The subtracted quantity represents the empty mass of the core cutter, leaving only the soil mass.

**Step 2 — Compute the bulk (wet) density:**

$$
\rho_{\text{wet}} = \frac{M_{\text{wet}}}{V_{\text{core}}}
$$

$$
\rho_{\text{wet}} = \frac{1450 \text{ g}}{1000 \text{ cm}^3} = 1.450 \text{ g/cc}
$$

Bulk density is the mass of the wet soil per unit volume, which includes both solids and water.

**Step 3 — Compute the dry density using the moisture correction:**

The relationship between dry density ($\rho_d$), bulk density ($\rho_{\text{wet}}$), and moisture content ($w$) is derived from the mass-volume definitions:

$$
\rho_d = \frac{M_{\text{dry}}}{V} = \frac{M_{\text{wet}} / (1 + w)}{V} = \frac{\rho_{\text{wet}}}{1 + w}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\rho_d = \frac{1.450}{1 + 0.12} = \frac{1.450}{1.12} = 1.2946 \text{ g/cc}
$$

The denominator $(1 + w)$ converts wet mass to dry mass by removing the water component.

**Step 4 — Compute the Relative Compaction:**

$$
\text{Relative Compaction} = \frac{\rho_{d(\text{field})}}{\rho_{d(\text{max, lab})}} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{1.2946}{1.92} \times 100 = 67.43 \;\; \%
$$

**Conclusion / Engineering Verdict:**

> [!WARNING]
> A relative compaction of **67.43%** is **grossly inadequate** for any pavement layer. The required minimum for subgrade is **97%** of Modified Proctor MDD. The site engineer must immediately **recompact the layer** (likely with additional passes of a vibratory roller) and re-test until the criterion is met. **No further layer can be placed** until the relative compaction exceeds the threshold.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Prime Coat vs. Tack Coat

A **highly examinable distinction** in KTU theory papers is the difference between Prime Coat and Tack Coat. This matrix summarizes the engineering rationale:

| Property | Prime Coat | Tack Coat |
|---|---|---|
| **Applied On** | Granular base (WBM/WMM) | Existing bituminous layer (DBM, BC) |
| **Purpose** | Seal the surface pores, water-proof the base, promote adhesion | Bond new bituminous layer to old bituminous layer |
| **Binder Type** | Slow-curing cutback (MC-70) or low-viscosity emulsion (SS-1) | Rapid-setting emulsion (RS-1) or bitumen emulsion |
| **Application Rate** | 0.6 to 0.9 kg/m² | 0.20 to 0.30 kg/m² |
| **Curing Time** | 24 to 48 hours | 2 to 4 hours |
| **Grade of Bitumen** | Soft grade to penetrate the voids | Sticky grade for surface adhesion |
| **Equipment** | Bitumen pressure distributor (tanker) | Same distributor, calibrated nozzle |
| **Failure Mode if Skipped** | Raveling of surface, water ingress, potholes | Slippage cracking, delamination of layers |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Layer Thickness Acceptance

**Problem:** A flexible pavement designed for a state highway has the following layer sequence. Verify whether the as-built thicknesses meet MoRTH tolerance.

- Granular Sub-Base: Designed = 200 mm; As-built (avg) = 195 mm
- WMM Base: Designed = 150 mm; As-built = 165 mm
- DBM: Designed = 90 mm; As-built = 92 mm
- BC: Designed = 40 mm; As-built = 38 mm

**Tolerance (MoRTH):** ± 10 mm for granular, ± 5 mm for bituminous.

| Layer | As-Built | Tolerance Limit | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| GSB | 195 mm | 200 ± 10 = [190, 210] | **ACCEPTABLE** |
| WMM | 165 mm | 150 ± 10 = [140, 160] | **REJECT** (165 > 160) |
| DBM | 92 mm | 90 ± 5 = [85, 95] | **ACCEPTABLE** |
| BC | 38 mm | 40 ± 5 = [35, 45] | **ACCEPTABLE** |

> [!WARNING]
> The WMM base course **exceeds** the upper tolerance by 5 mm. This is likely due to **insufficient compaction** or **uneven paver operation**. KTU examiner's note: candidates must clearly state the action to be taken (i.e., *rework required — additional compaction or milling before placing the DBM*).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Flexible Pavement Construction Flow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **chronological flow of operations**, including critical decision gates (Quality Check nodes) where the work either proceeds to the next layer or triggers rework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Step 00: Project Award and Mobilization]
    A1[Step 01: Site Clearance and Grubbing]
    A2[Step 02: Survey and Setting Out with Total Station]
    A3[Step 03: Earthwork - Excavation or Embankment Fill]
    A4{QC1: Subgrade Level and Moisture OK?}
    A5[Step 04: Subgrade Compaction using Vibratory Roller]
    A6{QC2: Subgrade CBR and Compaction >= 97 percent MDD?}
    A7[Step 05: Laying of Side Drains and Subdrains]
    A8[Step 06: Granular Sub-Base GSB - Plant Mix or Crusher Run]
    A9[Step 07: WMM Base Course via Paver Finisher]
    A10[Step 08: Compaction with 8 to 10 tonne Roller]
    A11{QC3: Base Density >= 98 percent MDD?}
    A12[Step 09: Prime Coat MC-70 at 0.6 to 0.9 kg per sqm]
    A13[Step 10: Curing Period 24 to 48 hours]
    A14[Step 11: Tack Coat RS-1 at 0.2 to 0.3 kg per sqm]
    A15[Step 12: DBM Binder Course 50 to 100 mm via Paver]
    A16[Step 13: Tack Coat Application]
    A17[Step 14: BC Surface Course 40 to 50 mm via Paver]
    A18[Step 15: Final Compaction with Pneumatic Tyre Roller]
    A19{QC4: Surface Regularity, Skid, and Density OK?}
    A20[Step 16: Shoulder Construction and Road Furniture]
    A21[Step 17: Final Handover to Client]

    A0 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4
    A4 -- No --> A5
    A4 -- Yes --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A6 -- No --> A3
    A6 -- Yes --> A7
    A7 --> A8 --> A9 --> A10 --> A11
    A11 -- No --> A9
    A11 -- Yes --> A12
    A12 --> A13 --> A14 --> A15 --> A16 --> A17 --> A18 --> A19
    A19 -- No --> A15
    A19 -- Yes --> A20 --> A21
```

### 4.2 Equipment-Stage Mapping Block Diagram

This block diagram maps **construction equipment to the operational stage** at which they are deployed. KTU examiners frequently ask "which equipment is used for which layer?"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Earthwork_Phase[Earthwork and Subgrade]
        EQ1[Bull Dozers and Excavators]
        EQ2[Motor Graders]
        EQ3[Vibratory Soil Compactors]
        EQ4[Water Bowsers]
    end

    subgraph Granular_Phase[Granular Sub-Base and Base]
        EQ5[Stone Crushers and Screening Plants]
        EQ6[Wet Mix Macadam Plant]
        EQ7[Paver Finishers for WMM]
        EQ8[Smooth Wheeled Rollers 8 to 10 tonnes]
    end

    subgraph Bituminous_Phase[Bituminous Layers]
        EQ9[Hot Mix Plant 120 to 160 tph]
        EQ10[Bitumen Pressure Distributor]
        EQ11[Insulated Tipper Trucks]
        EQ12[Mechanical Paver Finisher]
        EQ13[Tandem Vibratory Roller]
        EQ14[Pneumatic Tyred Roller 8 to 12 tonnes]
    end

    Earthwork_Phase --> Granular_Phase
    Granular_Phase --> Bituminous_Phase
```

### 4.3 Layer Cross-Section (Schematic Block Representation)

A visual block representation of the **layered cross-section** of a typical flexible pavement, with typical thickness and material references.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Surface Course: Bituminous Concrete BC, 40 to 50 mm]
    L2[Prime Coat and Tack Coat Layer, less than 1 mm]
    L3[Binder Course: Dense Bituminous Macadam DBM, 50 to 100 mm]
    L4[Tack Coat Layer, less than 1 mm]
    L5[Base Course: Wet Mix Macadam WMM, 150 to 300 mm]
    L6[Granular Sub-Base: GSB Crusher Run, 100 to 300 mm]
    L7[Subgrade: Compacted Natural Soil, CBR greater than 8 percent]
    L8[Natural Ground Formation Level]

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6 --> L7 --> L8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Drainage:** In the above block schematic, the **side drains** are not shown for clarity, but in KTU answers, always mention that **drainage must be constructed *before* the sub-base** to prevent waterlogging of the pavement foundation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PECET745]**  
*Define 'Prime Coat' in flexible pavement construction. State its purpose and the rate of application.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **prime coat** is a low-viscosity liquid bituminous binder (typically **medium-curing cutback MC-70** or **slow-setting emulsion SS-1**) applied to the surface of a **non-bituminous granular base course** (WBM or WMM) before laying the first bituminous layer.

**Purpose (2 marks):**
1. To **penetrate the surface voids** of the granular base, sealing them against water ingress.
2. To **waterproof the base course** and protect it from rain damage during construction delays.
3. To **promote adhesion** between the granular base and the overlying bituminous binder course (DBM).

**Application Rate (1 mark):** **0.6 to 0.9 kg/m²**, depending on the surface texture of the base course. A curing period of **24 to 48 hours** is mandatory before laying the next layer.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, PECET745]**  
*Differentiate between WBM and WMM as base course materials in flexible pavement construction.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | WBM (Water Bound Macadam) | WMM (Wet Mix Macadam) |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction Method** | Layer-by-layer, with screenings and water added on the road | Pre-mixed at the plant, transported, laid by paver |
| **Compaction** | 8–10 tonne roller with 3–4 days water sprinkling | Vibratory roller, single operation |
| **Quality Control** | Variable, depends on field workmanship | Consistent, plant-controlled gradation |
| **Modern Relevance** | Largely replaced by WMM | **IRC and MoRTH preferred option** |
| **Binding Mechanism** | Water-bound (fines washed into voids) | Mechanical interlock of graded aggregates |
| **Construction Speed** | Slow (multi-day) | Fast (single-shift) |

WMM is the **modern, mechanized successor** to WBM and is the **standard specification under MoRTH Section 400** for new highway construction.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks):**

> **Q. (a)** Describe in detail the **step-by-step construction procedure for a flexible pavement** for a new state highway project, mentioning the equipment used at each stage. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Explain the **quality control tests** conducted on each pavement layer, with reference to Indian Standard codes. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — Step-by-Step Construction (7 marks)

**1. Site Clearance and Earthwork (1 mark):**  
The site is cleared of vegetation, topsoil (150–300 mm depth), and organic matter. **Equipment:** Bull dozers, excavators, and dump trucks. Embankment, if required, is constructed in loose layers of 200–300 mm.

**2. Subgrade Preparation (1 mark):**  
The natural soil is graded to formation level using motor graders. Subgrade is compacted using **vibratory soil compactors** to achieve **97% of Modified Proctor MDD** as per **IS 2720 (Part 8)**. The in-situ CBR is tested; the minimum required is **8–10%**.

**3. Sub-Base Course Construction (1 mark):**  
Granular Sub-Base (GSB) material conforming to **MoRTH Table 400-1** is spread by a **paver finisher** (or motor grader for lower-volume roads) and compacted in layers of ≤ 200 mm to **98% MDD**. **Test:** Sand replacement method (IS 2720 Part 28).

**4. Base Course Construction (1 mark):**  
Wet Mix Macadam (WMM) is produced in a **WMM plant**, transported by dump trucks, laid by a **paver finisher**, and compacted with **8–10 tonne smooth-wheeled rollers** and **vibratory rollers** to **98% MDD**. **Test:** Gradation sieve analysis, sand replacement density.

**5. Prime Coat Application (1 mark):**  
A **medium-curing cutback bitumen (MC-70)** is sprayed uniformly on the prepared WMM base at **0.6–0.9 kg/m²** using a **bitumen pressure distributor**. The surface is allowed to cure for **24–48 hours**.

**6. Tack Coat and DBM Binder Course (1 mark):**  
A **rapid-setting emulsion (RS-1)** is sprayed at **0.20–0.30 kg/m²** as a tack coat. Hot Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM) mix from a **hot mix plant (150–165 °C)** is transported in **insulated tipper trucks**, laid by a **mechanical paver finisher**, and compacted with **tandem vibratory roller** followed by **pneumatic tyred roller** to **96% of Marshall Density**.

**7. Surface Course (BC) and Finishing (1 mark):**  
A tack coat is again applied. Bituminous Concrete (BC) mix is laid at **40–50 mm** thickness using the same paver-roller sequence. Final compaction is by **8–12 tonne pneumatic tyred roller** to seal the surface. **Test:** Surface regularity using a 3-metre straight edge (max deviation **4 mm**), skid resistance, and core density.

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — Quality Control Tests (7 marks)

| Layer | Tests Conducted | IS Code | Acceptance Criteria |
|---|---|---|---|
| Subgrade | CBR test, Proctor compaction, field density | IS 2720 (Part 8, 16) | CBR ≥ 8%, Rel. Compaction ≥ 97% |
| GSB | Gradation, field density, Atterberg limits | IS 2720 (Part 4, 5) | Rel. Compaction ≥ 98% MDD |
| WMM Base | Gradation, Los Angeles Abrasion, field density | IS 2386 (Part 4) | Rel. Compaction ≥ 98% MDD, LAA ≤ 40% |
| DBM / BC | Marshall Stability, Flow, Bulk density of cores, Air voids, VFB | ASTM D6927 / D2726 | Stability ≥ 9 kN, VFB = 65–75% |
| Surface | Surface regularity (3 m straight edge), Skid resistance | IS 5317 | Deviation ≤ 4 mm, Skid Number ≥ 50 SN |

**1 mark** for mentioning **sand replacement** and **core cutter** methods.  
**1 mark** for citing **ASTM D6926** for Marshall Mix design.  
**1 mark** for skid resistance and surface regularity tests.  
**1 mark** for the consolidated table with IS code references.  
**2 marks** for the justification of each criterion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often *list tests* without citing the **IS/ASTM code number** or the **acceptance criteria** (numerical thresholds). This is the single most common reason for losing 2–3 marks in a 7-mark question. Always pair *what test* with *what code* and *what limit*.

---

**Question B (Alternative 14-Mark Option):**

> **Q. (a)** Explain the **role and mechanism of prime coat and tack coat** in flexible pavement construction. What are the consequences of their **omission**? **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Discuss the **compaction requirements** for different layers of a flexible pavement, with reference to Modified Proctor density and the various rollers used. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — Prime & Tack Coat (7 marks)

**Prime Coat (3 marks):**
- **Definition:** A low-viscosity cutback bitumen (MC-70) applied on a granular base.
- **Mechanism:** Capillary penetration into the base voids, sealing and water-proofing.
- **Rate:** 0.6 to 0.9 kg/m²; **Curing:** 24 to 48 hours.
- **Consequence of Omission:** Water ingress into the base, **raveling of the surface course**, and **premature pothole formation** within 1–2 years.

**Tack Coat (3 marks):**
- **Definition:** A rapid-setting bitumen emulsion (RS-1) applied between two bituminous layers.
- **Mechanism:** Forms a thin sticky film that bonds the new overlay to the existing bituminous surface.
- **Rate:** 0.20 to 0.30 kg/m²; **Curing:** 2 to 4 hours.
- **Consequence of Omission:** **Slippage cracking** and **delamination** between DBM and BC layers under braking or accelerating traffic.

**Comparative Justification (1 mark):** A consolidated table differentiating prime vs. tack coat (refer to Section 3.2 matrix above).

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — Compaction Requirements (7 marks)

**1. Modified Proctor Reference (2 marks):**  
Compaction is measured as a percentage of the **Modified Proctor Maximum Dry Density (MDD)** as per **IS 2720 (Part 8)**, which applies 2,693 kJ/m³ of compaction energy (5 layers × 25 blows × 4.9 kg hammer).

**2. Layer-wise Density Requirements (3 marks):**
- Subgrade / Embankment: **97%** of MDD
- GSB / WMM Base: **98%** of MDD
- DBM Binder Course: **96%** of Marshall Density (or **94%** of Gmm)
- BC Surface Course: **97%** of Marshall Density (or **95%** of Gmm)

**3. Roller Selection (2 marks):**
- **Smooth-Wheeled Roller (8–10 tonnes):** For finish rolling of bituminous layers, gives a smooth, tight surface.
- **Vibratory Roller:** For granular layers (GSB, WMM) — provides impact + vibration for deep compaction.
- **Pneumatic Tyred Roller (8–12 tyres, 8–12 tonnes):** For bituminous layers — kneading action seals the surface voids.
- **Tandem Roller:** Final rolling of BC for smoothness.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often state "compaction is to 100% of MDD" — this is **incorrect**. Field compaction **cannot** reach 100% of lab MDD in practical conditions; the standard acceptance is **97–98%**, depending on the layer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Subgrade** is compacted to **97% of Modified Proctor MDD** (IS 2720 Part 8).
- [ ] **Granular layers** (GSB, WMM) are compacted to **98% of MDD**.
- [ ] **Bituminous layers** are compacted to **96% (DBM) and 97% (BC) of Marshall Density**.
- [ ] **Prime coat** is applied on the **granular base** (WBM/WMM) at **0.6–0.9 kg/m²** with **MC-70**; cures for **24–48 hours**.
- [ ] **Tack coat** is applied on **bituminous layers** at **0.20–0.30 kg/m²** with **RS-1**; cures for **2–4 hours**.
- [ ] **WMM is the modern replacement for WBM** (plant-mixed, mechanized).
- [ ] **Drainage (side drains) must be installed BEFORE** laying the sub-base, not after.
- [ ] **Hot mix temperature** at the plant: **150–165 °C**; at the time of laying: **> 130 °C**; final rolling: **> 80 °C**.
- [ ] **Surface regularity** of finished BC: max deviation **4 mm under a 3-metre straight edge**.
- [ ] **Tandem roller → Vibratory roller → Pneumatic tyred roller** is the standard rolling sequence for bituminous layers.
- [ ] **CBR minimum** for highway subgrade: **8% (rural), 10% (highways)**.
- [ ] **Embankment fills** in layers of loose **200–300 mm**, each compacted.
- [ ] **VFB (Voids Filled with Bitumen)** for BC = **65–75%**.
- [ ] **Air voids (Va)** in compacted bituminous layer = **3–5%** (DBM = 3–6%).
- [ ] **Tolerance:** Granular layers = **± 10 mm**; Bituminous layers = **± 5 mm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** The most frequently asked 14-mark question in this module is the *"step-by-step construction procedure"* — always answer in **chronological order**, mention **equipment**, cite **IS codes**, and conclude with **quality control parameters**. This structure covers all marking levels of **Remember, Understand, Apply, and Analyze** as per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy used in the KTU 2024 Scheme.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipment Used and Quality Control Checks of Subgrade
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET745 - PAVEMENT CONSTRUCTION AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equipment Used and Quality Control Checks of Subgrade

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subgrade** is the top layer of the natural soil (or improved soil) upon which the pavement structure is built, extending to a depth where the stress influences from traffic loads are negligible. It serves as the foundational platform that transmits traffic loads to the lower soil mass, and its engineering quality directly dictates the structural performance and service life of the entire flexible or rigid pavement system.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, **subgrade preparation** is the sequence of operations—loosening, mixing, shaping, watering, and compacting the in-situ soil (or imported select material) to achieve specified **density**, **moisture content**, and **strength (CBR / k-value)** targets prior to laying the sub-base course.

The **Equipment Used** refers to the fleet of earth-moving, grading, mixing, and compaction machinery deployed in this preparation sequence, while **Quality Control (QC) Checks** refer to the standardized field and laboratory tests (MoRTH / IRC / IS codified) executed to verify that the constructed subgrade satisfies the specified engineering parameters.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cake Foundation" Approach

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are baking a multi-tier cake. The *subgrade* is the **bottom-most cake board (the base plate)** sitting on the dining table. If this base board is wobbly, cracked, or uneven, the entire tower of cake tiers (sub-base, base course, surface course) will eventually tilt, crack, or collapse.
>
> - The **motor grader** is like the *spirit-level* that flattens the board.
> - The **compactor** is the *pressing hand* that hardens and stabilizes the board.
> - The **quality control tests** are the *ruler and weighing scale* that confirm the board is level, dense, and strong enough to hold the cake.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Constants & Standard Metrics

The following performance parameters are universally referenced in KTU examination answers:

| Parameter | Standard Metric / Range | Governing Code |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------|
| **Degree of Compaction** | **95 % to 98 %** of Maximum Dry Density (MDD) for subgrade | MoRTH Table 300-2 |
| **Optimum Moisture Content (OMC)** | Within **± 2 %** of OMC during field compaction | IRC:36 / MoRTH |
| **Minimum CBR for Subgrade** | **≥ 8 %** (general), **≥ 10 %** for expressways | IRC:37-2018 |
| **Modulus of Subgrade Reaction (k)** | **5.5 to 7.5 kg/cm³** (typical clayey subgrade) | IRC:58 |
| **Maximum Particle Size** | **75 mm** (passing 75 µm sieve for fines content ≤ 5%) | MoRTH 305 |
| **Frequency of Field Density Test** | **1 test per 500 m²** (or 1 per km for 3 m wide lane) | MoRTH 903 |
| **Liquid Limit (LL)** | **≤ 50 %**, **Plasticity Index (PI) ≤ 25** for subgrade soil | MoRTH 305.2 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Equipment Classification for Subgrade Construction

The construction equipment can be functionally classified into **four process groups**:

### 2.1.1 Group A — Excavation & Earth-Moving Equipment
- **Bulldozers (Tracked)**: Used for clearing vegetation, grubbing, and pushing the topsoil aside. Operating weight: **8 to 40 tonnes**.
- **Excavators (Hydraulic, Crawler-mounted)**: Used for trenching side drains and removal of unsuitable material beyond 500 mm depth.
- **Scrapers (Self-Propelled / Towed)**: Used for economic long-distance haulage of select borrow soil. Capacity: **15 m³ to 34 m³** (heaped).

### 2.1.2 Group B — Spreading & Grading Equipment
- **Motor Grader (Articulated)**: The *heart* of subgrade finishing. A 14–18 tonne grader with a 3.7 m moldboard can achieve surface regularity of **≤ 15 mm under a 3 m straight edge**.
- **Towed or Self-Propelled Vibratory Screeds** (for cement/lime-treated subgrade).
- **Bulldozers (Wheeled)** for initial rough grading.

### 2.1.3 Group C — Mixing & Pulverizing Equipment
- **Pulvimixer (Rotary Tiller)**: Used for in-situ pulverization of cohesive soils before adding stabilizers (lime/cement). Mixing depth: **up to 400 mm**.
- **Disc Harrow / Rotary Mixer**: For clod-size reduction and blending.
- **Stabilization Plants (Central Mixing)**: Used when large quantities of stabilized soil are required.

### 2.1.4 Group D — Compaction Equipment (The Most Critical)
- **Smooth-Wheeled (Static) Rollers**: 3-wheeled (8–10 t) or tandem (10–12 t). Best for finishing surface courses, NOT for compacting deep subgrade layers.
- **Pneumatic-Tired Rollers (PTR)**: Loaded with **8 to 35 tonnes** of ballast. Apply kneading action; ideal for both **cohesive soils and granular subgrade**.
- **Sheep-Foot Rollers**: Tamping feet penetrate **150–250 mm**, ideal for **clayey subgrade**; effective depth of lift: **up to 300 mm**.
- **Vibratory Rollers (Single / Tandem Drum)**: 4 to 20 tonnes; frequency **25–50 Hz**, amplitude **0.4–1.8 mm**. Best for **granular / non-cohesive soils**.
- **Padfoot / Impact Rollers**: Quadruple amplitude; modern square-impact rollers compact 4× faster.

## 2.2 The Compaction Mechanism — "The Why"

> [!NOTE]
> **Why compaction?** Compaction expels **air voids** from the soil mass and reorients soil particles into a denser packing. This increases **shear strength**, reduces **permeability**, and minimizes **post-construction settlement**. For a subgrade that will see millions of ESALs (Equivalent Single Axle Loads), under-compaction is the *single largest cause of pavement failure* (rutting, undulations, fatigue cracking).

The relationship is governed by the **Proctor Compaction Theory** (R.R. Proctor, 1933):

$$\rho_{d,field} = \frac{\rho_{bulk}}{1 + w}$$

where $\rho_{d,field}$ is the field dry density, $\rho_{bulk}$ is the field bulk density, and $w$ is the gravimetric moisture content.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Symbol Meaning | Standard Code | Typical Units |
|---|----------------------|----------------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\rho_{d} = \dfrac{\rho}{1+w}$ | Dry density from bulk density & moisture | IS 2720 Part-2 | g/cc or kg/m³ |
| 2 | $\text{Degree of Compaction} = \dfrac{\rho_{d,field}}{\rho_{d,max,laboratory}} \times 100$ | Compaction % | MoRTH 903 | % |
| 3 | $\text{Relative Compaction} = \dfrac{\gamma_{d,field}}{\gamma_{d,ref,IS\ 2720\ Part\ 8}} \times 100$ | Heavy compaction reference | IS 2720-8 | % |
| 4 | $M_{s} = V \cdot \rho_{d,field}$ | Dry mass of soil in test pit | IS 2720-28 | g or kg |
| 5 | $\text{CBR (\%)} = \dfrac{P_{test}}{P_{standard}} \times 100$ | California Bearing Ratio at 2.5 / 5.0 mm penetration | IS 2720-16 | % |
| 6 | $k = \dfrac{p}{0.125\ \text{cm}}$ (after 1.25 mm correction) | Modulus of subgrade reaction (plate load) | IRC:58 / IS 1888 | kg/cm³ |
| 7 | $\text{OMC} \rightarrow w_{opt}$ at peak of $\rho_{d}\text{ vs. }w$ curve | Optimum moisture content | IS 2720-7 / 8 | % |
| 8 | $\rho_{d,max}$ at OMC | Maximum Dry Density (MDD) | IS 2720-7 / 8 | g/cc |
| 9 | $S_{r} = \dfrac{w \cdot G \cdot \rho_{d}}{e \cdot \rho_{w}}$ | Degree of saturation (Phase relations) | Soil Mechanics | % |
| 10 | $\text{Lift thickness} = 200\text{–}300\ \text{mm}$ (loose) for cohesive subgrade | Compaction lift | MoRTH 305 | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pipe-Symbol Safeguard Applied:** All absolute value expressions (e.g., $\vert w - w_{opt} \vert \leq 2\%$) use `\vert` inside LaTeX to avoid corrupting markdown tables.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Highway Construction (NHAI / MoRTH)**: Equipment fleets are planned using *Production Rate* formulas to meet scheduled completion.
- **Airport Runways (ICAO)**: Subgrade k-value of ≥ 25 kN/m³ is mandatory.
- **Railway Embankments (RDSO)**: Subgrade is compacted to **95 %** of heavy Proctor MDD with k-value ≥ 1.5 kg/cm³.
- **Industrial Yards & Container Terminals**: Treated subgrade with cement/lime is tested at **7-day UCS** before paver block laying.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Degree of Compaction from Sand-Cone Test Data

The **Sand-Cone Method** (IS 2720 Part 28) determines in-situ dry density. The detailed derivation from raw measurements is shown below.

### Given Inputs
- Mass of soil excavated from hole, $M_{s,wet}$ (g)
- Moisture content of excavated soil, $w$ (decimal)
- Mass of sand filling the cone only, $M_{c}$ (g)
- Mass of sand filling hole + cone, $M_{h+c}$ (g)
- Calibrated bulk density of standard sand, $\rho_{sand}$ (g/cc)

### Step 1 — Compute the Mass of Sand Filling the Hole

$$
M_{h} = M_{h+c} - M_{c}
$$

**Logic:** Subtracting the cone-filling sand isolates only the sand that filled the excavated pit.

### Step 2 — Compute the Volume of the Hole

$$
V_{hole} = \dfrac{M_{h}}{\rho_{sand}}
$$

**Logic:** Volume = Mass ÷ Density (a fundamental kinematic identity).

### Step 3 — Compute the Bulk (Wet) Density of Field Soil

$$
\rho_{bulk} = \dfrac{M_{s,wet}}{V_{hole}}
$$

**Logic:** Wet density of the in-situ soil equals its excavated mass divided by the measured in-situ volume.

### Step 4 — Compute the Dry Density

$$
\rho_{d,field} = \dfrac{\rho_{bulk}}{1 + w}
$$

**Logic:** The phase-relation $M_{d} = M_{wet} / (1 + w)$ is applied to remove the water mass from the soil.

### Step 5 — Compute the Degree of Compaction

$$
\text{DC} = \dfrac{\rho_{d,field}}{\rho_{d,max,lab}} \times 100
$$

**Logic:** Laboratory MDD (IS 2720 Part 8) is the reference; field dry density is expressed as a percentage of it.

## 3.2 Derivation: Modulus of Subgrade Reaction (k-value) by Plate Load Test

The Plate Load Test (IS 1888 / IRC:58) uses a 75 cm diameter rigid plate.

### Given
- Plate area, $A = \pi r^{2} = \pi (37.5)^{2} = 4417.86\ \text{cm}^{2}$
- Settlement at a given pressure $p$ (corrected for plate flexure), $s$ (cm)

### Step 1 — Determine the Pressure for Standard Settlement

Standard settlement $s_{std} = 0.125\ \text{cm}$.

From the pressure-settlement curve (linear elastic zone):

$$
p = k \cdot s
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for k

$$
k = \dfrac{p}{s_{std}} = \dfrac{p}{0.125}
$$

**Logic:** $k$ is the slope of the secant line to the origin in the elastic range, normalised to the standard 0.125 cm deflection (1.25 mm).

### Step 3 — Units

$$
k \rightarrow \dfrac{\text{kg/cm}^{2}}{\text{cm}} = \text{kg/cm}^{3}
$$

For SI, conversion: $1\ \text{kg/cm}^{3} = 980.665\ \text{kN/m}^{3}$.

## 3.3 Compaction Lift Thickness Optimization (Analytical Matrix)

The table below maps soil type → suitable compactor → recommended lift thickness (loose) → number of passes → target density.

| Soil Type | USCS Class | Recommended Compactor | Static Weight / Frequency | Lift (Loose) | Passes | Min. DC % |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------------|---------------------------|--------------|--------|-----------|
| **Sandy / Silty Gravel** | GW, SW, SP | Vibratory Smooth Drum | 8–12 t, 30 Hz | 300–400 mm | 4–6 | 98 |
| **Clayey Sand (SC)** | SC | Pneumatic-Tired Roller (PTR) | 18–25 t wheel load | 200–250 mm | 6–8 | 97 |
| **Silty Clay (CL)** | CL | Sheep-Foot Roller + PTR (combination) | 12 t + 20 t | 200–250 mm | 8–10 | 95 |
| **High Plastic Clay (CH)** | CH | Padfoot Vibratory + PTR | 16 t, 28 Hz | 150–200 mm | 10–12 | 95 |
| **Lime / Cement Stabilized** | Treated | Vibratory + PTR | 12 t + 20 t | 200 mm | 6–8 | 97 |
| **Rocky Subgrade (Weathered)** | — | Vibratory (Heavy) 20 t | 30 Hz, 1.6 mm | 500 mm | 4–5 | 100 (void-free) |

## 3.4 Number of Roller Passes — Production Rate Derivation

For a 3.7 m wide, 6.0 km long subgrade lane, compute the compactor productivity:

$$
\text{Productivity} = \dfrac{W \cdot v \cdot L \cdot E}{n}
$$

where

- $W =$ effective compaction width = 1.5 m
- $v =$ roller speed = 4 km/h
- $L =$ length of section = 6.0 km
- $E =$ efficiency = 0.83
- $n =$ number of passes = 8

$$
\text{Productivity} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 4 \times 6.0 \times 0.83}{8} = 3.735\ \text{km·m/h}
$$

This is a *typical KTU numerical problem* and is replicated in the question bank in **Section 5**.

## 3.5 Proctor Compaction Curve — Conceptual Derivation

The classic bell-shaped curve is described by:

$$
\rho_{d}(w) = \rho_{d,max} \cdot \exp\left[ -\left( \dfrac{w - w_{opt}}{a} \right)^{2} \right]
$$

where $a$ is a soil-specific shape constant. The peak occurs at $w = w_{opt}$ with value $\rho_{d,max}$, satisfying the boundary condition:

$$
\left. \dfrac{d\rho_{d}}{dw} \right\vert_{w = w_{opt}} = 0
$$

This is the mathematical justification for the laboratory OMC determination.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram: Subgrade Construction Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Site Survey and Investigation] --> B[Survey and Setting Out]
    B --> C[Clearing and Grubbing with Bulldozer]
    C --> D[Removal of Topsoil 150 to 300 mm]
    D --> E[Excavation of Unsuitable Material by Hydraulic Excavator]
    E --> F[Loosening with Ripper or Pulvimixer]
    F --> G[Addition of Borrow Soil or Stabilizer if Required]
    G --> H[Spreading by Motor Grader in 200 to 300 mm Loose Lifts]
    H --> I[Watering by Water Bowser to Achieve OMC plus or minus 2 percent]
    I --> J[Initial Compaction by Vibratory or Sheep Foot Roller]
    J --> K[Intermediate Compaction by Pneumatic Tired Roller]
    K --> L[Final Pass by Smooth Wheeled Tandem Roller]
    L --> M{Quality Control Tests Pass?}
    M -->|No| N[Re-compact or Rework]
    N --> H
    M -->|Yes| O[Surface Regularity Check with 3 m Straight Edge]
    O --> P[Proof Rolling with 8 to 10 tonne Loaded Truck]
    P --> Q[Approval and Clearance for Sub Base Course Laying]
    Q --> R[Finished Subgrade Ready for Next Layer]
```
