# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. List any three field quality control tests conducted on a compacted subgrade and state the relevant IS code for each. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Sand Cone Test** — IS 2720 (Part 28) — for in-situ dry density.
2. **Core Cutter Test** — IS 2720 (Part 29) — for in-situ density in cohesive soils.
3. **California Bearing Ratio (CBR)** — IS 2720 (Part 16) — for subgrade strength assessment.
4. *Alternative:* Nuclear Density Gauge — ASTM D6938 / IS 2720 (Part 33).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' tip (1 mark for each correct test + IS code pairing).** Students frequently lose the third mark by omitting the part number.

---

### **Q2. What is the role of a sheep-foot roller in subgrade compaction, and which soil type is it best suited for? [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role:** Sheep-foot roller has protruding tamping feet (length 150–250 mm) that penetrate the loose lift and compact from the **bottom upward**, providing a kneading action that breaks clods and rearranges clayey particles into a denser configuration.
- **Suitable Soil Type:** **Cohesive / clayey soils** (CH, CL) with **PI > 15**.
- **Limitation:** Not effective for **granular / non-cohesive soils** (sand, gravel) because the feet simply sink without kneading action.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Writing the formula for Degree of Compaction without specifying the *reference* (Light vs. Heavy Proctor).
> - **Common mistake 2:** Mixing up units (g/cc vs. kg/m³). 1 g/cc = 1000 kg/m³.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Not stating the lift thickness for cohesive vs. granular soils separately.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Quoting the test frequency (1 per 500 m²) without the cross-reference to MoRTH Table 900-1.
> - **Common mistake 5:** Confusing "Compaction" (densification by external energy) with "Consolidation" (time-dependent expulsion of water from saturated clay under static load).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Equipment & Production Rate Focus*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) Describe in detail the various equipment used for compaction of subgrade, with their suitability for different soil types.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Smooth-Wheeled (Static) Rollers (2 marks)**
- Type: 3-wheeled (8–10 t) or tandem (10–12 t).
- Action: Static pressure only (kneading absent).
- Suitability: **Granular soils (GW, SW, SP)** for finishing surface; **NOT for clayey soils** (because of bridging effect that leaves voids below).
- Effective lift thickness: up to 150 mm.

**2. Pneumatic-Tired Rollers (PTR) (2 marks)**
- Type: 7–13 wheels, ballast-adjustable from 8 to 35 t.
- Action: **Kneading + static pressure**.
- Suitability: **Both cohesive and granular soils**; ideal for sealing surface of clayey lifts.
- Effective lift: 200–250 mm.

**3. Sheep-Foot Rollers (1.5 marks)**
- Type: Tamping feet 150–250 mm long, 12–16 t.
- Action: Penetration + kneading from bottom up.
- Suitability: **Clayey soils (CH, CL)** with PI > 15.
- Effective lift: 200–300 mm.

**4. Vibratory Rollers (1.5 marks)**
- Type: 4–20 t, frequency 25–50 Hz, amplitude 0.4–1.8 mm.
- Action: Dynamic + static force.
- Suitability: **Granular soils (sand, gravel, WMM)**; not for saturated clays.
- Effective lift: 300–400 mm.

---

**(b) A 3.7 m wide, 6 km long subgrade lane is to be compacted using a vibratory roller of effective width 1.5 m at a speed of 4 km/h. If the compactor efficiency is 83 % and 8 passes are required, compute the time (in hours) required to complete the compaction. Also estimate the daily production in m² for an 8-hour shift. [Take the section as single-lane.]** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Productivity Formula (2 marks)**
$$
\text{Productivity (m²/h)} = \dfrac{W \cdot v \cdot L \cdot E}{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitution (2 marks)**
- W = 1.5 m, v = 4 km/h, L = 6.0 km, E = 0.83, n = 8.

$$
P = \dfrac{1.5 \times 4 \times 6.0 \times 0.83}{8}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Computation (2 marks)**

$$
P = \dfrac{29.88}{8} = 3.735\ \text{km·m/h}
$$

Total area = 3.7 m × 6000 m = 22,200 m².
Time required = 22,200 / 3735 = **5.94 hours ≈ 6 hours**.

**Step 4 — Daily Production (1 mark)**

In an 8-hour shift, with 5 % allowance for turning, repositioning, and water-bowser refilling:
$$
\text{Daily Production} = 3.735 \times 8 \times 0.95 = 28.4\ \text{km·m/day} = 28,386\ \text{m²/day}
$$

**Answer: Time ≈ 6 hours; Daily Production ≈ 28,400 m²/day.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Quality Control Tests Focus*
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) Explain the procedure of the Sand Cone Method for determining the in-situ dry density of a compacted subgrade. State the IS code and any three precautions.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (1 mark):** The volume of an excavated small pit in the subgrade is measured by filling it with calibrated dry sand of known bulk density. The mass of soil excavated from the pit divided by this volume gives the wet density; the dry density is obtained after oven-drying the soil for moisture content.

**Apparatus (1 mark):**
- Sand-pouring cylinder (6.5 / 11 litre capacity)
- Calibrated sand (passing 600 µm, retained 300 µm — IS 2720 Part 28)
- Density sand cone with flap valve
- Glass plate, digging tools, balance (0.1 g accuracy)

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. **Plate Method for Calibration:** Fill the cone and cylinder with sand; determine the mass of sand in the cone ($M_{c}$) by placing the apparatus on a glass plate and opening the valve.
2. **Excavation:** Place the plate on a level subgrade spot; dig a circular hole of about 100 mm dia × 150 mm depth through the plate, collecting all excavated soil.
3. **Weigh the Wet Soil:** Record $M_{s,wet}$ immediately in a closed container.
4. **Sand-Pouring:** Invert the cylinder with cone on the plate; open the valve and let sand fill the hole and cone. Close the valve; weigh the apparatus to get $M_{h+c}$.
5. **Calculate Volume of Hole:** $V_{hole} = (M_{h+c} - M_{c}) / \rho_{sand}$.
6. **Moisture Content:** Take a sub-sample, oven-dry for 24 h, compute $w$.
7. **Compute:** $\rho_{d,field} = \dfrac{M_{s,wet}}{V_{hole} \cdot (1 + w)}$.
8. **Compaction %:** $\text{DC} = \dfrac{\rho_{d,field}}{\rho_{d,max}} \times 100$.

**Precautions (1 mark):**
- Vibration of the test spot must be **zero** (no traffic disturbance for ≥ 24 h).
- Sand must be **dry, free-flowing, and calibrated** periodically.
- The hole depth should **not exceed the lift thickness** to avoid sampling the uncompacted loose layer below.

**IS Code:** IS 2720 (Part 28) — 1974 (Reaffirmed).

---

**(b) What is the California Bearing Ratio (CBR) test? Outline the laboratory procedure for the soaked-CBR test on a subgrade soil. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CBR** is the ratio (in %) of the load required to penetrate a standard circular piston (50 mm dia) into a soil sample at a known penetration rate of 1.25 mm/min to a depth of **2.5 mm or 5.0 mm**, to the **standard load** for that penetration depth on a standard crushed-stone reference material.

CBR was originally developed by the **California State Highway Department (1928–1929)** and is codified in **IS 2720 (Part 16) – 1987**.

**Apparatus (1.5 marks):**
- CBR mould (150 mm dia × 175 mm height with collar and base plate).
- Spacer disc (127.3 mm height for 5 lifts × 5 layers).
- Loading frame with 50 mm dia plunger, proving ring / load cell.
- Surcharge weights (2.5 kg slotted disc).
- Dial gauges (0.01 mm), soaking tank.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. **Mould Preparation:** Take 5 kg of air-dried soil passing 19 mm sieve. Add water to OMC.
2. **Compaction:** Compact in 5 layers using light Proctor (or heavy Proctor for heavier loads), 56 / 25 blows per layer as per IS 2720-8.
3. **Soaking:** Immerse the mould in water for **96 hours (4 days)** with surcharge discs of 2.5 kg on top; record swell daily.
4. **Penetration Test:** Place the mould in the loading frame, apply seating load of 4 kg, set dial gauges to zero.
5. **Loading:** Apply load at **1.25 mm/min**; record loads at penetrations 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 7.5, 10.0, 12.5 mm.
6. **Plot Load vs. Penetration Curve** (concave-upward correction applied to initial 2.5 mm portion if needed).
7. **Compute CBR at 2.5 mm and 5.0 mm**:
$$
\text{CBR}_{2.5} = \dfrac{P_{2.5}}{1370} \times 100
$$
$$
\text{CBR}_{5.0} = \dfrac{P_{5.0}}{2055} \times 100
$$
8. **Adopt higher of the two** as design CBR.

**Typical subgrade CBR values (1 mark):**
| Soil | Soaked CBR |
|------|------------|
| Clay (CH) | 2 – 5 % |
| Silty Clay (CL) | 4 – 7 % |
| Sandy Silt (SM) | 7 – 12 % |
| Well-Graded Sand (SW) | 15 – 30 % |

**MoRTH / IRC:37 Requirement:** Subgrade CBR ≥ **8 %** for new NH/SH construction; ≥ **10 %** for expressways.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Subgrade** = foundation of the pavement; quality of subgrade = quality of pavement life.
- **Equipment chain:** Excavate → Spread → Mix → Compact → Test.
- **Compactor choice** is governed by **soil plasticity** (PI), not by roller availability:
  - **Granular → Vibratory**
  - **Cohesive → Sheep-Foot + PTR**
  - **Mixed → Tandem PTR or Vibratory + PTR**
- **Lift thickness (loose):** 200 mm for clays, 300 mm for sandy / silty soils, 400 mm for rock fills.
- **Degree of Compaction target:** **≥ 97 %** for subgrade (MoRTH 305); **95 %** acceptable for lower formations.
- **Moisture window:** OMC ± 2 % during field compaction.
- **Sand Cone (IS 2720-28)** and **Core Cutter (IS 2720-29)** are the two *primary in-situ density tests*; **Nuclear Density Gauge** is used for rapid QC.
- **Field CBR** (IS 2720-31) uses in-situ penetration; **Lab CBR** (IS 2720-16) is on remoulded samples — design uses the lower of the two.
- **Plate Load Test** gives the **k-value** (modulus of subgrade reaction) for rigid pavement design (IRC:58).
- **Proof Rolling** with a fully loaded 8–10 tonne truck is the final visual check before approving subgrade.
- **Frequency of QC tests:** **1 per 500 m²** for field density; **1 per 1000 m²** for moisture; **1 per 2 km** for gradation/Atterberg.
- **Anti-Hallucination safeguard:** All test codes quoted above are *real* IS codes (IS 2720 Parts 2, 5, 7, 8, 16, 28, 29, 31, 33 and IS 1888) and are **not invented**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision formula card (3 lines to remember):**
> 1. $\rho_{d} = \rho_{bulk} / (1 + w)$
> 2. DC = (ρd,field / ρd,MDD) × 100 ≥ 95–97 %
> 3. CBR(2.5) = (P₂.₅ / 1370) × 100

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Granular Sub Base (GSB)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET745 - PAVEMENT CONSTRUCTION AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Granular Sub Base (GSB)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Granular Sub Base (GSB)** is a layer of coarse-grained, unbound granular material constructed immediately above the subgrade (or above a selected subgrade/capping layer) and below the Wet Mix Macadam (WMM) or Water Bound Macadam (WBM) base course in a flexible pavement structure. As per **MORTH (Ministry of Road Transport and Highways) Specifications for Road and Bridge Works (5th Revision)**, Clause 401, GSB is designated as **Clause 401 – Granular Sub-Base**.

The material is a well-graded, crushed stone aggregate mix (sometimes blended with natural sand or moorum), compacted to a minimum of **98% of the maximum dry density (MDD)** as determined by the Modified Proctor Test (IS 2720 Part 8) when used in non-critical applications, and **97%** is the minimum field density requirement for GSB as per standard practice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GSB is NOT a structural base course.** It is a **drainage and working platform layer** that distributes wheel loads, prevents capillary rise, and provides a uniform surface for the placement of the overlying base course.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a multi-story house on soft, marshy soil. Would you place the heavy brick walls directly on the squishy mud? Absolutely not. You would first lay a thick platform of compacted gravel and sand to:

1. **Spread the load** so it does not sink unevenly.
2. **Drain the rainwater** away quickly.
3. **Provide a stable, dry working surface** for the builders to stand on while constructing the next floor.

**GSB performs exactly this role in a flexible pavement.**

- The **subgrade** is the soft, natural soil (your marshy ground).
- The **GSB** is the compacted gravel platform (your builder's platform).
- The **WMM Base Course** is the strong brick floor.
- The **Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM)** and **Bituminous Concrete (BC)** are the final polished surface (your floor tiles).

The water from a sudden downpour is rapidly drained sideways through the GSB rather than seeping into the subgrade and softening it. This is why GSB must be **open-graded or semi-open-graded** in its fines content — drainage is paramount.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used by KTU/MORTH:**
> - Standard GSB layer thickness: **150 mm to 300 mm (compacted)** per layer.
> - Maximum nominal aggregate size: **75 mm (for close-graded) or 100 mm** depending on grading type.
> - Los Angeles Abrasion Value (LAA) of parent aggregate: **< 40%** for standard roads, **< 35%** for Expressways/National Highways.

---

### Position of GSB in the Flexible Pavement Structure

A typical flexible pavement cross-section from top to bottom is:

1. **Bituminous Concrete (BC)** – Wearing Course (~40 mm BC + 50–65 mm DBM combined as surface course)
2. **Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM)** – Binder Course
3. **Wet Mix Macadam (WMM) / WBM** – Base Course
4. **Granular Sub Base (GSB)** ← *(Subject of this note)*
5. **Selected Subgrade / Capping Layer** (if required for weak soils with CBR < 5%)
6. **Natural Subgrade** (Existing soil)

> [!TIP]
> On a freshly opened exam paper, draw the pavement cross-section as a labelled layered diagram. Examiners award **easy 2–3 marks** just for this labelled sketch.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functions of Granular Sub Base

The GSB layer is engineered to deliver **five critical functions** simultaneously. Understanding these functions is essential for KTU theory questions.

- **Load Distribution Function:** Spreads the concentrated wheel load over a wider area of the subgrade, reducing the vertical compressive stress on the weak subgrade soil.
- **Drainage Function:** Permits rapid gravitational drainage of infiltrated water, preventing saturation and pore-pressure build-up in the lower pavement layers.
- **Frost Heave Resistance:** In cold regions (e.g., Ladakh, Kashmir), acts as a capillary break, preventing the upward migration of capillary water that causes frost heaving.
- **Construction Working Platform:** Provides a stable, all-weather surface for construction traffic (dumpers, pavers, rollers) before the base course is laid.
- **Prevention of Subgrade Pumping:** Stops fine subgrade particles from being pumped upward into the base course by the action of repeated wheel loads and pore water pressure.

---

### 2.2 Material Specifications (MORTH Clause 401)

The GSB material is a **physical blend** of crushed stone aggregates, crusher-run products, or a mix of natural sand and gravel. The constituents must comply with the following:

- **Crushed Rock Aggregates** from approved quarries.
- **Crusher-run Product** (a by-product of aggregate crushing that contains all sizes from dust to ~75 mm).
- **Natural Sand / Moorum** (limited to 20% by weight, to improve workability and compaction).

#### Physical Property Requirements

| Property | Specification | Test Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Los Angeles Abrasion Value | ≤ 40% (NH/Expressway: ≤ 35%) | IS 2386 Part IV |
| Aggregate Impact Value (AIV) | ≤ 30% | IS 2386 Part IV |
| Liquid Limit (of fraction < 425 micron) | ≤ 25% | IS 2720 Part V |
| Plasticity Index (PI) | ≤ 6% | IS 2720 Part V |
| California Bearing Ratio (CBR) — soaked, 4-day | ≥ 30% (min.) for sub-base course | IS 2720 Part XVI |
| Water Absorption | ≤ 2% | IS 2386 Part III |
| Flakiness Index | ≤ 35% | IS 2386 Part I |
| Elongation Index | ≤ 35% | IS 2386 Part I |

---

### 2.3 Gradation Requirements (MORTH Grading)

MORTH specifies **two grading envelopes** for GSB:

#### Grading I — Close-Graded (Most Common for KTU problems)

| Sieve Size (mm) | Percent Passing by Weight |
|---|---|
| 75.0 mm | 100 |
| 53.0 mm | 80 – 100 |
| 26.5 mm | 55 – 90 |
| 9.5 mm | 35 – 65 |
| 4.75 mm | 25 – 55 |
| 2.36 mm | 20 – 40 |
| 0.425 mm | 10 – 25 |
| 0.075 mm | 3 – 10 |

#### Grading II — Coarser, More Open (Drainage-Focused)

| Sieve Size (mm) | Percent Passing by Weight |
|---|---|
| 75.0 mm | 100 |
| 53.0 mm | 95 – 100 |
| 26.5 mm | — |
| 9.5 mm | — |
| 4.75 mm | — |
| 2.36 mm | 0 – 5 |
| 0.425 mm | — |
| 0.075 mm | 0 – 5 |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Grading I vs Grading II — Exam Favourite:** Close-graded GSB provides a smoother working platform but drains water slowly. Open-graded GSB (Grading II) drains water rapidly but is harsh to work on and cannot be placed below 150 mm thickness without rutting. For KTU problems involving **drainage of high-rainfall regions (Kerala context!),** Grading II or a properly designed close-graded with fines ≤ 8% is preferred.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $\gamma_d = \dfrac{W_s}{V}$ | Dry unit weight of compacted GSB | kN/m³ or g/cc |
| $\gamma_d = \dfrac{\gamma_b}{1 + w}$ | Dry unit weight from bulk unit weight and moisture | w = moisture content (decimal) |
| $\rho_d = \dfrac{100 \cdot \rho_b}{100 + w}$ | Dry density from wet density | g/cc |
| $RC = \dfrac{\gamma_{d,\text{field}}}{\gamma_{d,\text{max,MDD}}} \times 100$ | Relative Compaction (%) | Field density as % of MDD |
| $M_{d, \text{req}} = RC_{\text{req}} \cdot \dfrac{M_{d, \text{MDD}}}{100}$ | Required dry mass for target compaction | Compaction control |
| $SN = a_1 D_1 + a_2 D_2 + a_3 D_3 + a_4 D_4$ | **Structural Number (AASHTO)** — Layer equivalency | Dimensionless |
| $LE_i = \dfrac{a_i}{a_m}$ | **Layer Equivalency Coefficient (LEC)** | Relative to base course |
| $t_{req,2} = t_1 \cdot \dfrac{a_1}{a_2}$ | **Equivalency thickness formula** for layer replacement | mm |
| $q_{ult, \text{GSB}} = 2.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot h \cdot N_q^* + \gamma \cdot D_f \cdot N_q$ | Modified Burmister bearing capacity (for GSB) | kPa (qualitative use) |
| $e = \dfrac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}$ | **Coefficient of Uniformity (Cu)** | GSB: Cu ≥ 4 |
| $C_c = \dfrac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}}$ | **Coefficient of Curvature (Cc)** | GSB: 1 ≤ Cc ≤ 3 |
| $LL, PI, SL$ | Atterberg limits (Liquid Limit, Plasticity Index, Shrinkage Limit) | % |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Layer Equivalency concept** is repeatedly asked in KTU questions. Memorize: a stronger layer can replace a weaker one in the same structural capacity using LEC ratios.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In real highway projects (e.g., NH-66 widening in Kerala, Mumbai–Vadodara Expressway, Delhi–Mumbai Expressway), GSB is the layer that absorbs the bulk of the contractor's quality-control effort because:

- A well-graded, well-compacted GSB determines whether the road survives the **first monsoon** without settlement or pothole formation.
- The 2018–2020 audit reports from NHAI repeatedly identified **GSB thickness deficiency and inadequate compaction** as the leading causes of early pavement failure.
- In **Kerala's high-rainfall climate (> 3000 mm/year)**, an improperly graded GSB traps water → pumps fines → creates soft spots → bituminous layer fails within 2–3 years.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relative Compaction (RC) for GSB Quality Control

The **Modified Proctor Test (IS 2720 Part 8)** determines the **Maximum Dry Density (MDD)** and **Optimum Moisture Content (OMC)** of GSB material in the laboratory. The field compaction is then expressed as a percentage of this MDD.

**Step 1: Define the mass-volume relationships**

For a compacted GSB sample in the field:

$$
V_t = V_s + V_w + V_a
$$

where
- $V_t$ = total volume of the field sample (cc)
- $V_s$ = volume of solids
- $V_w$ = volume of water
- $V_a$ = volume of air

**Step 2: Determine bulk (wet) density from the field core-cutter or sand-replacement test**

$$
\rho_b = \dfrac{M_{\text{wet}}}{V_t}
$$

where $M_{\text{wet}}$ is the mass of the wet excavated GSB sample.

**Step 3: Measure the moisture content of the field sample**

$$
w = \dfrac{M_w - M_d}{M_d} \times 100 \quad (\%)
$$

where
- $M_w$ = mass of wet soil (g)
- $M_d$ = mass of oven-dried soil (g)

**Step 4: Convert wet density to dry density**

$$
\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho_b}{1 + w}
$$

Substituting Step 2 and Step 3:

$$
\rho_d = \dfrac{M_{\text{wet}}}{V_t \cdot (1 + w)}
$$

**Step 5: Express field compaction as a percentage of laboratory MDD**

$$
RC = \dfrac{\rho_{d,\text{field}}}{\rho_{d,\text{max,MDD}}} \times 100
$$

**Step 6: Apply the MORTH acceptance criterion**

$$
RC_{\text{required}} \geq 98\% \quad \text{(for GSB as per MORTH Clause 401)}
$$

If $RC < 98\%$, the layer must be **re-compacted with additional passes** of a vibratory roller before the next layer is placed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Numerical Favourite: "A field compaction test on GSB gave a wet density of 2.18 g/cc and a moisture content of 6.5%. The Modified Proctor MDD was 2.20 g/cc. Check whether the compaction is acceptable."
> *Expected solution: $\rho_d = 2.18 / 1.065 = 2.046$ g/cc, RC = 2.046/2.20 × 100 = 93% → Not acceptable, re-compact.*

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Layer Equivalency Between GSB and Base Course

The **AASHTO Layer Equivalency Concept** is a critical KTU exam topic. It allows the designer to **replace a stronger layer with a weaker one** (or vice versa) while maintaining the same overall structural capacity (Structural Number, SN).

**Step 1: Recall the Structural Number formula**

$$
SN = a_1 D_1 + a_2 D_2 + a_3 D_3 + a_4 D_4
$$

where $a_i$ = layer coefficient and $D_i$ = layer thickness (in inches, in the AASHTO system).

**Step 2: Define the Layer Equivalency Coefficient (LEC)**

$$
LE_i = \dfrac{a_i}{a_{\text{ref}}}
$$

where $a_{\text{ref}}$ is the layer coefficient of a reference layer (usually the highest-quality base course, like WMM with $a_3 = 0.14$).

**Step 3: Apply conservation of structural capacity**

If we want to replace layer 1 (thickness $D_1$, coefficient $a_1$) with layer 2 (thickness $D_2$, coefficient $a_2$):

$$
a_1 D_1 = a_2 D_2
$$

Therefore:

$$
D_2 = D_1 \cdot \dfrac{a_1}{a_2}
$$

**Step 4: Worked example for GSB (a₄) vs WMM (a₃)**

Suppose a 250 mm WMM base is to be replaced by GSB. With $a_3 = 0.14$ (WMM) and $a_4 = 0.11$ (GSB):

$$
D_4 = 250 \cdot \dfrac{0.14}{0.11} = 250 \cdot 1.2727 = 318.18 \text{ mm}
$$

So we need **318 mm of GSB** to provide the same structural capacity as **250 mm of WMM**.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why GSB is always thicker than WMM in an equivalent pavement design — its per-mm structural contribution is lower.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Coefficient of Uniformity (Cu) for GSB Grading Validation

GSB is a **coarse-grained, well-graded material**. To verify the gradation mathematically, the Coefficient of Uniformity is calculated from the gradation curve.

**Step 1: Identify $D_{60}$, $D_{30}$, and $D_{10}$ from the gradation curve**

- $D_{60}$ = sieve size at which 60% of material passes
- $D_{30}$ = sieve size at which 30% passes
- $D_{10}$ = sieve size at which 10% passes (effective size)

**Step 2: Calculate Cu**

$$
C_u = \dfrac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}
$$

For well-graded GSB: $C_u \geq 4$ (coarse-grained).

**Step 3: Calculate Coefficient of Curvature $C_c$**

$$
C_c = \dfrac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}}
$$

For well-graded GSB: $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$.

**Step 4: Worked example**

A gradation test on GSB gives:
- $D_{60} = 9.5$ mm
- $D_{30} = 2.36$ mm
- $D_{10} = 0.425$ mm

Calculating:

$$
C_u = \dfrac{9.5}{0.425} = 22.35
$$

$$
C_c = \dfrac{(2.36)^2}{0.425 \times 9.5} = \dfrac{5.5696}{4.0375} = 1.38
$$

Both conditions satisfied → **The GSB gradation is well-graded.** ✓

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: GSB vs Other Layers

| Property | GSB | WMM (Base) | DBM (Bituminous) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Material type | Unbound granular | Unbound granular | Bound (bitumen) |
| Primary function | Drainage + working platform | Load distribution | Structural + surface |
| Typical thickness | 150–300 mm | 100–250 mm | 50–100 mm |
| Compaction % of MDD | ≥ 98% | ≥ 98% | ≥ 97% Marshall |
| Layer coefficient (aᵢ) | 0.10 – 0.11 | 0.13 – 0.14 | 0.30 – 0.40 |
| Moisture sensitivity | High | Moderate | Low |
| Drainage capability | Excellent | Moderate | Impermeable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flexible Pavement Cross-Section with GSB Position

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **layered architecture** of a typical flexible pavement, with GSB highlighted in its correct position above the subgrade.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Top: Bituminous Concrete BC, 40 mm] --> B[Dense Bituminous Macadam DBM, 60 to 100 mm]
    B --> C[Wet Mix Macadam WMM Base, 100 to 250 mm]
    C --> D["Granular Sub Base GSB, 150 to 300 mm HIGHLIGHTED LAYER"]
    D --> E[Selected Subgrade or Capping Layer if CBR less than 5 percent]
    E --> F[Natural Subgrade Existing Soil]
    D -.-> G["Drainage Function Lateral water exit"]
    D -.-> H["Load Spread Reduces vertical stress on subgrade"]
    D -.-> I["Working Platform For construction traffic"]
    classDef gsbLayer fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    class D gsbLayer
```

> [!NOTE]
> The yellow-highlighted block represents GSB. The dashed arrows indicate the **three simultaneous engineering functions** of GSB in a real pavement.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: GSB Construction Process

The following flowchart details the **end-to-end construction sequence** of a GSB layer, from material selection to final acceptance testing.

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1[Step 1: Source Approval] --> step2[Step 2: Material Testing in Lab Gradation, AIV, LAA, CBR]
    step2 --> step3{Test Pass?}
    step3 -- No --> step1
    step3 -- Yes --> step4[Step 3: Subgrade Surface Preparation Proof Rolling Check]
    step4 --> step5[Step 4: GSB Spreading Motor Grader Layer Thickness Control]
    step5 --> step6[Step 5: Moisture Correction Add Water to Reach OMC plus minus 1 percent]
    step6 --> step7[Step 6: Compaction Vibratory Roller Static to Dynamic Transition]
    step7 --> step8[Step 7: Field Density Test Sand Replacement or Core Cutter]
    step8 --> step9{Compaction greater than 98 percent MDD?}
    step9 -- No --> step6
    step9 -- Yes --> step10[Step 8: Surface Evenness Test 3 m Straight Edge]
    step10 --> step11[Step 9: Final Approval and Layer Thickness Verification]
    classDef gsbFlow fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class step1,step2,step4,step5,step6,step7,step8,step10,step11 gsbFlow
    classDef gsbDecision fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class step3,step9 gsbDecision
```

---

### 4.3 Decision Matrix: Grading I vs Grading II Selection

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Project Conditions] --> Q{High Rainfall Region}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Use Grading II Open graded for rapid drainage]
    Q -- No --> S[Use Grading I Close graded for smoother working platform]
    P --> T{Traffic Intensity}
    T -- Heavy more than 100 msa --> U[Grading I preferred for structural rigidity]
    T -- Light less than 20 msa --> V[Grading II acceptable and economical]
    P --> W{Subgrade CBR}
    W -- Less than 5 --> X[Increase GSB thickness to 300 mm or add Capping Layer]
    W -- Greater than 8 --> Y[Standard 200 mm GSB is sufficient]
    classDef gsbOpt fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class R,S,U,V,X,Y gsbOpt
    classDef gsbIn fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class Q,T,W gsbIn
```

> [!TIP]
> For **Kerala (very high rainfall, average 3000 mm/year)**, Grading II with rapid drainage is preferred. However, MORTH specifies Grading I for **National Highways and Expressways** regardless. Always check the project specifications.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Granular Sub Base (GSB). List any four functions of GSB in a flexible pavement.*

**Model Answer:**

Granular Sub Base (GSB) is a layer of well-graded, unbound, crushed stone aggregate (with or without sand/moorum blend) constructed between the subgrade and the base course in a flexible pavement, as per MORTH Clause 401.

**Four functions of GSB:**

1. **Load Distribution:** Spreads concentrated wheel loads over a wider area of the subgrade, reducing vertical stress.
2. **Drainage:** Provides a rapid lateral drainage path for infiltrated water, preventing saturation of the subgrade.
3. **Working Platform:** Acts as a stable, all-weather surface for construction traffic before the base course is laid.
4. **Prevention of Pumping:** Acts as a filter, preventing fine subgrade particles from migrating up into the base course.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the MORTH gradation limits for GSB (Grading I) for the following sieve sizes: 75 mm, 26.5 mm, 4.75 mm, and 0.075 mm. Also state the minimum field compaction requirement.*

**Model Answer:**

| Sieve Size (mm) | Percent Passing by Weight |
|---|---|
| 75.0 | 100 |
| 26.5 | 55 – 90 |
| 4.75 | 25 – 55 |
| 0.075 | 3 – 10 |

**Minimum field compaction requirement:** 98% of Maximum Dry Density (MDD) as obtained from the Modified Proctor Test (IS 2720 Part 8).

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the material specifications and gradation requirements of Granular Sub Base as per MORTH Clause 401. List the quality-control tests conducted on GSB material. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A field compaction test on a GSB layer using the **sand replacement method** gave the following data:
- Mass of wet soil from the hole = 1250 g
- Mass of sand filling the calibration container = 1150 g
- Mass of sand in the cone portion = 350 g
- Moisture content of the field soil = 5.8%
- Modified Proctor MDD of GSB = 2.21 g/cc

Determine the **field dry density** and the **relative compaction (%)**. State whether the layer is acceptable. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Material Specifications (7 Marks)**

The material for GSB shall be a physical mix of crushed stone aggregates, crusher-run product, and limited natural sand/moorum (≤ 20% by weight). Key specifications:

1. **Los Angeles Abrasion Value:** ≤ 40% (NH/Expressway: ≤ 35%)
2. **Aggregate Impact Value:** ≤ 30%
3. **Liquid Limit of fines (< 425 μm):** ≤ 25%
4. **Plasticity Index (PI):** ≤ 6%
5. **4-day Soaked CBR:** ≥ 30% minimum
6. **Water Absorption:** ≤ 2%
7. **Flakiness/Elongation Index:** ≤ 35% each

**Gradation (Grading I – Close-Graded):**
The material must comply with the percent-passing limits specified in the MORTH table (refer to Section 2.3 of these notes).

**Quality-Control Tests on GSB:**
- Gradation analysis (Sieve Test, IS 2386 Part I)
- Atterberg Limits (IS 2720 Part V)
- Modified Proctor Compaction (IS 2720 Part 8)
- California Bearing Ratio, soaked (IS 2720 Part XVI)
- Los Angeles Abrasion (IS 2386 Part IV)
- Aggregate Impact Value (IS 2386 Part IV)
- Field Density (Sand Replacement IS 2720 Part XXVIII, or Core Cutter IS 2720 Part XXIX)
- Surface Evenness (3 m Straight Edge)

**Part (b) – Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Volume of the hole**

Mass of sand filling the hole = (Mass of sand in container) − (Mass of sand in cone)

$$
M_{\text{sand,hole}} = 1150 - 350 = 800 \text{ g}
$$

Calibration bulk density of sand, $\rho_{\text{sand}} = 1.50$ g/cc (assumed; must be given in the question; if not, assume the calibration constant or state as 1.5 g/cc).

$$
V_{\text{hole}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{sand,hole}}}{\rho_{\text{sand}}} = \dfrac{800}{1.50} = 533.33 \text{ cc}
$$

**Step 2: Bulk (wet) density of field soil**

$$
\rho_b = \dfrac{M_{\text{wet}}}{V_{\text{hole}}} = \dfrac{1250}{533.33} = 2.344 \text{ g/cc}
$$

**Step 3: Dry density**

$$
\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho_b}{1 + w} = \dfrac{2.344}{1 + 0.058} = \dfrac{2.344}{1.058} = 2.215 \text{ g/cc}
$$

**Step 4: Relative Compaction (RC)**

$$
RC = \dfrac{\rho_{d,\text{field}}}{\text{MDD}} \times 100 = \dfrac{2.215}{2.21} \times 100 = 100.23\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A value greater than 100% is impossible in practice — it indicates either a **calibration error in the sand density** or **over-compaction affecting the top thin layer**. In an exam, if 100% is obtained, state: "Compaction is fully acceptable; value at theoretical max due to rounding."

**Conclusion:** The compaction **meets the MORTH requirement of ≥ 98% MDD**. The GSB layer is **acceptable**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **layer equivalency concept** in flexible pavement design. A pavement originally designed with 250 mm of WMM base is to be redesigned using GSB. Given the layer coefficients $a_3 = 0.14$ (WMM) and $a_4 = 0.11$ (GSB), calculate the **equivalent thickness of GSB**. Also, list the key differences between GSB and WMM. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A gradation test on a GSB sample gives the following sieve analysis data:

| Sieve (mm) | 75 | 26.5 | 9.5 | 4.75 | 2.36 | 0.425 | 0.075 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| % Passing | 100 | 88 | 62 | 45 | 32 | 18 | 7 |

Determine the **Coefficient of Uniformity (Cu)** and **Coefficient of Curvature (Cc)**. Comment on whether the gradation is **well-graded** as per standard classification. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Layer Equivalency (7 Marks)**

The **Layer Equivalency Concept** (AASHTO 1993) is used to substitute one pavement layer with another of different quality while maintaining the same **Structural Number (SN)**. This is necessary when construction materials are constrained or when the engineer wants to optimize cost.

The total structural number is:

$$
SN = a_1 D_1 + a_2 D_2 + a_3 D_3 + a_4 D_4
$$

For equivalent capacity (equal SN contribution), equating the contribution of WMM and GSB:

$$
a_3 D_3 = a_4 D_4
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
0.14 \times 250 = 0.11 \times D_4
$$

$$
D_4 = \dfrac{0.14 \times 250}{0.11} = \dfrac{35}{0.11} = 318.18 \text{ mm} \approx 320 \text{ mm}
$$

**Therefore, 320 mm of GSB is required to replace 250 mm of WMM in structural capacity.**

**Key Differences between GSB and WMM:**

| Aspect | GSB | WMM |
|---|---|---|
| Position in pavement | Above subgrade, below WMM | Above GSB, below bituminous layer |
| Material | Crusher-run or mixed aggregates | Crusher-run only, no natural sand |
| Gradation | Coarser (Grading I or II) | Finer, denser (close-graded) |
| Layer coefficient | 0.10 – 0.11 | 0.13 – 0.14 |
| Primary function | Drainage + working platform | Load distribution |
| Plastic fines | Permitted (up to 10%) | Strictly limited (< 5%) |

**Part (b) – Cu and Cc (7 Marks)**

From the gradation data, we extract:

- $D_{60} = $ sieve at 60% passing. From the table, 60% lies between 9.5 mm (62%) and 26.5 mm (88%). Using log-linear interpolation:
  - At 9.5 mm → 62%; at 26.5 mm → 88%.
  - Slope = (88 − 62) / log(26.5/9.5) = 26 / 0.4453 = 58.39
  - To drop from 62% to 60% (drop of 2): log size change = 2 / 58.39 = 0.03426
  - $D_{60} = 9.5 \times 10^{0.03426} = 9.5 \times 1.0817 = 10.28$ mm (approximate)

- $D_{30} = $ sieve at 30% passing. From the table, 30% lies between 2.36 mm (32%) and 4.75 mm (45%).
  - Slope = (45 − 32) / log(4.75/2.36) = 13 / 0.3035 = 42.83
  - To drop from 32% to 30% (drop of 2): log size change = 2 / 42.83 = 0.0467
  - $D_{30} = 2.36 \times 10^{0.0467} = 2.36 \times 1.1132 = 2.627$ mm (approximate)

- $D_{10} = $ sieve at 10% passing. From the table, 10% lies between 0.425 mm (18%) and 0.075 mm (7%). Wait — 7% is below 10%, so we interpolate between these:
  - Slope = (18 − 7) / log(0.425/0.075) = 11 / 0.7533 = 14.60
  - From 7% (at 0.075 mm) we need to reach 10% (rise of 3): log size change = 3 / 14.60 = 0.2055
  - $D_{10} = 0.075 \times 10^{0.2055} = 0.075 \times 1.605 = 0.120$ mm (approximate)

**Step 1: Calculate Cu**

$$
C_u = \dfrac{D_{60}}{D_{10}} = \dfrac{10.28}{0.120} = 85.67
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Cc**

$$
C_c = \dfrac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}} = \dfrac{(2.627)^2}{0.120 \times 10.28} = \dfrac{6.901}{1.2336} = 5.59
$$

**Step 3: Verify well-graded conditions**

- For coarse-grained soil, well-graded requires: $C_u \geq 4$ and $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$.

Here:
- $C_u = 85.67 \geq 4$ ✓
- $C_c = 5.59$, which is **outside the range 1–3** ✗

**Conclusion:** Although $C_u$ is well above 4, the $C_c$ value of 5.59 is **outside the standard range of 1 to 3**. The gradation is therefore **NOT well-graded** as per IS classification. It is either **gap-graded** (missing intermediate sizes) or has too steep a curvature. The gradation requires modification (typically, by adding intermediate-size aggregate) to become well-graded.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students skip the $C_c$ check and conclude "well-graded" based on $C_u$ alone. **This costs 2–3 marks.** Both $C_u$ and $C_c$ must be evaluated together. Also, in Part (a), students often forget to **list the table of differences** between GSB and WMM — examiners expect a minimum of 3–4 distinct differences to award full 7 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **GSB** is the granular layer between the **subgrade and the base course** (WMM/WBM) — never above the base course.
- The GSB material is a **physical blend of crushed aggregates and (limited) sand/moorum**, compacted to **≥ 98% of MDD (Modified Proctor)**.
- The **MORTH reference for GSB is Clause 401** — remember this clause number.
- **CBR of GSB (soaked, 4-day)** must be **≥ 30%** for it to qualify as a sub-base course.
- **Plasticity Index (PI) of fines < 425 μm** must be **≤ 6%** — non-plastic fines are ideal.
- **Two gradations exist** (Grading I close-graded and Grading II open-graded); Grading I is more common for National Highways.
- **Layer Equivalency Formula:** $D_2 = D_1 \cdot (a_1 / a_2)$ — used to substitute layers while keeping SN constant.
- **Compaction control** in the field uses the formula: $\rho_d = \rho_b / (1 + w)$, then $RC = \rho_d / MDD \times 100$.
- **Well-graded GSB** must satisfy: $C_u \geq 4$ AND $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$. Skipping the $C_c$ check is the most common reason for losing marks.
- **Field quality tests** mandatory on GSB: (i) Gradation, (ii) Atterberg limits, (iii) Modified Proctor, (iv) Soaked CBR, (v) Field density (sand replacement or core cutter), (vi) Surface evenness (3 m straightedge).
- **Three simultaneous functions** of GSB to remember in one line: **Drainage + Load Distribution + Working Platform**.
- **Standard GSB thickness:** 150–300 mm (compacted) per layer. If subgrade CBR < 5%, increase to 300 mm or add a capping layer of moorum/select soil.
- **Kerala-specific note:** In high-rainfall areas, prefer open-graded GSB or a close-graded GSB with fines ≤ 8% to ensure rapid drainage and prevent subgrade softening.
- **Burmister and AASHTO** are the two common design philosophies for GSB thickness; AASHTO layer coefficients are most commonly tested in KTU.
- The **Los Angeles Abrasion Value (LAA)** test is conducted on the **parent rock**, not the GSB mix — be precise with the wording in your answer.
- **GSB is NOT bituminous** — there is **no bitumen, no cement, and no binding agent** in GSB. The strength comes from **inter-particle friction and mechanical interlock** only.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bituminous Macadam and Bituminous Concrete Layers of Flexible Pavement; Construction of Cement Concrete Pavement: Material Characterization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET745 - PAVEMENT CONSTRUCTION AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bituminous Macadam and Bituminous Concrete Layers of Flexible Pavement; Construction of Cement Concrete Pavement: Material Characterization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Flexible Pavement Bituminous Layers: The Big Picture

A flexible pavement is a layered system of bituminous and granular materials engineered to distribute traffic loads to the subgrade through successive layers of decreasing stiffness. Within this layered cake, **Bituminous Macadam (BM)** sits deep as a *base/binder course*, while **Bituminous Concrete (BC)** sits at the very top as the *wearing course* in direct contact with vehicle tyres.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECET745 Module 3):**
> This module directly maps to **CO3** — *Construct flexible and rigid pavements by selecting appropriate materials, mixes, and laying methods in accordance with MoRTH/IRC specifications.* Both the structural design intent and the construction method of BM and BC must be understood for the 14-mark ESE questions.

### 1.2 Bituminous Macadam (BM)

**Formal Definition (MoRTH/IRC:SP:53):**
*Bituminous Macadam is a dense, open-graded or semi-dense bituminous mix used as a base/binder course in flexible pavement, consisting of coarse aggregates, filler, and bitumen (typically 30/40 or 60/70 penetration grade), compacted to high density through roller compaction. It is laid in compacted thickness of **50 mm to 100 mm** per layer.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Grading Distinction:** BM is traditionally a **medium-graded mix** (nominal maximum aggregate size 26.5 mm or 19 mm), distinctly coarser than BC. It is the *load-distribution* course, not the *skid-resistance* course.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine building a multi-storey office on soft soil. You cannot lay carpet (BC) directly on marshy ground. You first pour a thick concrete raft (BM) to spread the column loads over a wider area, then finish the upper floors with marble. **BM is the concrete raft, BC is the marble finish.** BM's job is *strength and load spreading*; BC's job is *smoothness, comfort, and skid resistance*.

### 1.3 Bituminous Concrete (BC)

**Formal Definition (MoRTH Specification Clause 507; IRC:SP:53):**
*Bituminous Concrete is a dense, continuously-graded, gap-graded or open-graded bituminous mix used as the **wearing/surface course** of flexible pavement. It is composed of coarse aggregates, fine aggregates, mineral filler, and bitumen (typically 60/70 or modified bitumen), with nominal maximum aggregate size **13.2 mm** (or 9.5 mm for finer surface), laid in compacted thickness of **30 mm to 50 mm**.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** BC has a **much finer grading** than BM, **higher filler content** (4–8% by weight of aggregate), and **lower air voids** (3–5%) for impermeability. Its primary function is to be *impermeable to water*, *resistant to rutting*, and *provide skid resistance*.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Continuing the building analogy — BC is the marble or vinyl flooring. It must be smooth underfoot (ride quality), waterproof (prevent water reaching the BM/base), and slip-resistant (skid resistance). It is thin but engineered to perfection.

### 1.4 Cement Concrete (Rigid) Pavement Construction

**Formal Definition (IRC:15, IRC:SP:46, MoRTH Clause 602):**
*Cement Concrete Pavement (CCP) is a rigid pavement constructed using Pavement Quality Concrete (PQC) of grade M40/M50 with characteristic compressive strength of 40 MPa/50 MPa, laid over a Dry Lean Concrete (DLC) sub-base, with carefully designed contraction, expansion, and longitudinal joints equipped with load-transfer devices (dowels/tie bars).*

> [!NOTE]
> **Material Characterization** for rigid pavement covers the engineering properties and quality tests for: **Cement (OPC 43/53), Coarse Aggregates (crushed), Fine Aggregates (natural/manufactured sand), Water (potable), Admixtures (plasticizers, retarders, air-entrainers), and Steel (dowels, tie bars).**

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A flexible pavement is like a *carpet over sand* — it bends and deforms. A rigid pavement is like a *concrete bridge deck* — it is a stiff slab that bridges over weak spots in the subgrade. Because of this bridging action, rigid pavements are designed primarily by **slab thickness and joint detailing**, not by layer strength.

---

### 1.5 Quick Visual Hierarchy of Pavement Layers

| Pavement Type | Top → Bottom Layer Sequence | Function of Each Layer |
|---|---|---|
| **Flexible** | BC → BM → WMM → GSB → Subgrade | Impermeable wearing → Load spreading base → Drainage → Foundation |
| **Rigid** | PQC → DLC → GSB → Subgrade | Structural slab → Levelling/working platform → Drainage → Foundation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Physical Constants/Standards to Remember for KTU Exam:**
> - BM nominal max aggregate size: **26.5 mm (Grade 1) or 19 mm (Grade 2)**
> - BC nominal max aggregate size: **13.2 mm** (with 9.5 mm option)
> - BC compacted thickness: **30–50 mm**
> - BM compacted thickness: **50–100 mm**
> - PQC grade for highway CC pavement: **M40 minimum, M50 for expressways**
> - PQC flexural strength target: **4.5 MPa (28 days)**

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bituminous Macadam (BM) — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Constituents and Their Roles

BM is a bituminous mix where the **coarse aggregate skeleton** carries the load, and bitumen merely **binds** the skeleton. The mix follows Marshall or Superpave design methodology.

| Constituent | Role | Typical Proportion (% by weight of mix) |
|---|---|---|
| Coarse aggregate (retained 2.36 mm) | Skeleton/load bearing | **55–65%** |
| Fine aggregate (passing 2.36 mm) | Filling voids | **25–35%** |
| Mineral filler (passing 0.075 mm) | Stiffness, reduces drain-down | **2–6%** |
| Bitumen (60/70 or 30/40 grade) | Binder/cohesion | **3.5–5.0%** by weight of mix |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why coarser than BC?** BM sits in a low-stress zone (only indirect tensile stress from bending of full pavement), so coarse stones are acceptable. Coarse stones interlock better and resist deformation from heavy truck loads.

### 2.1.2 Aggregate Grading (MoRTH Table 500-7 for BM Grade 1)

```
Sieve Size (mm)   |   % Passing by Weight
------------------|----------------------
26.5              |   100
19.0              |   90–100
13.2              |   56–80
4.75              |   29–54
2.36              |   19–42
0.30              |   5–17
0.075             |   0–8
```

### 2.1.3 Marshall Design Criteria (MORTH Table 500-9)

| Marshall Property | Specified Value (BM) |
|---|---|
| Number of blows per face | **75** (heavy traffic) or **50** (light) |
| Stability (kN, min) | **9.0 kN** |
| Flow (mm) | **2–4 mm** |
| Air voids (%) | **3–5%** |
| VMA (%) min | **13%** (for 26.5 mm NMAS) |
| VFB (%) | **65–75%** |
| Optimum Bitumen Content | From stability–voids flow curves |

### 2.1.4 The 'Why' Behind Each BM Specification

- **Stability ≥ 9 kN**: Heavy commercial vehicle loading (trucks/buses) creates vertical + horizontal shear stresses. The mix must resist plastic deformation (rutting).
- **Air voids 3–5%**: Below 3% → bleeding (bitumen squeezes out under traffic). Above 5% → permeability increases → water infiltration damages pavement.
- **VMA ≥ 13%**: Adequate space for bitumen film around each aggregate particle — ensures durability and fatigue life.
- **75 blows**: Simulates heavy-traffic compaction in the lab.

---

## 2.2 Bituminous Concrete (BC) — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Constituents and Their Roles

BC is a **finely graded, dense mix** designed for surface performance — smoothness, impermeability, skid resistance, and rutting resistance.

| Constituent | Typical Proportion | Special Function |
|---|---|---|
| Coarse aggregate (crushed, 100% crushed face) | **35–45%** | Skid resistance (PSV ≥ 60 for expressways) |
| Fine aggregate | **45–55%** | Workability, smoothness |
| Mineral filler (lime/cement) | **4–8%** of aggregate weight | Anti-stripping, stiffens mastic |
| Bitumen (60/70 or modified PMB 40/70) | **5.0–6.5%** | Mastic cohesion |
| Anti-stripping agent (e.g., Zycosoil) | **0.1–0.5%** of bitumen | Moisture resistance |

### 2.2.2 Aggregate Grading (MORTH Table 500-17 for BC Grade 1, 13.2 mm NMAS)

```
Sieve Size (mm)   |   % Passing by Weight
------------------|----------------------
19.0              |   100
13.2              |   90–100
9.5               |   70–88
4.75              |   53–71
2.36              |   42–58
1.18              |   34–48
0.60              |   26–38
0.30              |   18–28
0.15              |   12–20
0.075             |   4–10
```

### 2.2.3 BC Design Requirements (MORTH Clause 507)

| Property | Specified Value |
|---|---|
| Stability (kN) min | **9.0 kN** |
| Flow (mm) | **2–4** |
| Air voids (%) | **3–5** |
| VMA (%) min | **14%** |
| VFB (%) | **65–78** |
| **TSR (Tensile Strength Ratio, retained strength after moisture conditioning)** | **≥ 80%** (AASHTO T 283) |
| **Loss of Marshall Stability after 60°C water bath (24 h)** | **≤ 25%** |
| **Number of blows/side** | **75** |
| **Bitumen grade** | 60/70 (VG-30); or PMB for heavy traffic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why TSR ≥ 80% is critical for BC (not emphasized for BM):** BC is in *direct contact with water* (rain, splash) and *repeated traffic suction* (tyre–pavement interaction creates negative pore pressure pulling water in). Stripping failure is the most common distress of BC. Hence BC must undergo moisture-susceptibility testing.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table — No Pipes Inside)

| Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{VMA} = 100 - \dfrac{G_{mb} \times P_{s}}{G_{sb}}$ | Voids in Mineral Aggregate | % | Mix design — durability check |
| $\text{Vv} = \dfrac{G_{mm} - G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100$ | Air Voids (Va) | % | Mix design — permeability/bleed control |
| $\text{VFB} = \dfrac{VMA - Vv}{VMA} \times 100$ | Voids Filled with Bitumen | % | Mix design — bitumen richness check |
| $\dfrac{P_{b}}{100 - P_{b}} = \dfrac{G_{b}}{G_{sb}} \times \left(\dfrac{1}{G_{se}} - \dfrac{1}{G_{sb}}\right)$ | Effective specific gravity of aggregate $G_{se}$ | — | Volumetric mix design |
| $\text{TSR} = \dfrac{S_{t,\text{cond}}}{S_{t,\text{dry}}} \times 100$ | Tensile Strength Ratio | % | Moisture susceptibility of BC |
| $\text{CBR} = \dfrac{\text{Load at 2.5 mm penetration}}{\text{Standard load (1370 kg)}} \times 100$ | California Bearing Ratio | % | Subbase/base strength |
| $f_{cr} = 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | Target flexural strength of PQC | MPa | Concrete mix design (IRC:44) |
| $w/c = 0.40 \text{ to } 0.45$ (PQC M40) | Water–cement ratio | — | Durability + strength |
| $T_{slab} = \left[\dfrac{1.2 P (1-\mu^{2})}{k \cdot f}\right]^{0.5}$ | Westergaard critical stress | m | Rigid pavement slab thickness (conceptual) |
| $\text{Dowel diameter} = \dfrac{L_{j}}{8}$ | Dowel bar design | mm | Joint load transfer |
| $\text{Tie bar spacing} = \dfrac{A_{s} \cdot f_{s}}{f_{t} \cdot h}$ | Tie bar longitudinal | mm | Longitudinal joint design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** These formulas appear in KTU ESE as direct 3-mark "Define and state" questions, and 7-mark design calculations for the 14-mark questions. VMA, Vv, and VFB are the **three pillars** of every bituminous mix design question.

---

## 2.4 Cement Concrete Pavement — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.4.1 The PQC Mix Design Philosophy

PQC is *Pavement Quality Concrete* — designed primarily for **flexural strength** (not compressive), because the slab bends over subgrade.

**Target properties (IRC:44, MORTH Clause 602):**

| Property | Specification |
|---|---|
| Compressive strength (28 days) | **M40 minimum (40 MPa), M50 for high-volume roads** |
| Flexural strength (28 days) | **≥ 4.5 MPa** |
| Max nominal aggregate size | **31.5 mm** (or 26.5 mm for slip-form paving) |
| Workability (slump) | **25–50 mm** (manual), **30–65 mm** (slip-form) |
| w/c ratio | **0.40–0.45** (max 0.45) |
| Air content (if AE admixture) | **3.5–5.5%** for frost-prone areas |
| Minimum cement content | **350 kg/m³** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse *compressive* and *flexural* strength. PQC is rated by **compressive** grade (M40) but **designed** by **flexural strength** (because slab failure is by flexure/bending). The empirical relationship $f_{cr} = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ gives ≈ 4.42 MPa for M40.

### 2.4.2 Why PQC Needs Joints

Concrete shrinks due to:
1. **Drying shrinkage** (loss of free water) — occurs over weeks/months
2. **Thermal contraction** (night cooling) — daily cycle
3. **Thermal expansion** (day heating)

Uncontrolled shrinkage → **random cracking** → pavement fails. Hence **joints** are deliberately placed cracks.

| Joint Type | Purpose | Spacing (M40) | Load Transfer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Contraction** (transverse) | Control drying + thermal shrinkage cracking | **4.5 m to 5.0 m** | Dowel bars (32 mm dia, 500 mm long @ 300 mm c/c) |
| **Expansion** | Allow thermal expansion; only at bridges/structures | **90–140 m** (or omit entirely in modern practice) | Dowel bars with one end capped and debonded |
| **Longitudinal** | Control longitudinal shrinkage | One per 1–2 lanes (3.5 m for single lane) | Tie bars (12 mm dia deformed bars @ 600 mm c/c) |
| **Construction** | End of day's work | At day's end | Dowel bars |

### 2.4.3 Material Characterization for PQC (Detailed)

| Material | Critical Tests (IS Code) | Acceptance Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| **Cement (OPC 43/53)** | Fineness (IS 4031), Setting time, Soundness, Compressive strength (IS 269/8112) | Initial setting ≥ 30 min; 28-day strength ≥ 43/53 MPa |
| **Coarse aggregate** | Los Angeles Abrasion (IS 2386-IV), Aggregate Impact Value, Aggregate Crushing Value, Flakiness/Elongation Index | LA Abrasion ≤ 30%, AIV ≤ 30%, FI+EI ≤ 30% |
| **Fine aggregate** | Grading, Fineness Modulus, Silt content, Chloride/Sulphate content | FM 2.6–3.2; silt ≤ 3% by volume |
| **Water** | pH, chlorides, sulphates, organic content (IS 456) | pH 6–8; Cl⁻ ≤ 500 mg/L for non-pretensioned; SO₄²⁻ ≤ 400 mg/L |
| **Admixtures** | Compatibility test, Setting time, Slump retention (IS 9103) | As per manufacturer's TDS; verified by trial mix |
| **Steel (dowels/tie bars)** | Tensile strength, bend/re-bend (IS 1786) | Fe 415/500 grade; epoxy-coated for corrosion-prone zones |
| **Concrete (fresh)** | Slump (IS 1199), Air content (pressure method) | Slump 25–50 mm; air 3.5–5.5% |
| **Concrete (hardened)** | Compressive strength on cube (IS 516), Flexural strength on beam (IS 516) | 28-day cube ≥ 40 MPa, beam ≥ 4.5 MPa |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Volumetric Bituminous Mix Design Equations (from First Principles)

### Step 1: Set up the volume components of compacted mix

Consider a compacted bituminous mix sample. Its total volume $V$ consists of three phases:

$$V = V_{agg} + V_{bit} + V_{air}$$

Where:
- $V_{agg}$ = volume of aggregate particles (including their internal pores, if any)
- $V_{bit}$ = volume of bitumen
- $V_{air}$ = volume of air voids between coated particles

### Step 2: Express volumes using bulk specific gravities

By definition, **bulk specific gravity of compacted mix**:

$$G_{mb} = \dfrac{W}{V \cdot \rho_{w}}$$

So total volume is:

$$V = \dfrac{W}{G_{mb} \cdot \rho_{w}}$$

Similarly, **theoretical maximum specific gravity** (no air voids, only aggregate + bitumen):

$$G_{mm} = \dfrac{W}{V_{agg} + V_{bit}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\rho_{w}}$$

### Step 3: Derive Air Voids (Vv) formula

Air voids are the percentage of total volume occupied by air:

$$V_v = \dfrac{V_{air}}{V} \times 100 = \dfrac{V - (V_{agg} + V_{bit})}{V} \times 100$$

Substituting the specific-gravity expressions:

$$V_v = \dfrac{ \dfrac{W}{G_{mb} \cdot \rho_{w}} - \dfrac{W}{G_{mm} \cdot \rho_{w}} }{ \dfrac{W}{G_{mb} \cdot \rho_{w}} } \times 100$$

The $W / \rho_w$ terms cancel, giving:

$$\boxed{\,V_v = \dfrac{G_{mm} - G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100\,}$$

### Step 4: Derive VMA formula

**VMA is the percentage of total mix volume occupied by aggregate's inter-granular void space** (i.e., the space between aggregate particles — filled partly by bitumen, partly by air).

$$VMA = \dfrac{V_{agg,\text{voids}} + V_{bit} + V_{air}}{V} \times 100$$

But equivalently, VMA = (volume not occupied by aggregate solids) / total volume:

$$VMA = \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{agg,\text{solids}}}{V}\right) \times 100$$

The volume of aggregate solids in a mix of total mass $W$ is $V_{agg,\text{solids}} = \dfrac{P_s \cdot W}{G_{sb} \cdot \rho_w}$ (where $P_s$ = % aggregate by weight of mix, $G_{sb}$ = bulk SG of aggregate).

Total volume of compacted mix $V = \dfrac{W}{G_{mb} \cdot \rho_w}$. Substituting:

$$VMA = \left(1 - \dfrac{ P_s \cdot W / (G_{sb} \cdot \rho_w) }{ W / (G_{mb} \cdot \rho_w) }\right) \times 100$$

$$VMA = \left(1 - \dfrac{P_s \cdot G_{mb}}{G_{sb}}\right) \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\,VMA = 100 - \dfrac{G_{mb} \cdot P_s}{G_{sb}}\,}$$

### Step 5: Derive VFB (Voids Filled with Bitumen)

VFB is the percentage of VMA that is filled with bitumen (not air):

$$VFB = \dfrac{V_{bit}}{V_{agg,\text{voids}} + V_{bit} + V_{air}} \times 100 = \dfrac{VMA - V_v}{VMA} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\,VFB = \dfrac{VMA - V_v}{VMA} \times 100\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world use:** These three formulas are evaluated for every trial Marshall briquette in the lab. The lab technician plots $V_v$, VMA, VFB, stability, and flow vs bitumen content, then reads the **Optimum Bitumen Content (OBC)** as the bitumen content that gives *simultaneously* 4% air voids, maximum stability, and acceptable flow. This OBC is the mix design output for the paving job.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Marshall Stability of BM Mix — Sample Calculation

**Given problem:** A BM mix is designed in the lab. Trial mix with 4.0% bitumen by weight of mix gives:
- $G_{mm} = 2.518$
- $G_{mb} = 2.395$ (compacted)
- $G_{sb} = 2.682$ (bulk SG of aggregate blend)
- Marshall Stability = 11.2 kN
- Flow = 3.2 mm

**Find:** Air voids, VMA, VFB, and verify against MoRTH BM specification.

### Step 1: Air Voids

$$V_v = \dfrac{G_{mm} - G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100 = \dfrac{2.518 - 2.395}{2.518} \times 100$$

$$V_v = \dfrac{0.123}{2.518} \times 100 = 4.88\%$$

**Check:** Specified range 3–5% → **PASS ✓**

### Step 2: VMA

$P_s$ (aggregate % by weight of mix) = $100 - P_b = 100 - 4.0 = 96.0\%$

$$VMA = 100 - \dfrac{G_{mb} \cdot P_s}{G_{sb}} = 100 - \dfrac{2.395 \times 96.0}{2.682}$$

$$VMA = 100 - \dfrac{229.92}{2.682} = 100 - 85.73 = 14.27\%$$

**Check:** Specified minimum 13% → **PASS ✓**

### Step 3: VFB

$$VFB = \dfrac{VMA - V_v}{VMA} \times 100 = \dfrac{14.27 - 4.88}{14.27} \times 100$$

$$VFB = \dfrac{9.39}{14.27} \times 100 = 65.8\%$$

**Check:** Specified 65–75% → **PASS ✓**

### Step 4: Stability

Marshall Stability = 11.2 kN → Specified min 9.0 kN → **PASS ✓**

**Verdict:** The trial mix with 4.0% bitumen satisfies all MoRTH BM specifications. It would be a valid OBC candidate (subject to confirming 3 trial points bracket 4% to plot curves).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: PQC Mix Design (IRC:44 Method) — Worked Example

**Problem:** Design a PQC mix of grade M40 (flexural strength 4.5 MPa) for an Expressway. Given:
- Cement OPC 53, SG = 3.15
- Coarse aggregate: 20 mm and 10 mm crushed, $G_{ca}$ = 2.72, $G_{fa}$ = 2.65 (combined bulk SG of aggregate = 2.69)
- River sand, FM = 2.85
- Target slump = 30 mm (slip-form paver)
- No air-entrainment (tropical India)

**Step 1: Target mean strength (using 1.65 σ for 5% defectives; assume σ = 4.5 MPa for PQC):**

$$f_{t,\text{target}} = f_{ck} + 1.65\sigma = 40 + 1.65 \times 4.5 = 47.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Effective w/c from IRC:44 Abrams curve (for 20 mm aggregate, no air):**

For target 47.4 MPa compressive, IRC:44 table gives w/c ≈ **0.42**

**Step 3: Water content for 30 mm slump (IRC:44 Table 5, 20 mm MSA):**

$$W = 186 \text{ kg/m}^{3} \text{ (approx)}$$

**Step 4: Cement content:**

$$C = \dfrac{W}{w/c} = \dfrac{186}{0.42} = 443 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$$

**Check:** Minimum cement per MORTH 602 = 350 kg/m³ → **OK ✓**

**Step 5: Coarse aggregate fraction (IRC:44 recommends 0.60 of total aggregate for 20 mm MSA):**

Mass of total aggregate = (1 m³ − volume of cement − volume of water) × Gagg × ρw

$$V_{agg} = 1 - \dfrac{C}{\rho_c} - \dfrac{W}{\rho_w} = 1 - \dfrac{443}{3150} - \dfrac{186}{1000} = 1 - 0.1406 - 0.186 = 0.6734 \text{ m}^{3}$$

$$W_{agg} = 0.6734 \times 2.69 \times 1000 = 1811 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$$

CA (60%) = 0.60 × 1811 = **1087 kg/m³**
FA (40%) = 0.40 × 1811 = **724 kg/m³**

**Step 6: Mix proportions per m³ of concrete:**

| Material | Quantity (kg/m³) |
|---|---|
| Cement (OPC 53) | **443** |
| Water | **186** |
| Coarse aggregate (20+10 mm) | **1087** |
| Fine aggregate (river sand) | **724** |
| Total | 2440 |
| w/c | 0.42 |
| Theoretical density | 2440 kg/m³ |

**Step 7: Verify flexural strength relationship:**

$$f_{cr} = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}} = 0.7 \times \sqrt{40} = 0.7 \times 6.32 = 4.43 \text{ MPa}$$

This is below 4.5 MPa target → **Slightly under-designed** → either (a) reduce w/c to 0.40, OR (b) use M50. For exam answer, recommend using **w/c = 0.40** or selecting M50 concrete for expressway as per IRC:SP:46.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the **mix design code reference** (IRC:44, IS 10262, ACI 211.1) before starting the design. Examiners allocate 1 mark for "method stated."

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Dowel Bar Sizing (IRC:58)

**Problem:** Design dowel bars for contraction joint of M40 PQC slab, 250 mm thick, with 4.5 m joint spacing.

**Step 1: Dowel diameter formula (IRC:58, Clause 5.5):**

$$d = \dfrac{L_d}{8} = \dfrac{500}{8} = 62.5 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{Use 32 mm standard (next lower even) } *$$

> *Note: 500 mm is the standard dowel length. Using 32 mm dia is the **standard MoRTH specification**; the formula $L/8$ is approximate. Use **32 mm dia, 500 mm long** for slabs up to 250 mm thick.

**Step 2: Dowel spacing (IRC:58, Clause 5.5.2):**

$$s_d = 300 \text{ mm c/c (constant for full slab width)}$$

**Step 3: Number of dowels per joint for 3.5 m lane width:**

$$n = \dfrac{3500}{300} + 1 = 12.67 \rightarrow \text{Use 13 dowels per joint, or 12 @ 300 c/c}$$

**Step 4: Embedment length (bonded half length, not debonded portion):**

$$L_e = \dfrac{d \cdot f_s}{2 \tau_b} = \dfrac{32 \times 230}{2 \times 2.5} = 1472 \text{ mm} \text{ (theoretical)}$$

In practice, the embedded half is 250 mm (half of 500 mm dowel), and the bond-breaking bitumen coating extends to 50 mm of the bar tip to allow slab end movement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering judgment:** The above calculation shows that a 32 mm dowel is much stronger than required for shear transfer; the **practical limit** is corrosion resistance and slab reinforcement cover, not shear capacity. Hence MoRTH/IRC:58 fixes 32 mm × 500 mm @ 300 mm c/c as a *standard*.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: BM vs BC vs PQC Comparison

| Parameter | Bituminous Macadam (BM) | Bituminous Concrete (BC) | PQC (Rigid) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Position in pavement | Base/binder course | Wearing/surface course | Surface + structural slab |
| NMAS | 26.5 or 19 mm | 13.2 mm | 31.5 mm |
| Layer thickness | 50–100 mm | 30–50 mm | 200–350 mm |
| Air voids target | 3–5% | 3–5% | — (concrete) |
| VMA min | 13% | 14% | — |
| Stability min | 9.0 kN | 9.0 kN | Flexural ≥ 4.5 MPa |
| Bitumen/Cement content | 3.5–5.0% | 5.0–6.5% | 350–450 kg/m³ |
| Design method | Marshall | Marshall / Superpave | IRC:44 (abs. vol. + IS 10262) |
| Construction equipment | Paver + roller | Paver + roller (initial + intermediate + final) | Slip-form / fixed-form paver + texturing + curing |
| Joints | None (continuous) | None (continuous) | Mandatory (doweled/tyred) |
| Curing | Not required (cools naturally) | Not required | 14–28 days water/burlap/curing compound |
| Critical test | Marshall stability, TSR | TSR ≥ 80%, ride quality, surface texture | 28-day flexural strength, skid resistance, dowel alignment |
| Reference | MoRTH 504, IRC:SP:53 | MoRTH 507, IRC:SP:53 | MoRTH 602, IRC:15, IRC:44, IRC:58 |

    C --> D[Wet Mix Macadam WMM Laying]
    D --> E[Prime Coat on WMM]
    E --> F[Drying and Heating of Aggregate to 160 180 deg C]
    F --> G[Heating Bitumen to 150 165 deg C]
    G --> H[Mixing in Hot Mix Plant Batch Type]
    H --> I[Transportation in Insulated Trucks]
    I --> J[Spreading by Mechanical Paver]
    J --> K[Initial Rolling 8 to 10 ton Static Tandem]
    K --> L[Intermediate Rolling Vibratory Roller 8 to 12 ton]
    L --> M[Final Rolling Pneumatic Tyred Roller 12 to 15 ton]
    M --> N[Quality Control Density > 97 Marshall and Surface Levels]
    N --> O[Tack Coat before Next Layer]
    O --> P[BC Laying Same Sequence]
    P --> Q[Finished Bituminous Surface]

    style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333
    style Q fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#333

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation for KTU students:** The flowchart above shows the **strict sequence of bituminous pavement construction**. Skipping any stage (e.g., omitting prime coat, or cooling below 120°C before rolling) is a common KTU pitfall question — examiners expect students to know that prime coat is **mandatory on WMM** before BM, and **tack coat is mandatory on BM** before BC.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: PQC Construction Sequence (Slip-Form Method)

flowchart TD

    A7 --> A8[Surface Finishing with Longitudinal Float]
    A8 --> A9[Texturing Broom Drag for Skid Resistance]
    A9 --> A10[Joint Cutting Contraction Joints 6 to 18 hours]
    A10 --> A11[Joint Sealing with Hot Pour Sealant]
    A11 --> A12[Curing by Wet Burlap or Curing Compound 14 to 28 days]
    A12 --> A13[Quality Control Compressive and Flexural Tests]
    A13 --> A14[Opened to Traffic after 28 days or Strength Verification]

    style A14 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#333
```

## 4.4 Cross-Section Schematic of Flexible Pavement (Text-Based Architecture)

```
=================================================================
LAYER          | MATERIAL                  | THICKNESS RANGE
=================================================================
1. SURFACE     | Bituminous Concrete (BC)  | 30 - 50 mm
               | (Dense, impermeable)
-----------------------------------------------------------------
2. BINDER      | Dense Bituminous Macadam  | 50 - 100 mm
               | (DBM) or BM
-----------------------------------------------------------------
3. BASE        | Wet Mix Macadam (WMM)     | 150 - 250 mm
               | (Granular, cement-bound)
-----------------------------------------------------------------
4. SUBBASE     | Granular Sub-base (GSB)   | 150 - 300 mm
               | (Crushed stone, Moorum)
-----------------------------------------------------------------
5. SUBGRADE    | Compacted natural soil    | 500 mm+ (compacted)
               | (CBR >= 8% for NH/SH)
=================================================================
```

## 4.5 Cross-Section Schematic of Rigid Pavement (Text-Based Architecture)

```
=================================================================
LAYER          | MATERIAL                  | THICKNESS RANGE
=================================================================
1. SLAB        | Pavement Quality Concrete | 200 - 350 mm
               | (M40/M50) + Joints + Steel
-----------------------------------------------------------------
2. SEPARATION  | Polythene sheet 125 micron | 1 layer
               | (Prevents bond with DLC)
-----------------------------------------------------------------
3. LEVELLING   | Dry Lean Concrete (DLC)   | 100 - 150 mm
               | (M10, dry consistency)
-----------------------------------------------------------------
4. SUBBASE     | Granular Sub-base (GSB)   | 150 - 300 mm
               | (Drainage + load spread)
-----------------------------------------------------------------
5. SUBGRADE    | Compacted natural soil    | 500 mm+ (compacted)
               | (CBR >= 8% for NH/SH)
=================================================================
```
