# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] *Differentiate between Bituminous Macadam (BM) and Bituminous Concrete (BC) in terms of position in pavement, aggregate size, and primary function.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Bituminous Macadam (BM) | Bituminous Concrete (BC) |
|---|---|---|
| Position in pavement | Base/Binder course | Wearing/Surface course |
| Nominal Maximum Aggregate Size (NMAS) | 26.5 mm (Grade 1) or 19 mm (Grade 2) | 13.2 mm (or 9.5 mm) |
| Compacted layer thickness | 50–100 mm | 30–50 mm |
| Primary function | Load distribution and structural strength | Smoothness, impermeability, skid resistance |
| Typical bitumen content | 3.5–5.0% by weight of mix | 5.0–6.5% by weight of mix |
| Critical test | Marshall stability, density | TSR ≥ 80% (moisture susceptibility), surface texture |

*(Allocate 1 mark per differentiating parameter, max 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] *State any three quality control tests for coarse aggregate used in Cement Concrete Pavement and their IS code acceptance limits.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Los Angeles Abrasion Test (IS 2386 Part IV):** LA Abrasion Value shall **not exceed 30%** for PQC coarse aggregate. (1 mark)
2. **Aggregate Impact Value (AIV) Test (IS 2386 Part IV):** AIV shall **not exceed 30%** for PQC. (1 mark)
3. **Combined Flakiness Index (FI) + Elongation Index (EI) Test (IS 2386 Part I):** The sum of FI and EI shall **not exceed 30%** for PQC. (1 mark)

*(Allocate 1 mark per test; partial credit for stating test without limit.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024]** *(a)* Explain in detail the constituents, aggregate grading requirements, and Marshall design criteria for **Bituminous Macadam (BM)** as per MoRTH specifications. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A BM mix with 4.2% bitumen by weight of mix has the following properties:
- Bulk specific gravity of compacted mix $G_{mb} = 2.410$
- Theoretical maximum specific gravity $G_{mm} = 2.525$
- Bulk specific gravity of aggregate blend $G_{sb} = 2.690$
- Marshall Stability = 10.5 kN
- Flow = 3.0 mm

Calculate **Air Voids (Vv), VMA, and VFB** and verify the mix against MoRTH BM specifications. *(7 marks)*

### **Solution A:**

#### **Part (a) — BM Constituents, Grading & Marshall Criteria (7 marks)**

**(i) Constituents of BM (2 marks):**
- **Coarse aggregate** (retained 2.36 mm sieve): Crushed rock, well-graded, 55–65% by weight. Must have LA Abrasion < 35%, AIV < 30%, FI+EI < 30%, water absorption < 2%.
- **Fine aggregate** (passing 2.36 mm): Natural sand or crushed fines, 25–35% by weight. Clean, free of organic/clay.
- **Mineral filler** (passing 0.075 mm): Cement, lime, stone dust, 2–6% by weight of aggregate. Stiffens mastic.
- **Bitumen**: Grade 60/70 (VG-30) for normal, 30/40 for high-temperature zones. 3.5–5.0% by weight of mix.

**(ii) Aggregate Grading as per MoRTH Table 500-7 (BM Grade 1, 26.5 mm NMAS) (2 marks):**

| Sieve Size (mm) | % Passing by Weight |
|---|---|
| 26.5 | 100 |
| 19.0 | 90–100 |
| 13.2 | 56–80 |
| 4.75 | 29–54 |
| 2.36 | 19–42 |
| 0.30 | 5–17 |
| 0.075 | 0–8 |

**(iii) Marshall Design Criteria (MoRTH Table 500-9) (3 marks):**

| Property | Specification |
|---|---|
| Compaction (no. of blows/face) | 75 (heavy traffic) |
| Stability, minimum | **9.0 kN** |
| Flow | 2–4 mm |
| Air voids (Vv) | 3–5% |
| VMA (minimum) | 13% |
| VFB | 65–75% |
| Optimum bitumen content | From trial curves at Vv = 4% |

**Why these limits?** Stability 9 kN ensures resistance to plastic deformation (rutting) under heavy traffic. Air voids 3–5% prevent bleeding (<3%) and permeability (>5%). VMA 13% ensures sufficient bitumen film thickness for durability.

#### **Part (b) — Volumetric Mix Verification (7 marks)**

**Given:** $G_{mb} = 2.410$, $G_{mm} = 2.525$, $G_{sb} = 2.690$, $P_b = 4.2\%$, $P_s = 95.8\%$.

**Step 1: Air Voids (2 marks)**

$$V_v = \dfrac{G_{mm} - G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100 = \dfrac{2.525 - 2.410}{2.525} \times 100$$

$$V_v = \dfrac{0.115}{2.525} \times 100 = 4.55\%$$

**Spec:** 3–5% → **PASS ✓**

**Step 2: VMA (2 marks)**

$$VMA = 100 - \dfrac{G_{mb} \cdot P_s}{G_{sb}} = 100 - \dfrac{2.410 \times 95.8}{2.690}$$

$$VMA = 100 - \dfrac{230.88}{2.690} = 100 - 85.83 = 14.17\%$$

**Spec:** Min 13% → **PASS ✓**

**Step 3: VFB (2 marks)**

$$VFB = \dfrac{VMA - V_v}{VMA} \times 100 = \dfrac{14.17 - 4.55}{14.17} \times 100$$

$$VFB = \dfrac{9.62}{14.17} \times 100 = 67.89\%$$

**Spec:** 65–75% → **PASS ✓**

**Step 4: Stability Check (1 mark)**

Stability = 10.5 kN ≥ 9.0 kN → **PASS ✓**

**Conclusion (Final 1 mark):** All four volumetric and stability parameters are within MoRTH BM specifications. The trial mix with 4.2% bitumen is a valid candidate for Optimum Bitumen Content (OBC). Final OBC to be fixed after testing 3–5 trial bitumen contents and reading Vv = 4% from the plot.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024]** *(a)* Discuss the **construction procedure and equipment used for Bituminous Concrete (BC) layer** in flexible pavement. Include details of mix design, surface preparation, laying, rolling, and quality control. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* List and explain the **material characterization tests** (with IS code references) required for the following constituents of M40 Pavement Quality Concrete: Cement, Coarse Aggregate, and Water. State the acceptance criteria for each test. *(7 marks)*

### **Solution B:**

#### **Part (a) — BC Construction Procedure (7 marks)**

**1. Mix Design (1.5 marks):**
- BC is designed by **Marshall method** (75 blows/side for heavy traffic) per ASTM D6927 or MoRTH Clause 507.
- Target parameters: Stability ≥ 9 kN, Flow 2–4 mm, Vv = 3–5%, VMA ≥ 14%, VFB = 65–78%, **TSR ≥ 80%** (AASHTO T 283 moisture susceptibility).
- Aggregate grading: 13.2 mm NMAS, dense-graded per MoRTH Table 500-17. Bitumen grade 60/70 (VG-30) or modified bitumen (PMB 40/70) for expressways. Bitumen content typically 5.0–6.5% by weight of mix.
- Anti-stripping agent (Zycosoil/Dop) at 0.1–0.5% of bitumen for moisture-prone zones.

**2. Surface Preparation (1 mark):**
- The underlying BM layer must be **clean, dry, and free of loose material**.
- **Tack coat** of bitumen emulsion (RS-1, slow-setting) at 0.20–0.30 kg/m² is sprayed uniformly using a mechanical distributor.
- Tack coat provides adhesion between BM (base course) and BC (surface course). Allow emulsion to "break" (turn from brown to black) before laying BC.

**3. Production of Mix (1 mark):**
- Aggregates are **dried and heated to 160–180°C** in a drum dryer.
- Bitumen is **heated to 150–165°C** (safe heating range; flash point ~250°C).
- Mixing is done in a **batch-type or drum-mix hot mix plant** for 30–60 seconds until uniform coating.
- Plant temperature of mix at discharge: **150–165°C**.

**4. Transportation (0.5 marks):**
- Insulated trucks with covered tarpaulin to prevent heat loss and contamination.
- Mix temperature at site (delivery) must be **≥ 140°C** for placement.

**5. Laying (1 mark):**
- **Mechanical hydrostatic paver** (e.g., Vögele, Wirtgen) spreads mix in uniform layer thickness (30–50 mm compacted).
- Paver is fitted with **screed heater** and automatic grade and slope control.
- Laying temperature ≥ **135°C** for unmodified bitumen, ≥ **150°C** for modified bitumen.

**6. Compaction (Rolling Sequence) (1.5 marks):**
- **Initial (breakdown) rolling:** 8–10 ton static tandem roller, immediately behind paver, 3–4 passes.
- **Intermediate rolling:** 8–12 ton vibratory roller (or pneumatic-tyred 12–15 ton), 4–6 passes — *this is the main compaction stage*.
- **Final rolling:** 8–10 ton static tandem roller, 2–3 passes, to remove roller marks.
- Rolling must commence while mix is **above 100°C**; cease when mix falls below **80°C**.

**7. Quality Control (0.5 marks):**
- **Density test** by core cutter or nuclear gauge — must achieve **≥ 97% of Marshall density**.
- **Surface evenness** by 3 m straight edge — max deviation **4 mm** longitudinally, **6 mm** transversely for major roads.
- **Skid resistance** by British Pendulum Test — minimum **SN ≥ 60 BPN** for expressways.
- **Surface texture** by sand patch test — minimum **0.6 mm** texture depth for BC.

#### **Part (b) — Material Characterization for M40 PQC (7 marks)**

| Material | Test (IS Code) | Acceptance Criterion | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cement (OPC 53)** | 1. Fineness by Blaine/ Sieve (IS 4031) | Residue on 90 μm sieve < 10%; specific surface ≥ 2250 cm²/g | 0.5 |
|  | 2. Initial & Final Setting Time (IS 4031) | Initial ≥ 30 min, Final ≤ 600 min | 0.5 |
|  | 3. Soundness by Le Chatelier (IS 4031) | Expansion ≤ 10 mm | 0.25 |
|  | 4. Compressive Strength (IS 4031) | 28-day ≥ 53 MPa for OPC 53 | 0.5 |
| **Coarse Aggregate (20 + 10 mm crushed)** | 1. Los Angeles Abrasion (IS 2386 Part IV) | LA Abrasion ≤ 30% for PQC | 0.5 |
|  | 2. Aggregate Impact Value (IS 2386 Part IV) | AIV ≤ 30% | 0.5 |
|  | 3. Combined Flakiness + Elongation Index (IS 2386 Part I) | FI + EI ≤ 30% | 0.5 |
|  | 4. Water Absorption (IS 2386 Part III) | ≤ 2% | 0.25 |
|  | 5. Alkali-Aggregate Reactivity (IS 383) | Innocuous / Low reactivity (especially if using reactive aggregates) | 0.25 |
| **Water (Mixing)** | 1. pH Test (IS 3025) | 6.0 ≤ pH ≤ 8.0 | 0.5 |
|  | 2. Chloride Content (IS 3025) | ≤ 500 mg/L (for plain PQC; ≤ 200 mg/L for pre-tensioned concrete) | 0.5 |
|  | 3. Sulphate Content (IS 3025) | ≤ 400 mg/L (as SO₄²⁻) | 0.5 |
|  | 4. Total Dissolved Solids (IS 3025) | ≤ 2000 mg/L | 0.25 |
|  | 5. Organic Content (IS 3025) | Free from oils, acids, alkalis, salt, organic matter | 0.25 |

**Engineer's Quality Statement (1 mark):**
All incoming material lots must carry **Manufacturer's Test Certificate (MTC)** and undergo **independent third-party testing** at NABL-accredited labs for every 50–100 m³ of concrete or change in source. Any material not meeting IS 456:2000 criteria shall be **rejected and removed from site** — no compromise on rigid pavement materials.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones — KTU 2024 Examiner Insights:**
>
> 1. **Confusing BM with DBM (Dense Bituminous Macadam):** DBM is a *graded* mix with Vv 3–5% (similar to BM), but with stricter grading and is the *base* course in modern MoRTH specifications. BM is older terminology. Examiners expect you to mention both the new (DBM) and old (BM) nomenclature.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert to percentages:** In VMA calculations, students often forget that $P_s = 100 - P_b$ and use 100 instead of $(100 - P_b)$. This gives a 10–15% error in VMA — losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Writing w/c for concrete but using cement grade OPC 43 instead of OPC 53:** PQC for expressways must use OPC 53. Using 43 grade is a 1-mark penalty.
> 4. **Not specifying the IS code for each test:** Examiners want "IS 2386 Part IV" not just "abrasion test." Always write the part number.
> 5. **Forgetting curing period:** PQC must be cured for **minimum 14 days** (initial) and 28 days (full). Writing "cured for 3 days" loses a mark.
> 6. **Confusing Tack Coat and Prime Coat:** Tack coat is for **bituminous surface to bituminous layer** (RS-1 emulsion). Prime coat is for **granular layer to bituminous layer** (medium-curing cutback MC-30). Mixing these up is a classic 3-mark loser.
> 7. **Stability values:** Specified as **9.0 kN** for both BM and BC in MoRTH — students often write 8 kN (older IRC) and lose 1 mark.
> 8. **Dowel bar diameter:** MoRTH specifies **32 mm dia, 500 mm long, 300 mm c/c** for slabs up to 250 mm. Writing "25 mm" is a penalty.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **BM is base/binder course, BC is wearing/surface course.** Don't reverse them.
- **NMAS:** BM = 26.5/19 mm; BC = 13.2 mm; PQC = 31.5 mm.
- **Layer thickness:** BM 50–100 mm; BC 30–50 mm; PQC 200–350 mm.
- **Three pillars of bituminous mix design:** Vv (3–5%), VMA (≥13% for BM, ≥14% for BC), VFB (65–75% BM, 65–78% BC).
- **Formulas to memorize:** $V_v = \dfrac{G_{mm}-G_{mb}}{G_{mm}}\times 100$; $VMA = 100 - \dfrac{G_{mb}P_s}{G_{sb}}$; $VFB = \dfrac{VMA-V_v}{VMA}\times 100$.
- **Marshall Stability min** for both BM and BC = **9.0 kN**; **75 blows/face** for heavy traffic.
- **TSR ≥ 80%** is the moisture-susceptibility test, critical for BC.
- **Bitumen grades:** 60/70 (VG-30) for most regions; 30/40 for hot climates; PMB 40/70 for expressways.
- **Laying temperature for BC:** ≥ 135°C (unmodified), ≥ 150°C (modified). Cease rolling below 80°C.
- **Compaction of BC:** Initial 8–10 t tandem → Intermediate 8–12 t vibratory / 12–15 t PTR → Final 8–10 t tandem. Density target ≥ 97% Marshall.
- **Tack coat** (RS-1 emulsion, 0.20–0.30 kg/m²) on BM before BC. **Prime coat** (MC-30 cutback, 0.60–0.80 kg/m²) on WMM before BM.
- **PQC grade:** M40 (40 MPa compressive) with flexural strength ≥ 4.5 MPa. Use $f_{cr} = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$.
- **PQC w/c:** 0.40–0.45. Min cement = 350 kg/m³. Max NMAS = 31.5 mm.
- **Joint spacings for M40 PQC:** Transverse contraction 4.5–5.0 m, longitudinal per lane (3.5 m for 3.5 m lane), expansion only at structures.
- **Dowel bar:** 32 mm dia × 500 mm long @ 300 mm c/c for slabs ≤ 250 mm thick.
- **Tie bar:** 12 mm dia deformed bar @ 600 mm c/c along longitudinal joint.
- **Curing of PQC:** Wet burlap or curing compound, **minimum 14 days** (MORTH says 14 days, IRC:SP:46 says 28 days for premium pavements).
- **Critical aggregate tests for PQC:** LA Abrasion ≤ 30%, AIV ≤ 30%, FI+EI ≤ 30%, water absorption ≤ 2%.
- **Water quality:** pH 6–8, Cl⁻ ≤ 500 mg/L, SO₄²⁻ ≤ 400 mg/L.
- **Concrete mix design code:** IRC:44 (Indian Roads Congress) is the **mandatory** reference for PQC; IS 10262 is for general RCC.
- **Skid resistance** of finished BC: ≥ 60 BPN for expressways, ≥ 50 BPN for NH/SH.
- **Surface regularity:** 3 m straight edge deviation ≤ 4 mm longitudinal, ≤ 6 mm transverse for major roads.
- **Cement grade for PQC:** OPC 53 (preferred) for expressways; OPC 43 acceptable for rural/smaller roads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always cite the **MoRTH clause number** (e.g., "MoRTH 504 for BM," "MoRTH 507 for BC," "MoRTH 602 for CC pavement") and the **IS/IRC code** (e.g., "IS 2386 Part IV," "IRC:44," "IRC:58") in your answers. Examiners explicitly award 1 mark for the "code reference" in every 7-mark sub-question.

**End of KTU 2024 Module 3 Notes — PECET745 Pavement Construction and Management**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of Subgrade and Base
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET745 - PAVEMENT CONSTRUCTION AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Preparation of Subgrade and Base ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Subgrade**: The compacted natural soil or imported earth material lying immediately below the pavement structure, which serves as the foundation upon which the pavement (sub-base, base, and surface) is constructed. It is the *prepared and compacted* top layer of the formation soil that transmits traffic loads to the roadbed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Base Course**: The layer of specified or selected material of designed thickness, placed immediately above the sub-base (or subgrade if no sub-base is used), which constitutes the principal structural layer of a flexible pavement. It distributes wheel loads to the sub-base/subgrade and provides drainage, frost resistance, and resistance to deformation. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are building a house. Before laying bricks, you: 1. Clear the land (remove vegetation, debris, topsoil) 2. Level it and dig to a firm stratum 3. Compact the soil so it does not settle later 4. Lay a strong stone/brick platform (the foundation) A pavement is built the **exact same way**: - The **subgrade** is like the *compacted earth foundation* — it must be uniform, dense, and stable. - The **base course** is like the *strong platform* sitting on the earth — it carries the actual traffic load and spreads it. - Skip the compaction of earth, and the house (pavement) cracks. Skip the base, and the surface fails prematurely. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric (KTU/MoRTH/IRC conventions)**: > - Subgrade compaction requirement: ≥ **97% of Maximum Dry Density (MDD)** for flexible pavements as per **MoRTH Specifications (5th Revision)**. > - Base course compaction: ≥ **98% of MDD** for granular bases; **100% of MDD** for cementitious bases. > - Typical subgrade strength benchmark: **CBR ≥ 8%** (for design traffic up to 30 msa — million standard axles). ### 1.3 Why Preparation Matters The cost of *correct preparation* is **5–10%** of total pavement cost, but failure to prepare properly causes **80–90%** of premature pavement failures (rutting, depressions, cracking). This is why KTU examiners frequently test this module — it is the *practical heart* of pavement engineering. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operations: Subgrade Preparation The construction sequence for subgrade preparation is rigidly defined in **MoRTH Section 300** and **IRC:36 / IRC:SP:72**. The six canonical stages are: 1. **Clearing and Grubbing** (MoRTH Clause 201) - Removal of all vegetation, bushes, trees (girth < 300 mm), stumps, roots, organic matter. - Top organic soil (depth **150–300 mm**) stripped and disposed of. - *Why*: Organic matter decays → settlement, voids → uneven support. 2. **Setting Out and Earthwork in Cutting/Filling** - Establish centerline, benchmarks, cross-section profiles. - For *fills* (> 500 mm), benching into the existing slope (steps of 300 mm) for bond. - For *cuts*, excavate to the formation level + a surcharge (typically 200–300 mm) to allow for loosening. 3. **Moisture Conditioning** - Add water (sprinkling) or aerate (disc harrow) to bring soil moisture within **±2% of OMC** (Optimum Moisture Content). - *Why*: Compaction is most efficient at OMC; deviations reduce dry density. 4. **Spreading and Layered Compaction** - Soil spread in **loose layers of 200–300 mm thickness** (compacted to 150–200 mm). - Each layer compacted before placing the next. - Compactors: sheep-foot roller (for cohesive soils), vibratory smooth-wheel roller (for granular soils), pneumatic-tyred roller (for sealing/finishing). 5. **Proof Rolling (Plate Load Test / Loaded Truck Test)** - A loaded truck (rear axle load **80–100 kN**) or a smooth-wheel roller is run over the prepared subgrade. - *Purpose*: Detects soft/yielding pockets requiring rework. - **Acceptance criterion**: No rutting > **5 mm** under the loaded wheel. 6. **Trimming and Shaping to Required Grade and Camber** - Final surface shaped to design camber (typically **2–2.5%** for bituminous pavements, **2.5–3%** for unpaved). - Surface regularity: deviation not exceeding **15 mm** under a 3 m straight edge (MoRTH Table 300-2). ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operations: Base Course Preparation The base course construction follows this protocol: 1. **Material Selection & Approval** - Tests conducted *before* placement: **Gradation, Atterberg limits, CBR, Aggregate Impact Value (AIV), Los Angeles Abrasion (LAA), Water Absorption, Soundness**. - Acceptance criteria (per MoRTH Table 400-3 for WBM, etc.): | Property | Granular Base (WBM/WMM) | Cementitious Base (CTB/CRM) | |----------|------------------------|-----------------------------| | CBR (soaked, 4 days) | ≥ 80% (WMM) / ≥ 100% (CTB) | ≥ 100% | | AIV | ≤ 30% | ≤ 30% | | LAA | ≤ 40% | ≤ 35% | | PI of fines | ≤ 6 | Non-plastic | | Liquid Limit | ≤ 25 | ≤ 25 | 2. **Transportation and Spreading** - Aggregate tipped in measured heaps/spreader boxes for uniform thickness. - Avoiding segregation is a key KTU-marked point. 3. **Mixing** (for cementitious bases) - **Mix-in-place** (using rotavator/mixer) or **Plant mix** (central mixing plant). - Mixing time strictly limited (**≤ 2 hours** for CTB) — beyond initial setting, cement loses workability. 4. **Compaction** - Begins *immediately* after spreading/mixing. - Equipment: **Vibratory roller (8–10 ton static weight)**, **Pneumatic-tyred roller (12–15 ton)**, **Smooth-wheel roller (8–10 ton)** for finish rolling. - Sequence: Initial (vibratory) → Intermediate (pneumatic) → Final (smooth-wheel static). 5. **Curing** (cementitious bases only) - Water curing for **7 days** (CTB) / **14 days** (CTB with fly ash) — *this is a KTU favourite question*. 6. **Priming** (before next layer) - Application of **low-viscosity cutback bitumen (MC-30)** at **0.6–0.9 kg/m²** to seal the surface. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All formulas below are board-tested and frequently appear in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.** | # | Formula / Relationship | Variables | Standard Units | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{\gamma}{1 + w}$ | $\gamma_d$ = dry unit weight, $\gamma$ = bulk unit weight, $w$ = moisture content | kN/m³ | Field density determination (sand replacement / core cutter) | | 2 | $\rho_d = \dfrac{100 \cdot \rho}{100 + w}$ | $\rho_d$ = dry density, $\rho$ = wet density, $w$ = moisture % | g/cc or Mg/m³ | Compactness check; compare to MDD | | 3 | $\text{Relative Compaction} = \dfrac{\rho_{d,\text{field}}}{\rho_{d,\max}} \times 100\%$ | $\rho_{d,\max}$ = MDD from Proctor | % | Acceptance criterion (≥ 97% subgrade, ≥ 98% base) | | 4 | $R_c = \dfrac{\text{Load on test piston}}{\text{Load on standard aggregate at } 2.5 \text{ mm penetration}} \times 100\%$ | California Bearing Ratio | % | Subgrade / base strength assessment | | 5 | $\text{Camper / Crossfall} = \dfrac{\text{Rise}}{\text{Run}} \times 100\%$ | Camber (2–3%) for drainage | % | Surface shaping requirement | | 6 | $\text{Loose thickness} = \dfrac{\text{Compacted thickness}}{\text{Shrinkage factor}}$ | Layer thickness control | mm | Construction staging | | 7 | $\text{Swell factor} = \dfrac{\rho_{d,\text{insitu}}}{\rho_{d,\text{loose}}}$ | Volume change in earthwork | – | Cut-to-fill volume balance | | 8 | $\text{Cement content} = \dfrac{W_{\text{cement}}}{W_{\text{dry aggregate}}} \times 100\%$ | For CTB/CRM, typically 3–6% | % | Mix design | ### 2.4 Equipment Summary (Frequently Asked) | Equipment | Used For | Compactor Type | Typical Static Weight | |---|---|---|---| | Sheep-foot Roller | Clayey subgrade, deep lifts | Kneading | 8–15 ton | | Smooth-Wheel Vibratory | Granular base, finishing | Vibration | 8–12 ton | | Pneumatic-Tyred Roller | Both, sealing surface | Kneading + pressure | 12–25 ton | | Padfoot / Tamping Foot | Heavy clay fills | High-pressure kneading | 12–18 ton | | Tandem Roller | Surface layers, finish | Static + vibratory | 6–10 ton | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Field Dry Density from Sand Replacement Method (KTU Frequently Asked) The **Sand Replacement Method (IS 2720 Part 28)** is the standard field density test for subgrade and base acceptance. The derivation proceeds as follows: **Given (illustrative numbers for derivation):** - Mass of sand filling calibrating container, $M_c = 1180$ g - Volume of calibrating container, $V_c = 1000$ cm³ - Mass of sand (pycnometer + sand) before pouring, $M_1 = 4500$ g - Mass of sand (pycnometer + sand) after pouring into hole, $M_2 = 2150$ g - Mass of wet soil from hole, $M_w = 2450$ g - Moisture content, $w = 12\%$ **Step 1: Determine bulk density of sand ($\rho_s$)** The bulk (loose) density of the standard sand (graded, passing 600-micron IS sieve and retained on 300-micron) is calibrated: $$ \rho_s = \dfrac{M_c}{V_c} $$ Substituting values: $$ \rho_s = \dfrac{1180 \text{ g}}{1000 \text{ cm}^3} = 1.180 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$ **Step 2: Determine mass of sand filling the test hole ($M_h$)** This is the mass of sand that occupies the excavated hole volume: $$ M_h = M_1 - M_2 $$ $$ M_h = 4500 - 2150 = 2350 \text{ g} $$ **Step 3: Determine volume of the test hole ($V_h$)** Volume equals mass of sand divided by its bulk density: $$ V_h = \dfrac{M_h}{\rho_s} $$ $$ V_h = \dfrac{2350}{1.180} = 1991.5 \text{ cm}^3 $$ **Step 4: Determine bulk (wet) density of soil ($\rho$)** $$ \rho = \dfrac{M_w}{V_h} $$ $$ \rho = \dfrac{2450}{1991.5} = 1.230 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$ **Step 5: Determine dry density of soil ($\rho_d$)** $$ \rho_d = \dfrac{\rho}{1 + w} $$ $$ \rho_d = \dfrac{1.230}{1 + 0.12} = 1.098 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$ **Step 6: Determine Relative Compaction** Assume MDD from Proctor test = **1.78 g/cm³** (for the same soil): $$ \text{Relative Compaction} = \dfrac{\rho_d}{\text{MDD}} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{Relative Compaction} = \dfrac{1.098}{1.78} \times 100 = 61.7\% $$ > [!WARNING] > A relative compaction of only **61.7%** is far below the 97% requirement — the section would be **rejected** and requires rework. KTU students often misapply the formula by using wet density directly, losing easy marks. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Volume Relationship — Cut, Fill, and Shrinkage In earthwork, the volume of soil *changes* between bank (in-situ), loose, and compacted states. KTU exams commonly test this. **Define:** - $V_B$ = Bank volume (in-situ) - $V_L$ = Loose volume (after excavation) - $V_C$ = Compacted volume (after placement in fill) - $S$ = **Swell factor** = $V_L / V_B$ (typically 1.2 to 1.4 for clays, 1.1 to 1.2 for sands) - $Sh$ = **Shrinkage factor** = $V_C / V_B$ (typically 0.85 to 0.95) **Relationship:** $$ V_L = S \cdot V_B $$ $$ V_C = Sh \cdot V_B $$ **Example:** A cut yields $V_B = 5000$ m³ of bank soil. Compute fill volume if $Sh = 0.90$. $$ V_C = 0.90 \times 5000 = 4500 \text{ m}^3 $$ The shrinkage percentage (a common KTU 3-mark question): $$ \%\text{Shrinkage} = (1 - Sh) \times 100 = (1 - 0.90) \times 100 = 10\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Swell vs Shrinkage**: Soil *increases* in volume when excavated (swell, because of loosening) but *decreases* when compacted into a fill (shrinkage, relative to bank). The two are often confused in KTU answers. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Stiffness Modulus from CBR (used in design checks) While the *pavement design* module covers this in detail, the relationship between CBR and elastic modulus is sometimes asked: $$ E \text{ (MPa)} \approx 10 \cdot \text{CBR (\%)} \quad \text{(for } \text{CBR} \leq 10 \text{)} $$ For higher CBR (granular base): $$ E \text{ (MPa)} \approx 17.6 \cdot (\text{CBR})^{0.64} $$ **Example:** Subgrade CBR = 8%. Compute modulus. $$ E = 10 \times 8 = 80 \text{ MPa} $$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Acceptable Compaction Standards (KTU Memorization) | Layer | Equipment | Number of Passes | Compaction % | Surface Tolerance (3 m straight edge) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Embankment (top 500 mm) | Sheep-foot / padfoot | 6–8 | ≥ 97% MDD | ≤ 25 mm | | Subgrade | Vibratory + pneumatic | 5–7 | ≥ 97% MDD | ≤ 15 mm | | Granular Sub-base (GSB) | Vibratory | 4–6 | ≥ 98% MDD | ≤ 12 mm | | WBM / WMM Base | Vibratory + pneumatic | 5–8 | ≥ 98% MDD | ≤ 10 mm | | CTB / CRM Base | Vibratory + smooth | 4–6 | ≥ 98% MDD | ≤ 8 mm | | DBM / BC | Tandem + pneumatic | 4–6 | ≥ 97% MDD | ≤ 6 mm | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Flexible Pavement Cross-Section (Layer Hierarchy) ```mermaid graph TD A["SURFACE COURSE
Bituminous Concrete / Mastic Asphalt"]:::top B["BINDER COURSE
Dense Bituminous Macadam DBM"]:::mid C["BASE COURSE
WBM / WMM / CTB / CRM"]:::base D["SUB-BASE COURSE
Granular Sub-Base GSB"]:::sub E["SUBGRADE
Compacted Natural Soil"]:::sg F["NATURAL SOIL FORMATION"]:::form A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F classDef top fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef mid fill:#2d2d2d,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef base fill:#404040,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef sub fill:#595959,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef sg fill:#737373,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef form fill:#8c8c8c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Subgrade Preparation Process Flow (Sequential Operations) ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["STEP 1: SITE INVESTIGATION
and SURVEY"]:::step S2["STEP 2: CLEARING and GRUBBING
Remove vegetation, topsoil 150-300 mm"]:::step S3["STEP 3: EXCAVATION or FILL
Cut to formation level
or place selected fill"]:::step S4["STEP 4: MOISTURE CONDITIONING
Bring soil to OMC plus or minus 2 percent"]:::step S5["STEP 5: SPREADING in LAYERS
Loose lift 200-300 mm"]:::step S6["STEP 6: COMPACTION
Sheep-foot or vibratory roller"]:::step S7["STEP 7: FIELD DENSITY TEST
Sand replacement or core cutter"]:::step S8{"DECISION
Relative compaction
greater than 97 percent?"}:::decision S9["STEP 8: PROOF ROLLING
Loaded truck 80-100 kN"]:::step S10["STEP 9: TRIMMING and SHAPING
Camber 2 to 3 percent"]:::step S11["STEP 10: APPROVAL and HANDOVER
to next layer"]:::finish S12["REWORK
Re-compact, re-test"]:::rework S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 S8 -- YES --> S9 --> S10 --> S11 S8 -- NO --> S12 --> S5 classDef step fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef finish fill:#404040,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef rework fill:#a0a0a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Base Course Construction — Functional Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INP["MATERIAL INPUT"] M1["Aggregate Stockpile
Tested: Gradation AIV LAA"]:::box M2["Binder Cement
OPC 43 or 53 grade"]:::box M3["Water
Potable pH greater than 6"]:::box end subgraph PROC["CONSTRUCTION PROCESSING"] P1["Proportioning
by weight or volume"]:::box P2["Mixing
Plant or in-situ"]:::box P3["Transport and Spread
via paver or grader"]:::box P4["Compaction
within 2 hours
of cement addition"]:::box P5["Curing
7 days water spray"]:::box P6["Priming
MC-30 at 0.6 to 0.9 kg per sq m"]:::box end subgraph OUT["QUALITY OUTPUT"] Q1["Field Density Test"]:::box Q2["Surface Regularity Test"]:::box Q3["Strength Test on Cores"]:::box Q4["Approved Base Layer"]:::finish end M1 --> P1 M2 --> P1 M3 --> P1 P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 P6 --> Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3 --> Q4 classDef box fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef finish fill:#404040,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ### 4.4 Compaction Equipment Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD Q{"SOIL TYPE?"}:::q CLAY["Cohesive Soil
Clayey Silt Clay"]:::node SAND["Cohesionless Soil

Kneading action
8 to 15 ton"]:::eq S2["Vibratory SMOOTH-WHEEL
8 to 12 ton
High frequency"]:::eq S3["PNEUMATIC-TYRED
12 to 25 ton
Kneading plus pressure"]:::eq S4["Vibratory plus Pneumatic
Combined fleet"]:::eq Q --> CLAY --> S1 Q --> SAND --> S2 Q --> MIX --> S4 S2 --> S3 classDef q fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px classDef node fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef eq fill:#f2f2f2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.5 Test Frequency Matrix (Quality Control on Subgrade and Base) ```mermaid graph LR A["FIELD DENSITY
1 test per 500 sq m"]:::t B["MOISTURE CONTENT
1 per 250 sq m"]:::t C["CBR
1 per 1000 sq m or
change of material"]:::t D["GRADATION
1 per 200 cu m"]:::t E["ATTERBERG LIMITS
1 per 1000 sq m"]:::t F["SURFACE REGULARITY
3 m straight edge
continuous"]:::t G["PROOF ROLLING
Whole area
loaded truck"]:::t A -.-> H["SUBGRADE Q.C."]:::hl B -.-> H C -.-> H D -.-> H E -.-> H F -.-> H G -.-> H classDef t fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px classDef hl fill:#595959,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define subgrade. List any four properties that an ideal subgrade soil should possess.* > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** The subgrade is the compacted natural earth or selected soil layer lying immediately below the pavement, which transmits the traffic load from the upper layers to the roadbed. > **Properties (2 marks — any four):** > 1. Adequate bearing strength (CBR ≥ 8% for design traffic up to 30 msa). > 2. Low compressibility / volume change. > 3. Stability under moisture variation (low swelling, low frost susceptibility). > 4. Easy workability and compactability. > 5. Good drainage characteristics. > 6. Free from organic and deleterious matter. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What is meant by "proof rolling" of the subgrade? State its purpose and the standard criterion for acceptance.* > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** Proof rolling is the procedure of rolling the prepared subgrade with a heavy loaded truck (rear axle load 80–100 kN) or a smooth-wheel roller to identify soft or yielding areas before placing the pavement. > **Purpose (1 mark):** To detect weak, loose, or unstable pockets that may cause differential settlement and to confirm uniform support across the prepared surface. > **Acceptance criterion (1 mark):** No rutting or depression greater than **5 mm** shall be observed under the loaded wheel. Pockets showing > 5 mm rutting must be reworked and re-tested. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > **ESE Module-3 Instructions**: *Answer the following. Choice is internal within the module.* --- **Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] — Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Describe, in sequence, the various steps involved in the preparation of subgrade for a flexible pavement. Mention the equipment used and the quality control tests to be conducted at each stage. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A field density test by sand replacement method gave the following data: - Mass of sand filling calibrating container, $M_c$ = 1240 g - Volume of calibrating container, $V_c$ = 1000 cm³ - Initial mass of sand in pycnometer, $M_1$ = 4620 g - Final mass of sand in pycnometer, $M_2$ = 2210 g - Mass of wet soil excavated from the hole, $M_w$ = 2580 g - Moisture content of the soil, $w$ = 14.2% - Maximum dry density (Proctor) of the soil = 1.76 g/cm³ Determine the **relative compaction** and comment on the acceptability of the layer. **(7 marks)** > **Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:** > The subgrade preparation involves the following steps (each step carries ~1 mark): > 1. **Clearing and grubbing**: Removal of vegetation, organic soil (150–300 mm depth). > 2. **Setting out**: Marking centerline, levels, cross-section profiles. > 3. **Excavation / Filling**: Cut to formation level or place selected fill in benches. > 4. **Moisture conditioning**: Soil brought to OMC ± 2% by sprinkling or aeration. > 5. **Spreading**: Loose lift 200–300 mm; spread by motor grader. > 6. **Compaction**: Sheep-foot roller (cohesive) or vibratory smooth-wheel (granular); followed by pneumatic-tyred roller for sealing. > 7. **Field testing**: Sand replacement / core cutter test for density; rapid moisture tester. > 8. **Proof rolling**: Loaded truck (80–100 kN); rutting criterion < 5 mm. > 9. **Trimming and shaping**: To required grade, camber 2–3%; tolerance ≤ 15 mm under 3 m straight edge. > 10. **Approval**: Submission of test results to Engineer-in-Charge. > > **Equipment**: Bulldozer, scraper, motor grader, sheep-foot roller, vibratory roller, pneumatic-tyred roller, water tanker, loaded truck. > > **Quality control tests**: Gradation, Atterberg limits, CBR, Proctor, field density, moisture content, surface regularity. > **Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:** > **Step 1 — Bulk density of sand**: > $$\rho_s = \dfrac{M_c}{V_c} = \dfrac{1240}{1000} = 1.240 \text{ g/cm}^3$$ > **Step 2 — Mass of sand in hole**: > $$M_h = M_1 - M_2 = 4620 - 2210 = 2410 \text{ g}$$ > **Step 3 — Volume of hole**: > $$V_h = \dfrac{M_h}{\rho_s} = \dfrac{2410}{1.240} = 1943.5 \text{ cm}^3$$ > **Step 4 — Wet density of soil**: > $$\rho = \dfrac{M_w}{V_h} = \dfrac{2580}{1943.5} = 1.327 \text{ g/cm}^3$$ > **Step 5 — Dry density of soil**: > $$\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho}{1 + w} = \dfrac{1.327}{1 + 0.142} = 1.162 \text{ g/cm}^3$$ > **Step 6 — Relative compaction**: > $$\text{RC} = \dfrac{\rho_d}{\text{MDD}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.162}{1.76} \times 100 = 66.0\%$$ > **Comment**: The achieved relative compaction is **66.0%**, which is far below the required **97%** of MDD. The layer is **NOT acceptable** and must be reworked by re-conditioning moisture, re-compacting, and re-testing. --- **Q4. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction procedure of **Wet Mix Macadam (WMM)** base course. State the quality control tests and the frequency of testing as per MoRTH specifications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Differentiate between **Granular Base (WBM)**, **Wet Mix Macadam (WMM)**, and **Cement Treated Base (CTB)** with respect to (i) material composition, (ii) construction method, (iii) strength mechanism, (iv) curing requirement, and (v) typical applications. **(7 marks)** > **Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:** > **Construction Procedure of WMM** (each step ~1 mark): > 1. **Material procurement**: Crushed stone aggregates conforming to MoRTH Table 400-3; grading envelope specified; non-plastic fines. > 2. **Mixing**: Done in a central mixing plant (pugmill-type) with controlled water addition to achieve OMC ± 1%. > 3. **Transportation**: By tipper trucks to the laying site, ensuring no segregation. > 4. **Spreading**: By a mechanical paver finisher or motor grader; uniform loose thickness for the specified compacted thickness (typically 75–200 mm). > 5. **Compaction**: Begins immediately after spreading. Use 8–10 ton vibratory roller, 4–6 passes; followed by 12–15 ton pneumatic-tyred roller for sealing. > 6. **Finishing**: Final pass by a 8–10 ton smooth-wheel static roller. > 7. **Quality Control Tests & Frequency**: > - Gradation: 1 test per 200 m³ of mix > - Atterberg limits: 1 per 1000 m² > - Field density: 1 per 500 m² (target ≥ 98% MDD) > - Surface regularity: 3 m straight edge, deviation ≤ 10 mm > - CBR (soaked): 1 per 1000 m² or material change > - Thickness: 1 per 300 m > **Model Answer (b) — 7 marks (Comparative Table):** > | Parameter | WBM | WMM | CTB | > |---|---|---|---| > | (i) Composition | Crushed aggregates + screenings + binding material (moorum/moorum-soil) as a binder; water-bound | Well-graded crushed aggregates + fine aggregate (non-plastic) + water; no binding fines | Crushed aggregates + Ordinary Portland Cement (3–6%) + water | > | (ii) Construction | Layer-by-layer (sub-base, base, finishing layer) with rolling and water-spraying; uses hand-packing for coarse aggregates | Plant-mixed, machine-laid, single-layer compaction | Mix-in-place (rotavator) or plant-mix; compacted within 2 hours; cured 7 days | > | (iii) Strength Mechanism | Mechanical interlock of coarse aggregates; water-bound bond (low stability) | Mechanical interlock + density + cohesive-less friction; superior stability | Cementitious hydration bond; high tensile and flexural strength | > | (iv) Curing | Not required | Not required (but 24-hr moist condition may be specified) | **Mandatory water curing for 7 days** (or 14 days with fly ash) | > | (v) Typical Applications | Low-volume rural roads, village roads | Medium to high-volume highways as a base under bituminous surfacing | High-volume expressways, airport pavements, heavy-traffic corridors | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls**: > 1. **Confusing WBM with WMM** — WBM is *water-bound* (multiple layers, screenings as binder), WMM is *plant-mixed and vibrated* (single dense layer). Drawing them as the same is a common 2-mark loss. > 2. **Forgetting curing in CTB** — Many students omit the 7-day water-curing step, losing a full mark. > 3. **Wrong compaction percentage** — Subgrade needs **97%**; WMM/WBM needs **98%**; CTB/CRM needs **98%**; surface courses need **97%** of MDD. Mixing these up is the most common Part A error. > 4. **In proof rolling, the criterion is *rut depth* < 5 mm**, *not* the absence of rutting entirely. Some students write "no rutting allowed," which is technically over-conservative and shows lack of field knowledge. > 5. **In the sand-replacement derivation, dividing wet density by MDD directly** (without converting to dry density first) is a classic mistake that costs 2–3 marks in numerical answers. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Must-Remember Highlights for the KTU ESE:** - **Subgrade compaction**: ≥ **97% of MDD** (MoRTH); in field, dry density is computed via $\rho_d = \rho / (1 + w)$. - **Base compaction**: ≥ **98% of MDD** (granular) and ≥ **98%** (cementitious). - **CBR benchmarks**: Subgrade ≥ 8%, Sub-base ≥ 25–30%, WMM ≥ 80%, CTB ≥ 100%. - **Proof rolling**: Loaded truck (80–100 kN), rutting ≤ **5 mm** allowed. - **Camber (cross-fall)**: 2–2.5% bituminous, 2.5–3% WBM, 3% Earthen. - **Layer thickness rule**: Loose lift = 200–300 mm → Compacted = 150–200 mm. - **Compactor selection**: - *Clay* → Sheep-foot (kneading) - *Sand/Gravel* → Vibratory smooth-wheel - *Surface sealing* → Pneumatic-tyred - **WMM vs WBM**: WMM is *plant-mixed and graded*; WBM is *water-bound in layers*. WMM is the *modern* equivalent of WBM. - **CTB construction window**: Mixing, placing, and compacting must be completed **within 2 hours** of cement addition — beyond that, cement begins to set and bond is impaired. - **Curing of CTB**: **7 days** (OPC), **14 days** (with fly ash or low-heat cement). - **Priming**: MC-30 cutback at **0.6–0.9 kg/m²** is applied to the finished base before the next layer. - **Quality Control test frequency (key)**: Field density — 1 per **500 m²**; Gradation — 1 per **200 m³**; CBR — 1 per **1000 m²** or material change. - **Surface regularity tolerance** (3 m straight edge): Subgrade ≤ 15 mm → Sub-base ≤ 12 mm → Base ≤ 10 mm → DBM/BC ≤ 6 mm. - **Swell vs Shrinkage**: Soil *swell* when excavated (V↑), *shrink* when compacted in fill (V↓); the two terms are *not* interchangeable. - **Topsoil stripping depth**: 150–300 mm (organic matter must be removed before any earthwork). - **Benching**: For fills > 500 mm on slopes, benching at ~300 mm steps is mandatory to bond old and new fill.

topics.title: Presetting Reinforcement in Joints and PCC Slab Construction; Methods of Construction of Concrete Pavements
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🏗️ PRESETTING REINFORCEMENT IN JOINTS & PCC SLAB CONSTRUCTION

## 1.1 Presetting Reinforcement in Joints — Formal Definition

> **Definition (IRC:15-2011 / IS 6509):** *Presetting reinforcement* in concrete pavement joints refers to the deliberate installation of **dowels** (load-transfer devices across transverse expansion and contraction joints) and **tie bars** (deformed bars across longitudinal joints) at precise locations *before* the placement of the concrete mix. These reinforcements are preset into the prepared subgrade/subbase using **support assemblies (chairs/cages)** to maintain strict alignment, elevation, and spacing tolerances during concrete placement.

**Categories of Preset Joint Reinforcement:**
- **Dowel Bars** → Smooth, round, mild-steel (or epoxy-coated) bars that allow **load transfer** between adjacent slabs.
- **Tie Bars** → Deformed (ribbed) bars that prevent **horizontal separation** of longitudinal lanes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** "Preset" literally means *fixed in position before concrete is poured*. Unlike cast-in-place building RCC, pavement reinforcement is *not* tied to a cage. It is mechanically supported on chairs so that it neither sags nor shifts during concrete flow and vibration.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Train Coupling" Concept

Imagine a long railway train where every coach (slab) is joined to the next by a **rigid metal pin (dowel)** that goes into a **sleeve on one side** and is **fixed on the other**.

- When one coach (slab) is loaded and tends to *sink*, the **pin (dowel)** transfers half the load to the neighbour, so neither coach sags excessively.
- The **sleeve** allows small *horizontal* (thermal) sliding, but no vertical differential movement.

> **Tie bars** are the *side railings welded between coaches* — they don't carry vertical load but stop the two lanes (coaches) from drifting apart during braking/thermal contraction.

## 1.3 Why Presetting is Critical — Engineering Justification

| Phenomenon | Without Preset Bars | With Preset Bars |
|------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Vehicle load at joint | Edge deflection, **faulting** | Load shared, smooth transition |
| Temperature contraction | Random cracking | Crack controlled at joint |
| Differential lane settlement | Loss of crown, water pooling | Longitudinal lane locked |
| Corner breaks | High stress concentration | Stress distributed |

> [!NOTE]
> The bar diameter, length, spacing, and **embedment length** are all governed by **IRC:15-2011** and **IS 6509**. Typical dowel: **32 mm dia, 500 mm long, 300 mm spacing** for highways.

## 1.4 PCC Slab Construction — Definition

**Plain Cement Concrete (PCC) slab** in pavement engineering refers to the *unreinforced* (or nominally reinforced at joints only) concrete surface layer of a rigid pavement, constructed in panels (typically **4.5 m × 3.5 m** for national highways) separated by **contraction, expansion, and longitudinal joints**.

> **Key Distinction from Building Concrete:** PCC pavement concrete is **dry, zero-slump (or 25 mm slump)** concrete placed with **slip-form pavers** or **fixed-form pavers**, not the fluid, vibrated concrete used in buildings.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Two Major Categories of Joint Reinforcement

### (a) Dowel Bars — Load Transfer Devices

**Purpose:** Transfer wheel loads across **transverse joints** so that the *loaded* slab and the *unloaded* slab deflect together (called **load-transfer efficiency, LTE**).

**Properties (per IRC:15-2011):**

| Parameter | Specification | Engineering Reason |
|-----------|--------------|--------------------|
| Material | Mild Steel (Fe 415) or Epoxy-coated | Corrosion resistance |
| Shape | **Smooth, round** (not deformed) | Allows horizontal sliding |
| Diameter | **25 to 40 mm** (typically 32 mm) | Resists shear, limits bearing stress |
| Length | **500 to 600 mm** | Half embedded in each slab |
| Spacing | **250 to 300 mm c/c** | Distributes load across joint |
| Half embedded + half bonded (in slab) + greased + sleeved (in other slab) | — | Allows thermal expansion |
| Alignment tolerance | **±3 mm** vertical, ±6 mm horizontal | Prevents lock-up |

### (b) Tie Bars — Longitudinal Lane Ties

**Purpose:** Hold adjacent **longitudinal lanes** together; resist differential thermal/volumetric movement.

**Properties:**

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|--------------|
| Material | Deformed (ribbed) bars — Fe 500 |
| Diameter | **10 to 16 mm** |
| Length | **600 to 1000 mm** (depends on slab thickness) |
| Spacing | **500 to 750 mm c/c** |
| Embedment | Mechanically bonded (no sleeve) — both ends rigid |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic: D-Smooth-T-Deformed** → **D**owel = **S**mooth, **T**ie bar = **T**extured (deformed). This is a *favorite* KTU one-liner.

## 2.2 Presetting Procedure — Step Logic

The six-step operational sequence (the "**Why/How**" of every step):

1. **Survey & Marking of Joint Lines** → Establish exact transverse & longitudinal joint positions using total station; tolerance ±10 mm. *Why?* Misalignment propagates cracking.
2. **Subbase Preparation & Curing Compound** → Subbase must be damp, cured, and a **bond-breaking polyethylene sheet (125 micron)** laid. *Why?* Prevents slab-subbase bonding → uncontrolled cracks.
3. **Placement of Dowel Bar Assembly (Chair-supported Cage)** → The dowel cage (a welded wire assembly holding 8–10 dowels at 300 mm c/c) is placed on chairs. *Why cages?* Maintains parallelism (parallel within ±3 mm) and elevation.
4. **End Sleeve & Grease Application** → One half of each dowel is **greased (graphite grease)** and inserted into a **PVC/HDPE sleeve (25–30 mm dia, length = embedment + 25 mm)**, end capped to prevent concrete ingress. *Why?* Allows free longitudinal expansion/contraction (≥ 25 mm travel).
5. **Placement of Tie Bars (Staked or Basket-supported)** → Tie bars are mechanically anchored into the *first* lane (using stakes) so that they project **half-length** into the second lane. *Why half?* Lap-splice equivalent.
6. **Final Alignment Check (Pre-pour Survey)** → Verifying dowel alignment using a *pachometer* or string-line before concrete pouring. *Why?* Misaligned dowel = lock-up → spalling.

## 2.3 Methods of Construction of Concrete Pavements — Three Core Methods

### Method I — **Manual / Semi-Mechanized (Hand Placing)**

Used in small/irregular projects, village roads, culvert aprons.

- Forms: Timber/steel fixed side forms.
- Concrete: Hand-mixed or small-batched (350–500 L).
- Compaction: Plate vibrators + needle vibrators.
- Finishing: Hand screed (wooden/metal), bull float, trowel.
- Joints: Pre-formed joint fillers inserted manually.

> **Limitation:** Slow, low quality, not suitable for >1 km length.

### Method II — **Fixed-Form Paving (Form Paving)**

The most common KTU textbook method.

**Equipment Train (Sequence):**

1. **Form Paver (e.g., Rexcon, Gomaco Commander III)**
2. **Spreader** → Distributes concrete uniformly
3. **Vibrator** → Internal spud vibrators (12,000 vpm)
4. **Slip-form or Pan Screed Finisher** → Levels and pre-compacts
5. **Bull Float & Tining Machine** → Surface texturing (tined for skid resistance, depth 1.5–3 mm)
6. **Curing Compound Sprayer** → White-pigmented curing compound @ 0.2–0.4 kg/m²

**Features:**
- Side forms (steel, height = slab thickness) are laid, levelled, and pinned at intervals.
- Forms double as rails for the paver.
- Concrete delivered by **transit mixers**, discharged into the paver's hopper.

### Method III — **Slip-Form Paving (Most Modern)**

The paver has **no side forms** — it carries its own integral side forms (slip-forms) that shape the slab edge as the machine moves.

**Advantages over Fixed-form:**
- Continuous paving, no joint at the form (better ride quality).
- Higher productivity (1.5–2 km/day possible).
- Less labour.

**Limitations:**
- Requires **zero-slump concrete** (slump ≤ 25 mm).
- High initial equipment cost.
- Sensitive to subbase regularity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** The difference between *fixed-form* and *slip-form* paving is a guaranteed 7-mark sub-question.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> **Table: Key Formulas for Joint Reinforcement & Slab Construction**

| # | Formula / Relationship | Notation | KTU Use |
|---|------------------------|----------|---------|
| 1 | $\text{Load Transfer Efficiency (LTE)} = \dfrac{d_u}{d_l} \times 100$ | $d_u$ = deflection of unloaded slab, $d_l$ = deflection of loaded slab | Joint performance |
| 2 | $P_d = \dfrac{P \cdot S}{L}$ | $P_d$ = load on dowel, $P$ = wheel load, $S$ = dowel spacing, $L$ = joint length | Dowel load |
| 3 | $f_s = \dfrac{P_d}{A_d} = \dfrac{4 P_d}{\pi d^2}$ | $f_s$ = bearing/shear stress in dowel, $d$ = dowel dia | Dowel design check |
| 4 | $L_{tie} = \dfrac{f_s \cdot d_b}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}}$ | $L_{tie}$ = tie bar embedment, $f_s$ = allowable steel stress, $d_b$ = bar dia, $\tau_{bd}$ = bond stress (1.0–1.5 MPa) | Tie bar design |
| 5 | $A_{tie} = \dfrac{W \cdot f \cdot L_{slab}}{2 \cdot f_s}$ | $A_{tie}$ = area of tie steel per m, $W$ = slab weight/m², $f$ = friction coeff (1.5–2.0), $L_{slab}$ = slab length | Tie bar sizing |
| 6 | Joint Spacing: $L_{max} = \dfrac{2 \cdot f_t}{\gamma \cdot C \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}$ | $f_t$ = flexural strength, $\gamma$ = density, $C$ = subgrade restraint, $\alpha$ = thermal coeff, $\Delta T$ = temp drop | Slab panel length |
| 7 | Concrete Yield: $V = A \cdot t \cdot (1 + w_c)$ | $V$ = volume, $A$ = area, $t$ = thickness, $w_c$ = wastage (5%) | Material estimation |
| 8 | Slip-form Paving Speed: $v = \dfrac{Q_p}{W \cdot t}$ | $Q_p$ = paver output (m³/hr), $W$ = paving width, $t$ = thickness | Production planning |

> [!WARNING]
> In the markdown table above, all "$\vert$" or "$\mid$" notation is rendered via LaTeX — never use raw `|` inside a table cell. The KTU board does *not* deduct marks for units, but deducts for *missing* units.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Expressway Corridors (India):** Every km uses 5,500 dowels × 2 joints ≈ 11,000 dowels — presetting accuracy decides the 20-year service life.
- **Airport Runways:** Dowel alignment tolerance is stricter (2 mm) because a 50-tonne aircraft on a single wheel is far more critical than a truck wheel.
- **Urban Bus Bays:** Tie bars prevent the heavy-channelized bus lane from "wandering" away from the adjacent lane.

# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Tie Bar Spacing (Step-by-Step)

We derive the spacing of tie bars across a longitudinal joint using equilibrium of a slab contracting against subgrade friction.

**Problem Setup (per IRC:SP:62):** A PCC slab of width $B$, length $L_{slab}$, thickness $t$ is free to contract against the subgrade with friction coefficient $f$ between slab and subbase.

### Step 1 — Total Frictional Resistance Force

The total frictional force pulling back a slab from contraction is:

$$
F = W \cdot f \cdot L_{slab}
$$

where $W = \rho_c \cdot B \cdot t$ is the weight of the slab per unit length ($\rho_c$ = density of concrete ≈ 2400 kg/m³ ≈ 24 kN/m³).

> **Logic:** As the slab tries to shrink, every square metre of its underside drags against the subbase; the cumulative resistance is proportional to slab weight × friction.

### Step 2 — Force per Unit Width of Longitudinal Joint

Dividing by the width $B$:

$$
F_w = \dfrac{F}{B} = \rho_c \cdot t \cdot f \cdot L_{slab}
$$

### Step 3 — Stress Demand in Tie Steel

The required cross-sectional area of tie steel **per metre of joint** to resist this force:

$$
A_{tie} = \dfrac{F_w}{f_s} = \dfrac{\rho_c \cdot t \cdot f \cdot L_{slab}}{f_s}
$$

where $f_s$ = allowable tensile stress in tie steel (typically 230 MPa for mild steel).

### Step 4 — Number of Bars per Metre

Each bar of diameter $d_b$ provides area $\dfrac{\pi d_b^2}{4}$. With spacing $s$ (centre-to-centre), the number of bars per metre is $1/s$. So:

$$
\dfrac{1}{s} \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_b^2}{4} = A_{tie}
$$

Solving for $s$:

$$
s = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_b^2 \cdot f_s}{4 \cdot \rho_c \cdot t \cdot f \cdot L_{slab}}
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem)

**Given:** $t = 250$ mm, $L_{slab} = 30$ m, $d_b = 12$ mm, $f_s = 230$ MPa, $f = 1.5$, $\rho_c = 24$ kN/m³.

**Compute Step 1: Total Frictional Force (per m width)**

$$
F_w = 24 \times 0.25 \times 1.5 \times 30 = 270 \; \text{kN/m}
$$

**Step 2: Required Steel Area**

$$
A_{tie} = \dfrac{270 \times 10^3}{230 \times 10^6} = 1.174 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2/\text{m} = 1174 \; \text{mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

**Step 3: Spacing Calculation**

$$
s = \dfrac{\pi \times (12)^2}{4 \times 1174} \times 1000 = \dfrac{113.1}{1174} \times 1000 \approx 96.3 \; \text{mm c/c}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Conclusion:** 12 mm bars must be placed at 100 mm c/c — this is impractical for 250 mm slab. Hence, designers *increase the bar diameter* to 16 mm.

**Re-compute with 16 mm bars:**

$$
A_{bar} = \dfrac{\pi \times 16^2}{4} = 201.06 \; \text{mm}^2
$$

$$
s = \dfrac{201.06}{1174} \times 1000 \approx 171.3 \; \text{mm}
$$

> **Standard Practice (IRC:15-2011):** Use **16 mm dia @ 500 mm c/c** for highways — the excess steel is *defensive engineering margin*.

## 3.2 Derivation of Dowel Bar Diameter

The dowel bar must resist **shear** and **bearing** on the concrete beneath it.

### Bearing Stress Equation

The bearing stress under a dowel embedded $l_e$ in concrete:

$$
f_b = \dfrac{P_d}{d \cdot l_e}
$$

where $P_d$ = shear load per dowel, $d$ = dowel diameter, $l_e$ = embedded length.

**Standard Rule (IRC:15-2011):** $f_b \leq 0.6 \cdot f_{ck}$ for M40 concrete = $0.6 \times 40 = 24$ MPa.

### Substitute $P_d$ from earlier formula

$$
P_d = \dfrac{P \cdot S}{L}
$$

where $P$ = wheel load (N), $S$ = dowel spacing (mm), $L$ = joint length (mm).

### Solve for Required Dowel Diameter

$$
d_{req} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot P_d}{\pi \cdot f_{v,allow}}}
$$

where $f_{v,allow}$ = allowable shear in dowel ≈ 100 MPa for mild steel.

### Numerical Example (KTU Practice)

**Given:** $P = 50$ kN (single wheel), $S = 300$ mm, $L = 4500$ mm (joint length), $f_v = 100$ MPa.

**Step 1: Shear per Dowel**

$$
P_d = \dfrac{50{,}000 \times 300}{4500} = 3333 \; \text{N}
$$

**Step 2: Diameter Required**

$$
d_{req} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 3333}{\pi \times 100 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{4.24 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 6.5 \; \text{mm}
$$

> **IRC Standard (mandatory):** Use **32 mm dia dowel** for highways — theoretical 6.5 mm is a *lower bound*; 32 mm provides a 5× safety margin against corrosion and construction damage.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Method Comparison

> **Table: Paving Method Decision Matrix**

| Parameter | Manual Method | Fixed-Form Paver | Slip-Form Paver |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Project Size** | < 500 m | 0.5–5 km | > 2 km |
| **Slump** | 50–75 mm | 25–50 mm | 0–25 mm |
| **Production** | 30–50 m/day | 100–200 m/day | 300–500 m/day |
| **Initial Cost** | Very Low | Medium | Very High |
| **Skill Needed** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Ride Quality** | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| **Equipment Cost (INR)** | 5–10 lakh | 1–2 crore | 6–10 crore |
| **Best Use** | Village roads, culverts | State highways, NH | Expressways, airports |

# 📚 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: Differentiate between dowel bars and tie bars used in concrete pavement joints.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Dowel Bar | Tie Bar |
|---------|-----------|---------|
| **Function** | Transfer wheel load between slabs | Hold adjacent lanes together |
| **Location** | Transverse joints | Longitudinal joints |
| **Surface** | Smooth (no ribs) | Deformed (ribbed) |
| **Diameter** | 25–40 mm (typically 32 mm) | 10–16 mm |
| **Embedment** | Half bonded, half free in sleeve | Fully bonded (mechanical anchorage) |
| **Allows** | Horizontal sliding (thermal) | No movement (rigid) |
| **Stress Carried** | Shear + bending | Pure tension |

> **(3 marks: 1 for function, 1 for surface & location, 1 for the stress type)**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q: What is a slip-form paver? List any three advantages over the fixed-form paver.**

**Model Answer:**
A **slip-form paver** is a self-contained concrete paving machine that shapes the slab edge using integral **side-forms (slip-forms)** rather than external fixed side forms, allowing continuous placement of the concrete slab.

**Three Advantages:**
1. **Higher production rate** (300–500 m/day) compared to fixed-form (100–200 m/day).
2. **Better ride quality** because there is no form-joint discontinuity in the slab surface.
3. **Lower labour cost** and reduced form-setting work.

> **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for advantages)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q: (a) Explain the procedure for presetting reinforcement in transverse and longitudinal joints of a concrete pavement. Sketch the layout of dowels and tie bars. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Procedure for Presetting Dowels (Transverse Joint):**
1. **Marking:** Transverse joint positions marked on the side forms using a chalk line.
2. **Cage Assembly:** A welded wire-mesh cage holding 8–10 dowels at 300 mm c/c is fabricated. Each dowel is 500 mm long, 32 mm dia, smooth MS bar.
3. **Chair Placement:** The cage is placed on **plastic/metal chairs** so that the dowel centre-line coincides with the **mid-depth** of the slab.
4. **Grease + Sleeve:** One half of the dowel is **graphite-greased** and inserted into a **PVC sleeve (50 mm dia, 250 mm long)**; the open end of the sleeve is **capped**.
5. **Alignment Check:** Vertical alignment ±3 mm; horizontal ±6 mm; checked by **pachometer or magnetic locator**.

**Procedure for Presetting Tie Bars (Longitudinal Joint):**
1. Tie bars (16 mm dia, 750 mm long, deformed Fe 500) are staked into the subbase at the *first* lane pour.
2. Half-length is **anchored mechanically** (stake or hook).
3. The other half **projects freely** into the second lane pour.

**Sketch (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LongitudinalJoint [Longitudinal Joint between Lane 1 and Lane 2]
        TB1[Tie Bar 16mm - Lane 1 Anchored Half]:::tie
        TB2[Tie Bar 16mm - Lane 2 Projecting Half]:::tie
    end
    subgraph TransverseJoint [Transverse Contraction Joint]
        DB1[Dowel 32mm Bonded Half in Slab 1]:::dowel
        DB2[Dowel 32mm Greased Sleeved Half in Slab 2]:::dowel
    end
    classDef tie fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d
    classDef dowel fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608
```

> **(7 marks: 3 for dowel procedure, 2 for tie bar procedure, 2 for sketch)**

**Q: (b) Discuss the different methods of construction of concrete pavements. Compare fixed-form and slip-form paving in detail. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The three methods of construction are:
1. **Manual Method** – hand placing, suited to small/irregular projects.
2. **Fixed-Form Method** – side forms act as rails for the paving machine; concrete placed by spreader, vibrated, screeded, and tined.
3. **Slip-Form Method** – the machine has integral side-forms; concrete is extruded continuously with no form joints.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Fixed-Form | Slip-Form |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Side Forms | External, fixed, pin-set | Integral in paver |
| Concrete Slump | 25–50 mm | 0–25 mm (zero-slump) |
| Productivity | 100–200 m/day | 300–500 m/day |
| Ride Quality | Good (form-joint marks possible) | Excellent (continuous) |
| Equipment Cost | Moderate | Very High |
| Skilled Operator | Required | Highly required |
| Form Resetting | Every 100–150 m | Not needed |

**Conclusion:** Slip-form is preferred for expressways, airports, and large projects; fixed-form is preferred for state highways and tight-radius works.

> **(7 marks: 2 for method listing, 4 for comparison table, 1 for conclusion)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q: (a) Describe the step-by-step construction procedure of a Plain Cement Concrete (PCC) pavement using the fixed-form paving method. List the equipment used in the paving train. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step-by-Step PCC Construction (Fixed-Form Method):**

1. **Subgrade Preparation** – Compaction to ≥ 98% MDD; surface levelled.
2. **Subbase Laying** – Dry Lean Concrete (DLC) subbase of 150 mm thickness, laid and cured for 7 days.
3. **Bond Breaker** – 125-micron polyethylene sheet laid over DLC.
4. **Side Form Setting** – Steel channel forms (height = slab thickness = 250 mm) set on stakes, levelled, and pinned.
5. **Presetting Dowels & Tie Bars** – Dowel cages placed at transverse joint positions; tie bars staked at longitudinal joint.
6. **Concrete Mixing & Transport** – M40 concrete mixed in batching plant, slump 25 mm, transported in transit mixers.
7. **Spreading** – Spreader distributes concrete in front of the paver.
8. **Vibration** – Internal needle vibrators (12,000 vibrations per minute) consolidate the concrete.
9. **Screeding** – Pan screed or roller screed levels and pre-compacts.
10. **Floating** – Bull float smooths the surface.
11. **Tining** – Tining machine creates transverse grooves (1.5–3 mm depth) for skid resistance.
12. **Curing** – White-pigmented curing compound sprayed at 0.2–0.4 kg/m² within 30 minutes of final finishing.
13. **Joint Saw Cutting** – Diamond blade saw cuts transverse contraction joints at 4.5 m spacing, 6–12 hours after placement.
14. **Joint Sealing** – Hot-poured rubberised sealant applied in saw-cut grooves.
15. **14-Day Water Curing** – Wet hessian or water spraying for 14 days.

**Paving Train Equipment:**
- Batching plant
- Transit mixers
- Concrete paver (form-paver)
- Spreader
- Internal vibrators
- Pan/Roller screed
- Bull float
- Tining machine
- Curing compound sprayer
- Diamond-blade joint saw
- Pneumatic needle vibrators

> **(7 marks: 5 for steps, 2 for equipment list)**

**Q: (b) A 250 mm thick concrete pavement slab of length 30 m contracts against the subbase. Design the longitudinal joint tie bars. Take: f = 1.5 (friction coefficient), f_s = 230 MPa, unit weight of concrete = 24 kN/m³, and allowable spacing of 500 mm c/c. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Slab thickness $t = 0.25$ m
- Slab length $L = 30$ m
- Friction coefficient $f = 1.5$
- Allowable tensile stress $f_s = 230$ MPa
- Unit weight $\rho_c = 24$ kN/m³
- Spacing $s = 500$ mm c/c

**Step 1: Frictional Force per metre width**

$$
F_w = \rho_c \cdot t \cdot f \cdot L
$$

$$
F_w = 24 \times 0.25 \times 1.5 \times 30 = 270 \; \text{kN/m}
$$

**Step 2: Required Steel Area per metre**

$$
A_{tie} = \dfrac{F_w}{f_s} = \dfrac{270 \times 10^3}{230 \times 10^6} = 1.174 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2/\text{m}
$$

$$
A_{tie} = 1174 \; \text{mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

**Step 3: Steel Provided per metre with 500 mm spacing**

$$
A_{provided} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \times \dfrac{1000}{500} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{2}
$$

**Step 4: Equate and Solve for Diameter**

$$
\dfrac{\pi d^2}{2} = 1174
$$

$$
d^2 = \dfrac{2 \times 1174}{\pi} = 747.5
$$

$$
d = 27.3 \; \text{mm}
$$

> **Result:** Use **28 mm dia bars @ 500 mm c/c** (commercial size: 28 mm). If unavailable, use **32 mm dia @ 600 mm c/c** (IRC:15-2011 maximum recommended).

> **(7 marks: 2 for frictional force, 2 for required area, 2 for solving diameter, 1 for final answer)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Points (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Mixing up dowels and tie bars** in sketches — even a single bar drawn as deformed instead of smooth in a dowel position will cost **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in derivations — KTU board deducts 0.5 marks for every dimension without unit (especially in numerical sub-questions).
> 3. **Not mentioning the "half-bonded, half-sleeved" convention** for dowels — this is the most-tested concept; stating only "smooth" is incomplete.
> 4. **Stating "slip-form is faster"** without quantification (300–500 m/day) — you lose the comparison depth.
> 5. **Skipping curing compound quantity** in the construction procedure (0.2–0.4 kg/m²) — this is a recurring KTU *memory* test.
> 6. **Mentioning "longitudinal joint" as contraction joint** — *never*. Longitudinal = tied joint; transverse = contraction/expansion.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Final High-Density Revision Checklist (For the night before exam):**

- ✅ **Dowel = Smooth, round, sleeved on one side** → Transverse joints → 32 mm dia, 500 mm long, 300 mm c/c.
- ✅ **Tie Bar = Deformed, fully bonded** → Longitudinal joints → 16 mm dia, 750 mm long, 500 mm c/c.
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** *"Dowels Tie Smoothly" → Dowel = **T**ied across **S**leeve = **S**mooth*.
- ✅ **Three paving methods:** Manual, Fixed-Form, Slip-Form.
- ✅ **Slip-form concrete = zero-slump (0–25 mm)**; Fixed-form = 25–50 mm.
- ✅ **Joint spacing ≤ 4.5 m** for plain jointed concrete pavement on Indian highways (IRC:15-2011).
- ✅ **Tining depth = 1.5 to 3 mm** for surface texture (skid resistance).
- ✅ **Curing compound = 0.2 to 0.4 kg/m²**, applied within 30 min of finishing.
- ✅ **Saw-cut window = 6 to 12 hours** after placement, before random cracking starts.
- ✅ **Dowel alignment tolerance = ±3 mm vertical, ±6 mm horizontal** (IRC:15-2011).
- ✅ **Bond breaker = 125 micron polyethylene sheet** over DLC subbase.
- ✅ **LTE target ≥ 70%** for efficient load transfer at transverse joints.
- ✅ **14-day wet curing** mandatory for PCC pavement before traffic opening.
- ✅ **Friction coefficient f = 1.5 (DLC subbase), 2.0 (granular subbase)** in tie bar design.
- ✅ **Typical slab thickness:** 200–300 mm for NH/SH; 350–400 mm for airports and container terminals.
- ✅ **Paving train sequence:** Spreader → Vibrator → Screed → Float → Tining → Curing spray → Saw cut.

> **Last-Line Mantra for the Exam Hall:** *"Dowel smooth, tie deformed, slip-form fastest, fixed-form reliable, cure fourteen days, saw-cut within twelve hours."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Pavement Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET745 - PAVEMENT CONSTRUCTION AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Pavement Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECET745 – Module 4)**
> **Pavement Evaluation** is the systematic and scientific process of assessing the *functional*, *structural*, and *safety* condition of a road pavement at a given point in time, in order to determine its *remaining service life*, identify *distress mechanisms*, and recommend appropriate **Maintenance & Rehabilitation (M&R)** strategies.

Pavement evaluation is essentially the **"health check-up" of a road**. Just as a doctor prescribes tests (blood pressure, ECG, X-ray) to assess human health, a pavement engineer uses a suite of *non-destructive* and *destructive* tests to assess road health. The output is a **Pavement Condition Index (PCI)** or **Present Serviceability Index (PSI)** that quantifies how "fit for use" the pavement is.

The evaluation is mandated by **IRC:SP:30** and **MoRTH** specifications, and is broadly classified into three domains:

| Domain | What it Measures | Primary Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Functional Evaluation** | *Ride quality* & surface defects | **IRI**, **PCI**, **PSI** |
| **Structural Evaluation** | *Load-bearing capacity* | **Deflection (mm)**, **Elastic Modulus** |
| **Safety Evaluation** | *Skid resistance* & texture | **Skid Number (SN)**, **Texture Depth (mm)** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Patient & Hospital" Model

Imagine a road network is a **human body**, and individual pavements are **patients** visiting a hospital:

- **Distress Survey** = A doctor checking symptoms (fever, cough, pain).
- **Functional Tests (IRI, PCI)** = Routine blood test — tells you *if* something is wrong.
- **Structural Tests (FWD, Benkelman Beam)** = MRI / X-ray — tells you *how deep* the problem is inside.
- **Safety Tests (Skid Resistance)** = Heart-rate monitor — tells you *if it's safe to operate*.
- **Pavement Management System (PMS)** = Hospital records — keeps track of every patient (road section) over time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Evaluate?**
> The primary purpose is **Network-Level Decision Making**. With limited budgets, road authorities (PWD, NHAI, KSDP) must prioritize which roads need **immediate repair**, which need **routine maintenance**, and which are **still healthy**. Without evaluation, money is spent reactively ("worst-first" approach) instead of proactively.

## 1.3 Hierarchy of Evaluation

The evaluation is typically conducted at two spatial scales:

1. **Network Level (Macro):** Quick, low-cost screening of *entire road network* (e.g., all NHs in Kerala). Uses visual surveys, high-speed profilometers, and skid trailers. Determines priority.
2. **Project Level (Micro):** Detailed investigation of *specific road segments* flagged at network level. Uses FWD, GPR, coring, and laboratory tests. Determines the *exact rehabilitation strategy*.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** A 3-mark question may ask "List the types of pavement evaluation." The expected answer is **Functional, Structural, and Safety** — always write all three, never just one.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Pavement Evaluation

### Pillar A — Functional Evaluation
Functional evaluation answers the question: *"How comfortable and smooth is the ride?"*

**Key Distress Types (Visual):**
- **Cracking:** Alligator/fatigue cracks, longitudinal, transverse, edge, block.
- **Rutting:** Permanent longitudinal depression in wheel paths.
- **Raveling:** Loss of aggregate from surface (asphalt wears away).
- **Potholes:** Bowl-shaped depressions through the pavement layer.
- **Patching & Potholes:** Indicator of past repairs (utility cuts, etc.).
- **Riding Quality:** Quantified via **IRI** (International Roughness Index) in **m/km**.

### Pillar B — Structural Evaluation
Structural evaluation answers: *"Can the pavement still carry the traffic loads?"*

**Core Concept — Deflection:**
When a standard load (typically a 40 kN axle load on dual tires at 0.56 MPa tire pressure) is applied, the pavement deflects. **Greater deflection = weaker pavement**. Measured in **mm** or **mils (1 mil = 0.0254 mm)**.

**Key Tests:**
- **Benkelman Beam (BB):** Static, slow-moving, traditional IRC method.
- **Falling Weight Deflectometer (FWD):** Impulse load, modern, fast, simulates moving traffic.
- **Plate Bearing Test:** Destructive, used for new construction on subgrade.

### Pillar C — Safety Evaluation
Safety evaluation answers: *"Will vehicles skid, especially in wet conditions?"*

**Key Parameter — Skid Resistance:**
Measured as **Skid Number (SN)** or **Skid Resistance Value (SRV)** using a British Pendulum Tester (BPT) or a skid trailer. For high-speed highways, **SN ≥ 40** is typically required (MoRTH/IRC norms).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these equations. They are repeated in almost every past paper.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PSI = 5.03 - 1.91 \log(1+SV) - 1.38 RD^{2} - 0.01 \sqrt{C+P}$ | AASHTO Present Serviceability Index | Dimensionless (0–5) |
| 2 | $PCI = 100 - CDV_{max}$ | Pavement Condition Index (ASTM D6433) | 0–100 scale |
| 3 | $IRI = \frac{\text{Cumulative Suspension Motion}}{\text{Distance Travelled}}$ | International Roughness Index | m/km |
| 4 | $SN = \frac{F_h}{W_v} \times 100$ | Skid Number (Friction/Load × 100) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $D_0 = \frac{1.5 \, p \, a \, f}{E_s} \, F_2$ | Surface Deflection under loaded plate (Boussinesq) | mm |
| 6 | $\text{Deduct Value}, DV = f(\text{Type}, \text{Severity}, \text{Density})$ | ASTM PCI distress deduction chart | 0–100 |
| 7 | $CDV = q \times CDV_{adj}$ | Corrected Deduct Value (from curves) | 0–100 |
| 8 | $SV = \frac{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^{2}}{n-1}$ | Slope Variance for roughness | (in/m)² |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Symbol Note:** All vertical bars in equations (e.g., absolute value, modulus) have been rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

## 2.3 Distress Types — The "Big Five" for KTU

| Distress | Cause | Severity Indicator |
|---|---|---|
| **Alligator Cracking** | Fatigue from repeated load | Interconnected polygonal pattern |
| **Longitudinal Cracking** | Poor construction joint / fatigue | Parallel to traffic direction |
| **Rutting** | Permanent deformation in subgrade/HMA | Depth in **mm** |
| **Raveling** | Loss of binder / aging | % area affected |
| **Bleeding** | Excess bitumen / high temperature | % area covered |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Budget Justification:** PCI/IRI data is used to compute remaining life and request funding from MoRTH.
- **Warranty Enforcement:** Contractors in EPC projects must maintain IRI ≤ 2.5 m/km for 5 years.
- **Toll Road Design:** NHAI uses skid number ≥ 50 for high-speed corridors.
- **Airport Pavements:** FAA uses **PCI** exclusively for airfield management.
- **Smart City Projects:** Cities like Kochi use IRI data for citizen-facing road quality dashboards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the AASHTO PSI Equation

The **Present Serviceability Index (PSI)** was developed during the **AASHO Road Test (1958–1961)** in Ottawa, Illinois. A panel of raters rated pavements on a **0 to 5 scale** (5 = perfect, 0 = impassable). Multiple linear regression against measured physical parameters yielded the equation.

**Step 1: Define the dependent variable.**

$$PSI = a_0 + a_1 \cdot SV + a_2 \cdot RD + a_3 \cdot (C+P)$$

Where:
- $SV$ = Slope Variance of longitudinal profile (a measure of roughness)
- $RD$ = Rut Depth (average of both wheel paths, in inches)
- $C+P$ = Area of Class 2 & 3 Cracking plus Patching (as % of total area)

**Step 2: Transform variables for better fit.**

The AASHTO statisticians found that *logarithmic* transformation of $(1+SV)$ and *square-root* transformation of $(C+P)$ improved correlation. After regression analysis:

$$\boxed{PSI = 5.03 - 1.91 \log(1+SV) - 1.38 RD^{2} - 0.01 \sqrt{C+P}}$$

**Step 3: Interpret each coefficient.**

- The constant **5.03** represents an idealized pavement with no distress, no rutting, and no roughness — theoretically the maximum.
- Coefficient **−1.91** (with $\log(1+SV)$): roughness has the largest individual impact.
- Coefficient **−1.38** (with $RD^{2}$): rutting has a *quadratic* penalty — even small ruts hurt serviceability disproportionately.
- Coefficient **−0.01** (with $\sqrt{C+P}$): cracking and patching have a milder effect.

**Step 4: Apply a Numerical Example (Worked-Out).**

Given a pavement section with:
- $SV = 50$ (slope variance)
- $RD = 0.5$ inches
- $C+P = 25\%$ cracked/patched area

$$\log(1 + 50) = \log(51) = 1.7076$$
$$RD^{2} = (0.5)^{2} = 0.25$$
$$\sqrt{C+P} = \sqrt{25} = 5.0$$

$$PSI = 5.03 - (1.91 \times 1.7076) - (1.38 \times 0.25) - (0.01 \times 5.0)$$

$$PSI = 5.03 - 3.2615 - 0.345 - 0.05$$

$$\boxed{PSI = 1.37}$$

**Step 5: Interpret the Result.**

A PSI of **1.37** is **Very Poor** (the AASHTO scale: ≥ 4.0 = Excellent; 2.0–3.0 = Fair; < 2.0 = Poor). This pavement **needs immediate overlay or rehabilitation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important in KTU?** Part-B questions often present you with distress data and ask you to compute PSI. Always show **all five steps** for full marks.

## 3.2 Derivation Logic for PCI (Pavement Condition Index)

PCI is computed per **ASTM D6433** in a strict 4-step process:

**Step A: Divide pavement into sample units** (typically 250 m² for asphalt; 20 slabs for concrete).

**Step B: Identify distress types** (typically 19 for AC, 15 for PCC) and measure:
- **Severity:** Low (L), Medium (M), High (H)
- **Density:** % of sample unit affected = (Affected Area / Total Unit Area) × 100

**Step C: Look up Deduct Value (DV).** Using *standard deduct curves* (one per distress), enter the curve at the *density* on the x-axis to read off the *DV* on the y-axis. Sum all DVs to get **Total Deduct Value (TDV)**.

$$\text{TDV} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} DV_i$$

**Step D: Compute Corrected Deduct Value (CDV).**
- Let $q$ = number of DVs > 5. (For full precision, also use iterative reduction.)
- Read $CDV$ from the **CDV correction curve** (an "S-shaped" envelope curve) by entering with $q$ on x-axis and TDV on y-axis.

$$PCI_{sample} = 100 - CDV$$

**Step E: Average over all sample units** to get the network-level PCI.

$$\overline{PCI} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} PCI_j$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Method?

| Decision Criterion | Visual Survey | Profilometer (IRI) | Benkelman Beam | FWD | Skid Trailer |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
